content:
[1] Then Job answered:

[2] If only my anguish could be weighed, my ruin loaded onto the scales—

[3] it would tip heavier than all the sand of the sea. That is why my words come out wild.

[4] The Almighty's arrows are buried in me. My spirit drinks their poison. God's terrors line up against me in battle formation.

[5] Does a wild donkey bray when it has grass? Does an ox bellow over a full trough?

[6] Can tasteless food be eaten without salt? Is there any flavor in the slime of an egg white?

[7] I refuse to touch such things—they turn my stomach like rotten meat.

[8] If only my request would land. If only God would grant what I'm begging for—

[9] that he would crush me, finally. Let his hand fall and cut me loose.

[10] Then at least I'd have this comfort, this one fierce joy through unrelenting pain: I never silenced the words of the Holy One.

[11] What strength do I have left to keep hoping? What future am I holding out for, to keep enduring?

[12] Am I made of stone? Is my flesh forged from bronze?

[13] I have nothing left in me. Every resource has been stripped away.

[14] A man drowning deserves loyalty from his friend, even if he's abandoned the fear of the Almighty.

[15] But my brothers? Treacherous as a desert streambed—a flash flood that vanishes by noon,

[16] dark with ice in winter, swollen with melting snow,

[17] then gone the moment the heat comes. When you need them most, they evaporate.

[18] Caravans turn off course searching for them, wander into wasteland, and die there.

[19] The traders from Tema scan the horizon. The travelers from Sheba pin their hopes on water.

[20] They arrive certain—and stand humiliated. They came expecting life and found nothing but sand.

[21] That is what you have become to me. You looked at my disaster and recoiled in fear.

[22] Did I ever say, "Give me something"? Did I ask you to bribe somebody on my behalf?

[23] Did I beg you to rescue me from an enemy, to ransom me from the hand of some tyrant?

[24] Teach me, and I'll go quiet. Show me where I went wrong.

[25] Honest words cut deep—but what does your scolding actually prove?

[26] Do you intend to correct what I said? Are you treating the cries of a desperate man like empty wind?

[27] You'd cast lots for an orphan. You'd haggle over the body of your friend.

[28] Look at me. Really look. I am not lying to your face.

[29] Reconsider. Don't twist this into injustice. Reconsider—my integrity is still on the line.

[30] Is there deceit on my tongue? Can my mouth not taste the difference between right and wrong?

[1] Isn't human life on earth nothing but forced labor? Aren't our days just the grinding shifts of a hired hand?

[2] Like a slave gasping for the shade, like a worker counting the hours until payday—

[3] that's me. Months of emptiness handed to me like an inheritance. Nights of misery assigned to me like a job.

[4] I lie down and immediately wonder: when do I get up? The night drags on, and I toss until dawn finally bleeds through.

[5] My skin is crawling with maggots and crusted dirt. My flesh splits open and oozes.

[6] My days fly faster than a weaver's shuttle and snap off without thread, without hope.

[7] Remember this, God: my life is one breath. My eyes will never see anything good again.

[8] The eye watching me now won't find me tomorrow. You'll look for me—and I'll be gone.

[9] Like a cloud thinning into nothing, that's how it works: down to the grave, and you don't come back.

[10] Never returning home. The place you lived forgets you ever existed.

[11] So I won't shut up. I'll speak from the suffocation in my chest. I'll complain from the bitterness in my soul.

[12] What am I—the Sea? Some chaos monster? Is that why you've set a guard over me?

[13] When I tell myself, "My bed will comfort me, my couch will absorb my grief"—

[14] you terrorize me with dreams. You ambush me with visions until I'm shaking.

[15] I'd rather be strangled. I'd rather be dead than live in this body.

[16] I'm done. I won't live forever. Leave me alone—my days are nothing but vapor.

[17] What is a human being that you make such a big deal of him? Why fix your attention on him at all?

[18] Why inspect him every morning, test him every moment?

[19] Will you ever look away from me? Will you give me one second to swallow my own spit?

[20] So I sinned. What did I actually do to you, you watcher of humanity? Why have you made me your target? Why have I become a burden to you?

[21] Why not just forgive what I did? Why not let my guilt go? Soon I'll lie down in the dust. You'll come looking—and I won't be there.

[1] Then Bildad the Shuhite spoke up:

[2] "How long are you going to keep this up? Your words are nothing but hot wind.

[3] Does God twist justice? Does the Almighty pervert what's right?

[4] If your children sinned against him, he handed them over to the consequences of their own rebellion.

[5] But you — if you would seek God now, plead with the Almighty for mercy,

[6] if you are clean and upright, he will rouse himself for you and restore the house that should be yours.

[7] Your beginnings will look small compared to what comes after.

[8] Ask the generations before us. Listen to what their fathers learned.

[9] We were born yesterday — we know nothing. Our days on earth are a passing shadow.

[10] But they will teach you. They will speak from the depth of their understanding:

[11] Does papyrus shoot up where there's no marsh? Can reeds flourish without water?

[12] Still green, uncut — they wither faster than any other plant.

[13] That's the path of everyone who forgets God. The hope of the godless dies on the vine.

[14] What he leans on snaps. What he trusts is a spider's web —

[15] he grabs at his house and it won't hold. He clings to it and it collapses.

[16] He's like a lush plant in the sunlight, sending shoots across the whole garden,

[17] roots wrapping around the stone pile, gripping the rocks for life.

[18] But pull him up from his place and the ground denies him: 'I never saw you.'

[19] So much for the joy of his existence. Out of that same soil, others spring up to take his spot.

[20] God will not throw away a man of integrity, and he will not strengthen the hand of those who do evil.

[21] He will yet fill your mouth with laughter and your lips with shouts of joy.

[22] Those who hate you will be dressed in shame. The tent of the wicked will be no more."

[1] Then Job answered:

[2] "Yes, I know it. That's exactly how it works. But how can a mortal stand acquitted before God?

[3] If someone wanted to argue the case with him, he couldn't answer one question in a thousand.

[4] His mind is deep. His strength is staggering. Who has ever braced against him and walked away whole?

[5] He moves mountains before they know what hit them, overturns them in his fury.

[6] He shakes the earth loose from its foundation; its pillars tremble.

[7] He gives the sun an order and it doesn't rise. He puts the stars under seal.

[8] He stretched out the heavens alone. He walks across the back of the sea.

[9] He made the Bear, Orion, the Pleiades, the constellations of the south.

[10] He does immense things, past finding out — wonders beyond counting.

[11] He passes right by me and I don't see him. He sweeps past and I don't catch a glimpse.

[12] If he snatches something away, who stops him? Who dares ask, 'What are you doing?'

[13] God does not pull back his anger. Even the helpers of Rahab cower at his feet.

[14] So how could I answer him? How could I find the right words to argue my case?

[15] Even if I were in the right, I couldn't reply — I could only beg my judge for mercy.

[16] Even if I called and he answered, I wouldn't believe he was actually listening to me.

[17] He would crush me with a storm and multiply my wounds for no reason.

[18] He wouldn't let me catch my breath — he would saturate me with bitterness.

[19] If it's a contest of strength — look, he's overwhelming. If it's a courtroom — who could summon him?

[20] Even if I were innocent, my own mouth would condemn me. Even blameless, it would prove me twisted.

[21] I am blameless. I don't even know myself anymore. I despise my life.

[22] It's all the same. So I'll say it: he destroys the blameless and the wicked alike.

[23] When disaster swings down and kills without warning, he laughs at the despair of the innocent.

[24] The earth is handed over to the wicked. He blindfolds its judges. If it isn't him, then who?

[25] My days run faster than a sprinter. They flee. They never see anything good.

[26] They glide by like reed boats, like an eagle diving on its prey.

[27] If I say, 'I'll forget my complaint, change my expression, put on a smile' —

[28] I'm still terrified of every ache, because I know you won't declare me innocent.

[29] I've already been condemned. Why exhaust myself for nothing?

[30] If I scrubbed myself with snow and washed my hands with lye,

[31] you would still dunk me in a pit until even my own clothes recoiled from me.

[32] He's not a man like me, that I could answer him, that we could meet in court together.

[33] There's no referee between us, no one to lay a hand on us both,

[34] no one to pull back his rod from me, to keep his terror from leveling me.

[35] Then I would speak up and not be afraid of him. But as things stand — I can't."

[1] I'm sick of living. I'll let my complaint run free. I'll speak from the bitterness pooled in my soul.

[2] I'll say to God: don't condemn me. Tell me what charge you're bringing against me.

[3] Does it please you to crush me, to despise the work of your own hands while smiling on the schemes of the wicked?

[4] Do you see with human eyes? Do you look the way mortals look?

[5] Are your days like a man's days, your years like ours,

[6] that you have to hunt down my guilt and dig through my sins,

[7] when you already know I'm not guilty — and no one can pull me out of your hand?

[8] Your hands shaped me, formed me, every contour. And now you turn around and destroy me?

[9] Remember — you molded me out of clay. Will you grind me back to dust?

[10] Didn't you pour me out like milk, set me like cheese?

[11] You clothed me in skin and flesh, wove me together with bones and sinews.

[12] You gave me life. You poured out faithful love on me. Your care kept my breath going.

[13] But this — this is what you were hiding all along. I know it now.

[14] If I sinned, you were watching. You wouldn't let my guilt slide.

[15] If I'm guilty — disaster. If I'm innocent — I still can't lift my head, drowning in shame, soaked in misery.

[16] If I rise up, you stalk me like a lion. You unleash wonders against me, just to amaze yourself.

[17] You bring fresh witnesses against me, stoke your anger hotter, send wave after wave of assault.

[18] So why did you drag me out of the womb? I should have died before any eye saw me —

[19] as if I never existed, carried straight from belly to grave.

[20] My days are nearly done. Back off. Let me have a moment of relief

[21] before I go where there is no coming back — to the land of darkness, of deepest shadow,

[22] a land of midnight, of chaos, of black gloom where even the light is darkness.

[1] Then Zophar of Naamah spoke up:

[2] "All these words — should they go unanswered? Does a man talk himself into being right?

[3] Your empty boasting — should it silence us? When you mock, will no one shame you?

[4] You claim: 'My doctrine is pure. I am clean in God's sight.'

[5] Oh, if only God would speak — open his lips against you —

[6] reveal the secrets of wisdom, for true understanding has two sides. Then you'd see: God is letting you off easy. He's exacting less than your guilt deserves.

[7] Can you fathom the deep things of God? Can you trace the outer limits of the Almighty?

[8] Higher than heaven — what can you do? Deeper than Sheol — what can you know?

[9] Longer than the earth in its measure. Wider than the sea.

[10] If he sweeps through, locks a man up, convenes the court — who can stop him?

[11] He sees through worthless men. He spots evil without even looking twice.

[12] But a hollow man will get wisdom about the same time a wild donkey is born tame.

[13] Now — if you would steady your heart, stretch out your hands to him,

[14] if you'd put away the wrong in your grip and refuse to let injustice sleep in your tent —

[15] then you could lift your face without shame. You'd stand firm, no fear in you.

[16] You'd forget your suffering — remember it like floodwater that drained away.

[17] Your life would shine brighter than noon. The darkness would become like morning.

[18] You'd be secure — there is hope. You'd look around and lie down in safety.

[19] You'd stretch out, and no one would startle you. Many would seek your favor.

[20] But the eyes of the wicked will fail. Every escape route — closed. Their only hope: the last breath leaving the body."

[1] Then Job answered:

[2] "Oh, no doubt you are the people, and wisdom will die with you.

[3] But I have a mind too. I am not beneath you. Who doesn't know what you've been saying?

[4] I have become a joke to my friends — me, the one who called on God and got an answer. A righteous man, blameless — a punchline.

[5] Comfortable people sneer at disaster, ready to shove anyone whose foot slips.

[6] Meanwhile, the tents of robbers stand untouched. Those who provoke God live secure — gods in their own hands.

[7] But ask the animals — they'll teach you. The birds of the sky — they'll tell you.

[8] Or speak to the earth, and it will instruct you. The fish of the sea will spell it out.

[9] Which one of them doesn't know that the hand of the LORD did this?

[10] Every living thing breathes at his pleasure. Every human breath is his to give.

[11] Doesn't the ear test words the way the tongue tastes food?

[12] Wisdom belongs to the old, they say. Long life delivers understanding.

[13] But wisdom and power belong to him. Counsel and understanding are his.

[14] What he tears down stays down. Who he locks in, no one frees.

[15] He withholds the rain, and the land cracks. He releases it, and the land drowns.

[16] Strength and clear sight are his. The deceiver and the deceived — both belong to him.

[17] He marches counselors away barefoot. He turns judges into fools.

[18] He strips kings of their authority and ties a rag around their waist.

[19] He marches priests away barefoot. He topples the ones who seemed unshakable.

[20] He silences the trusted advisors and takes away the judgment of the elders.

[21] He pours contempt on princes and loosens the belt of the strong.

[22] He drags hidden things out of the dark and hauls the deepest shadow into daylight.

[23] He builds nations up, then destroys them. He spreads peoples wide, then scatters them.

[24] He strips the wits from the leaders of the earth and sets them wandering in a trackless waste.

[25] They grope in darkness with no light. He makes them stagger like drunks."

[1] My eyes have seen all this. My ears have heard it and understood. [2] What you know, I know too. I am not beneath you. [3] But I would rather speak to the Almighty. I want to argue my case with God himself.

[4] You — you smear lies like plaster. You are worthless physicians, every one of you. [5] If only you would shut your mouths. That would be your wisdom. [6] Now listen to my argument. Pay attention to the plea on my lips.

[7] Will you speak wickedly on God's behalf? Will you lie for him? [8] Will you play favorites for him? Will you argue God's case like hired lawyers? [9] What happens when he examines you? Can you fool him the way you fool a man? [10] He will rip into you — he will — if you secretly take sides. [11] His majesty alone should terrify you. His dread should fall on you. [12] Your wise sayings are ashes. Your defenses are walls of mud.

[13] Be quiet. Let me speak. Then let whatever comes, come. [14] I will take my flesh in my teeth. I will hold my life in my own hands. [15] Even if he kills me, I will hope in him. Still — I will defend my ways to his face. [16] And this itself will be my deliverance: no godless man would dare come before him.

[17] Listen carefully to my words. Let what I say ring in your ears. [18] I have prepared my case. I know I will be vindicated. [19] Who is there to contend with me? If anyone can, I will be silent and die.

[20] Only grant me two things, God, and I will not hide from your face: [21] Lift your hand off me. Stop terrifying me with your dread. [22] Then call, and I will answer. Or let me speak, and you reply.

[23] How many sins do I have? How many wrongs? Show me my rebellion. Show me my offense. [24] Why do you hide your face? Why treat me like your enemy? [25] Will you terrorize a leaf the wind is already chasing? Will you hunt down a piece of dry straw? [26] You write bitter charges against me. You make me inherit the sins of my youth. [27] You shackle my feet. You watch every path I take. You carve marks into the soles of my feet.

[28] And the man himself wastes away like something rotten, like a garment chewed through by moths.

[1] Mortals, born of women — short on days, packed with trouble.

[2] We bloom like wildflowers and wither. We pass like shadows across a wall, never staying.

[3] And on this — this fragile, fleeting thing — you fix your gaze? You drag me into your courtroom?

[4] Who can pull something clean out of something filthy? No one.

[5] Our days are counted. You set the number of our months. You drew the line we cannot step past.

[6] So look away from us. Give us peace. Let us at least enjoy our day like a hired hand finishing his shift.

[7] A tree has more hope than we do. Cut it down and it sprouts again — fresh shoots keep coming.

[8] Its roots may grow old in the dirt, its stump may die in the ground —

[9] but catch the scent of water and it bursts back to life, throwing out branches like a young sapling.

[10] But a man? A man dies and lies broken. He breathes his last — and where is he?

[11] Water drains from the sea. Rivers run dry and crack open.

[12] So we lie down and do not rise. Not until the heavens themselves are gone, we will not wake, we will not stir from our sleep.

[13] If only you would hide me in the grave — tuck me away until your anger burns out — set a time, then remember me.

[14] When a man dies, will he live again? I would wait out every day of my hard service, waiting for my relief to come.

[15] You would call, and I would answer. You would ache for the creature your own hands made.

[16] But now — you count every step I take. You miss nothing of my sin.

[17] My offenses are sealed up in a bag, stitched shut. You plaster over my guilt and keep it.

[18] Mountains erode and fall. Cliffs are torn from their place.

[19] Water grinds stones to dust. Floods wash away the soil. And so you wash away human hope.

[20] You overpower us, once and for all — we are gone. You change the look on our face and send us away.

[21] His sons rise to honor, and he never knows. They sink into disgrace, and he never sees it.

[22] He feels only the pain in his own body. He mourns only for himself.

[1] Then Eliphaz the Temanite came back at him:

[2] "Does a wise man fill his belly with hot air, stuffing his gut with the east wind?

[3] Does he argue with empty talk, with speeches that accomplish nothing?

[4] You — you're dismantling reverence itself, gutting the very practice of prayer before God.

[5] Your guilt is coaching your mouth. You've picked the tongue of the cunning.

[6] Your own mouth condemns you, not mine. Your own lips testify against you.

[7] Were you the firstborn of humanity? Were you delivered before the hills came up?

[8] Did you sit in on God's private council? Did you corner the market on wisdom?

[9] What do you know that we don't? What insight do you have that's somehow missed us?

[10] We have gray hair on our side — old men, far older than your father.

[11] Are God's comforts too small for you? Is the word that came gently to you not enough?

[12] Why does your heart drag you off? Why the flash in your eyes,

[13] that you'd turn your spirit against God and let words like this come pouring out of your mouth?

[14] What is a mortal that he should be clean? What is a man born of woman that he should stand righteous?

[15] Look — God doesn't even trust his holy ones. The heavens themselves are not pure in his sight.

[16] How much less a human being — vile, corrupt, swallowing wrongdoing like water.

[17] Listen to me. I'll tell you what I've seen. I'll spell out what I know,

[18] what the wise have passed down — what their fathers told them and didn't hide,

[19] back when the land belonged to them alone and no stranger walked among them.

[20] The wicked man writhes in pain all his days, all the years stored up for the tyrant.

[21] Terror screams in his ears. When everything looks peaceful, the destroyer comes for him.

[22] He doesn't believe he'll make it back from the dark. The sword has his name on it.

[23] He wanders looking for bread — where is it? He knows the day of darkness is right there at his elbow.

[24] Distress and anguish overwhelm him, charging him like a king ready for battle.

[25] Because he raised his fist against God. Because he played the strongman against the Almighty —

[26] running at him head down, shield thick and braced —

[27] though his face has gone fat with prosperity and his thighs bulge with excess,

[28] he'll settle in ruined cities, in houses no one inhabits, doomed to crumble into rubble.

[29] He won't grow rich. His wealth won't last. His possessions won't take root in the earth.

[30] He won't escape the darkness. A flame will wither his shoots. The breath of God's mouth will sweep him away.

[31] Let him not fool himself by trusting in emptiness — emptiness is all he'll get back.

[32] Before his time it will all be finished. His branch will not stay green.

[33] He'll be like a vine that drops its unripe grapes, like an olive tree shedding its blossoms.

[34] The company of the godless is barren. Fire eats the tents of those who took bribes.

[35] They conceive misery and birth disaster. Their womb is busy producing deceit."

[1] Then Job answered:

[2] "I've heard all this before. What miserable comforters you are, every one of you.

[3] Is there no end to your windy speeches? What's eating at you that you keep firing back?

[4] I could talk like you if our places were swapped. I could string together fine phrases against you and shake my head in your direction.

[5] But I wouldn't. I'd use my mouth to strengthen you. The movement of my lips would bring relief.

[6] Yet when I speak, my pain doesn't lift. When I hold my tongue, it doesn't leave me.

[7] God has worn me down. He's emptied my whole household.

[8] He's shriveled me up — and that itself stands as testimony against me. My gauntness rises up and accuses me to my face.

[9] His anger tears at me, hunts me down. He grinds his teeth at me. My enemy sharpens his eyes on me like blades.

[10] People gape at me with open mouths. They slap my cheek in contempt. They mass against me as one.

[11] God has handed me over to the wicked, dumped me into the grip of the guilty.

[12] I was at ease — and he shattered me. He grabbed me by the neck and smashed me to pieces. He set me up as his target.

[13] His archers surround me. He splits my kidneys open without mercy. He pours my bile onto the ground.

[14] He breaks me open — breach after breach — charges at me like a warrior.

[15] I've stitched sackcloth onto my skin. I've buried my crown in the dust.

[16] My face is swollen red from weeping. Deep shadows ring my eyes.

[17] And there's no violence in my hands. My prayer has been clean.

[18] Earth — do not cover my blood. Let my cry never find a resting place.

[19] Even now — look — my witness is in heaven. The one who vouches for me is on high.

[20] My friends mock me, but my eyes weep tears toward God,

[21] pleading the case of a man with God the way a man pleads for his friend.

[22] Because the years are short, and I'm walking the road I won't walk back."

[1] My spirit is shattered. My days, snuffed out. The grave gapes open for me.

[2] Mockers swarm around me. My eyes can't escape their contempt.

[3] God, You be my guarantor. Put up the bond Yourself — who else would shake hands on my behalf?

[4] You've sealed their minds against understanding. That's why You won't let them have the last word.

[5] A man who sells out his friends for a meal — his own children's eyes will fail watching for relief that never comes.

[6] He's made me a byword among the nations, a face people spit in.

[7] Grief has hollowed out my eyes. My limbs are shadows of themselves.

[8] Honest men are stunned at this; the innocent rise up against the godless.

[9] Yet the righteous holds his course, and the man with clean hands grows stronger still.

[10] But come back at me, all of you — try again. I won't find one wise man among you.

[11] My days have run out. My plans, ripped apart. The desires of my heart — gone.

[12] These so-called comforters call night day, claim light is breaking when darkness presses in.

[13] If my only hope is the grave — if I make my bed in the dark —

[14] if I call the pit "Father" and the worm "Mother" and "Sister" —

[15] then where is my hope? Who can even see it?

[16] It descends with me to the bars of death. Together we sink into the dust.

[1] Then Bildad the Shuhite fired back:

[2] "How long until you stop this flood of words? Get a grip, then we'll talk.

[3] Why do you treat us like cattle? Like we're too dense to follow?

[4] You tear yourself apart in your rage — does the earth get abandoned for your sake? Do mountains uproot themselves to accommodate you?

[5] No — the lamp of the wicked goes dark. The fire on his hearth gives up its last spark.

[6] The light in his tent dims to nothing. The lamp above him snuffs out.

[7] His confident stride shortens to a stumble. His own schemes trip him.

[8] His feet walk him straight into the net. He wanders into the snare.

[9] The trap catches his heel. The noose tightens around him.

[10] The cord lies hidden in the dust, the trap waiting on the path.

[11] Terrors stalk him from every side, nipping at his heels.

[12] His strength wastes away with hunger. Disaster crouches at his door, ready to pounce.

[13] Disease eats his skin piece by piece. Death's firstborn devours his limbs.

[14] He's ripped from the tent he trusted, dragged to face the king of terrors.

[15] Strangers move into what was his home. Sulfur is scattered over the ruins.

[16] His roots dry up below. His branches wither above.

[17] His memory dies on the earth. No one remembers his name in the streets.

[18] He's shoved from light into darkness — driven clean out of the world.

[19] No son. No grandson. No survivor anywhere he once lived.

[20] The west stands stunned at his end. The east shudders in horror.

[21] This — this is what happens to the dwelling of the wicked. This is the address of the one who never knew God."

[1] Job answered:

[2] "How long will you grind me down, crush me with words?

[3] Ten times now you've shamed me, and you feel no shame attacking me.

[4] Even if I have strayed, my error stays with me alone.

[5] If you must puff yourselves up against me and use my disgrace as your evidence,

[6] then know this: God is the one who wronged me. He's the one who threw the net.

[7] I cry, 'Violence!' — no answer. I shout for help — no justice.

[8] He's walled off my road. I cannot pass. He's draped my paths in darkness.

[9] He's stripped my honor from me, torn the crown off my head.

[10] He demolishes me on every side until I'm finished. He uproots my hope like a tree.

[11] His anger blazes against me. He counts me among his enemies.

[12] His troops mass for the assault. They build siege ramps against me. They camp around my tent.

[13] My own brothers — he's pushed them far from me. My friends are strangers now.

[14] My relatives have vanished. My close companions have forgotten me.

[15] The guests in my house, even my servant girls — they look at me like a foreigner. I'm an alien in my own home.

[16] I call my servant. He doesn't answer. I have to beg him with my own mouth.

[17] My breath disgusts my wife. My own brothers find me repulsive.

[18] Even small children despise me. When I stand up, they mock me.

[19] Every man I trusted hates me. Those I loved have turned against me.

[20] I'm nothing but skin stretched over bone. I've escaped by the skin of my teeth.

[21] Pity me, friends — pity me — for the hand of God has struck me.

[22] Why do you hunt me down like God does? Will you never get enough of my flesh?

[23] Oh, if only my words were written down. If only they were carved into a scroll —

[24] cut with an iron stylus, filled with lead, etched into rock forever.

[25] But this I know: my Redeemer lives. And in the end he will stand upon the earth.

[26] And after my skin has been destroyed, yet in my flesh I will see God.

[27] I will see him myself — with my own eyes, not another's. My heart aches inside me with the longing.

[28] If you say, 'How shall we hunt him? The root of the matter is in him' —

[29] then tremble at the sword yourselves, for wrath brings the sword's punishment. Then you'll know there is judgment."

[1] Then Zophar from Naamah came back at him:

[2] "My thoughts won't let me rest — I have to answer. The agitation inside me demands it.

[3] I'm hearing rebuke that insults me, but my spirit, my understanding, drives a response out of me.

[4] Don't you know how it has always been, ever since humanity was set upon the earth —

[5] that the triumph of the wicked is brief, the joy of the godless lasts only a heartbeat?

[6] Even if his arrogance scales the heavens and his head brushes the clouds,

[7] he will vanish forever like his own waste. Those who saw him will ask, 'Where did he go?'

[8] He will fly off like a dream and never be found, chased away like a vision in the night.

[9] The eye that watched him will see him no more. His place will never lay eyes on him again.

[10] His children will grovel before the poor; his own hands will give back what he stole.

[11] His bones, packed with youthful strength, will lie down with him in the dust.

[12] Though evil tastes sweet in his mouth, though he tucks it under his tongue,

[13] though he savors it, won't let it go, holds it on his palate —

[14] in his stomach the food turns. It becomes cobra venom inside him.

[15] He swallowed wealth, and he will vomit it up. God will wrench it out of his gut.

[16] He sucked the venom of vipers; the snake's fang will kill him.

[17] He will never see the streams, the rivers running with honey and cream.

[18] He gives back what he worked for — never tastes it. The profits of his trade bring him no pleasure.

[19] Because he crushed the poor and left them with nothing, seized houses he never built —

[20] because his greed knew no limit, he cannot keep a single thing he craved.

[21] Nothing escaped his appetite — that's why his prosperity won't last.

[22] At the height of his abundance, anguish hits him. The full weight of misery comes crashing down.

[23] Just when he fills his belly, God unleashes burning fury on him, raining it down on him as he eats.

[24] He runs from an iron weapon — a bronze arrow pierces him through.

[25] He pulls it out from his back; the gleaming point comes dripping from his liver. Terror sweeps over him.

[26] Total darkness lies in wait for his treasures. A fire no one kindled will devour him and consume whatever survives in his tent.

[27] The heavens will expose his guilt; the earth will rise up against him.

[28] A flood will carry his house away, waters surging on the day of God's wrath.

[29] This is what God hands the wicked man — the inheritance God has decreed for him."

[1] Then Job spoke:

[2] "Listen. Just listen to me. Let that be the comfort you offer.

[3] Bear with me while I speak. After I'm done, mock away.

[4] Is my complaint with some human? If it were, I'd have every right to lose patience.

[5] Look at me — really look — and be stunned. Put your hand over your mouth.

[6] When I think about it, I'm shaken. My body trembles.

[7] Why do the wicked keep on living? Why do they grow old and gather power?

[8] Their children stand secure around them. Their grandchildren thrive before their eyes.

[9] Their homes are safe — no terror knocks. The rod of God never touches them.

[10] Their bulls breed without fail. Their cows calve and never miscarry.

[11] Their kids run wild like a flock. Their little ones dance.

[12] They sing to tambourines and lyres. They throw back their heads at the sound of the flute.

[13] They spend their days in prosperity, then slip down to the grave in peace.

[14] And to God they say: 'Leave us alone. We have no interest in your ways.

[15] Who is the Almighty that we should serve him? What do we get out of prayer?'

[16] Look — their prosperity isn't in their own hands. But the counsel of the wicked? I want nothing to do with it.

[17] How often does the lamp of the wicked actually go dark? How often does disaster fall on them, or God in his fury hand out their portion of pain?

[18] How often are they straw before the wind, chaff in the storm's grip?

[19] You say, 'God stores up a man's guilt for his sons.' Let him repay the man himself — let *him* feel it.

[20] Let his own eyes see his ruin. Let him drink down the wrath of the Almighty.

[21] Once he's gone, when his months are cut off, what does he care what happens to his family?

[22] Can anyone teach God knowledge — the One who judges even the heights?

[23] One man dies in full strength, completely at ease, untroubled,

[24] his body well-fed, his bones still rich with marrow.

[25] Another man dies with a bitter soul, never having tasted anything good.

[26] Side by side they lie down in the dust. The worms cover them both.

[27] I know exactly what you're thinking. I see the schemes you're using to wrong me.

[28] You ask, 'Where's the house of the great man now? Where's the tent the wicked lived in?'

[29] Have you never asked the travelers? Have you ignored their stories,

[30] how the evil man is spared on the day of disaster, rescued on the day of wrath?

[31] Who confronts him to his face? Who pays him back for what he's done?

[32] He's carried to the grave with honor. Someone keeps watch over his tomb.

[33] The soil of the valley feels sweet to him. A whole procession follows behind, and countless others went before.

[34] So how can you comfort me with empty words? Your answers are nothing but lies."

[1] Then Eliphaz the Temanite answered:

[2] "Can a man be of any use to God? Even the wisest only profits himself.

[3] Does the Almighty gain anything from your righteousness? Is there profit for him in your blameless ways?

[4] Is it your reverence he's correcting? Is that why he hauls you into court?

[5] No — your evil is enormous. Your sins, endless.

[6] You demanded collateral from your own brothers for nothing. You stripped the clothes off the naked.

[7] You withheld water from the exhausted. You held back bread from the starving.

[8] The land belonged to the strong-armed man, the favored one who lived on it.

[9] You sent widows away empty-handed and crushed the arms of orphans.

[10] That's why traps surround you now. That's why sudden terror unhinges you,

[11] why the darkness blinds you and floodwaters cover you over.

[12] Isn't God in the heights of heaven? Look at the highest stars — see how far above they sit.

[13] And you say, 'What does God know? Can he judge through the thick darkness?

[14] Clouds curtain him off — he can't see. He just strolls the dome of heaven.'

[15] Will you keep walking the ancient road that wicked men have trodden?

[16] Men snatched away before their time, their foundation washed out by a flood.

[17] They told God, 'Leave us alone. What can the Almighty do to us?'

[18] Yet he was the one who filled their houses with good things — and the counsel of the wicked is far from me.

[19] The righteous see their ruin and rejoice. The innocent laugh at them, saying,

[20] 'Our adversaries are wiped out. Fire has eaten what was left of them.'

[21] So make peace with God. Submit. Then good will come back to you.

[22] Take his instruction from his own mouth. Plant his words deep in your heart.

[23] Return to the Almighty and you will be rebuilt. Drive injustice far from your tent.

[24] Throw your gold into the dirt. Toss the gold of Ophir among the river stones —

[25] and the Almighty himself will be your gold, your stockpile of silver.

[26] Then you'll delight in the Almighty. You'll lift your face to God.

[27] You'll pray, and he'll listen. You'll keep your vows.

[28] Whatever you decide will stand. Light will fall on your path.

[29] When others are brought low, you'll say, 'Lift them up.' And God saves the downcast.

[30] He'll rescue even the guilty — delivered through the cleanness of your hands."

[1] Job answered:

[2] "Even now my complaint is bitter rebellion— his hand presses heavy against my groaning.

[3] If only I knew where to find him, how to reach the place where he sits!

[4] I would lay my case before him, fill my mouth with arguments,

[5] learn what answer he would give me, understand what he would say.

[6] Would he overwhelm me with raw power? No—he would actually listen.

[7] There, an honest man could reason with him, and I would walk away from my Judge acquitted forever.

[8] But I go east—he isn't there. West—I cannot find him.

[9] He works in the north—I catch no glimpse. He turns south—I see nothing.

[10] Yet he knows the road I travel. When he has tested me, I will come out gold.

[11] My foot has held his trail. I have kept his way without veering.

[12] I have not abandoned the command from his lips— I have treasured the words of his mouth more than my daily bread.

[13] But he stands alone, and who can turn him? Whatever he wants, he does.

[14] He will finish what he has decreed for me, and a thousand more such things wait in his stockpile.

[15] That is why I am unhinged in his presence. When I think on it, I am terrified of him.

[16] God has melted my courage; the Almighty has filled me with dread—

[17] yet still I am not silenced by the darkness, by the thick gloom that covers my face."

[1] Why doesn't the Almighty set times for judgment? Why don't those who know him ever see his court days?

[2] People shove the boundary stones aside. They steal flocks and graze them as their own.

[3] They drive off the orphan's donkey. They take the widow's ox as collateral on a debt.

[4] They push the poor off the road. The land's afflicted have to hide together just to survive.

[5] Look at them — like wild donkeys in the wasteland, the poor go out to their labor, scavenging for food at dawn, the badlands feeding their children.

[6] They harvest in fields that aren't theirs. They glean the leftover grapes of the wicked.

[7] They spend the night naked, no clothing, nothing to cover them in the cold.

[8] Mountain rains soak them through. With no shelter, they huddle against the rock.

[9] The fatherless child is torn from the breast. The infant of the poor is seized as security on a loan.

[10] Naked they wander, no clothes on their backs. Starving, they haul sheaves for someone else.

[11] They press olive oil between the walls of the rich. They tread the winepresses — and go thirsty.

[12] From the city, the dying groan. The throats of the wounded cry out. And God charges no one with the crime.

[13] There are those who rebel against the light — they don't know its ways, they refuse to walk its paths.

[14] The killer rises before dawn to murder the poor and the needy. By night he becomes a thief.

[15] The adulterer's eye waits for dusk. *No one will see me,* he tells himself, and veils his face.

[16] In the dark they break into houses. By day they shut themselves in. They have no use for light.

[17] For all of them, morning is the deep dark — they are at home with the terrors of midnight.

[18] *They are foam on the water,* you say. *Their fields are cursed. No one walks toward their vineyards anymore.*

[19] *As drought and heat snatch the snowmelt, the grave snatches those who sinned.*

[20] *The womb forgets them. The worm finds them sweet. They're remembered no more. Wickedness snaps like a brittle stick.*

[21] *They preyed on the barren woman who bore no child and showed no kindness to the widow.*

[22] *But God drags off the mighty by his power. When he rises against them, no one's life is safe.*

[23] *He may let them feel secure, and they lean on it — but his eyes are tracking every road they walk.*

[24] *They rise high for a moment, then they're gone — brought low, gathered up like everything else, snapped off like heads of grain.*

[25] If it's not so, who can prove me a liar? Who can reduce my words to nothing?

[1] Then Bildad the Shuhite spoke up:

[2] "Dominion and dread belong to God. He orders peace in the heights of heaven.

[3] Can anyone count his armies? On whom does his light not rise?

[4] So how can a mortal stand righteous before God? How can anyone born of woman be clean?

[5] Look — even the moon lacks brightness in his eyes. Even the stars are not pure before him.

[6] How much less a human — that maggot. A son of Adam — that worm."

[1] Job answered:

[2] "What a help you've been to the powerless! What a rescue for the arm without strength!

[3] What counsel you've poured out on the clueless! What a flood of wisdom you've revealed!

[4] Who fed you these words? Whose breath was speaking through you?

[5] The dead tremble beneath the waters, and everything that lives down there with them.

[6] The grave lies naked before God. Destruction has nowhere to hide.

[7] He stretches the north over empty space. He hangs the earth on nothing.

[8] He bundles the waters in his clouds, and the clouds do not split under the weight.

[9] He covers the face of the full moon, spreading his cloud across it.

[10] He scored a circle on the face of the waters — the boundary line where light meets dark.

[11] The pillars of heaven shudder, stunned when he rebukes them.

[12] With his power he stilled the sea. With his skill he shattered Rahab.

[13] His breath cleared the skies. His hand pierced the fleeing serpent.

[14] And these — these are only the edges of his ways. Only a whisper of him reaches us. Who could withstand the thunder of his full power?"

[1] Job picked up his discourse again and said:

[2] As surely as God lives — the One who has denied me justice, the Almighty who has made my soul bitter —

[3] as long as breath remains in me and God's spirit fills my nostrils,

[4] my lips will not speak deceit, my tongue will not whisper a lie.

[5] I will never — never — concede that you three are right. Until I die, I will not let go of my integrity.

[6] I'm holding tight to my righteousness; I won't release it. My conscience finds nothing to accuse me of in any of my days.

[7] Let my enemy be counted with the wicked, my opponent with the unjust.

[8] What hope does the godless man have when God cuts him off, when God yanks out his life?

[9] Will God listen to his cry when disaster crashes down on him?

[10] Will he find his delight in the Almighty? Will he call on God in every season? No.

[11] Let me teach you something about the hand of God. I won't hide what the Almighty is really like.

[12] You've all seen it yourselves — so why this empty babble?

[13] Here's what the wicked get from God, the inheritance the ruthless receive from the Almighty:

[14] His children multiply only to meet the sword. His descendants never have enough bread.

[15] Plague buries whoever survives him, and their widows don't even weep.

[16] He piles up silver like dirt and stacks up clothing like clay —

[17] but the righteous will wear what he stacked, and the innocent will divide his silver.

[18] The house he builds is a moth's cocoon, a flimsy shelter a watchman throws together.

[19] He goes to bed rich — but not again. He opens his eyes, and it's all gone.

[20] Terror floods him like a wall of water. A whirlwind snatches him away in the night.

[21] The east wind lifts him and he's gone — swept clean out of his place.

[22] It hurls itself at him without mercy as he scrambles desperately to escape its grip.

[23] It claps its hands at him in mockery and hisses him out of his own home.

[1] There's a place where silver is mined, a place where gold is refined.

[2] Iron is pulled from the dirt; copper is melted from stone.

[3] Miners push back the darkness, hunt to the farthest edge, chase ore through shadow and deep gloom.

[4] They sink shafts far from any path, in places forgotten by feet above, dangling in the void, swinging far from anyone.

[5] The earth that grows bread on its surface is churned below as if by fire.

[6] Its stones cradle sapphires; its dust holds grains of gold.

[7] No bird of prey knows that path. The falcon's eye has never traced it.

[8] No proud beast has walked it. No lion has prowled there.

[9] Man takes a hammer to the flint and tears mountains up by the roots.

[10] He cuts channels through the rock, and his eye catches every precious thing.

[11] He dams the underground streams and drags hidden treasures into the light.

[12] But wisdom — where can it be found? Where does understanding live?

[13] No human knows its price. It cannot be located in the land of the living.

[14] The deep says, "Not in me." The sea says, "Not here."

[15] You can't buy it with the finest gold. You can't weigh out silver for it.

[16] It's beyond the gold of Ophir, beyond precious onyx, beyond sapphire.

[17] Gold and crystal can't match it. Vessels of pure gold can't trade for it.

[18] Coral and quartz? Don't even mention them. Wisdom is worth more than rubies.

[19] The topaz of Cush can't compete. Pure gold can't buy it.

[20] So where does wisdom come from? Where does understanding live?

[21] It's hidden from every living eye, concealed even from the birds of the sky.

[22] Destruction and Death say, "We've only caught a rumor of it."

[23] God — only God — knows the way. He alone knows where it lives.

[24] Because He sees to the ends of the earth. He takes in everything under heaven.

[25] When He set the weight of the wind and measured out the waters,

[26] when He drew the rules for the rain and the path for the thunderbolt —

[27] that's when He saw wisdom, named it, established it, searched it out to the depths.

[28] And to humanity He said: "Look — the fear of the Lord, that is wisdom. To turn from evil — that is understanding."

[1] Job picked up his discourse again and said:

[2] If only I could go back — back to the months when God watched over me, when his lamp burned above my head and I walked through darkness by its light.

[3] when his lamp burned above my head, and by his light I walked through the dark —

[4] Those were my golden days — when God's friendship rested on my tent like a covering, [5] when the Almighty was still with me and my children stood around me, [6] when my steps were bathed in cream and the rock poured out rivers of oil for me.

[7] When I walked out to the city gate and took my seat in the public square, [8] young men saw me and stepped back. Old men rose to their feet. [9] Princes held their tongues, pressed hands to their mouths. [10] The voices of nobles dropped to a hush — their tongues stuck to the roofs of their mouths.

[11] Every ear that heard me blessed me. Every eye that saw me approved. [12] Because I rescued the poor when they cried out, and the orphan with no one to help. [13] The blessing of a dying man fell on me. I made the widow's heart sing. [14] I wore righteousness like a robe; justice was my turban and my mantle. [15] I was eyes to the blind, feet to the lame. [16] I was a father to the needy. I investigated cases I knew nothing about — I dug until I found the truth. [17] I broke the fangs of the unjust and pried their prey loose from their teeth.

[18] So I thought: I will die in my own nest, with days as many as the grains of sand. [19] My roots will reach down to water and dew will rest all night on my branches. [20] My honor will stay fresh on me, and the bow in my hand will always be new.

[21] Men listened to me, waited in silence for my counsel. [22] After I spoke, no one added a word — my words fell on them like rain. [23] They waited for me as for rain. They opened their mouths as for the spring showers. [24] When I smiled at them, they could hardly believe it. They cherished the light of my face. [25] I chose their road for them and sat at the head — I lived like a king among his troops, like one who brings comfort to mourners.

[1] But now they laugh at me — men younger than I am, whose fathers I would have refused to set with the dogs guarding my flock.

[2] What use was the strength of their hands to me? Their vigor had already withered.

[3] Gaunt from hunger and need, they gnawed the parched ground — desolation and waste their inheritance by night.

[4] They plucked salt-herbs from the brush, and the roots of the broom tree were their bread.

[5] Driven out from human company, shouted after like thieves,

[6] forced to camp in dry gullies, in holes in the dirt, in caves among the rocks.

[7] They brayed in the scrub, huddled together under the nettles —

[8] sons of fools, sons of nobodies, lashed and driven from the land.

[9] And now — now I am their tavern song. I am the punchline on their lips.

[10] They despise me. They keep their distance. They do not hesitate to spit in my face.

[11] God has cut the cord and crushed me, so they throw off all restraint in my presence.

[12] On my right, this rabble rises up. They kick my feet out from under me. They throw up their siege ramps against me.

[13] They tear up my road. They pile on my ruin. No one stops them.

[14] They pour through a wide breach — rolling in over the wreckage.

[15] Terror has turned on me. My dignity is scattered like dust on the wind. My deliverance has drifted off like a cloud.

[16] And now my life pours out of me. Days of misery have clamped down hard.

[17] At night, the ache bores into my bones. The pain that gnaws at me never sleeps.

[18] With brute force my disease grips me by the collar of my robe and chokes me.

[19] He has thrown me down into the mud. I have become dust and ashes.

[20] I cry out to you and you do not answer. I stand before you, and you only stare.

[21] You have turned cruel toward me. With the full force of your hand you persecute me.

[22] You snatch me up into the wind, mount me on it, and let the storm dissolve me.

[23] I know where you are taking me — to death, to the house appointed for everything that breathes.

[24] Yet surely no one strikes at a man already in ruins, when in his disaster he cries out for help.

[25] Did I not weep for the man whose days were hard? Did my soul not grieve for the poor?

[26] I waited for good — and evil came. I hoped for light — and darkness fell.

[27] My insides churn and will not be still. Days of affliction rush at me.

[28] I walk around blackened, but not by the sun. I stand up in the assembly and cry for help.

[29] I have become brother to the jackals, companion of ostriches.

[30] My skin is black and peeling off. My bones burn with fever.

[31] My harp has been tuned to mourning, my flute to the voices of those who weep.

[1] I made a covenant with my eyes. How could I let my gaze linger on a young woman?

[2] What share would I get from God above, what inheritance from the Almighty on high?

[3] Doesn't disaster wait for the unjust, ruin for those who do wrong?

[4] Doesn't he see my every path, count every step I take?

[5] If I've walked with lies, if my foot has hurried toward deceit —

[6] let God weigh me on honest scales. Let him see my integrity for himself.

[7] If my step has wandered from the path, if my heart has chased what my eyes saw, if any stain clings to my hands —

[8] then let someone else eat what I plant. Let my harvest be torn up by the roots.

[9] If my heart has been seduced by a woman, if I've crouched at my neighbor's door —

[10] then let my wife grind for another man. Let other men bend over her.

[11] That would be a sordid thing, a crime for the judges to handle.

[12] It would be a fire that devours all the way to destruction, that would burn my whole harvest to the ground.

[13] If I ever brushed aside the complaint of my servant or my maid when they had a grievance against me —

[14] what would I do when God rose up? When he called me to account, what could I answer?

[15] The one who made me in the womb made him too. The same one shaped us both inside our mothers.

[16] If I ever denied the poor what they needed, or let a widow's eyes go dim with waiting —

[17] if I ate my crust alone and never shared it with the orphan —

[18] no, from my youth I raised that child like a father. From birth I guided the widow.

[19] If I ever saw someone dying for lack of clothes, a poor man with nothing to cover him,

[20] and his body didn't bless me for the warmth of fleece from my own sheep —

[21] if I ever raised my hand against an orphan because I knew I had backing in the court —

[22] then let my shoulder fall from its socket, let my arm break off at the joint.

[23] Because the terror of God was real to me. His majesty — I couldn't stand against it.

[24] If I made gold my confidence, if I called pure gold my security —

[25] if I gloated over my great wealth, over all my hands had gathered —

[26] if I ever looked at the sun blazing or the moon walking in splendor

[27] and my heart was secretly seduced, and my hand blew them a kiss —

[28] that too would be a crime for the judges, a betrayal of the God above.

[29] Did I ever celebrate my enemy's ruin, gloat when disaster found him?

[30] No — I never let my mouth sin by cursing his life.

[31] The men in my tent will tell you: whoever came was filled with meat from my table.

[32] No stranger ever slept in the street. My door opened to every traveler.

[33] Did I cover my sins like Adam, hiding my guilt deep in my chest,

[34] because I was terrified of the crowd, because the contempt of clans made me cower so I shut my mouth and wouldn't go outside?

[35] If only someone would listen to me! Here — I sign my name to this defense. Let the Almighty answer me. Let my accuser write his indictment —

[36] I'd carry it on my shoulder. I'd wear it like a crown.

[37] I'd give him an accounting of every step. I'd approach him like a prince.

[38] If my land has cried out against me, if its furrows have wept together —

[39] if I ate its yield without paying, if I crushed the life out of its tenants —

[40] then let thorns grow instead of wheat, stinking weeds instead of barley.

The words of Job are finished.

[1] So the three men stopped answering Job — because in his own eyes he was righteous, and they had hit a wall.

[2] Then Elihu son of Barakel the Buzite, of the family of Ram, blazed up. He blazed at Job for justifying himself instead of God,

[3] and he blazed at the three friends because they had found no answer — yet still condemned Job.

[4] Elihu had held back from speaking to Job because the others were older than he was.

[5] But when he saw the three of them had nothing left to say, his anger caught fire.

[6] So Elihu son of Barakel the Buzite spoke up: "I am young, and you are old. That is why I held back, afraid to tell you what I know.

[7] I thought, *Let age speak. Let the long years teach wisdom.*

[8] But there is a spirit in a person — the breath of the Almighty — and *that* is what gives understanding.

[9] It is not the gray-haired who are wise. It is not the elders who always grasp what is right.

[10] So listen to me. Let me, too, declare what I know.

[11] I waited while you spoke. I leaned in while you searched for words, listening to your reasoning —

[12] paying close attention. And look — none of you refuted Job. Not one of you answered what he said.

[13] Do not tell yourselves you have found wisdom. Only God can rout him, not man.

[14] He did not aim his words at me, and I will not answer him with your arguments.

[15] They are stunned. They have nothing more to say. Words have abandoned them.

[16] Should I keep waiting because they have gone silent? Because they stand there with nothing left?

[17] No — I will take my turn. I will say what I know.

[18] I am full of words. The spirit inside me presses hard.

[19] My chest is like wine with no vent, like new wineskins ready to split open.

[20] I have to speak — only then will I find relief. I have to open my mouth and answer.

[21] I will show no favoritism to anyone. I will flatter no one.

[22] I do not know how to flatter — if I did, my Maker would sweep me away in an instant."

[1] So listen now, Job. Hear me out. Catch every word I'm about to say.

[2] I'm opening my mouth. My tongue is forming the words.

[3] What I say comes from an honest heart. My lips speak what I know is true.

[4] The Spirit of God made me. The breath of the Almighty gives me life.

[5] Answer me if you can. Line up your arguments. Take your stand.

[6] In God's sight, I'm just like you. I was pinched from the same clay.

[7] So you don't need to fear me. I won't crush you with the weight of my position.

[8] But I heard what you said. Every word reached my ears:

[9] "I'm clean. I haven't sinned. I'm innocent. There's no guilt in me.

[10] But God keeps finding excuses to attack me. He treats me like his enemy.

[11] He clamps my feet in the stocks. He watches every step I take."

[12] Here's where you're wrong, Job. Let me tell you. God is greater than any human.

[13] Why are you fighting him? He doesn't owe you an explanation for anything he does.

[14] God speaks. He speaks again. People just don't notice.

[15] In a dream. In a vision at night. When deep sleep falls on us and we're stretched out in bed —

[16] that's when he opens our ears. That's when he stamps his warning on us.

[17] He's trying to turn us back from what we're doing. He's trying to deflate our pride.

[18] He's keeping us from the pit. He's pulling us back from the edge of the sword.

[19] Or he disciplines us with pain on the sickbed — bones aching with a constant grinding ache.

[20] Food turns the stomach. The favorite meal becomes nauseating.

[21] Flesh wastes away until you can see through to nothing. Bones that were hidden now jut out.

[22] The soul drifts toward the grave. Life drifts toward the executioners.

[23] But if a messenger stands beside him — an angel, one in a thousand — to tell him what's right,

[24] to be gracious to him and say, "Spare him from going down to the pit. I've found his ransom" —

[25] then his flesh grows fresh as a child's. He returns to the days of his youth.

[26] He prays to God, and God welcomes him. He sees God's face and shouts for joy. God restores him to righteousness.

[27] He'll sing in front of everyone: "I sinned. I twisted what was straight. I deserved worse than I got.

[28] But God ransomed my soul from the pit. My life sees the light."

[29] Listen — God does all this. Twice, three times for a single person —

[30] pulling their soul back from the pit so the light of life can shine on them again.

[31] Pay attention, Job. Listen to me. Stay quiet, and I'll keep speaking.

[32] If you have something to say, answer me. Speak — I want you to be cleared.

[33] But if not, listen to me. Stay quiet, and I'll teach you wisdom.

[1] Then Elihu picked up where he left off:

[2] "Listen to me, you wise ones. You men who know things — hear me out.

[3] The ear tests words the way the tongue tastes food.

[4] Let us figure out together what is right. Let us discern among ourselves what is good.

[5] Job has said: 'I am innocent, and God has stripped me of justice.

[6] Despite being right, I am called a liar. My wound will not heal, though I have done nothing wrong.'

[7] Is there a man like Job — who drinks contempt like water,

[8] Who keeps company with evildoers, who walks with the wicked?

[9] He says, 'There is no profit in pleasing God.'

[10] So listen to me, you who have any sense. Far be it from God to do evil — from the Almighty to do wrong.

[11] He pays each person back for what they have done. He gives them what their conduct deserves.

[12] God will not act wickedly. The Almighty will not pervert justice. This is certain.

[13] Who gave him charge of the earth? Who appointed him over the whole world?

[14] If he decided to pull back — if he gathered his Spirit and breath to himself —

[15] Every living thing would die together. Humanity would return to dust.

[16] If you have understanding, hear this. Listen to the sound of my words.

[17] Could one who hates justice govern? Will you condemn the Mighty and Righteous One?

[18] He is the one who says to kings, 'You are scum,' and to nobles, 'You are corrupt.'

[19] He shows no favoritism to princes. He does not regard the rich over the poor. They are all the work of his hands.

[20] In a moment they die. In the middle of the night people are shaken and pass away. The mighty are removed without a hand lifted.

[21] His eyes are on every step a man takes. He sees every move.

[22] No darkness, no deep shadow can hide the workers of evil.

[23] God does not need to examine anyone further before bringing them to judgment.

[24] He shatters the mighty without inquiry and sets up others in their place.

[25] Because he knows what they do, he overturns them in the night and they are crushed.

[26] He strikes them as criminals in plain view —

[27] Because they turned away from following him, refusing to consider any of his ways,

[28] So that the cry of the poor came up to him — and he heard the cry of the afflicted.

[29] When he is silent, who can condemn? When he hides his face, who can find him? Yet he watches over both nation and individual,

[30] To keep the godless from ruling and from ensnaring the people.

[31] Has anyone said to God: 'I have taken my punishment. I will offend no more.

[32] Teach me what I cannot see. If I have done wrong, I will not do it again' —

[33] Should God then repay you on your terms when you refuse to repent? You must choose, not me. Speak — say what you know.

[34] Men of understanding will say to me — any wise person who hears me will say —

[35] 'Job speaks without knowledge. His words lack insight.'

[36] May Job be tested to the limit, because he answers like a wicked man.

[37] He piles rebellion on top of his sin. He claps his hands among us in scorn and multiplies his words against God."

[1] Then Elihu pressed on:

[2] "You really think this is justice? You actually said, 'I'm more in the right than God.'

[3] You ask, 'What's in it for me? What do I gain by not sinning?'

[4] Let me answer you — and your friends along with you.

[5] Look up. Stare at the sky. Look at those clouds far above your head.

[6] If you sin, what damage does that do to him? Pile transgression on transgression — what's it to him?

[7] If you're righteous, what are you handing him? What does he receive from your open palm?

[8] Your wickedness only touches other humans like yourself. Your righteousness only matters to your fellow man.

[9] People cry out under the crushing weight of oppression. They scream beneath the arm of the powerful.

[10] But no one asks, 'Where is God my Maker — the One who puts songs in the night,

[11] who teaches us more than the beasts of the earth, who makes us wiser than the birds of the sky?'

[12] They cry out — but he doesn't answer. Why? Because of the arrogance of evil men.

[13] God does not listen to empty noise. The Almighty pays it no attention.

[14] How much less when you claim you can't see him — that your case sits before him and you're still waiting for him to act.

[15] And now, because his anger has not yet fallen, because he seems to ignore reckless folly —

[16] Job opens his mouth and pours out nothing. He stacks words upon words without a clue."

[1] Elihu kept going:

[2] "Stay with me a little longer. I have more to say on God's behalf.

[3] I'll draw my knowledge from far and wide and give my Maker the credit he's owed.

[4] What I'm telling you is no lie — you're sitting with someone whose understanding runs deep.

[5] God is mighty, yes — but he doesn't despise anyone. He's mighty in strength and clarity of purpose.

[6] He won't let the wicked live on forever, but he gives the afflicted their due.

[7] He never takes his eyes off the righteous. He seats them with kings — enthroned, exalted for good.

[8] And when they end up in chains, tangled in the ropes of their own suffering,

[9] he shows them what they've done — their crimes, their arrogance.

[10] He opens their ears to correction and commands them to turn from evil.

[11] If they listen and serve him, they finish their days in prosperity, their years in pleasure.

[12] But if they refuse to listen, they go down by the sword — they die clueless, with no understanding at all.

[13] The godless hoard their anger. Even when God binds them, they refuse to cry for help.

[14] Their lives end young. Their existence dies among the temple prostitutes.

[15] But the suffering — God rescues them through their suffering. Affliction is the megaphone he uses to open their ears.

[16] And he's trying to coax you, too — out of the jaws of distress into open ground where there's no constraint, to a table loaded with rich food.

[17] But you — you're full of the verdict due the wicked. Judgment and justice have hold of you.

[18] Don't let your rage seduce you into mockery. Don't let the size of a bribe twist you sideways.

[19] Will your cry for help keep you out of trouble? Will all your strength and striving?

[20] Don't long for the night, when whole peoples vanish from their places.

[21] Watch yourself. Don't turn to evil — that's what you've chosen over suffering.

[22] Look — God towers in his power. Who teaches like he does?

[23] Who has ever told him how to run things? Who has ever said, 'You've done wrong'?

[24] Instead, remember to praise his work — the work that people put to song.

[25] Everyone has seen it. We stare from a distance.

[26] God is great — beyond our grasp. The number of his years can't even be searched out.

[27] He pulls up the drops of water; they distill into rain from his mist.

[28] The clouds pour it down, drenching humanity in abundance.

[29] Who can understand how the clouds spread out, the thunder of his pavilion?

[30] Watch — he scatters his lightning around himself and covers the roots of the sea.

[31] This is how he governs the nations and provides food in plenty.

[32] He fills his hands with lightning and aims it at its mark.

[33] The thunder announces him. Even the cattle sense the storm coming."

[1] At this, my heart pounds and leaps out of its place. [2] Listen — listen to the roar of his voice, the rumbling that breaks from his mouth. [3] He unleashes it under the whole sky, his lightning to the ends of the earth. [4] After it, a voice roars — he thunders with his majestic voice, and when his voice is heard, he holds nothing back. [5] God thunders with that voice in wonders past understanding; he does great things we cannot fathom.

[6] To the snow he says, "Fall on the earth," and to the rain, "Pour" — and the cloudburst of his power obeys. [7] He seals every human hand from its labor, so that all he has made will know it was him. [8] The animals slip into their dens and crouch in their lairs. [9] Out of its chamber comes the whirlwind, and cold from the scattering winds. [10] By the breath of God, ice forms; the wide waters freeze solid. [11] He loads the thick clouds with moisture; he scatters his lightning through them. [12] They wheel and turn at his direction, doing whatever he commands across the face of the inhabited world. [13] Whether for discipline, or for his land, or for steadfast love — he makes it happen.

[14] Listen to this, Job. Stop. Stand still and consider the wonders of God. [15] Do you know how God commands them, how he flashes the lightning from his cloud? [16] Do you know how the clouds hang balanced — the wonders of him whose knowledge is perfect? [17] You, whose clothes burn hot when the earth lies still under the south wind — [18] can you, with him, hammer out the sky, hard as a mirror of cast bronze?

[19] Tell us what to say to him — we cannot lay out our case; it is too dark. [20] Should he be told that I want to speak? Would any man ask to be swallowed?

[21] And now — no one can even look at the sun when it blazes through the cleared skies after the wind has swept them. [22] Out of the north comes golden splendor; around God hangs terrifying majesty. [23] The Almighty — we cannot reach him. Towering in power, overflowing in justice and righteousness, he does not crush. [24] Therefore people fear him. He pays no attention to those who think themselves wise.

[1] Then, out of the storm, the LORD answered Job:

[2] Who is this, throwing shadows across my counsel with words that have no weight?

[3] Brace yourself like a fighter. I will question you, and you will answer me.

[4] Where were you when I laid the foundations of the earth? Tell me, if you understand so much.

[5] Who measured it out — surely you know — who stretched the line across it?

[6] What were its footings sunk into? Who set its cornerstone

[7] while the morning stars sang in unison and every son of God shouted for joy?

[8] Who slammed the doors on the sea when it came bursting out of the womb,

[9] when I dressed it in clouds and wrapped it in thick darkness like swaddling bands,

[10] when I carved out its boundary, set its bars and doors in place,

[11] and said: This far and no farther. Here your proud waves break.

[12] Have you ever, even once, commanded the morning? Have you assigned dawn its post,

[13] told it to grab the edges of the earth and shake the wicked out like dust from a rug?

[14] The land takes shape under it like clay under a seal — everything stands out in relief, dressed in form.

[15] The wicked are stripped of their light; the raised arm is broken mid-swing.

[16] Have you walked the springs at the bottom of the sea? Strolled the trenches of the deep?

[17] Have the gates of death been opened to you? Have you seen the gates of the shadow?

[18] Do you grasp how wide this earth runs? Speak up — if you know all of it.

[19] Where does the light live? And darkness — where is its address,

[20] so you can lead each one home, knowing the path to its house?

[21] You know, of course — you were born back then, your days stretch that far back.

[22] Have you entered the vaults where snow is kept? Seen the armories of hail

[23] that I have stockpiled for times of trouble, for the day of battle and war?

[24] Where is the road to the place light is divided, where the east wind scatters across the earth?

[25] Who cut a channel for the downpour and a path for the thunderbolt,

[26] to bring rain to land where no one lives, on wilderness with no human in it,

[27] to drench the wasteland and coax green out of barren ground?

[28] Does the rain have a father? Who fathered the drops of dew?

[29] From whose womb came the ice? The frost of heaven — who gave it birth,

[30] when the waters harden like stone and the face of the deep locks shut?

[31] Can you tie up the chains of the Pleiades or loosen the belt of Orion?

[32] Can you bring out the constellations in their season, or lead the Bear with her cubs?

[33] Do you know the laws of the heavens? Can you set their rule over the earth?

[34] Can you raise your voice to the clouds and have a flood answer back?

[35] Can you send out the lightning bolts so they go — and report to you, Here we are?

[36] Who put wisdom in the heart? Who gave the mind its understanding?

[37] Who has the skill to number the clouds? Who tips the jars of heaven

[38] when the dust hardens into a crust and the clods cling together?

[39] Do you hunt prey for the lion? Do you fill the appetite of her cubs

[40] when they crouch in their dens, lying in ambush in the thicket?

[41] Who provides the raven its meal when its young cry out to God and wander, starving?

[1] Do you know when the wild goats give birth on the cliffs? Have you watched the deer in her labor? [2] Can you count the months they carry their young? Do you know the hour they deliver? [3] They crouch, push out their offspring, and the labor pains are over. [4] Their young grow strong in the open country, leave, and never come back.

[5] Who turned the wild donkey loose? Who cut the ropes off this untamed thing? [6] I gave it the wasteland for a home, the salt flats for a dwelling. [7] It laughs at the noise of the city, deaf to the driver's shouts. [8] It roams the mountains for pasture, hunting every patch of green.

[9] Will the wild ox agree to serve you? Will it spend the night beside your feeding trough? [10] Can you rope it to the plow? Will it harrow the valleys at your command? [11] Will you trust it because of its raw strength? Will you hand off your hard labor to it? [12] Can you count on it to haul in your grain and gather it to your threshing floor?

[13] The ostrich flaps her wings in joyful chaos — but are those wings and feathers like the stork's? [14] She drops her eggs on the ground and lets the dirt warm them, [15] forgetting that a foot might crush them, that a wild animal might trample them. [16] She treats her young harshly, as if they were not hers — unbothered that her labor might come to nothing — [17] because God denied her wisdom, gave her no share in understanding. [18] But when she spreads herself to run — she laughs at the horse and its rider.

[19] Did you give the horse his strength? Did you clothe his neck with that thundering mane? [20] Did you teach him to leap like a locust, his proud snorting a terror? [21] He paws the valley, exulting in his power, charging straight into the weapons. [22] He mocks fear, refuses to flinch, does not turn back from the sword. [23] The quiver rattles against him, the flashing spear, the javelin. [24] In wild frenzy he devours the ground — he cannot stand still when the trumpet sounds. [25] At every blast he says, *Aha!* He smells the battle from miles off — the shouting of commanders, the war cry.

[26] Is it your wisdom that lifts the hawk into flight, that stretches its wings toward the south? [27] Does the eagle climb at your command, building its nest in the heights? [28] It lives on the cliff, makes its fortress on a jagged crag. [29] From there it spies its prey; its eyes see across vast distances. [30] Its young lap up blood — wherever the slain are, there it is.

[1] Then the LORD turned to Job and said:

[2] "Will the one who picks fights with the Almighty now offer correction? Let God's accuser step forward and answer."

[3] And Job answered the LORD:

[4] "I am nothing — what could I possibly say? I cover my mouth with my hand.

[5] I spoke once. I will not speak again. Twice — and I am finished."

[6] Then the LORD spoke to Job out of the whirlwind:

[7] "Stand up like a man. I will question you, and you will answer me.

[8] Will you actually annul my justice? Will you condemn me so you can come out righteous?

[9] Do you have an arm like God's? Can you thunder with a voice like his?

[10] Then deck yourself out in majesty and grandeur. Put on splendor and glory like a robe.

[11] Unleash the fury of your wrath. Look at every proud man and bring him down.

[12] Look at every proud man and humble him. Crush the wicked where they stand.

[13] Bury them all together in the dust. Wrap their faces in the grave.

[14] Then — and only then — will I admit to you that your own right hand can save you.

[15] Now look at Behemoth, which I made just as I made you. He grazes on grass like an ox.

[16] Feel the strength in his loins, the power coiled in the muscles of his belly.

[17] His tail stiffens like a cedar. The sinews of his thighs are braided tight.

[18] His bones are tubes of bronze. His limbs are bars of iron.

[19] He is the masterwork of God's ways — only his Maker can approach him with a sword.

[20] The hills bring him their harvest, and every wild creature plays nearby.

[21] He sprawls beneath the lotus trees, hidden in the reeds of the marsh.

[22] The lotuses cover him with their shade. The willows of the riverbank ring him round.

[23] When the river rages, he does not flinch. He is unshaken even when the Jordan surges against his face.

[24] Can anyone capture him while he watches? Can anyone hook his nose with a snare?"

[1] Can you haul Leviathan up with a fishhook? Can you tie down his tongue with a rope? [2] Can you thread a cord through his nose, drive a hook through his jaw? [3] Will he plead with you for mercy? Will he whisper soft words to soften you? [4] Will he cut a deal with you, sign himself over as your slave for life? [5] Will you put him on a leash like a sparrow for your daughters to play with? [6] Will the fishing guilds haggle over him, butcher him for the marketplace? [7] Can you riddle his hide with harpoons, his head with fishing spears? [8] Lay a hand on him once — you'll remember the fight, and you'll never try again.

[9] Any hope of bringing him down is a lie. The mere sight of him knocks a man flat. [10] No one is reckless enough to provoke him. So who, then, can stand against me? [11] Who has put me in their debt, that I should owe them anything? Everything beneath the sky belongs to me.

[12] I will not stay silent about his limbs, his power, his magnificent frame. [13] Who could strip off his outer coat? Who could approach him with a bridle? [14] Who would dare pry open the gates of his face — those teeth ringed with terror? [15] His back: rows of shields locked tight, sealed like a fortress. [16] Each one fitted to the next so close that not even air slips between. [17] They cling together, fused, inseparable. [18] His sneezes throw off flashes of light. His eyes — like the first rays of dawn. [19] Torches leap from his mouth. Sparks of fire spray out. [20] Smoke pours from his nostrils like steam from a boiling cauldron over burning reeds. [21] His breath sets coals ablaze. Flame rolls from his jaws. [22] Strength makes its home in his neck. Terror dances ahead of him wherever he goes. [23] The folds of his flesh are welded solid, immovable, cast in iron. [24] His heart is stone — hard as the lower millstone. [25] When he rises, even the mighty are unmanned; they stumble back, undone. [26] A sword strikes him and shatters. Spear, dart, arrow — useless. [27] Iron is straw to him. Bronze is rotten wood. [28] Arrows can't make him run. Slingstones are husks of chaff. [29] Clubs feel like twigs. He laughs at the whistle of a javelin. [30] His underside is jagged pottery, dragging through the mud like a threshing sled. [31] He turns the deep into a boiling pot, churns the sea into a perfumer's mixing bowl. [32] He leaves a wake of foam that gleams white — you'd think the deep had gone gray-haired. [33] Nothing on earth is his equal. He was made without fear. [34] He looks down on everything that towers. He is king over all the proud.

[1] Then Job answered the LORD:

[2] "I see it now. You can do anything. Nothing you intend can be blocked.

[3] You asked: Who is this clouding my counsel with ignorance? That was me. I spoke about things I never understood — wonders too high, too vast, beyond my reach.

[4] You said: Listen now, and I will speak. I will question you, and you will answer me.

[5] I had heard of you — rumors, secondhand reports. But now my own eyes have seen you.
