[4] He had thirty sons who rode thirty donkeys and ruled thirty towns in Gilead — towns still called Havvoth-Jair to this day.

[5] Jair died and was buried in Kamon.

[6] Then Israel went back to what Israel always does — evil in the eyes of the LORD. They chased the Baals and the Ashtaroth, the gods of Aram, the gods of Sidon, the gods of Moab, the gods of Ammon, the gods of the Philistines. They abandoned the LORD. They stopped serving him entirely.

[7] So the LORD's anger blazed against Israel, and he sold them into the hands of the Philistines and the Ammonites.

[8] That year, and for eighteen years after, those enemies crushed and ground down every Israelite east of the Jordan, in the land of the Amorites, in Gilead.

[9] The Ammonites even crossed the Jordan to wage war against Judah, Benjamin, and the house of Ephraim. Israel was in agony.

[10] So Israel cried out to the LORD: "We've sinned against you. We abandoned our God and served the Baals."

[11] And the LORD answered Israel: "When the Egyptians, the Amorites, the Ammonites, the Philistines —

[12] when the Sidonians, the Amalekites, the Maonites — when any of them crushed you and you cried to me, didn't I rescue you from their hand?

[13] But you — you abandoned me. You served other gods. So I'm done rescuing you.

[14] Go cry to the gods you chose. Let them save you in your hour of agony."

[15] But Israel said to the LORD: "We've sinned. Do to us whatever seems right in your eyes — only, please, rescue us today."

[16] Then they threw out the foreign gods from their midst and served the LORD. And the LORD could not bear Israel's misery any longer.

[17] The Ammonites mustered and camped in Gilead. Israel gathered and camped at Mizpah.

[18] The leaders of Gilead said to each other: "Whoever steps up first to fight the Ammonites — he will be head over everyone living in Gilead."

[1] Jephthah the Gileadite was a warrior — a man of brutal capability. His mother was a prostitute, and Gilead had fathered him.

[2] But Gilead's wife bore him legitimate sons, and when they grew up, they ran Jephthah off. "You won't inherit a thing in our father's house," they told him. "You're the son of another woman."

[3] So Jephthah fled from his brothers and settled in the land of Tob. A band of restless men gathered around him, and they rode out with him on raids.

[4] Some time later, the Ammonites went to war against Israel.

[5] When the fighting started, the elders of Gilead went looking for Jephthah in the land of Tob.

[6] "Come back," they said. "Be our commander. Fight the Ammonites for us."

[7] Jephthah threw it in their faces: "Weren't you the ones who hated me? Who drove me out of my father's house? Now you come crawling when you're in trouble?"

[8] The elders didn't flinch. "That's exactly why we're here. Come with us. Fight the Ammonites. And you'll be head over everyone in Gilead — every last one of us."

[9] Jephthah pressed them: "If you bring me home to fight the Ammonites, and the LORD hands them over to me — I'm your head. For real."

[10] "The LORD hears us," the elders said. "He's our witness. We'll do exactly what you say."

[11] So Jephthah went with the elders of Gilead, and the people made him head and commander over them. And Jephthah laid every word of it out before the LORD at Mizpah.

[12] Then Jephthah sent messengers to the king of Ammon: "What's your quarrel with me? Why have you come to invade my land?"

[13] The king of Ammon answered: "Israel stole my land when they came up from Egypt — from the Arnon to the Jabbok and all the way to the Jordan. Give it back. Peacefully."

[14] Jephthah sent messengers back again with this:

[15] "Here is what Jephthah says: Israel did not seize Moab's land or Ammon's land.

[16] When Israel came up from Egypt, they traveled through the wilderness to the Red Sea and arrived at Kadesh.

[17] Then Israel sent messengers to the king of Edom: 'Let us pass through your land.' Edom refused. They sent word to the king of Moab too — same answer. So Israel stayed at Kadesh.

[18] Then they trekked through the wilderness, skirted Edom and Moab, came around to the east of Moab, and camped on the far side of the Arnon. They never set foot inside Moab's border — the Arnon was the line.

[19] Then Israel sent messengers to Sihon, king of the Amorites, who ruled in Heshbon: 'Let us pass through your land to our destination.'

[20] But Sihon didn't trust Israel to pass through. He mustered his whole army at Jahaz and attacked.

[21] And the LORD, the God of Israel, gave Sihon and all his men into Israel's hand. Israel struck them down and took possession of all the Amorite land in that country —

[22] from the Arnon to the Jabbok, from the wilderness to the Jordan. Every inch of it.

[23] So the LORD, the God of Israel, dispossessed the Amorites for the sake of his people Israel — and now you want to take it back?

[24] Don't you keep whatever Chemosh your god gives you? Then whatever the LORD our God dispossesses for us — that's ours.

[25] Are you any better than Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab? Did he ever pick a fight with Israel? Did he ever go to war with us?

[26] Israel has lived in Heshbon and its villages, in Aroer and its villages, in every town along the Arnon — for three hundred years. Why didn't you reclaim them then?

[27] I haven't wronged you. You're the one wronging me by making war. Let the LORD, the Judge, decide today between Israel and Ammon."

[28] But the king of Ammon ignored every word Jephthah sent him.

[29] Then the Spirit of the LORD came over Jephthah. He swept through Gilead and Manasseh, passed through Mizpah of Gilead, and from Mizpah of Gilead he advanced on the Ammonites.

[30] And Jephthah made a vow to the LORD: "If you really hand the Ammonites over to me —

[31] then whatever comes out of the doors of my house to meet me when I return in peace from the Ammonites will be the LORD's. I will offer it up as a burnt offering."

[32] So Jephthah crossed over against the Ammonites to fight, and the LORD gave them into his hand.

[33] He struck them down from Aroer all the way to Minnith — twenty cities — and on to Abel-keramim. A devastating blow. And Ammon was crushed before Israel.

[34] Then Jephthah came home to Mizpah. And out of the house — out came his daughter to meet him, with tambourines and dancing. She was his only child. He had no other son, no other daughter. Just her.

[35] The moment he saw her, he ripped his clothes and cried out, "My daughter — you've brought me to my knees! You — of all people — the source of my ruin! I opened my mouth to the LORD. I cannot take it back."

[36] And she said to him, "Father — you opened your mouth to the LORD. Do to me what you said. The LORD avenged you on your enemies, on the Ammonites — do what you vowed."

[37] Then she said, "Grant me this one thing: give me two months. Let me go up into the hills and weep — with my friends — over the fact that I will never marry."

[38] "Go," he said. He sent her away for two months. And she went with her companions into the hills and wept that she would never know a husband.

[39] At the end of the two months, she returned to her father. And he did to her what he had vowed. She had never known a man. And it became a custom in Israel —

[40] that year after year the daughters of Israel go out for four days to commemorate the daughter of Jephthah the Gileadite.

[1] The men of Ephraim mobilized, crossed over to Zaphon, and confronted Jephthah: "Why did you march out against the Ammonites and not call us to fight with you? We'll burn your house down around you."

[2] Jephthah shot back: "My people and I were locked in a bitter struggle with the Ammonites. I called for you — you didn't come. You left me hanging.

[3] When I saw no rescue coming from you, I took my life in my own hands, crossed over against the Ammonites, and the LORD handed them to me. So why are you showing up today to fight me?"

[4] Then Jephthah rallied every man of Gilead and went to war with Ephraim. And the men of Gilead cut Ephraim down — because Ephraim had sneered, "You Gileadites are nothing but Ephraim's runaways, stranded between Ephraim and Manasseh."

[5] Gilead seized the fords of the Jordan that led back to Ephraim. Whenever an Ephraimite fugitive begged, "Let me cross," the men of Gilead would ask, "Are you from Ephraim?" If he said no,

[6] they'd order him: "Say 'Shibboleth.'" He'd say "Sibboleth" — couldn't form the sound right — and they'd grab him and kill him at the fords. Forty-two thousand Ephraimites fell that day.

[7] Jephthah judged Israel six years. Then Jephthah the Gileadite died and was buried in one of the towns of Gilead.

[8] After him, Ibzan of Bethlehem judged Israel.

[9] He had thirty sons and thirty daughters. He married the daughters off outside the clan and brought in thirty women from outside for his sons. He judged Israel seven years.

[10] Then Ibzan died and was buried in Bethlehem.

[11] After him, Elon the Zebulunite judged Israel for ten years.

[12] Then Elon the Zebulunite died and was buried at Aijalon in the territory of Zebulun.

[13] After him, Abdon son of Hillel, from Pirathon, judged Israel.

[14] He had forty sons and thirty grandsons who rode on seventy donkeys. He judged Israel eight years.

[15] Then Abdon son of Hillel the Pirathonite died and was buried at Pirathon in the land of Ephraim, in the hill country of the Amalekites.

[1] Israel kept doing what was evil in the LORD's sight, so he handed them over to the Philistines for forty years.

[2] There was a man from Zorah, from the clan of Dan. His name was Manoah. His wife was barren — she had never borne a child.

[3] The angel of the LORD appeared to her and said, "You're barren. You've never had a child. But you're going to conceive. You're going to bear a son.

[4] So watch yourself — no wine, no strong drink, nothing unclean.

[5] Because you will conceive, and you will bear a son, and no razor is ever to touch his head. The boy will be a Nazirite to God from the womb. He's the one who will begin to rescue Israel from the grip of the Philistines."

[6] The woman ran to her husband. "A man of God came to me," she said. "His face — it was like the face of the angel of God. Terrifying. I didn't ask where he came from, and he didn't tell me his name.

[7] But he said: 'You're going to conceive. You're going to bear a son. So now — no wine, no strong drink, nothing unclean. Because the boy will be a Nazirite to God from the womb until the day he dies.'"

[8] Manoah prayed to the LORD: "Please, Lord — let the man of God you sent come back to us. Teach us what to do with the boy who's going to be born."

[9] God listened. The angel of God came back to the woman while she was sitting in the field. Manoah wasn't with her.

[10] She bolted, running to find her husband. "He's here!" she said. "The man who came to me the other day — he's here!"

[11] Manoah got up and followed his wife. He came to the man and said, "Are you the one who spoke to my wife?" "I am," he said.

[12] Manoah said, "When your words come true — what's the rule for the boy? What's his work?"

[13] The angel of the LORD said, "Everything I told your wife — she must follow it.

[14] Nothing that comes from the grapevine. No wine. No strong drink. Nothing unclean. Everything I commanded her — she must keep."

[15] Manoah said to the angel of the LORD, "Let us keep you here. Let us prepare a young goat for you."

[16] The angel of the LORD said, "Even if you keep me, I won't eat your food. But if you want to prepare a burnt offering — offer it to the LORD." Manoah still didn't realize he was speaking to the angel of the LORD.

[17] Manoah said to the angel of the LORD, "What is your name? When your words come true, we want to honor you."

[18] The angel of the LORD said to him, "Why do you ask my name? It is beyond understanding."

[19] So Manoah took the young goat and the grain offering and offered them on the rock to the LORD. And the LORD did something astonishing while Manoah and his wife watched.

[20] As the flame leapt up from the altar toward the sky — the angel of the LORD went up in the flame. Manoah and his wife saw it. They dropped, faces to the ground.

[21] The angel of the LORD did not appear again to Manoah and his wife. Then Manoah knew — this had been the angel of the LORD.

[22] Manoah said to his wife, "We're going to die. We've seen God."

[23] But his wife said, "If the LORD had wanted to kill us, he wouldn't have taken our burnt offering and grain offering from our hands. He wouldn't have shown us all this. He wouldn't have told us what he just told us."

[24] The woman bore a son. She named him Samson. The boy grew, and the LORD blessed him.

[25] And the Spirit of the LORD began to stir in him at Mahaneh-dan, between Zorah and Eshtaol.

[1] Samson went down to Timnah and there, among the Philistine women, he saw her.

[2] He came back home and told his father and mother: "I saw a woman in Timnah—Philistine. Get her for me. I want her as my wife."

[3] His father and mother pushed back. "Is there really not one woman among your own people? Not one in all Israel? You have to go to those uncircumcised Philistines for a wife?" But Samson said to his father, "Get her for me. She's the one I want."

[4] His parents had no idea this was from the LORD—that He was looking for an opening against the Philistines. In those days, Philistia ruled Israel.

[5] So Samson went down to Timnah with his father and mother. As they approached the vineyards of Timnah, a young lion came roaring at him.

[6] The Spirit of the LORD rushed on him, and he tore the lion apart with his bare hands—as easily as ripping a young goat. He told no one, not his father, not his mother, what he had done.

[7] He went down and talked with the woman, and she was everything he wanted.

[8] Later, returning to marry her, he turned aside to look at the lion's carcass—and there inside it was a swarm of bees, and honey.

[9] He scooped the honey into his hands and ate as he walked. When he reached his father and mother, he gave them some, and they ate too. He never told them he had taken it from the body of a lion.

[10] His father went down to the woman, and Samson threw a feast there—the kind young men throw.

[11] When the locals saw him, they brought thirty companions to be with him.

[12] Samson said to them, "Let me put a riddle to you. If you can crack it during the seven days of the feast—solve it—I'll give you thirty linen garments and thirty changes of clothes.

[13] But if you can't, you give them to me." They said, "Pose your riddle. Let's hear it."

[14] So he said: "Out of the eater came something to eat. Out of the strong came something sweet." Three days passed and they couldn't solve it.

[15] On the fourth day they came to Samson's wife: "Coax the riddle out of your husband, or we'll burn you and your father's house to the ground. Did you invite us here to rob us?"

[16] So she came to Samson weeping. "You don't love me. You hate me. You set a riddle for my people and didn't even tell me the answer." He said, "I haven't told my own father and mother. Why would I tell you?"

[17] She wept on him the rest of the seven days of the feast. On the seventh day he finally caved—she had worn him down—and she told her people.

[18] Before sundown on the seventh day, the men of the town said to him: "What is sweeter than honey? What is stronger than a lion?" He shot back: "If you hadn't plowed with my heifer, you wouldn't have solved my riddle."

[19] Then the Spirit of the LORD rushed on him. He went down to Ashkelon, struck down thirty men, stripped them, and gave the clothes to those who had answered the riddle. Burning with fury, he went back to his father's house.

[20] And his wife was given to the companion who had stood beside him at the wedding.

[1] Sometime later, during the wheat harvest, Samson went to visit his wife, bringing a young goat as a gift. "I want to go to her in her room," he said. But her father blocked the door.

[2] "I was sure you hated her," he said. "So I gave her to your best man. Look — her younger sister is more beautiful anyway. Take her instead."

[3] Samson said, "This time, when I devastate the Philistines, no one can blame me."

[4] He went out, caught three hundred foxes, tied them tail to tail in pairs, and fastened a torch between every pair of tails.

[5] He lit the torches and let the foxes loose in the standing grain of the Philistines. He burned everything — the stacked sheaves, the standing wheat, even the vineyards and olive groves.

[6] "Who did this?" the Philistines demanded. The answer came back: "Samson — son-in-law of the Timnite. The Timnite gave Samson's wife to his best man." So the Philistines went up and burned her and her father alive.

[7] Samson said to them, "If this is how you act, I will not stop until I have my revenge on you."

[8] He attacked them, hip and thigh, slaughtering them in a great massacre. Then he went down and lived in the cleft of the rock of Etam.

[9] The Philistines marched up, made camp in Judah, and spread out near Lehi.

[10] The men of Judah asked, "Why have you come up against us?" They answered, "We came to bind Samson — to do to him what he did to us."

[11] Three thousand men of Judah went down to the cleft of the rock of Etam. They said to Samson, "Don't you understand? The Philistines rule over us. What have you done to us?" He answered, "I did to them what they did to me."

[12] They said, "We have come to bind you and hand you over to the Philistines." Samson said, "Swear to me you will not strike me down yourselves."

[13] "We won't," they said. "We will only bind you and hand you over. We will not kill you." So they tied him with two new ropes and brought him up from the rock.

[14] As he reached Lehi, the Philistines came rushing at him, shouting in triumph. Then the Spirit of the LORD rushed on him. The ropes on his arms became like flax burned with fire — the bonds melted off his hands.

[15] He spotted a fresh jawbone of a donkey, grabbed it, and struck down a thousand men.

[16] Then Samson said: "With the jawbone of a donkey — heap upon heap. With the jawbone of a donkey — I struck down a thousand men."

[17] When he finished speaking, he threw the jawbone away. That place was called Ramath-Lehi — Jawbone Hill.

[18] Then he was desperately thirsty. He cried out to the LORD: "You gave your servant this great victory. Will you now let me die of thirst and fall into the hands of the uncircumcised?"

[19] So God split open the hollow place at Lehi, and water came pouring out. Samson drank, his strength returned, and he revived. That spring is called En-hakkore — the spring of the one who cried out — and it is still there in Lehi to this day.

[20] Samson led Israel for twenty years in the days of the Philistines.

[1] Samson went to Gaza. He saw a prostitute there and went in to her.

[2] Word spread through the city: "Samson is here." So they surrounded the place, lay in wait for him all night at the city gate, and stayed quiet through the dark hours, telling themselves, "At dawn we kill him."

[3] But Samson slept only till midnight. At midnight he rose, grabbed the doors of the city gate along with the two posts, ripped them up — bar and all — hoisted them onto his shoulders, and carried them to the top of the hill that faces Hebron.

[4] After this he fell in love with a woman in the Sorek Valley. Her name was Delilah.

[5] The Philistine rulers came to her and said, "Coax him. Find out what makes him so strong, how we can overpower him, tie him up, break him. Do this and each of us will give you eleven hundred pieces of silver."

[6] So Delilah said to Samson, "Tell me — please — what makes you so strong? What could tie you down and break you?"

[7] Samson told her, "If they bind me with seven fresh bowstrings, never dried out, I'll be as weak as anyone else."

[8] The Philistine rulers brought her seven fresh bowstrings, still moist, and she tied him up with them.

[9] With men hidden in the inner room, she shouted, "Samson — the Philistines are on you!" He snapped the bowstrings like a strand of yarn near a flame. The secret of his strength stayed hidden.

[10] Delilah said to Samson, "You've played me. You lied. Now tell me — what can really tie you down?"

[11] He said, "If they bind me tight with brand-new ropes, never used, I'll be as weak as anyone else."

[12] So Delilah got new ropes and tied him up, then shouted, "Samson — the Philistines are on you!" The men were waiting in the inner room. He snapped the ropes off his arms like thread.

[13] Delilah said, "You're still mocking me. Still lying. Tell me — what can tie you down?" He said, "If you weave the seven braids of my hair into the fabric on the loom and pin it tight—"

[14] So while he slept, she wove his braids into the loom, pinned them tight, and shouted, "Samson — the Philistines are on you!" He woke up and tore loose — pin, loom, fabric, and all.

[15] Then she said, "How can you say you love me when your heart isn't with me? Three times you've mocked me. Three times you've refused to tell me what makes you so strong."

[16] Day after day she pressed him. Nagged him. Wore him down until he was sick to death of it.

[17] So he told her everything. "No razor has ever touched my head. I have been set apart for God from the womb. If my head is shaved, my strength leaves me. I become weak. I become like any other man."

[18] Delilah knew this time he had told her the truth. She sent for the Philistine rulers: "Come up — this time. He's told me everything." So they came, silver in hand.

[19] She lulled him to sleep on her lap. Then she called for a man, and he shaved off the seven braids of Samson's hair. She began to torment him. His strength was gone.

[20] She cried, "Samson — the Philistines are on you!" He woke from sleep and thought, "I'll get out of this like every other time. I'll shake myself free." He did not know the LORD had left him.

[21] The Philistines seized him. Gouged out his eyes. Marched him down to Gaza, bound him with bronze shackles, and set him grinding grain in the prison.

[22] But the hair on his head began to grow back after it had been shaved.

[23] The Philistine rulers gathered for a great sacrifice to their god Dagon — a celebration. They chanted: "Our god has handed Samson, our enemy, over to us."

[24] When the people saw him, they praised their god: "Our god has handed our enemy over to us — the destroyer of our land, the one who piled up our dead."

[25] In the high of the party they shouted, "Bring out Samson — let him entertain us." So they hauled him from the prison and he performed for them. They stood him between the pillars.

[26] Samson said to the boy holding his hand, "Put me where I can feel the pillars that hold this place up. I want to lean on them."

[27] The temple was packed — men, women, every Philistine ruler there. Three thousand more on the roof watching Samson perform.

[28] Samson cried out to the LORD: "Lord GOD — remember me. Strengthen me, God — just this once more. Let me pay back the Philistines with one blow for my two eyes."

[29] Samson reached for the two central pillars holding up the temple — one with his right hand, one with his left — and braced himself against them.

[30] He said, "Let me die with the Philistines." He pushed with everything he had, and the temple collapsed on the rulers and everyone in it. The dead he killed in his death outnumbered all he had killed in his life.

[31] His brothers and his father's whole household came down, took his body, carried him up, and buried him between Zorah and Eshtaol in the tomb of Manoah his father. He had led Israel twenty years.

[1] In the hill country of Ephraim there was a man named Micah.

[2] He said to his mother, "Those eleven hundred pieces of silver that were taken from you — the silver you cursed the thief over, the curse I heard with my own ears — look, I have it. I took it."

His mother said, "May the LORD bless you, my son."

[3] He handed the eleven hundred pieces of silver back to his mother, and she said, "I solemnly consecrate this silver to the LORD on behalf of my son — to make a carved image and a cast idol. So here, I'm giving it back to you."

[4] He returned the silver to his mother anyway, and she took two hundred pieces of it and gave it to a silversmith, who shaped it into a carved image and a cast idol. It stood in Micah's house.

[5] Micah had himself a shrine. He made an ephod and some household gods, and he ordained one of his sons to serve as his priest.

[6] In those days there was no king in Israel. Everyone did whatever seemed right in his own eyes.

[7] There was a young man from Bethlehem in Judah, from the clan of Judah — a Levite living there as a stranger.

[8] He left Bethlehem looking for somewhere to settle, wherever he could find it. Traveling through, he came to Micah's house in the hill country of Ephraim.

[9] Micah asked him, "Where are you from?"

He answered, "I'm a Levite from Bethlehem in Judah. I'm looking for a place to live."

[10] Micah said, "Stay with me. Be a father and a priest to me. I'll give you ten pieces of silver a year, a set of clothes, and your food." So the Levite went in.

[11] The Levite agreed to stay with the man, and the young man became like one of his own sons to him.

[12] Micah ordained the Levite, and the young man became his priest and lived in Micah's house.

[13] Then Micah said, "Now I know the LORD will treat me well — I have a Levite for a priest."

[1] Israel had no king in those days. And the tribe of Dan was still searching for a place to call home — they hadn't yet received an inheritance among the tribes of Israel.

[2] So the Danites sent out five fighting men from their clans, scouts from Zorah and Eshtaol, to comb the land and size it up. "Go," they said. "Survey the territory." The men made their way into the hill country of Ephraim, to Micah's house, and lodged there for the night.

[3] As they got close, they recognized the voice of the young Levite. They turned in and asked him, "Who brought you here? What are you doing in this place? What's your business?"

[4] He told them what Micah had done for him — "He hired me. I serve as his priest."

[5] So they said to him, "Ask God for us, then. We want to know if this journey of ours will succeed."

[6] The priest told them, "Go in peace. The LORD is watching over the road you're traveling."

[7] The five men moved on and came to Laish. They found the people there living in confidence — quiet, unsuspecting, after the manner of the Sidonians. No one in the land harassed them or held power over them. They were far from Sidon and had no dealings with anyone.

[8] When the scouts came back to their brothers at Zorah and Eshtaol, the others asked, "Well? What's your report?"

[9] "Up," they answered. "Let's move on them. We've seen the land — it's good. And you're just standing here? Don't hesitate. Go. Take possession.

[10] When you arrive, you'll find a people at ease and a land wide open. God has placed it in your hands. It's a place that lacks nothing on earth."

[11] So six hundred men from the clan of Dan, armed for war, set out from Zorah and Eshtaol.

[12] They marched up and camped at Kiriath-jearim in Judah — which is why that place is called Mahaneh-dan to this day. It sits just west of Kiriath-jearim.

[13] From there they pushed on into the hill country of Ephraim and arrived at Micah's house.

[14] Then the five who had scouted out Laish spoke up to their brothers: "Listen — do you know what's in these houses? An ephod, household gods, a carved image, a cast idol. Think about what you should do."

[15] So they turned aside and entered the young Levite's quarters at Micah's house and greeted him.

[16] The six hundred armed Danites stood at the gate.

[17] The five scouts went up inside and took the carved image, the ephod, the household gods, the cast idol — while the priest stood at the gate with the six hundred fighting men.

[18] When the priest saw them carrying off the carved image, the ephod, the household gods, and the cast idol, he said, "What are you doing?"

[19] They answered, "Quiet. Put your hand over your mouth. Come with us — be a father and priest to us. Which is better: serving as priest for one man's household, or for an entire tribe and clan in Israel?"

[20] The priest's heart leapt at this. He picked up the ephod, the household gods, and the carved image and joined the company.

[21] They turned and went on their way, putting the children, the livestock, and their possessions in front of them.

[22] They were already some distance from Micah's house when the men in the houses near Micah's were called together. They overtook the Danites.

[23] They shouted after them. The Danites turned around and said to Micah, "What's wrong with you, mustering a crowd like this?"

[24] He said, "You've taken the gods I made, and the priest, and walked off. What do I have left? And you ask me, 'What's wrong'?"

[25] The Danites told him, "Don't raise your voice around us — or some hot-tempered men might fall on you, and you'll lose your life and the lives of your household."

[26] Then the Danites went on their way. Micah saw they were too strong for him, so he turned and went back home.

[27] They took what Micah had made, and the priest who had served him, and arrived at Laish — at a quiet, unsuspecting people. They put the city to the sword and burned it down.

[28] No one came to the rescue, because Laish was far from Sidon and had no dealings with anyone. The city sat in the valley near Beth-rehob. The Danites rebuilt it and settled there.

[29] They renamed it Dan, after their ancestor Dan, son of Israel — though originally the city had been called Laish.

[30] The Danites set up the carved image for themselves. And Jonathan son of Gershom, son of Moses, along with his sons, served as priests for the tribe of Dan — right up until the day the land went into exile.

[31] They kept Micah's carved image standing the whole time the house of God was at Shiloh.

[1] In those days, when Israel had no king, a Levite living deep in the hill country of Ephraim took a concubine for himself from Bethlehem in Judah. [2] But she was unfaithful to him and left, going back to her father's house in Bethlehem. She stayed there four months. [3] Then her husband went after her, hoping to win her back. He brought his servant and two donkeys. She brought him into her father's house, and when her father saw him, he welcomed him with delight.

[4] His father-in-law — the girl's father — would not let him go. He stayed three days, eating, drinking, sleeping under that roof. [5] On the fourth day they rose early, and the Levite got ready to leave. But the girl's father said to his son-in-law, "Eat something first. Strengthen yourself for the road. Then you can go." [6] So the two of them sat down and ate and drank together. Then the father said, "Stay tonight. Please. Enjoy yourself." [7] The Levite stood up to leave, but his father-in-law pressed him, so he stayed another night. [8] On the fifth day he rose early to go, and again the father said, "Strengthen yourself. Wait until the day cools." So they ate together again.

[9] When the man finally got up to leave with his concubine and his servant, his father-in-law said, "Look — the day is almost gone. Stay the night. The light is fading. Stay here, enjoy yourself, and tomorrow you can head home early." [10] But this time the Levite refused. He saddled his two donkeys, took his concubine, and set out. They came opposite Jebus — that is, Jerusalem.

[11] By the time they reached Jebus the day was nearly spent. The servant said to his master, "Let's turn aside into this Jebusite city and spend the night here." [12] But his master said, "No. We're not going into a foreign city, one that doesn't belong to Israel. We'll push on to Gibeah." [13] He told the servant, "Come on. We'll try to reach Gibeah or Ramah and spend the night in one of them." [14] So they kept walking. The sun went down on them just as they approached Gibeah, the town belonging to Benjamin.

[15] They turned in to spend the night in Gibeah. The Levite went and sat down in the town square — but no one took them in for the night.

[16] Then, that evening, an old man came in from his work in the fields. He was from the hill country of Ephraim, but he was living in Gibeah — though the men of that place were Benjaminites. [17] He looked up and saw the traveler in the square. "Where are you going?" the old man asked. "Where have you come from?"

[18] The Levite answered, "We're traveling from Bethlehem in Judah to the far side of the hill country of Ephraim. That's where I'm from. I went to Bethlehem, and now I'm headed home — to the house of the LORD. But no one here will take me in. [19] We have straw and feed for our donkeys. We have bread and wine for me, for your servant girl, and for the young man with us. We need nothing."

[20] "Peace to you," the old man said. "Let me supply whatever you need. Just don't spend the night in the square." [21] So he brought him into his house, fed the donkeys, and they washed their feet and ate and drank.

[22] They were enjoying themselves — when the men of the city, sons of worthlessness, surrounded the house. They pounded on the door. They shouted to the old man, the owner: "Bring out the man who came to your house. We want to know him."

[23] The owner of the house went out to them and said, "No, my brothers. Please — don't do this evil. This man is a guest in my house. Don't commit this outrage. [24] Look — here is my virgin daughter, and his concubine. Let me bring them out. Ravish them. Do to them whatever you want. But against this man, do not commit this disgrace."

[25] But the men would not listen. So the Levite took his concubine and pushed her out to them. They raped her and abused her all night long, until morning, and let her go as the dawn began to break.

[26] At daybreak the woman came and collapsed at the door of the house where her master was. She lay there until it was light.

[27] In the morning her master got up, opened the door of the house, and stepped out to continue on his way — and there she was. His concubine. Fallen at the doorway, her hands on the threshold. [28] "Get up," he said. "Let's go."

No answer.

He lifted her onto the donkey and started for home.

[29] When he reached his house, he took a knife. He took hold of his concubine and cut her, limb by limb, into twelve pieces. He sent the pieces throughout every territory of Israel.

[30] And everyone who saw it said the same thing: "Nothing like this has ever happened, nothing has ever been seen — not since the day the Israelites came up out of Egypt until now. Think about it. Talk about it. Speak."

[1] Then every Israelite — from Dan in the north to Beersheba in the south, plus the land of Gilead — marched out as one body, the whole assembly gathering before the LORD at Mizpah.

[2] The chiefs of all the tribes took their positions in the assembly of God's people — four hundred thousand foot soldiers, swords drawn.

[3] (Word reached Benjamin that Israel had gone up to Mizpah.) Then Israel demanded: "Tell us. How did this atrocity happen?"

[4] So the Levite — the husband of the murdered woman — gave his account. "I came to Gibeah in Benjamin with my concubine to spend the night.

[5] That night the men of Gibeah surrounded the house where I was staying. They meant to kill me. They raped my concubine until she died.

[6] So I took her body, cut it apart, and sent the pieces across every region Israel calls its inheritance — because what they did was obscene, an outrage against everything we are.

[7] Now you — all of you, every Israelite — speak. Decide. Right here, right now."

[8] As one man the whole assembly rose. "Not one of us goes back to his tent. Not one of us returns home.

[9] Here's what we'll do to Gibeah: we cast lots and march against it.

[10] We'll pull ten men out of every hundred from every tribe — a hundred from every thousand, a thousand from every ten thousand — to supply the troops, so when the army reaches Gibeah of Benjamin they can repay them in full for the obscenity committed in Israel."

[11] So every fighting man in Israel converged on the city — united, one body, one purpose.

[12] The tribes sent runners throughout Benjamin: "What is this atrocity that happened among you?

[13] Hand over those degenerates in Gibeah. We'll execute them and purge this evil out of Israel." But Benjamin would not listen to their brothers.

[14] Instead, from every town they streamed to Gibeah, ready to fight Israel.

[15] That day Benjamin mustered twenty-six thousand swordsmen from its towns, plus the seven hundred elite warriors of Gibeah.

[16] Among all these forces were seven hundred left-handed marksmen — every one of them able to sling a stone at a hair and not miss.

[17] Israel — Benjamin not counted — mustered four hundred thousand swordsmen, every man a soldier.

[18] They went up to Bethel and inquired of God. "Which of us goes up first to fight Benjamin?" The LORD answered: "Judah first."

[19] At dawn Israel broke camp and took up position against Gibeah.

[20] The army of Israel advanced for battle and formed ranks against Benjamin at Gibeah.

[21] Benjamin poured out of the city and cut down twenty-two thousand Israelites that day, dead on the ground.

[22] But Israel's army steeled itself and re-formed at the same position they'd held the day before.

[23] They went up and wept before the LORD until evening. They asked Him: "Should we go back to battle against our brothers — Benjamin?" The LORD said: "Go up against them."

[24] So Israel closed with Benjamin a second day.

[25] And Benjamin came out of Gibeah a second day and cut down another eighteen thousand Israelite swordsmen — all of them dead.

[26] Then all Israel — the whole army — went up to Bethel. They sat there weeping before the LORD. They fasted that day until evening and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings to the LORD.

[27] Israel inquired of the LORD. (The ark of the covenant of God was there in those days,

[28] and Phinehas son of Eleazar, son of Aaron, was the priest in attendance.) They asked: "Do we go out again to fight our brother Benjamin — or do we stop?" The LORD said: "Go up. Tomorrow I deliver them into your hand."

[29] So Israel set ambushes around Gibeah.

[30] On the third day they advanced against Benjamin and formed ranks at Gibeah as before.

[31] Benjamin marched out to meet them and was drawn away from the city. They began striking down Israelites as they had the first two days — about thirty men cut down in the open country and on the roads, one running up to Bethel, the other to Gibeah.

[32] "We're crushing them just like before," Benjamin said. But Israel had agreed: "We'll run — pull them away from the city, out onto the highways."

[33] Every Israelite soldier broke from his position and formed up at Baal-tamar. Meanwhile the ambush burst out of its hiding place west of Gibeah.

[34] Ten thousand of Israel's best stormed Gibeah head-on. The fighting was savage — and Benjamin had no idea disaster was already on top of them.

[35] The LORD shattered Benjamin in front of Israel. That day Israel destroyed twenty-five thousand one hundred Benjaminite swordsmen — every one a warrior.

[36] Benjamin saw they were beaten. Israel had given ground earlier — only because they were trusting the ambush set against Gibeah.

[37] The men in ambush sprang at Gibeah, swept through it, and put the whole city to the sword.

[38] Israel and the ambush had a signal: a great column of smoke rising from the city.

[39] When Israel pivoted in the battle, Benjamin had already begun cutting them down — some thirty men — thinking, "We're crushing them like the first battle."

[40] Then the column began to climb out of the city — a pillar of smoke — and Benjamin looked back. The whole city was going up to the sky.

[41] At that moment Israel wheeled around. Benjamin panicked — they saw the catastrophe closing on them.

[42] They broke and ran toward the wilderness — but the battle ran with them. And men pouring out of the towns cut them down between the two forces.

[43] Israel surrounded Benjamin, hunted them down, trampled them from Nohah all the way east of Gibeah.

[44] Eighteen thousand Benjaminites fell — every one of them a fighting man.

[45] The rest turned and fled into the wilderness toward the rock of Rimmon. Israel picked off five thousand more on the highways. They pursued them as far as Gidom and struck down another two thousand.

[46] Twenty-five thousand swordsmen of Benjamin fell that day — every one a warrior.

[47] But six hundred men fled into the wilderness and made it to the rock of Rimmon. They held out there four months.

[48] Then Israel turned back on Benjamin and put every town to the sword — people, livestock, everything they found. Every town they came to, they burned.

[1] The men of Israel had sworn an oath at Mizpah: "Not one of us will give his daughter to a Benjaminite in marriage."

[2] Now the people came to Bethel and sat there before God until evening, raising their voices in bitter weeping.

[3] They cried out, "Lord, God of Israel, why has this happened? Why is one tribe missing from Israel today?"

[4] At dawn the people built an altar there and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings.

[5] Then the Israelites asked, "Who among all the tribes of Israel failed to come up to the assembly before the Lord?" Because they had sworn a heavy oath: anyone who did not come up to the Lord at Mizpah would die.

[6] And the Israelites grieved for Benjamin, their brother. "Today a tribe has been cut off from Israel," they said.

[7] "What can we do for the survivors? How do they get wives? We swore by the Lord we would not give them our daughters."

[8] So they asked again, "Which tribe of Israel didn't come up to the Lord at Mizpah?" And they discovered that not a single man from Jabesh-gilead had come to the camp, to the assembly.

[9] When they took the count, sure enough — not one inhabitant of Jabesh-gilead was there.

[10] So the assembly dispatched twelve thousand of their fiercest warriors with this order: "Go. Put the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead to the sword — the women and children too.

[11] Here's what you'll do: every male, and every woman who has slept with a man — devote them to destruction."

[12] Among the people of Jabesh-gilead they found four hundred young virgins who had never been with a man, and they brought them back to the camp at Shiloh, in the land of Canaan.

[13] Then the whole assembly sent word to the Benjaminites holed up at the rock of Rimmon, offering peace.

[14] Benjamin came back. They gave them the women they had spared from Jabesh-gilead — but it wasn't enough.

[15] The people still ached for Benjamin, because the Lord had torn a gap in the tribes of Israel.

[16] The elders of the assembly said, "What do we do about wives for the rest of them? The women of Benjamin have been wiped out."

[17] They said, "There must be an inheritance for the survivors of Benjamin. A tribe of Israel cannot be erased.

[18] But we can't give them our own daughters." For the Israelites had sworn: "Cursed is anyone who gives a wife to Benjamin."

[19] Then they had an idea: "Look — there's the yearly festival of the Lord at Shiloh, north of Bethel, east of the road that goes up from Bethel to Shechem, south of Lebonah."

[20] So they instructed the Benjaminites: "Go hide in the vineyards.

[21] Watch. When the young women of Shiloh come out to dance in the festival, rush out from the vineyards, each of you seize a wife from the daughters of Shiloh, and head back to the land of Benjamin.

[22] When their fathers or brothers come to us with complaints, we'll tell them, 'Have mercy on us for their sake. We couldn't get wives for each of them in the war, and you didn't actually give your daughters to them — so you haven't broken the oath.'"

[23] That's what the Benjaminites did. Each man grabbed a wife from the dancers and carried her off. They went home, rebuilt their towns, and settled in.

[24] Then the Israelites scattered — each back to his tribe and clan, each man to his own inheritance.

[25] In those days Israel had no king. Every man did whatever seemed right in his own eyes.

[1] Look at her now — the city once swollen with people, sitting alone. The one who reigned among nations is a widow. The queen of provinces has been pressed into forced labor.

[2] She weeps through the night, tears streaking her face. Of all her lovers, not one comes to comfort her. Every friend has betrayed her. They've become her enemies.

[3] Judah has gone into exile — crushed by suffering, ground down by hard labor. She lives now among the nations, finding no rest. Her pursuers caught her in the narrow places, where there was nowhere to run.

[4] The roads to Zion mourn — no one comes for the festivals anymore. Her gates stand desolate. Her priests groan. Her young women are stricken. She herself is in bitter anguish.

[5] Her enemies have become the masters now. Her adversaries thrive, because the LORD has brought her low for the sheer weight of her rebellions. Her children have been marched away — captives ahead of the enemy.

[6] All the splendor has drained out of Daughter Zion. Her princes are like deer that found no pasture — running, exhausted, before the hunter.

[7] In her days of suffering and homelessness, Jerusalem remembers — all the treasures that used to be hers, back in the old days. When her people fell to the enemy and no one came to help, the enemy stood there laughing at her collapse.

[8] Jerusalem sinned — sinned grievously — and so she has become something filthy. Everyone who once honored her now despises her. They've seen her nakedness. She groans and turns her face away.

[9] Her filth is in the folds of her skirt. She gave no thought to where this would end. So her fall has been staggering, and there is no one to comfort her. "LORD, look at my misery — the enemy has won."

[10] The enemy has reached out his hand and seized everything precious to her. She watched the nations push into her sanctuary — the very people you forbade to enter your assembly.

[11] All her people groan as they search for bread. They trade their treasures for food just to stay alive. "Look, LORD, and see — I have become worthless."

[12] You who pass by on the road — does none of this matter to you? Look around. Look hard. Is there any pain like the pain inflicted on me, the pain the LORD unleashed on the day of his burning anger?

[13] From above, he hurled fire into my bones and it consumed me from the inside. He spread a net for my feet and turned me back. He left me devastated — sick all day, every day.

[14] My rebellions have been bound into a yoke — woven together by his own hand and hung around my neck. He has drained my strength. The Lord handed me over to people I cannot stand against.

[15] The Lord swept aside every warrior in my midst. He called up an army against me to crush my young men. The Lord trampled Daughter Judah like grapes in a winepress.

[16] This is why I weep. My eyes — my eyes pour out water. Anyone who could comfort me, anyone who could revive my spirit, is far from me. My children are devastated, because the enemy has overpowered us.

[17] Zion stretches out her hands — and no one comforts her. The LORD has summoned Jacob's enemies from every side to surround him. Jerusalem has become something disgusting to them.

[18] The LORD is in the right. I defied his command. Listen, all you peoples — look at my pain. My young women, my young men, marched off into captivity.

[19] I called out to my lovers, but they deceived me. My priests and my elders perished in the city while they were searching for food just to keep themselves alive.

[20] Look, LORD — see how distressed I am. Everything inside me is churning. My heart is overturned within me because I rebelled, and I rebelled hard. Out in the streets the sword takes lives. Inside the houses — only death.

[21] People hear me groaning, but no one comforts me. All my enemies have heard about my disaster — and they're glad. They know you did this. Bring on the day you promised — let them become what I have become.

[22] Drag all their wickedness in front of you. Deal with them the way you dealt with me for all my rebellions. My groans are endless. My heart is sick.

[1] Look how the Lord has draped Daughter Zion in the black cloud of his fury. He flung Israel's splendor from heaven down to the dirt. On the day of his wrath, he forgot his own footstool.

[2] The Lord swallowed Jacob whole — no mercy, none. He tore down every stronghold of Daughter Judah in his rage, leveled them to the ground, profaned the kingdom and its princes.

[3] He hacked off every horn of Israel in burning anger, pulled back his right hand when the enemy came. He blazed through Jacob like a wildfire, devouring everything around.

[4] He bent his bow like an enemy. His right hand drew back like a foe. Everyone Zion treasured — he slaughtered them. He poured his fury out like fire over the tent of Daughter Zion.

[5] The Lord became the enemy. He swallowed Israel, swallowed all her palaces, smashed her strongholds, multiplied moaning and mourning in Daughter Judah.

[6] He ripped down his own dwelling like a garden hut, demolished the place of meeting. The Lord made Zion forget her festivals and Sabbaths, and in the storm of his anger he spurned king and priest alike.

[7] The Lord rejected his altar, disowned his sanctuary, handed over the walls of her palaces to the enemy. They raised a shout inside the house of the Lord — as if it were a festival day.

[8] The Lord planned to destroy the wall of Daughter Zion. He stretched the measuring line and did not pull back his hand from devouring. He made rampart and wall lament — together they crumbled.

[9] Her gates sank into the ground. He shattered her bars. Her king and princes are scattered among the nations. There is no more instruction. Her prophets find no vision from the Lord.

[10] The elders of Daughter Zion sit on the ground, silent. They have thrown dust on their heads, dressed themselves in sackcloth. The young women of Jerusalem bow their faces to the earth.

[11] My eyes are spent with tears. My insides churn. My liver pours out on the ground over the wreckage of my people — because children and nursing babies faint in the city streets.

[12] They keep asking their mothers: *Where is bread? Where is wine?* — fainting like the wounded in the squares of the city, their lives draining out into their mothers' arms.

[13] What can I say for you? What can I compare you to, Daughter Jerusalem? What can I match against you to comfort you, virgin Daughter Zion? Your ruin is wide as the sea. Who could heal you?

[14] Your prophets gave you visions — empty, whitewashed. They never exposed your guilt to turn back your captivity. The oracles they sold you were lies, mirages.

[15] Everyone who passes by claps at you. They hiss and shake their heads at Daughter Jerusalem: *Is this the city they called Perfect Beauty, Joy of the Whole Earth?*

[16] Your enemies open their mouths wide against you. They hiss, grind their teeth, snarl: *We swallowed her. Yes — this is the day we were waiting for. We have lived to see it.*

[17] The Lord did what he planned. He carried out the word he decreed long ago. He tore down without mercy. He let the enemy gloat over you. He raised up the horn of your foes.

[18] Their heart cries out to the Lord. Wall of Daughter Zion — let your tears run like a river day and night. Give yourself no rest. Do not let your eyes go still.

[19] Get up. Wail through the night, at every change of watch. Pour your heart out like water before the face of the Lord. Lift your hands to him for the lives of your children — fainting from hunger at every street corner.

[20] Look, Lord. See. Who have you ever done this to? Should women eat their own offspring — the babies they cradled? Should priest and prophet be slaughtered inside the sanctuary of the Lord?

[21] Young and old lie together in the dust of the streets. My young men and women have fallen by the sword. You killed them on the day of your wrath. You butchered them without mercy.

[22] You called terrors against me from every side — as if summoning guests to a festival. On the day of the Lord's anger, no one escaped, no one survived. The ones I cradled and raised — my enemy finished them off.

[1] I am the man who has seen affliction under the rod of his fury.

[2] He drove me into darkness, not light.

[3] Yes — against me, again and again, all day long, he turns his hand.

[4] He has worn out my flesh and my skin. He has shattered my bones.

[5] He built siegeworks around me — bitterness and hardship the walls.

[6] He made me sit in darkness like the long-dead.

[7] He walled me in. I cannot escape. He weighed me down with chains.

[8] Even when I scream for help, he shuts out my prayer.

[9] He blocked my paths with cut stone. He twisted every road I tried.

[10] He has been a bear lying in wait for me, a lion crouched in hiding.

[11] He dragged me off the road and ripped me apart. He left me devastated.

[12] He bent his bow and set me up as the target for his arrow.

[13] He drove the shafts of his quiver deep into my kidneys.

[14] I became the joke of my own people — their drinking song all day long.

[15] He stuffed me full of bitterness. He drowned me in wormwood.

[16] He broke my teeth on gravel. He pressed my face into ashes.

[17] My soul has been stripped of peace. I have forgotten what good even is.

[18] So I said: My strength is gone. My hope in the LORD — finished.

[19] Remember my affliction, my homelessness — the wormwood and the bile.

[20] My soul remembers. Remembers and sinks down inside me.

[21] But this I call to mind — and therefore I have hope:

[22] The LORD's faithful love never runs dry. His mercies never quit.

[23] New every morning. Vast is your faithfulness.

[24] "The LORD is my portion," my soul says. "Therefore I will wait for him."

[25] The LORD is good to those who wait for him, to the soul that seeks him out.

[26] It is good to wait in silence for the rescue of the LORD.

[27] It is good for a man to carry the yoke while he is young.

[28] Let him sit alone and silent — God has laid it on him.

[29] Let him press his mouth into the dust — there may yet be hope.

[30] Let him offer his cheek to the one who strikes him. Let him swallow his fill of insult.

[31] Because the Lord does not cast off forever.

[32] Though he brings grief, he will show compassion — the floodtide of his faithful love is that wide.

[33] He does not delight in afflicting, in grieving the sons of men.

[34] To crush every prisoner of the earth underfoot,

[35] to deny a man justice in the very face of the Most High,

[36] to twist a man's case in court — does the Lord not see all this?

[37] Who can speak it into being if the Lord has not decreed it?

[38] Is it not from the mouth of the Most High that both evil and good come?

[39] Why should any living person complain — a man, for the punishment of his own sins?

[40] Let us search our ways. Let us examine them. Let us turn back to the LORD.

[41] Let us lift our hearts along with our hands to God in heaven:

[42] We have rebelled. We have defied you. And you have not forgiven.

[43] You wrapped yourself in anger and chased us down. You killed. You did not spare.

[44] You wrapped yourself in a cloud so no prayer could pass through.

[45] You made us scum and refuse among the nations.

[46] Every enemy has opened wide his mouth against us.

[47] Terror and the pit have come on us — devastation and ruin.

[48] My eyes run rivers because my people lie shattered.

[49] My eyes stream and will not stop. There is no relief —

[50] until the LORD looks down from heaven and sees.

[51] What my eyes see breaks me — what has happened to the daughters of my city.

[52] Those who were my enemies for nothing hunted me down like a bird.

[53] They tried to end my life in the pit. They piled stones on top of me.

[54] The waters closed over my head. I said: I am cut off.

[55] Then I called your name, O LORD — from the deepest pit.

[56] You heard my voice. Do not close your ear to my plea for breath, my cry for help.

[57] You came near the day I called. You said: Do not be afraid.

[58] You took up my case, Lord. You redeemed my life.

[59] You have seen, LORD, the wrong done to me. Judge my cause.

[60] You have seen all their vengeance, every plot they hatched against me.

[61] You have heard their insults, O LORD — every scheme against me,

[62] the lips of my attackers, their muttering against me all day long.

[63] Look at them — sitting or standing, I am the song they sneer.

[64] Pay them back, LORD, what their hands deserve.

[65] Give them hardness of heart. Let your curse be on them.

[66] Hunt them down in fury and wipe them out from under the heavens of the LORD.

[1] Look how the gold has lost its shine — the pure gold, dulled beyond recognition. The sacred stones lie scattered at every street corner.

[2] Zion's children — once weighed against fine gold — now counted as cheap clay pots, the throwaway work of a potter's hands.

[3] Even jackals nurse their young. But my people? They've gone cruel as ostriches in the desert, deaf to their own children's cries.

[4] The infant's tongue sticks to the roof of its mouth, parched. Toddlers beg for bread, and no one tears off a piece.

[5] Those who once ate delicacies now starve in the streets. Those raised on crimson and silk now claw through ash heaps for scraps.

[6] My people's guilt outweighs Sodom's — and Sodom went down in an instant, with no hand raised against her. Ours has been the slower agony.

[7] Her nobles once gleamed brighter than snow, whiter than milk, bodies ruddy as coral, sapphire-cut.

[8] Now their faces are blacker than soot. No one recognizes them in the street. Skin shrunk tight to bone, dry as kindling.

[9] Better the ones cut down by the sword than the ones gutted by hunger — wasting away, bled dry by an empty field.

[10] Tender-hearted women cooked their own children. Their own babies became food when my people came undone.

[11] The Lord emptied his fury. He poured out his burning rage. He kindled a fire in Zion that ate her foundations whole.

[12] No king on earth, no one anywhere believed it could happen — that an enemy could breach Jerusalem's gates.

[13] But it happened. Because of her prophets' sins, her priests' crimes — they spilled the blood of the righteous right there in her streets.

[14] Now they stumble through those streets like blind men, smeared with that blood, untouchable — no one dares brush their robes.

[15] "Away! Unclean!" people shout at them. "Away, away — don't touch!" They wander, homeless among the nations: "They can't stay here either."

[16] The Lord himself scattered them. He won't look their way again. The priests got no respect; the elders, no mercy.

[17] Our eyes wore out watching for help that never came. From our towers we scanned for a nation that couldn't save us.

[18] They tracked our every step — we couldn't even walk our own streets. The end closed in. Our days ran out. The end had come.

[19] Our pursuers ran swifter than eagles in the sky. They chased us across the mountains, ambushed us in the wilderness.

[20] The Lord's anointed — the breath in our lungs — got trapped in their pits. The one we said would shelter us among the nations.

[21] Celebrate, Edom. Throw your party, you who live in Uz. The cup is coming for you too. You'll drink it. You'll strip yourself naked, drunk.

[22] Zion — your sentence is served. He won't drag you into exile again. But Edom — he's about to settle accounts with you. He's about to expose every sin.

[1] Remember, LORD, what has crashed down on us. Look. See our disgrace.

[2] Our inheritance — handed to strangers. Our houses — given to foreigners.

[3] We are orphans now, fatherless. Our mothers are widows.

[4] We pay for the water we drink. We buy the wood that was ours.

[5] The yoke is on our necks. They drive us hard. We are exhausted. No rest comes.

[6] We held out our hands to Egypt, to Assyria — just for a crust of bread.

[7] Our fathers sinned and are gone. We are the ones swallowing the bill.

[8] Slaves rule over us, and no one breaks us free from their grip.

[9] We risk our lives for bread, dodging the sword that prowls the wilderness.

[10] Our skin burns like an oven, scorched by the fever of famine.

[11] Women raped in Zion. Virgins violated in the towns of Judah.

[12] Princes strung up by their hands. Elders shown no respect.

[13] Young men hauled to the grinding mill. Boys collapsing under loads of wood.

[14] The elders have vanished from the city gate. The young men have stopped their music.

[15] Joy has drained from our hearts. Our dancing has turned to grief.

[16] The crown has fallen from our head. Disaster on us — we sinned.

[17] This is why our hearts are sick. This is why our eyes have gone dim:

[18] because Mount Zion lies desolate, and jackals slink across it.

[19] But you, LORD — you reign forever. Your throne stands generation after generation.

[20] Why do you keep forgetting us? Why abandon us for so long?

[21] Bring us back to you, LORD, and we will return. Make our days new again, like before —

[22] unless you have utterly rejected us, unless your fury against us has no end.

[1] The LORD called to Moses and spoke to him from the tent of meeting:

[2] Speak to the Israelites. Tell them: When any of you brings an offering to the LORD, bring it from the herd or the flock.

[3] If the offering is a burnt offering from the herd, it must be a male without defect. Bring it to the entrance of the tent of meeting so you will be accepted before the LORD.

[4] Lay your hand on the animal's head, and it will be accepted on your behalf — to make atonement for you.

[5] Slaughter the bull there before the LORD. Then Aaron's sons, the priests, will present the blood and splash it against every side of the altar at the entrance of the tent of meeting.

[6] Skin the burnt offering and cut it into pieces.

[7] The sons of Aaron the priest will set fire on the altar and arrange wood on the flames.

[8] Then the priests, Aaron's sons, will arrange the pieces — head and suet — on the wood burning on the altar.

[9] The entrails and legs must be washed with water, and the priest will send the whole thing up in smoke on the altar. A burnt offering. A fire-gift. An aroma that pleases the LORD.

[10] If the offering is from the flock — a sheep or a goat for a burnt offering — bring a male without defect.

[11] Slaughter it on the north side of the altar before the LORD, and Aaron's sons, the priests, will splash its blood against every side of the altar.

[12] Cut it into pieces — head, suet — and the priest will arrange them on the wood burning on the altar.

[13] The entrails and legs must be washed with water. The priest will present the whole offering and send it up in smoke on the altar. A burnt offering. A fire-gift. An aroma that pleases the LORD.

[14] If the offering to the LORD is a burnt offering of birds, bring turtledoves or young pigeons.

[15] The priest will bring it to the altar, wring off its head, and send it up in smoke. Its blood must be drained against the side of the altar.

[16] He will remove the crop with its contents and throw it east of the altar, into the place for ashes.

[17] He will tear it open by the wings without severing it, and the priest will send it up in smoke on the altar, on the wood burning in the fire. A burnt offering. A fire-gift. An aroma that pleases the LORD.

[1] When you bring a grain offering to the LORD, let it be fine flour. Pour oil over it. Lay frankincense on it. [2] Carry it to Aaron's sons, the priests. One of them takes a fistful — flour, oil, and all the frankincense — and turns it to smoke on the altar. A memorial portion. A fire-offering. A scent that pleases the LORD. [3] What remains belongs to Aaron and his sons — holy of holies, drawn from the fires of the LORD.

[4] If your grain offering comes from the oven, bring it as unleavened loaves of fine flour mixed with oil, or as unleavened wafers brushed with oil. [5] If it comes from the griddle, fine flour kneaded with oil, unleavened. [6] Break it into pieces. Pour oil over it. It is a grain offering. [7] If it comes from the pan, fine flour with oil.

[8] However it is made, carry the grain offering to the LORD. Present it to the priest. He brings it to the altar. [9] He lifts out the memorial portion and turns it to smoke — a fire-offering, a scent that pleases the LORD. [10] What remains belongs to Aaron and his sons — holy of holies, drawn from the fires of the LORD.

[11] No grain offering you bring to the LORD shall be made with leaven. No leaven, no honey — never burn these as a fire-offering to the LORD. [12] You may bring them as a firstfruits offering, but they must not climb the altar as a pleasing aroma. [13] Salt every grain offering. Never let the salt of your God's covenant go missing from your grain offering. On every offering, salt.

[14] When you bring the LORD a grain offering of firstfruits, bring fresh heads of grain, roasted in fire, crushed from ripe kernels. That is your firstfruits offering. [15] Pour oil on it. Lay frankincense on it. A grain offering. [16] The priest turns the memorial portion to smoke — crushed grain, oil, and all the frankincense. A fire-offering to the LORD.

[1] If someone brings a peace offering from the herd, male or female, it must come before the LORD without defect.

[2] He lays his hand on the animal's head and slaughters it at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting. Aaron's sons, the priests, splash the blood against every side of the altar.

[3] From the peace offering he presents this as a fire-gift to the LORD: the fat that wraps the entrails, all the fat connected to them,

[4] both kidneys with the fat on them at the loins, and the lobe over the liver—removed with the kidneys.

[5] Aaron's sons turn it to smoke on the altar, on top of the burnt offering laid over the wood on the fire—a fire-gift, a fragrance that pleases the LORD.

[6] If the peace offering to the LORD comes from the flock, male or female, it must be without defect.

[7] If he offers a lamb, he brings it before the LORD,

[8] lays his hand on its head, and slaughters it in front of the Tent of Meeting. Aaron's sons splash its blood against every side of the altar.

[9] From the peace offering he presents this as a fire-gift to the LORD: its fat, the entire fat tail cut off close to the backbone, the fat wrapping the entrails, all the fat connected to them,

[10] both kidneys with the fat on them at the loins, and the lobe over the liver—removed with the kidneys.

[11] The priest turns it to smoke on the altar—food, a fire-gift to the LORD.

[12] If his offering is a goat, he brings it before the LORD,

[13] lays his hand on its head, and slaughters it in front of the Tent of Meeting. Aaron's sons splash its blood against every side of the altar.

[14] From it he presents this as a fire-gift to the LORD: the fat wrapping the entrails, all the fat connected to them,

[15] both kidneys with the fat on them at the loins, and the lobe over the liver—removed with the kidneys.

[16] The priest turns them to smoke on the altar—food, a fire-gift, a pleasing fragrance. All the fat belongs to the LORD.

[17] This is a permanent statute through all your generations, wherever you live: you will eat no fat and no blood. None.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[2] "Tell the Israelites: When someone sins by accident—violating any of the LORD's commands, doing what should never be done—

[3] if it's the anointed priest who sins, dragging guilt onto the whole people, he must bring to the LORD a young bull without defect as a purification offering for the wrong he committed.

[4] He brings the bull to the entrance of the tent of meeting, before the LORD. He lays his hand on its head and slaughters it before the LORD.

[5] Then the anointed priest takes some of the bull's blood and carries it into the tent of meeting.

[6] He dips his finger in the blood and sprinkles it seven times before the LORD, in front of the curtain of the sanctuary.

[7] He smears some of the blood on the horns of the incense altar—the fragrant altar before the LORD inside the tent. The rest of the bull's blood he pours out at the base of the altar of burnt offering at the tent's entrance.

[8] He removes all the fat from the purification-offering bull: the fat covering the entrails and all the fat attached to them,

[9] the two kidneys with the fat on them near the loins, and the lobe of the liver, which he takes off along with the kidneys—

[10] just as it's done with the bull of the fellowship offering. The priest burns these on the altar of burnt offering.

[11] But the bull's hide, all its flesh, its head, legs, entrails, and dung—

[12] the entire animal—he carries outside the camp to a ritually clean place, the ash heap, and burns it there on wood. On the ash heap it burns.

[13] If the whole community of Israel sins by accident—something hidden from the assembly's eyes, a violation of any of the LORD's commands, bringing them guilt—

[14] when the sin they committed becomes known, the assembly brings a young bull as a purification offering and presents it before the tent of meeting.

[15] The elders of the community lay their hands on the bull's head before the LORD, and it is slaughtered before the LORD.

[16] The anointed priest carries some of the bull's blood into the tent of meeting.

[17] He dips his finger in the blood and sprinkles it seven times before the LORD, in front of the curtain.

[18] He smears blood on the horns of the altar before the LORD inside the tent, then pours the rest at the base of the altar of burnt offering at the tent's entrance.

[19] He removes all its fat and burns it on the altar.

[20] He handles this bull just as he handled the bull of the purification offering—the same procedure. The priest makes atonement for them, and they are forgiven.

[21] He carries the bull outside the camp and burns it as he burned the first one. This is the purification offering for the assembly.

[22] When a leader sins by accident—violating any command of the LORD his God, doing what should never be done—and incurs guilt,

[23] once the sin he committed is brought to his attention, he brings as his offering a male goat without defect.

[24] He lays his hand on the goat's head and slaughters it where the burnt offering is killed, before the LORD. It is a purification offering.

[25] The priest takes some of its blood on his finger, smears it on the horns of the altar of burnt offering, and pours the rest at the altar's base.

[26] He burns all its fat on the altar, as with the fat of the fellowship offering. The priest makes atonement for him for his sin, and he is forgiven.

[27] When one of the common people sins by accident—violating any of the LORD's commands, doing what should never be done—and incurs guilt,

[28] once the sin is brought to his attention, he brings as his offering a female goat without defect for the sin he committed.

[29] He lays his hand on the head of the purification offering and slaughters it at the place of burnt offering.

[30] The priest takes some of its blood on his finger, smears it on the horns of the altar of burnt offering, and pours the rest at the altar's base.

[31] He removes all the fat as fat is removed from the fellowship offering, and the priest burns it on the altar—a pleasing aroma to the LORD. The priest makes atonement for him, and he is forgiven.

[32] If he brings a lamb as his purification offering, it must be a female without defect.

[33] He lays his hand on its head and slaughters it as a purification offering at the place where the burnt offering is killed.

[34] The priest takes some of the blood on his finger, smears it on the horns of the altar of burnt offering, and pours the rest at the altar's base.

[35] He removes all the fat as the fat is removed from the lamb of the fellowship offering, and the priest burns it on the altar atop the LORD's fire offerings. The priest makes atonement for him for the sin he committed, and he is forgiven."

[1] If someone witnesses something — hears the public oath, knows what happened, saw it with their own eyes — and stays silent, they carry the guilt.

[2] If someone touches anything unclean — the carcass of a wild animal, livestock, or a creeping thing — even unknowingly, they are contaminated. They bear the guilt.

[3] Or if they touch human uncleanness of any kind, and only realize it later — they are guilty.

[4] Or if someone blurts out an oath — careless words, sworn rashly, for good or for ill, whatever people swear — and then it hits them what they've done: they are guilty.

[5] In any of these cases, the moment guilt becomes clear, they must confess what they did.

[6] Then they bring their reparation to the LORD for the wrong committed: a female from the flock, a lamb or a young goat, as a purification offering. The priest will make atonement for them.

[7] If they cannot afford a lamb, they bring two turtledoves or two young pigeons to the LORD — one as a purification offering, the other as a burnt offering.

[8] They hand them to the priest, who offers the purification bird first. He wrings its neck without severing the head,

[9] sprinkles its blood against the side of the altar, and drains the rest at the base. It is a purification offering.

[10] The second bird he offers as a burnt offering, by the prescribed ritual. So the priest makes atonement for the sin, and the person is forgiven.

[11] If even two birds are beyond their means, they bring a tenth of an ephah of fine flour as a purification offering. No oil. No frankincense. This is not a grain offering — it is for sin.

[12] They bring it to the priest, who takes a handful as the memorial portion and burns it on the altar over the LORD's fire offerings. It is a purification offering.

[13] The priest makes atonement for whichever sin they committed, and they are forgiven. The remainder belongs to the priest, like the grain offering.

[14] Then the LORD spoke to Moses:

[15] When someone breaks faith — commits an unintentional violation against the sacred things of the LORD — they must bring to the LORD a ram from the flock, unblemished, valued in silver shekels by the sanctuary standard. This is a reparation offering.

[16] They must restore what was taken from the sacred, add a fifth of its value, and hand it to the priest. The priest will make atonement with the reparation ram, and they will be forgiven.

[17] If someone sins, doing what the LORD's commands forbid — even unknowingly — they still bear the guilt. The weight is on them.

[18] They bring a ram from the flock, unblemished, of the proper value, to the priest as a reparation offering. The priest makes atonement for the mistake they made without knowing, and they are forgiven.

[19] It is a reparation offering. They were truly guilty before the LORD.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[2] When someone sins—betrays the LORD by deceiving a neighbor about something entrusted to them, or a deposit, or stolen property, or by extorting that neighbor,

[3] or by finding lost property and lying about it, swearing falsely about any of the things people do that count as sin—

[4] once they've sinned and know their guilt, they must give back what they stole, or what they extorted, or what was entrusted to them, or the lost item they found,

[5] or whatever it was they swore falsely about. They pay it back in full, then add a fifth on top, and hand it over to the rightful owner on the day they bring their guilt offering.

[6] To the priest, as their guilt offering to the LORD, they bring a ram without blemish from the flock—properly valued—as a guilt offering.

[7] The priest makes atonement for them before the LORD, and they are forgiven for whatever they did that brought guilt.

[8] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[9] Give Aaron and his sons these instructions about the burnt offering: The burnt offering stays on the altar hearth all night until morning, and the altar fire keeps burning on it.

[10] The priest puts on his linen tunic, with linen undergarments against his skin, and lifts away the ashes left after the fire consumed the burnt offering, placing them beside the altar.

[11] Then he changes his clothes and carries the ashes outside the camp to a clean place.

[12] The fire on the altar keeps burning—it must not go out. Every morning the priest feeds it wood, arranges the burnt offering on it, and turns the fat of the peace offerings into smoke on top.

[13] A perpetual fire—it must burn on the altar without ceasing.

[14] These are the instructions for the grain offering: Aaron's sons bring it before the LORD, in front of the altar.

[15] One of them scoops up a handful of the fine flour and oil, with all the frankincense on top of the grain offering, and turns this memorial portion into smoke on the altar—a pleasing aroma to the LORD.

[16] Aaron and his sons eat what's left. They eat it as unleavened bread in a holy place—in the courtyard of the tent of meeting.

[17] It must not be baked with leaven. I have given it as their share of my food offerings—most holy, like the sin offering and the guilt offering.

[18] Every male descendant of Aaron may eat it. It is the permanent portion from the LORD's food offerings, across all your generations. Whatever touches them becomes holy.

[19] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[20] This is what Aaron and his sons bring to the LORD on the day each is anointed: a tenth of an ephah of fine flour as a regular grain offering, half in the morning and half in the evening.

[21] Prepare it on a griddle with oil, well-mixed, and bring it in. Present the broken pieces of the grain offering as a pleasing aroma to the LORD.

[22] The anointed priest among Aaron's sons who succeeds him offers it. This is a permanent statute—it is wholly burned to the LORD.

[23] Every grain offering of a priest is burned entirely. It is not to be eaten.

[24] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[25] Tell Aaron and his sons—these are the instructions for the sin offering: At the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered, the sin offering is slaughtered before the LORD. It is most holy.

[26] The priest who offers it eats it. He eats it in a holy place—in the courtyard of the tent of meeting.

[27] Whatever touches its flesh becomes holy. If any of its blood splatters on a garment, you must wash that spot in a holy place.

[28] Any clay pot it was cooked in must be smashed; if it was cooked in a bronze pot, the pot must be scoured and rinsed with water.

[29] Every male among the priests may eat it. It is most holy.

[30] But any sin offering whose blood is brought into the tent of meeting to make atonement in the Holy Place must not be eaten. It is burned in the fire.

[1] Here is the law of the guilt offering — it is most holy.

[2] In the place where they slaughter the burnt offering, there they slaughter the guilt offering. Its blood is dashed against the altar on every side.

[3] All its fat is offered: the fat tail, the fat covering the entrails,

[4] the two kidneys with the fat on them at the loins, and the lobe of the liver, removed with the kidneys.
