[5] The priest sends it up in smoke on the altar — a fire-offering to the LORD. It is a guilt offering.

[6] Every male among the priests may eat it. It must be eaten in a holy place. It is most holy.

[7] The sin offering and the guilt offering follow the same rule: the priest who makes atonement with it — it belongs to him.

[8] The priest who presents anyone's burnt offering keeps the hide of that animal for himself.

[9] Every grain offering baked in the oven, or cooked in a pan or on a griddle — it belongs to the priest who presents it.

[10] But every grain offering mixed with oil or dry — that one belongs to all of Aaron's sons, shared equally.

[11] Here is the law of the peace offering brought to the LORD.

[12] If it is offered as thanksgiving, then alongside the thanksgiving sacrifice bring unleavened cakes mixed with oil, unleavened wafers brushed with oil, and cakes of fine flour kneaded and saturated with oil.

[13] Along with these cakes, bring loaves of leavened bread as part of the thanksgiving peace offering.

[14] From each of these, one piece is lifted as a contribution to the LORD. It goes to the priest who dashes the blood of the peace offering.

[15] The meat of the thanksgiving peace offering must be eaten the day it is offered. Leave none of it until morning.

[16] If the sacrifice is a vow or a freewill offering, it is eaten the day it is offered, and whatever is left over may be eaten the next day.

[17] But anything still left on the third day must be burned in fire.

[18] If any of it is eaten on the third day, the offering will not count. It will not be credited to the one who brought it — it becomes foul, and anyone who eats it bears the guilt.

[19] Meat that touches anything unclean must not be eaten — burn it. As for clean meat, anyone who is clean may eat it.

[20] But if someone unclean eats meat from the LORD's peace offering, that person is cut off from his people.

[21] And if anyone touches something unclean — human uncleanness, an unclean animal, any vile thing — and then eats from the LORD's peace offering, that person is cut off from his people.

[22] The LORD said to Moses:

[23] Tell the Israelites: You shall eat no fat — not of ox, not of sheep, not of goat.

[24] The fat of an animal found dead or torn by beasts may be put to any other use, but you must never eat it.

[25] Anyone who eats fat from an animal offered as a fire-offering to the LORD — that person is cut off from his people.

[26] And wherever you live, you shall consume no blood — not of bird, not of beast.

[27] Anyone who consumes any blood — that person is cut off from his people.

[28] The LORD said to Moses:

[29] Tell the Israelites: Whoever brings a peace offering to the LORD must present part of it with his own hands as the LORD's portion.

[30] His own hands bring the fire-offerings of the LORD. He brings the fat together with the breast — the breast to be lifted high as a wave offering before the LORD.

[31] The priest sends the fat up in smoke on the altar, and the breast goes to Aaron and his sons.

[32] You give the right thigh to the priest as a contribution from your peace offerings.

[33] Whichever of Aaron's sons offers the blood and the fat of the peace offering — the right thigh is his share.

[34] For I have taken from the Israelites the breast of the wave offering and the thigh of the contribution out of their peace offerings, and I have given them to Aaron the priest and to his sons as a perpetual due from the Israelites.

[35] This is the portion assigned to Aaron and his sons from the LORD's fire-offerings, from the day they were brought near to serve as priests to the LORD —

[36] what the LORD commanded to be given them from the Israelites on the day he anointed them. It is a lasting decree across the generations.

[37] This, then, is the law of the burnt offering, the grain offering, the sin offering, the guilt offering, the ordination offering, and the peace offering —

[38] which the LORD gave to Moses on Mount Sinai, on the day he commanded Israel to bring their offerings to the LORD, in the wilderness of Sinai.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[2] "Bring Aaron and his sons forward. Bring the vestments, the anointing oil, the bull for the sin offering, the two rams, and the basket of unleavened bread.

[3] Gather the whole assembly at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting."

[4] Moses did exactly as the LORD commanded, and the assembly gathered at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting.

[5] Moses addressed them: "This is what the LORD has commanded be done."

[6] He brought Aaron and his sons forward and washed them with water.

[7] He dressed Aaron in the tunic, cinched the sash, draped him in the robe, set the ephod over it, bound it with the ephod's woven band, and fastened it all to him.

[8] He placed the breastpiece on him, and into the breastpiece he set the Urim and the Thummim.

[9] He set the turban on Aaron's head, and on the front of the turban he fixed the gold medallion — the holy crown — exactly as the LORD had commanded Moses.

[10] Then Moses took the anointing oil and anointed the Tabernacle and everything in it, consecrating them.

[11] He sprinkled some of the oil on the altar seven times, then anointed the altar and all its vessels, the basin and its stand — setting them apart as holy.

[12] He poured anointing oil on Aaron's head and anointed him, consecrating him.

[13] Moses brought Aaron's sons forward, clothed them in tunics, bound them with sashes, and wrapped their heads with caps — exactly as the LORD had commanded Moses.

[14] He led out the bull for the sin offering, and Aaron and his sons pressed their hands down on its head.

[15] Moses slaughtered it. He took the blood, smeared it with his finger on the horns of the altar — purifying the altar — then poured the rest at its base. He set it apart, making atonement for it.

[16] He took all the fat covering the entrails, the lobe of the liver, both kidneys with their fat, and turned them into smoke on the altar.

[17] But the bull itself — its hide, its flesh, its waste — he burned outside the camp, exactly as the LORD had commanded him.

[18] He brought out the ram for the burnt offering, and Aaron and his sons pressed their hands down on its head.

[19] Moses slaughtered it and dashed the blood against every side of the altar.

[20] He cut the ram into its sections, and turned the head, the sections, and the suet into smoke.

[21] He washed the entrails and the legs with water, then turned the entire ram into smoke on the altar — a burnt offering, a pleasing aroma, a fire-gift to the LORD, exactly as the LORD had commanded Moses.

[22] He brought out the second ram — the ram of ordination — and Aaron and his sons pressed their hands down on its head.

[23] Moses slaughtered it. He took some of its blood and put it on the lobe of Aaron's right ear, on the thumb of his right hand, and on the big toe of his right foot.

[24] Then he brought Aaron's sons forward and put blood on the lobe of their right ears, the thumbs of their right hands, and the big toes of their right feet. He dashed the rest of the blood against every side of the altar.

[25] He took the fat — the tail, all the fat covering the entrails, the lobe of the liver, both kidneys with their fat — and the right thigh.

[26] From the basket of unleavened bread before the LORD, he took one unleavened loaf, one loaf made with oil, and one wafer, and placed them on top of the fat and the right thigh.

[27] He put all of it in the hands of Aaron and his sons and elevated it as a wave offering before the LORD.

[28] Then Moses took it from their hands and turned it into smoke on the altar with the burnt offering — an ordination offering, a pleasing aroma, a fire-gift to the LORD.

[29] Moses also took the breast and elevated it as a wave offering before the LORD. This was Moses' portion of the ordination ram, exactly as the LORD had commanded him.

[30] Moses took some of the anointing oil and some of the blood from the altar and sprinkled it on Aaron and his vestments, and on his sons and their vestments. So he consecrated Aaron, his vestments, his sons, and their vestments.

[31] Moses said to Aaron and his sons: "Boil the meat at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting and eat it there, along with the bread in the ordination basket — as I was commanded: 'Aaron and his sons shall eat it.'

[32] What remains of the meat and the bread, burn in the fire.

[33] Do not leave the entrance of the Tent of Meeting for seven days — until your days of ordination are complete. Your ordination takes seven days.

[34] What was done today the LORD has commanded to be done — to make atonement for you.

[35] You will remain at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting day and night for seven days, keeping the LORD's charge — so you do not die. This is what I have been commanded."

[36] Aaron and his sons did everything the LORD had commanded through Moses.

[1] On the eighth day, Moses called Aaron, his sons, and the elders of Israel together.

[2] He said to Aaron, "Take a bull calf for a sin offering and a ram for a burnt offering, both without defect, and bring them before the LORD.

[3] Then tell the Israelites: 'Take a male goat for a sin offering, and a calf and a lamb — both yearlings, both flawless — for a burnt offering,

[4] along with an ox and a ram for a peace offering, to sacrifice before the LORD, and a grain offering mixed with oil. Today the LORD is going to appear to you.'"

[5] So they brought what Moses commanded to the front of the tent of meeting, and the whole assembly drew near and stood before the LORD.

[6] Moses said, "This is what the LORD commanded you to do, so that his glory will appear to you."

[7] Then Moses said to Aaron, "Approach the altar. Offer your sin offering and your burnt offering — make atonement for yourself and for the people. Then present the people's offering and make atonement for them, exactly as the LORD commanded."

[8] So Aaron approached the altar and slaughtered the calf as his own sin offering.

[9] His sons brought him the blood. He dipped his finger in it, smeared it on the horns of the altar, and poured the rest at the base.

[10] He burned the fat, the kidneys, and the lobe of the liver from the sin offering on the altar — just as the LORD had commanded Moses.

[11] The meat and the hide he burned outside the camp.

[12] Then he slaughtered the burnt offering. His sons handed him the blood, and he splashed it against every side of the altar.

[13] They passed him the burnt offering piece by piece, including the head, and he burned it on the altar.

[14] He washed the entrails and the legs and burned them with the burnt offering on the altar.

[15] Then Aaron brought forward the people's offering. He took the goat for the people's sin offering, slaughtered it, and offered it for sin like the first one.

[16] He brought the burnt offering and presented it according to the procedure.

[17] He brought the grain offering, took a handful, and burned it on the altar, in addition to the morning burnt offering.

[18] He slaughtered the ox and the ram as the peace offering for the people. His sons handed him the blood, and he splashed it against every side of the altar.

[19] The fat from the ox and the ram — the fat tail, the layer covering the entrails, the kidneys, the lobe of the liver —

[20] they laid on the breasts, and Aaron burned the fat on the altar.

[21] The breasts and the right thigh Aaron lifted high before the LORD as a wave offering, as Moses had commanded.

[22] Then Aaron lifted his hands toward the people and blessed them. Having finished the sin offering, the burnt offering, and the peace offering, he came down.

[23] Moses and Aaron went into the tent of meeting. When they came out, they blessed the people — and the glory of the LORD appeared to everyone.

[24] Fire shot out from the LORD's presence and consumed the burnt offering and the fat on the altar. The people saw it, and a roar went up — they fell on their faces.

[1] Nadab and Abihu, Aaron's sons, each grabbed his censer, loaded it with coals, piled on incense, and offered fire before the LORD — fire he had never asked for, never authorized. [2] Fire came roaring out from the LORD's presence and consumed them. They died right there, in front of him.

[3] Moses turned to Aaron and said: "This is exactly what the LORD meant when he said,

*Among those who come close to me, I will be treated as holy.
In the sight of all the people, I will be honored.*"

Aaron said nothing.

[4] Moses called Mishael and Elzaphan, sons of Aaron's uncle Uzziel, and said, "Come here. Carry your cousins out of the sanctuary, beyond the camp." [5] They came, lifted them by their tunics — still on the bodies — and carried them outside the camp, just as Moses had ordered.

[6] Then Moses said to Aaron and his remaining sons, Eleazar and Ithamar: "Don't unbind your hair. Don't tear your clothes. If you do, you'll die too, and God's anger will spill onto the whole assembly. Let your kin — the whole house of Israel — weep for this burning the LORD has kindled. [7] But you — do not step outside the entrance of the tent of meeting. If you do, you die. The anointing oil of the LORD is on you."

They did as Moses said.

[8] The LORD spoke to Aaron directly: [9] "When you go into the tent of meeting, you and your sons with you — no wine, no strong drink. Or you die. This is permanent, generation after generation. [10] You must be able to tell the holy from the common, the clean from the unclean, [11] and teach Israel every statute the LORD has spoken to them through Moses."

[12] Moses said to Aaron and his two surviving sons, Eleazar and Ithamar: "Take the grain offering left over from the LORD's fire-offerings and eat it — unleavened — beside the altar. It's most holy. [13] Eat it in a holy place. It's your portion and your sons' portion from the LORD's fire-offerings. That's what I was commanded. [14] The breast that was waved and the thigh that was lifted up — eat those in a clean place, you and your sons and daughters with you. They're yours and your children's, given out of the Israelites' peace offerings. [15] The thigh and the breast they bring along with the fat offerings — to wave before the LORD — belong to you and your children forever, as the LORD commanded."

[16] Then Moses went looking for the goat of the sin offering — and discovered it had already been burned up. He turned on Eleazar and Ithamar, Aaron's surviving sons, furious: [17] "Why didn't you eat the sin offering in the holy place? It's most holy. He gave it to you to carry the guilt of the community, to make atonement for them before the LORD. [18] Look — its blood was never brought into the holy place. You should have eaten it there in the sanctuary, exactly as I commanded."

[19] Aaron answered: "Look — today they presented their sin offering and their burnt offering before the LORD, and *this* is what happened to me. If I had eaten the sin offering today, would that have pleased the LORD?"

[20] Moses heard him out. And he was satisfied.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron: [2] "Tell the Israelites: Of all the animals that walk the earth, here is what you may eat.

[3] Any animal with a fully split hoof that also chews the cud — that one is yours to eat.

[4] But these you must not eat, even though they chew the cud or have a split hoof:

The camel — it chews the cud but its hoof is not split. Unclean to you.

[5] The rock badger — chews the cud but no split hoof. Unclean.

[6] The hare — chews the cud but no split hoof. Unclean.

[7] The pig — its hoof is split, fully divided, but it does not chew the cud. Unclean to you.

[8] Do not eat their flesh. Do not touch their carcasses. They are unclean to you.

[9] From the waters, here is what you may eat: anything with fins and scales, whether from seas or rivers — eat it.

[10] But anything without fins and scales, anything swarming or teeming in the waters — these are repulsive to you.

[11] They will remain repulsive. Do not eat their flesh. Treat their carcasses as detestable.

[12] Anything in the water without fins and scales is repulsive to you.

[13] These birds you are to detest — do not eat them, they are vile: the eagle, the vulture, the black vulture,

[14] the kite, every kind of falcon,

[15] every kind of raven,

[16] the ostrich, the nighthawk, the seagull, every kind of hawk,

[17] the little owl, the cormorant, the great owl,

[18] the white owl, the desert owl, the osprey,

[19] the stork, every kind of heron, the hoopoe, and the bat.

[20] Every winged insect that walks on all fours is repulsive to you.

[21] But these you may eat among the winged insects that walk on all fours: those with jointed legs above their feet for leaping on the ground.

[22] Specifically: every kind of locust, every kind of bald locust, every kind of cricket, every kind of grasshopper.

[23] Every other winged insect with four legs is repulsive to you.

[24] You make yourselves unclean through these. Anyone who touches their carcasses is unclean until evening,

[25] and anyone who carries any part of their carcass must wash their clothes — unclean until evening.

[26] Every animal with a hoof that is not fully split, or that does not chew the cud — unclean to you. Touch the carcass and you become unclean.

[27] Anything that walks on its paws, among all four-footed animals — unclean to you. Touch the carcass, unclean until evening.

[28] Whoever carries the carcass must wash their clothes — unclean until evening. These are unclean to you.

[29] Among the creatures that swarm the ground, these are unclean to you: the mole, the mouse, every kind of great lizard,

[30] the gecko, the monitor lizard, the wall lizard, the skink, and the chameleon.

[31] Among all swarming things, these are unclean to you. Touch them when they are dead — unclean until evening.

[32] Anything one of them falls on, when dead, becomes unclean — whether wooden object, garment, leather, sackcloth, any tool used for work. Put it in water. It stays unclean until evening, then it is clean again.

[33] If one of them falls into a clay pot, everything inside becomes unclean — and you must smash the pot.

[34] Food that could be eaten, if water from such a pot touches it, becomes unclean. Any drink that could be drunk from such a pot — unclean.

[35] Anything their carcass touches becomes unclean. An oven or a stove must be broken down. They are unclean and they remain unclean to you.

[36] However, a spring or a cistern that collects water stays clean. But touch the carcass that fell in — unclean.

[37] If part of their carcass falls on seed meant for planting, the seed stays clean.

[38] But if water has been put on the seed and then the carcass falls on it — unclean to you.

[39] If an animal you are permitted to eat dies on its own, whoever touches the carcass is unclean until evening.

[40] Whoever eats from the carcass must wash their clothes — unclean until evening. Whoever carries the carcass must wash their clothes — unclean until evening.

[41] Every creature that swarms the ground is repulsive. Do not eat it.

[42] Anything that crawls on its belly, anything that walks on all fours, anything with many legs — every swarming thing on the ground — do not eat. They are repulsive.

[43] Do not make yourselves repulsive through any swarming creature. Do not make yourselves unclean through them. Do not let them defile you.

[44] I am the LORD your God. Consecrate yourselves and be holy, because I am holy. Do not defile yourselves with any creature that crawls the ground.

[45] I am the LORD who brought you up out of Egypt to be your God. Be holy, because I am holy.

[46] This is the instruction concerning beasts and birds, every living thing that moves through the waters, every creature that swarms the ground —

[47] so you can tell the unclean from the clean, what may be eaten from what may not."

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses: [2] Tell the Israelites this: When a woman conceives and gives birth to a boy, she will be ceremonially unclean for seven days — the same as during her monthly cycle. [3] On the eighth day, the boy is to be circumcised. [4] Then she waits another thirty-three days for her blood to purify. She must not touch anything sacred or enter the sanctuary until her time of purification is complete.

[5] If she gives birth to a girl, she will be unclean for two weeks — twice as long as her cycle — and then wait another sixty-six days for her blood to purify.

[6] When her days of purification are finished — whether for a son or a daughter — she is to bring a year-old lamb as a burnt offering and a young pigeon or a turtledove as a sin offering. She brings them to the priest at the entrance of the tent of meeting. [7] He will offer them before the LORD and make atonement for her, and she will be clean from the flow of her blood. This is the law for any woman who gives birth, whether to a son or a daughter.

[8] If she cannot afford a lamb, she is to bring two turtledoves or two young pigeons — one for a burnt offering, the other for a sin offering. The priest will make atonement for her, and she will be clean.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron:

[2] When someone develops a swelling, a rash, or a bright spot on the skin that could be a serious skin disease, bring them to Aaron the priest, or to one of his sons who serves as priest.

[3] The priest examines the spot. If the hair in it has turned white and the affliction looks deeper than the surrounding skin, it is a serious skin disease. The priest declares the person unclean.

[4] But if the bright spot is white but does not appear deeper than the skin, and the hair has not turned white, the priest isolates the person for seven days.

[5] On day seven the priest looks again. If the affliction looks unchanged and has not spread, the priest isolates them another seven days.

[6] On day seven the priest examines them once more. If the spot has faded and not spread, the priest declares them clean — it was only a rash. They wash their clothes and they are clean.

[7] But if the rash spreads after the priest examined and cleared them, they must appear before the priest again.

[8] The priest looks. If it has spread on the skin, the priest declares them unclean. It is the disease.

[9] When someone shows symptoms of the disease, bring them to the priest.

[10] The priest examines them. If there is a white swelling on the skin that has turned the hair white, with raw flesh exposed within it —

[11] this is a chronic, festering skin disease. The priest declares them unclean. No isolation period. They are already unclean.

[12] But if the disease breaks out across the entire body — covering them from head to foot, everywhere the priest can see —

[13] the priest examines them. If it covers their whole body, he declares them clean. They have turned completely white. They are clean.

[14] But the moment raw flesh shows on them, they are unclean.

[15] The priest sees the raw flesh and declares them unclean. Raw flesh is unclean. It is the disease.

[16] If the raw flesh changes back and turns white, they return to the priest.

[17] The priest examines them. If the affliction has turned white, the priest declares them clean. They are clean.

[18] When a boil heals on the skin —

[19] but then in the place of the boil a white swelling appears or a reddish-white bright spot — the person shows themselves to the priest.

[20] The priest looks. If it appears deeper than the skin, with the hair turned white, the priest declares them unclean. It is the disease, breaking out from the boil.

[21] But if the priest finds no white hair in it, no depth below the skin, and the spot has faded, the priest isolates them seven days.

[22] If it spreads, the priest declares them unclean. It is the disease.

[23] If the bright spot stays put and does not spread, it is only the scar of the boil. The priest declares them clean.

[24] When skin gets a burn from fire, and the raw flesh of the burn becomes a reddish-white or pure white spot,

[25] the priest examines it. If the hair in the bright spot has turned white and the spot appears deeper than the skin, it is the disease breaking out from the burn. The priest declares them unclean. It is the disease.

[26] But if the priest finds no white hair in the bright spot, no depth below the skin, and the spot has faded, the priest isolates them seven days.

[27] On day seven the priest examines them. If it has spread, the priest declares them unclean. It is the disease.

[28] But if the bright spot stays put, has not spread, and has faded, it is only the swelling from the burn. The priest declares them clean. It is just the scar of the burn.

[29] When a man or woman gets an affliction on the scalp or in the beard,

[30] the priest examines it. If it appears deeper than the skin and there is thin yellow hair in it, the priest declares them unclean. It is a scaly infection — the disease of the head or beard.

[31] But when the priest examines the scaly infection and it does not appear deeper than the skin, though there is no black hair in it, the priest isolates the person seven days.

[32] On day seven the priest examines the affliction. If the scaly area has not spread, no yellow hair has appeared in it, and the scaly area does not appear deeper than the skin,

[33] the person shaves — but not the scaly patch — and the priest isolates them another seven days.

[34] On day seven the priest examines the scaly patch. If it has not spread on the skin and does not appear deeper than the skin, the priest declares them clean. They wash their clothes and they are clean.

[35] But if the scaly patch spreads after they have been declared clean,

[36] the priest examines them. If it has spread, the priest does not even need to look for yellow hair. They are unclean.

[37] But if the scaly area looks unchanged and black hair has grown back in it, it has healed. They are clean. The priest declares them clean.

[38] When a man or woman has bright spots on the skin — white spots —

[39] the priest examines them. If the spots on the skin are a dull white, it is only a harmless rash that has broken out on the skin. They are clean.

[40] When a man loses the hair on his head — he is bald, but he is clean.

[41] If he loses hair at the front, his forehead is bald, but he is clean.

[42] But if a reddish-white affliction appears on the bald scalp or bald forehead, it is the disease breaking out there.

[43] The priest examines him. If the swelling of the affliction on his bald scalp or forehead is reddish-white, looking like the disease that erupts on skin,

[44] the man has the disease. He is unclean. The priest declares him unclean. The affliction is on his head.

[45] A person with the disease wears torn clothes, lets their hair hang loose, covers the mustache, and cries out: "Unclean! Unclean!"

[46] As long as the affliction lasts, they remain unclean. They live alone. Their dwelling is outside the camp.

[47] When the disease shows up on clothing — wool or linen —

[48] in the weave or in the knit, in linen or wool, or on leather or anything made from leather —

[49] if the mark on the garment, leather, weave, knit, or any leather item is green or red, it is the disease. Show it to the priest.

[50] The priest examines the mark and isolates the article seven days.

[51] On day seven he examines the mark. If it has spread on the garment — the weave, the knit, the leather, whatever the leather is used for — it is a corrosive disease. It is unclean.

[52] He burns the garment, the weave, the knit — wool or linen — or any leather item that has the mark. It is a corrosive disease. It must be burned in fire.

[53] But if the priest examines it and the mark has not spread on the garment,

[54] he orders the article washed and isolates it another seven days.

[55] The priest examines the article after washing. If the mark has not changed appearance, even though it has not spread, it is unclean. Burn it. It is a corrosive sore, whether on the inside or the outside.

[56] But if the priest examines it and the mark has faded after washing, he tears it out of the garment, the leather, the weave, or the knit.

[57] If it reappears on the garment — the weave, the knit, the leather article — it is breaking out. Burn whatever has the mark.

[58] The garment, weave, knit, or leather article that has been washed and the mark is gone — wash it again and it is clean.

[59] This is the instruction for the disease on clothing — wool or linen, weave or knit, any leather item — for declaring it clean or unclean.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[2] Here is the protocol for cleansing someone with a skin disease on the day they're brought back. They're brought to the priest,

[3] and the priest goes outside the camp to examine them. If the disease has healed,

[4] the priest orders two living clean birds, cedar wood, crimson yarn, and hyssop to be brought for the one being cleansed.

[5] The priest orders one bird killed over fresh water in a clay vessel.

[6] Then he takes the living bird, the cedar wood, the crimson yarn, and the hyssop, and dips them — along with the living bird — into the blood of the bird killed over the fresh water.

[7] He sprinkles the blood seven times on the one being cleansed from the disease, declares them clean, and releases the living bird into the open field.

[8] The one being cleansed washes their clothes, shaves off all their hair, bathes in water — and they are clean. After that, they may enter the camp, but they must stay outside their tent for seven days.

[9] On the seventh day, they shave all their hair again — head, beard, eyebrows, every hair. They wash their clothes, bathe in water, and they are clean.

[10] On the eighth day, they bring two unblemished male lambs, one unblemished yearling ewe, three-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil for a grain offering, and one log of oil.

[11] The priest performing the cleansing presents the person and these offerings before the LORD at the entrance of the tent of meeting.

[12] The priest takes one of the male lambs and offers it as a guilt offering, along with the log of oil, lifting them as an elevation offering before the LORD.

[13] He slaughters the lamb at the place where the sin offering and burnt offering are slaughtered, in the sanctuary. Like the sin offering, the guilt offering belongs to the priest — it is most holy.

[14] The priest takes some of the blood of the guilt offering and dabs it on the right earlobe of the one being cleansed, on the thumb of their right hand, and on the big toe of their right foot.

[15] The priest takes some of the oil and pours it into his own left palm.

[16] He dips his right finger into the oil in his palm and sprinkles it seven times before the LORD.

[17] From the oil still in his palm, the priest puts some on the right earlobe of the one being cleansed, on the thumb of their right hand, and on the big toe of their right foot — right on top of the guilt-offering blood.

[18] The rest of the oil in his palm he pours on the head of the one being cleansed. So the priest makes atonement for them before the LORD.

[19] Then the priest offers the sin offering and makes atonement for the one being cleansed from their impurity. After that, he slaughters the burnt offering

[20] and offers it on the altar with the grain offering. The priest makes atonement, and they are clean.

[21] If the person is poor and can't afford this, they bring one male lamb as a guilt offering to be elevated for atonement, plus one-tenth of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil for a grain offering, a log of oil,

[22] and two turtledoves or two young pigeons — whatever they can manage — one for a sin offering and one for a burnt offering.

[23] On the eighth day they bring these to the priest at the entrance of the tent of meeting, before the LORD, for their cleansing.

[24] The priest takes the guilt-offering lamb and the log of oil and elevates them before the LORD.

[25] He slaughters the guilt-offering lamb, takes some of its blood, and dabs it on the right earlobe of the one being cleansed, on the thumb of their right hand, and on the big toe of their right foot.

[26] The priest pours some of the oil into his own left palm

[27] and sprinkles some of it with his right finger seven times before the LORD.

[28] He dabs some of the oil in his palm on the right earlobe of the one being cleansed, on the thumb of their right hand, and on the big toe of their right foot — right where the guilt-offering blood went.

[29] The rest of the oil in his palm he pours on the head of the one being cleansed, making atonement for them before the LORD.

[30] Then he offers whichever they could afford — turtledoves or young pigeons —

[31] one as a sin offering and one as a burnt offering, with the grain offering. So the priest makes atonement before the LORD for the one being cleansed.

[32] This is the protocol for someone with a skin disease who can't afford the standard cleansing.

[33] The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron:

[34] When you enter the land of Canaan that I'm giving you as a possession, and I place an outbreak of mold in a house in your land,

[35] the owner of the house goes to the priest and reports: I've seen something like an infection in my house.

[36] The priest orders the house emptied before he goes in to examine it, so that nothing in the house becomes unclean. Then the priest enters to inspect.

[37] He examines the infection. If there are greenish or reddish streaks in the walls that appear to go deeper than the surface,

[38] the priest goes out of the house, to the doorway, and quarantines the house for seven days.

[39] On the seventh day the priest returns and inspects. If the infection has spread in the walls,

[40] he orders the contaminated stones pulled out and dumped in an unclean place outside the city.

[41] The house is scraped down inside, all around, and the scraped-off plaster is dumped in an unclean place outside the city.

[42] Replacement stones are brought in and set in place, and fresh plaster is applied to the house.

[43] If the infection breaks out again in the house after the stones have been removed and the house scraped and replastered,

[44] the priest comes and inspects. If the infection has spread, it's an aggressive mold — the house is unclean.

[45] The house is demolished — stones, timbers, all the plaster — and hauled out to an unclean place outside the city.

[46] Anyone who enters the house during its quarantine is unclean until evening.

[47] Anyone who sleeps or eats in the house must wash their clothes.

[48] But if the priest comes back and the infection hasn't spread after the house has been replastered, he declares the house clean — the infection is healed.

[49] To purify the house, he takes two birds, cedar wood, crimson yarn, and hyssop.

[50] He slaughters one bird over fresh water in a clay vessel.

[51] He takes the cedar wood, the hyssop, the crimson yarn, and the living bird, dips them in the blood of the slain bird and in the fresh water, and sprinkles the house seven times.

[52] So he purifies the house with the bird's blood, the fresh water, the living bird, the cedar wood, the hyssop, and the crimson yarn.

[53] Then he releases the living bird outside the city, into the open field. He makes atonement for the house, and it is clean.

[54] This is the protocol for every kind of skin disease and scaly outbreak,

[55] for mold in fabric and in a house,

[56] for a swelling, a rash, or a bright spot,

[57] to determine when something is unclean and when it is clean. This is the protocol for infectious disease.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron:

[2] Tell the Israelites: When any man has a discharge from his body, that discharge makes him unclean.

[3] Here is how the uncleanness works, whether his body keeps the flow going or seals it up — either way, he is unclean.

[4] Every bed he lies on is unclean. Every seat he sits on is unclean.

[5] Anyone who touches his bed must wash their clothes, bathe in water, and remain unclean until evening.

[6] Anyone who sits where he sat must wash their clothes, bathe in water, and remain unclean until evening.

[7] Anyone who touches his body must wash their clothes, bathe in water, and remain unclean until evening.

[8] If the man with the discharge spits on someone clean, that person must wash their clothes, bathe in water, and remain unclean until evening.

[9] Any saddle he rides is unclean.

[10] Anyone who touches anything that was under him is unclean until evening. Anyone who carries those things must wash their clothes, bathe in water, and remain unclean until evening.

[11] If the man with the discharge touches anyone without first rinsing his hands in water, that person must wash their clothes, bathe in water, and remain unclean until evening.

[12] Any clay pot he touches must be smashed. Any wooden vessel must be rinsed with water.

[13] When the man is finally cleansed of his discharge, he counts off seven days for his purification. He washes his clothes, bathes his body in fresh running water, and he is clean.

[14] On the eighth day he brings two turtledoves or two young pigeons, comes before the LORD at the entrance of the tent of meeting, and hands them to the priest.

[15] The priest offers one as a sin offering and the other as a burnt offering. That is how the priest makes atonement for him before the LORD, on account of his discharge.

[16] When a man has an emission of semen, he bathes his whole body in water and remains unclean until evening.

[17] Any clothing or leather touched by the semen must be washed in water and remains unclean until evening.

[18] When a man sleeps with a woman and there is an emission of semen, both of them must bathe in water and remain unclean until evening.

[19] When a woman has her monthly flow of blood, she is set apart for seven days. Anyone who touches her is unclean until evening.

[20] Everything she lies on during her period is unclean. Everything she sits on is unclean.

[21] Anyone who touches her bed must wash their clothes, bathe in water, and remain unclean until evening.

[22] Anyone who touches anything she sat on must wash their clothes, bathe in water, and remain unclean until evening.

[23] Whether it is her bed or what she was sitting on, touching it makes a person unclean until evening.

[24] If a man sleeps with her and her flow touches him, he is unclean for seven days, and every bed he lies on becomes unclean.

[25] When a woman has a flow of blood for many days outside her normal cycle, or her flow continues past it, she is unclean the whole time — just as she is during her period.

[26] Every bed she lies on during that long flow is treated like her bed during her period. Everything she sits on becomes unclean, like during her period.

[27] Anyone who touches those things is unclean. They must wash their clothes, bathe in water, and remain unclean until evening.

[28] When the flow finally stops, she counts off seven days, and after that she is clean.

[29] On the eighth day she brings two turtledoves or two young pigeons to the priest at the entrance of the tent of meeting.

[30] The priest offers one as a sin offering and the other as a burnt offering. That is how the priest makes atonement for her before the LORD, on account of her unclean flow.

[31] Keep the Israelites at a distance from their uncleanness, so they do not die in it by defiling my dwelling place that stands among them.

[32] These are the instructions for the man with a discharge, for the man whose emission of semen makes him unclean,

[33] for the woman in her monthly bleeding, for any man or woman with a flow, and for any man who sleeps with a woman who is unclean.

[1] After the two sons of Aaron died — the ones who walked into the LORD's presence with strange fire and didn't walk out — the LORD spoke to Moses. [2] He said: Tell your brother Aaron he cannot just stroll into the Holy Place behind the curtain, in front of the mercy seat on the ark, whenever he pleases. He'll die. Because I appear in the cloud, hovering over that mercy seat.

[3] Here is how Aaron enters the sanctuary: with a young bull for a sin offering and a ram for a burnt offering. [4] He puts on the sacred linen tunic, linen underclothes against his skin, the linen sash around his waist, the linen turban on his head. These are holy garments. He bathes his body in water before he puts them on.

[5] From the congregation of Israel he takes two male goats for a sin offering and one ram for a burnt offering. [6] Aaron presents the bull as his own sin offering and makes atonement for himself and his household. [7] Then he takes the two goats and stands them before the LORD at the entrance to the tent of meeting. [8] He casts lots over the two goats — one lot for the LORD, one lot for Azazel. [9] The goat that draws the LORD's lot, Aaron offers as a sin offering. [10] The goat that draws Azazel's lot is set alive before the LORD, used to make atonement, and then sent off into the wilderness to Azazel.

[11] Aaron presents the bull of his own sin offering and makes atonement for himself and his household. He slaughters the bull. [12] He takes a censer full of burning coals from the altar before the LORD, and two handfuls of finely ground fragrant incense, and carries them inside the curtain. [13] He places the incense on the fire before the LORD, and the smoke billows up and covers the mercy seat above the testimony — so he will not die. [14] He takes some of the bull's blood and sprinkles it with his finger on the front of the mercy seat — then seven times in front of it.

[15] Then he slaughters the goat of the people's sin offering and brings its blood inside the curtain. He does with this blood what he did with the bull's: sprinkles it on the mercy seat and in front of it. [16] This is how he makes atonement for the sanctuary — because of the impurities of Israel, their rebellions, all their sins. He does the same for the tent of meeting, which stands with them in the middle of all their uncleanness.

[17] No one else may be inside the tent of meeting from the moment Aaron enters to make atonement in the Holy Place until he comes out. He makes atonement for himself, for his household, and for the whole assembly of Israel.

[18] Then he steps out to the altar that is before the LORD and makes atonement for it. He takes some of the bull's blood and some of the goat's blood and puts it on the horns of the altar all around. [19] He sprinkles blood on it with his finger seven times, cleansing it and consecrating it from the uncleanness of Israel.

[20] When he has finished making atonement for the Holy Place, the tent of meeting, and the altar, he brings forward the live goat. [21] Aaron lays both hands on the head of the live goat and confesses over it every wrong of the Israelites — every rebellion, every sin — loading them onto the goat's head. Then he sends the goat away into the wilderness by the hand of a man appointed for the task. [22] The goat carries every one of their wrongs on itself into a barren land. The man releases it in the wilderness.

[23] Aaron goes back into the tent of meeting, takes off the linen garments he wore when he entered the Holy Place, and leaves them there. [24] He bathes his body in water in a holy place, puts on his regular vestments, and comes out to offer his burnt offering and the people's burnt offering — making atonement for himself and for the people. [25] He burns the fat of the sin offering on the altar.

[26] The man who released the goat to Azazel washes his clothes and bathes his body in water, and only then may he come back into the camp.

[27] The bull and the goat of the sin offering, whose blood was brought into the Holy Place to make atonement — their carcasses are carried outside the camp. Their hides, their flesh, and their dung are burned in the fire. [28] The one who burns them washes his clothes and bathes his body in water, and only then may he come back into the camp.

[29] This becomes a permanent statute for you: on the tenth day of the seventh month you will deny yourselves and do no work — neither the native-born nor the foreigner living among you. [30] Because on this day atonement is made for you, to cleanse you. From all your sins you will be clean before the LORD. [31] It is a Sabbath of complete rest for you, and you will deny yourselves. A permanent statute.

[32] The priest who is anointed and ordained to serve in his father's place makes the atonement. He puts on the sacred linen garments [33] and makes atonement for the Most Holy Place, the tent of meeting, and the altar — and for the priests and all the people of the assembly. [34] This will stand as a permanent statute for you: once a year, atonement is made for the Israelites for every one of their sins.

And it was done, exactly as the LORD commanded Moses.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses: [2] "Tell Aaron, his sons, and every Israelite — these are the LORD's orders:

[3] Any Israelite who slaughters an ox, a lamb, or a goat — inside the camp or outside it — [4] without bringing it to the entrance of the tent of meeting to present as an offering to the LORD before his dwelling — that man is guilty of blood. He has shed blood, and he will be cut off from his people.

[5] The point is this: the Israelites must bring the animals they would otherwise sacrifice in open fields — they must bring them to the LORD, to the priest at the entrance of the tent of meeting, and offer them there as peace offerings to the LORD. [6] The priest will splash the blood against the LORD's altar at the entrance of the tent of meeting and burn the fat as a pleasing aroma to the LORD. [7] They must stop sacrificing to the goat-demons they have been chasing after like unfaithful lovers. This is a permanent statute — for them and for every generation after them.

[8] Tell them: Any Israelite, or any foreigner living among them, who offers up a burnt offering or sacrifice [9] and does not bring it to the entrance of the tent of meeting to offer it to the LORD — that man will be cut off from his people.

[10] As for any Israelite, or any foreigner living among them, who consumes blood — I will set my face against that person. I will cut him off from his people. [11] Because the life of the flesh is in the blood. I have given it to you for the altar — to make atonement for your lives. Blood makes atonement — because of the life it carries. [12] That is why I have told the Israelites: none of you may eat blood, and no foreigner living among you may eat blood.

[13] Any Israelite, or any foreigner living among them, who hunts down an animal or bird that may be eaten — he must drain its blood and cover it with dirt. [14] Because the life of every living thing is its blood. That is its life. So I have told the Israelites: do not eat the blood of any creature, because the life of every creature is its blood. Anyone who eats it will be cut off.

[15] Anyone — native-born or foreigner — who eats an animal that died on its own or was torn by predators must wash his clothes, bathe in water, and remain unclean until evening. Then he will be clean. [16] But if he refuses to wash his clothes and bathe his body, he carries the guilt himself."

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[2] Tell the people of Israel this — I am the LORD your God.

[3] You will not live the way Egypt lived, where you came from. You will not live the way Canaan lives, where I am bringing you. You will not walk in their patterns.

[4] My rulings are what you follow. My statutes are what you keep. They define the path. I am the LORD your God.

[5] Keep my statutes and my rulings. The person who lives them out — lives. I am the LORD.

[6] No one is to approach a close relative for sex. I am the LORD.

[7] Do not strip your father by violating your mother. She is your mother. Do not touch her.

[8] Do not sleep with your father's wife. That nakedness belongs to your father.

[9] Do not sleep with your sister — your father's daughter or your mother's daughter, whether born in the house or born elsewhere. Off limits.

[10] Do not sleep with your son's daughter or your daughter's daughter. To uncover them is to uncover yourself.

[11] Do not sleep with the daughter of your father's wife, born to your father. She is your sister.

[12] Do not sleep with your father's sister. She is your father's blood.

[13] Do not sleep with your mother's sister. She is your mother's blood.

[14] Do not strip your father's brother by going near his wife. She is your aunt.

[15] Do not sleep with your daughter-in-law. She belongs to your son. Off limits.

[16] Do not sleep with your brother's wife. That nakedness belongs to your brother.

[17] Do not sleep with a woman and her daughter. Do not take her son's daughter or her daughter's daughter to uncover her. They are her blood. It is depravity.

[18] Do not take a woman as a rival to her sister, sleeping with one while the other still lives.

[19] Do not approach a woman for sex while she is bleeding, set apart in her impurity.

[20] Do not lie with your neighbor's wife and defile yourself with her.

[21] Do not hand over any of your children to be burned for Molech. Do not profane the name of your God. I am the LORD.

[22] Do not lie with a male as with a woman. It is detestable.

[23] Do not lie with any animal and defile yourself with it. A woman is not to present herself to an animal to mate with it. It is a perversion of the created order.

[24] Do not defile yourselves with any of these things. The nations I am driving out before you defiled themselves with all of it,

[25] and the land itself became foul. I called it to account for its corruption, and the land vomited out the people living on it.

[26] But you — keep my statutes and my rulings. Neither the native-born nor the foreigner among you is to do any of these abominations.

[27] The people of this land did all of these things before you, and the land was defiled.

[28] So do not let the land vomit you out for defiling it the way it vomited out the nation before you.

[29] Anyone who does any of these abominations — that person will be cut off from their people.

[30] Keep my charge. Do not practice any of the disgusting customs that were practiced here before you. Do not defile yourselves with them. I am the LORD your God.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[2] "Speak to the whole assembly of Israel. Tell them: Be holy, because I, the LORD your God, am holy.

[3] Every one of you — honor your mother and your father. Keep my Sabbaths. I am the LORD your God.

[4] Do not turn to worthless idols. Do not cast metal gods for yourselves. I am the LORD your God.

[5] When you sacrifice a peace offering to the LORD, offer it so you'll be accepted.

[6] Eat it the day you offer it, or the next day. Whatever's left by the third day — burn it.

[7] If anyone eats it on the third day, it's foul. It won't be accepted.

[8] Whoever eats it bears the guilt — they've desecrated what belongs to the LORD. That person is cut off from their people.

[9] When you harvest your land, do not reap right to the edges of your field. Do not gather what the harvest leaves behind.

[10] Do not strip your vineyard bare. Do not pick up the fallen grapes. Leave them for the poor and the foreigner. I am the LORD your God.

[11] Do not steal. Do not lie. Do not deceive one another.

[12] Do not swear falsely by my name and drag the name of your God through the mud. I am the LORD.

[13] Do not exploit your neighbor. Do not rob them. Do not hold back a laborer's wages overnight.

[14] Do not curse the deaf. Do not set a stumbling block in front of the blind. Fear your God. I am the LORD.

[15] Do not twist justice. Do not favor the poor. Do not flatter the powerful. Judge your neighbor fairly.

[16] Do not go around spreading slander among your people. Do not stand by while your neighbor's blood is shed. I am the LORD.

[17] Do not nurse hatred for your brother in your heart. Confront your neighbor directly — that way you won't carry guilt because of them.

[18] Do not take revenge. Do not hold a grudge against your own people. Love your neighbor as yourself. I am the LORD.

[19] Keep my statutes. Do not crossbreed your livestock. Do not plant your field with two kinds of seed. Do not wear clothing woven from two different materials.

[20] If a man sleeps with a slave woman who's been promised to another man but not redeemed or freed, there must be an inquiry. They won't be put to death — she wasn't free.

[21] He must bring his guilt offering to the LORD at the entrance of the tent of meeting — a ram for atonement.

[22] The priest will make atonement for him before the LORD with the ram, for the sin he committed. He'll be forgiven.

[23] When you enter the land and plant any fruit tree, treat its fruit as forbidden for three years. It must not be eaten.

[24] In the fourth year, all its fruit is holy — an offering of praise to the LORD.

[25] In the fifth year, you may eat its fruit. This way the harvest will multiply for you. I am the LORD your God.

[26] Do not eat meat with the blood still in it. Do not practice divination. Do not read omens.

[27] Do not trim the hair at the sides of your head. Do not cut the edges of your beard.

[28] Do not cut your body for the dead. Do not mark your skin with tattoos. I am the LORD.

[29] Do not degrade your daughter by making her a prostitute, or the land itself will fall into prostitution and fill up with depravity.

[30] Keep my Sabbaths. Revere my sanctuary. I am the LORD.

[31] Do not turn to mediums or seek out spiritists. Do not go looking for them — they'll defile you. I am the LORD your God.

[32] Stand up in the presence of the elderly. Show respect to the old. Fear your God. I am the LORD.

[33] When a foreigner lives among you in your land, do not mistreat them.

[34] Treat the foreigner living among you like one of your own. Love them as yourself — you were foreigners once, in Egypt. I am the LORD your God.

[35] Do not cheat when you measure — not in length, not in weight, not in volume.

[36] Use honest scales, honest weights, an honest ephah, an honest hin. I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of Egypt.

[37] Keep all my statutes and all my judgments. Live them out. I am the LORD."

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[2] "Tell the Israelites: Any Israelite—or any foreigner living in Israel—who hands over a child to Molech must die. The people of the land will stone him.

[3] I will set my face against that man and cut him off from his people, because he gave his child to Molech—defiling my sanctuary, dragging my holy name through the mud.

[4] And if the people of the land look the other way when a man gives his child to Molech, refusing to put him to death,

[5] then I myself will set my face against that man and his family. I will cut him off—and everyone who whores after Molech with him—from the community.

[6] If anyone turns to mediums or spiritists, prostituting himself to them, I will set my face against him and cut him off from his people.

[7] Consecrate yourselves. Be holy, because I am the LORD your God.

[8] Keep my statutes. Do them. I am the LORD who makes you holy.

[9] Anyone who curses his father or mother must die. He cursed his father or mother—his blood is on his own head.

[10] If a man commits adultery with another man's wife—with his neighbor's wife—both the adulterer and the adulteress must die.

[11] If a man sleeps with his father's wife, he has uncovered his father's nakedness. Both of them must die. Their blood is on their own heads.

[12] If a man sleeps with his daughter-in-law, both must die. What they did is a perversion. Their blood is on their own heads.

[13] If a man sleeps with another man as with a woman, both have committed an abomination. They must die. Their blood is on their own heads.

[14] If a man marries a woman and her mother, it is depravity. All three of them must be burned, so that no such depravity remains among you.

[15] If a man has sex with an animal, he must die—and you must kill the animal too.

[16] If a woman approaches an animal to mate with it, kill the woman and the animal. They must die. Their blood is on their own heads.

[17] If a man takes his sister—his father's daughter or his mother's daughter—and they see each other's nakedness, it is a disgrace. They must be cut off in the sight of their people. He has uncovered his sister's nakedness. He bears his guilt.

[18] If a man sleeps with a woman during her period and uncovers her nakedness, he has exposed her flow and she has bared the source of her blood. Both must be cut off from their people.

[19] Do not uncover the nakedness of your mother's sister or your father's sister—that is exposing your own flesh and blood. Both will bear their guilt.

[20] If a man sleeps with his aunt, he has uncovered his uncle's nakedness. They will bear their sin. They will die childless.

[21] If a man takes his brother's wife, it is impurity. He has uncovered his brother's nakedness. They will be childless.

[22] Keep all my statutes and all my rulings. Do them. Then the land where I am bringing you to live will not vomit you out.

[23] Do not walk in the customs of the nations I am driving out before you. They did all these things, and I loathed them.

[24] But I told you: You will inherit their land. I will give it to you as your possession—a land flowing with milk and honey. I am the LORD your God, who has set you apart from the peoples.

[25] So you must distinguish between clean and unclean animals, clean and unclean birds. Do not make yourselves disgusting through any creature that crawls on the ground—the ones I have set apart from you as unclean.

[26] Be holy to me, because I, the LORD, am holy. I have set you apart from the peoples to be mine.

[27] Any man or woman who is a medium or spiritist must die. They will be stoned. Their blood is on their own heads."

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses: Tell the priests, the sons of Aaron — none of them may defile himself by touching a corpse among his people, [2] except for his closest blood: mother, father, son, daughter, brother, [3] or his unmarried sister still belonging to him, who never went to another man. For her, he may make himself unclean. [4] But he must not defile himself for any in-law and profane himself.

[5] Priests must not shave bald patches into their heads, must not cut the edges of their beards, must not slash their flesh. [6] They belong to their God and must keep his name unprofaned. They handle the fire-offerings of the LORD — the very food of their God. They must stay holy.

[7] A priest cannot marry a woman defiled by prostitution, nor a woman cast out by her husband, because the priest is consecrated to his God. [8] Treat him as holy — he serves the food of your God. He is holy to you, because I, the LORD who makes you holy, am holy.

[9] If a priest's daughter profanes herself by prostitution, she profanes her father. She must be burned with fire.

[10] The high priest among his brothers — the one with anointing oil poured on his head, the one ordained to wear the sacred vestments — must never let his hair hang loose, must never tear his garments. [11] He must not go near any dead body. Not even for his father or mother may he defile himself. [12] He must not leave the sanctuary or desecrate the holy place of his God, because the consecration of the anointing oil of his God is upon him. I am the LORD.

[13] He must marry a woman in her virginity. [14] No widow, no divorcée, no woman defiled, no prostitute — none of these. He must take a virgin from his own people as his wife, [15] so he does not profane his offspring among his people. I am the LORD who sets him apart.

[16] The LORD spoke to Moses: [17] Tell Aaron — no descendant of yours, in any generation to come, who carries a physical defect may approach to offer the food of his God. [18] Anyone with a defect is barred: a blind man, a lame man, a man with a disfigured face or a deformed limb, [19] a man with a broken foot or a broken hand, [20] a hunchback, a dwarf, a man with an eye defect, a man with festering sores or scabs or crushed testicles — [21] no descendant of Aaron the priest with such a defect may step forward to present the fire-offerings of the LORD. He has a defect. He must not approach to offer the food of his God.

[22] He may still eat the food of his God — both the most holy and the holy. [23] But he must not go near the curtain or approach the altar, because he has a defect. He must not profane my sanctuaries, for I am the LORD who makes them holy.

[24] So Moses spoke this to Aaron, to his sons, and to all the Israelites.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[2] "Tell Aaron and his sons to handle the sacred offerings of the Israelites with care — the gifts they consecrate to me — so they do not desecrate my holy name. I am the LORD.

[3] Say this to them: For all generations to come, if any of your descendants approaches the sacred offerings the Israelites consecrate to the LORD while he is ritually unclean, that person is cut off from my presence. I am the LORD.

[4] No descendant of Aaron with a skin disease or a discharge may eat the sacred food until he is clean. Whoever touches anything defiled by a corpse, or has a seminal emission,

[5] or touches any swarming creature that makes him unclean, or any person whose uncleanness passes to him —

[6] anyone who touches such things is unclean until evening. He must not eat from the sacred offerings unless he has bathed his body in water.

[7] After sunset he is clean, and then he may eat from the sacred food — it is his sustenance.

[8] He must not eat anything found dead or torn by wild animals, defiling himself through it. I am the LORD.

[9] The priests must keep my charge, or they will bear the guilt of it and die for treating the holy as common. I am the LORD who sanctifies them.

[10] No outsider may eat the sacred food. Neither a priest's guest nor his hired worker may eat it.

[11] But a slave the priest has purchased with his own money may eat from it, and so may slaves born in his household — they share his food.

[12] If a priest's daughter marries an outsider, she can no longer eat from the sacred contributions.

[13] But if she is widowed or divorced, childless, and returns to her father's house as in her youth, she may eat her father's food again. No outsider, however, may eat it.

[14] If anyone eats sacred food by mistake, he must replace it and add a fifth of its value, giving it to the priest along with the sacred portion.

[15] The priests must not profane the sacred offerings the Israelites lift up to the LORD

[16] by letting unauthorized people eat them — that would load guilt requiring restitution onto the people. I am the LORD who sanctifies them."

[17] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[18] "Speak to Aaron, his sons, and all Israel. Tell them: When any Israelite or foreigner living among you presents a burnt offering to the LORD — whether to fulfill a vow or as a freewill gift —

[19] for it to be accepted on your behalf, it must be a male without defect, from cattle, sheep, or goats.

[20] Bring nothing with a defect, because it will not be accepted for you.

[21] When anyone offers a fellowship sacrifice to the LORD — to fulfill a vow or as a freewill gift — from the herd or flock, it must be flawless to be accepted. No defect whatsoever.

[22] Blind, injured, maimed, oozing sores, festering eruptions, scabs — do not present any of these to the LORD or place them as offerings on the altar.

[23] An ox or sheep with a deformed or stunted limb you may offer as a freewill gift, but it will not be accepted for a vow.

[24] Do not offer to the LORD any animal whose testicles are bruised, crushed, torn, or cut. You must not do this in your land,

[25] and you must not accept such animals from foreigners to present as food to your God. Their deformity is a defect — they will not be accepted for you."

[26] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[27] "When a calf, lamb, or kid is born, it must stay with its mother seven days. From the eighth day on, it is acceptable as an offering by fire to the LORD.

[28] Do not slaughter a cow or ewe and its young on the same day.

[29] When you sacrifice a thank offering to the LORD, do it in a way that is accepted on your behalf.

[30] Eat it the same day. Leave none of it until morning. I am the LORD.

[31] Keep my commands. Live them out. I am the LORD.

[32] Do not desecrate my holy name. I must be treated as holy among the Israelites. I am the LORD who sanctifies you,

[33] who brought you out of Egypt to be your God. I am the LORD."

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[2] "Tell the Israelites: These are my appointed times — the LORD's sacred assemblies, the times you will gather as holy convocations.

[3] Six days you work. The seventh day is a Sabbath of complete rest — a sacred assembly. Do no work of any kind. Wherever you live, it belongs to the LORD.

[4] These are the LORD's appointed festivals — the holy gatherings you will announce at their proper times:

[5] On the fourteenth of the first month, at twilight, comes the LORD's Passover.

[6] On the fifteenth of that same month, the LORD's Festival of Unleavened Bread begins. For seven days, eat bread without yeast.

[7] On the first day, hold a sacred assembly. Do no ordinary work.

[8] For seven days, present fire-offerings to the LORD. On the seventh day, gather again as a holy assembly — and again, no ordinary work."

[9] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[10] "Tell the Israelites: When you enter the land I am giving you and bring in its harvest, carry the first sheaf of your harvest to the priest.

[11] He will lift it before the LORD so you'll be accepted — the day after the Sabbath, the priest waves it.

[12] On the day you wave the sheaf, offer a year-old lamb without flaw as a burnt offering to the LORD.

[13] With it, a grain offering — two-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil, a fire-offering whose aroma pleases the LORD — and a drink offering of a quarter hin of wine.

[14] Eat no bread, no roasted grain, no fresh kernels until that very day — until you have brought your God his offering. This is a permanent statute through every generation, wherever you live.

[15] From the day after the Sabbath — the day you brought the sheaf to wave — count off seven full weeks.

[16] Count fifty days, up to the day after the seventh Sabbath. Then present a new grain offering to the LORD.

[17] From wherever you live, bring two loaves as a wave offering, each made from two-tenths of an ephah of fine flour, baked with yeast — firstfruits to the LORD.

[18] With the bread, present seven year-old lambs without flaw, one young bull, and two rams. They are a burnt offering to the LORD, along with their grain offerings and drink offerings — a fire-offering, an aroma that pleases the LORD.

[19] Then offer one male goat as a sin offering and two year-old lambs as a fellowship offering.

[20] The priest will wave the lambs together with the firstfruits bread as a wave offering before the LORD. They are holy to the LORD, set apart for the priest.

[21] On that same day, proclaim a sacred assembly. Do no ordinary work. This is a permanent statute, wherever you live, through every generation.

[22] When you harvest your land, don't reap to the very edges of your field, and don't pick up what you've dropped. Leave it for the poor and the foreigner. I am the LORD your God."

[23] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[24] "Tell the Israelites: On the first day of the seventh month, observe a day of complete rest — a sacred assembly announced by trumpet blasts.

[25] Do no ordinary work. Present a fire-offering to the LORD."

[26] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[27] "The tenth day of this seventh month is the Day of Atonement. Hold a sacred assembly, deny yourselves, and bring a fire-offering to the LORD.

[28] Do no work whatsoever on that day. It is the Day of Atonement — the day you are made right before the LORD your God.

[29] Anyone who refuses to deny themselves on that day will be cut off from their people.

[30] Anyone who works on that day — I will destroy them from among their people.

[31] Do no work at all. This is a permanent statute through every generation, wherever you live.

[32] It is a Sabbath of complete rest. Deny yourselves. From the evening of the ninth day of the month until the next evening, you will keep your Sabbath."

[33] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[34] "Tell the Israelites: On the fifteenth day of this seventh month, the LORD's Festival of Shelters begins. It lasts seven days.

[35] The first day is a sacred assembly — no ordinary work.

[36] For seven days, bring fire-offerings to the LORD. On the eighth day, gather again as a sacred assembly and present a fire-offering. It is a solemn closing — no ordinary work.

[37] These are the LORD's appointed festivals — the sacred assemblies when you bring fire-offerings: burnt offerings, grain offerings, sacrifices, and drink offerings, each on its proper day.

[38] These are in addition to the LORD's Sabbaths and your gifts, your vows, and your freewill offerings to the LORD.

[39] But starting on the fifteenth day of the seventh month, after you've gathered in the land's harvest, celebrate the LORD's festival for seven days. The first day is rest. The eighth day is rest.

[40] On the first day, take fruit from your finest trees, palm fronds, leafy branches, and willows from the streams — and rejoice before the LORD your God for seven days.

[41] Celebrate this festival to the LORD seven days each year. A permanent statute through every generation. Celebrate it in the seventh month.

[42] Live in shelters for seven days. Every native-born Israelite must live in shelters,

[43] so your descendants will know that I made the Israelites live in shelters when I brought them out of Egypt. I am the LORD your God."

[44] So Moses announced the LORD's appointed festivals to the Israelites.

[1] The LORD said to Moses: [2] Command the Israelites to bring you pure oil, pressed from olives, for the lamp—so the flame burns steady, unbroken. [3] In the Tent of Meeting, outside the veil that shields the Testimony, Aaron will tend it from evening to morning, before the LORD, always. This is a statute that does not expire, generation after generation. [4] On the pure gold lampstand he will keep those lamps burning before the LORD, without fail.

[5] Take fine flour and bake twelve loaves, two-tenths of an ephah each. [6] Arrange them in two stacks of six on the pure gold table before the LORD. [7] Set pure frankincense on each row—a memorial portion, a fire-offering to the LORD. [8] Every Sabbath, without exception, Aaron will set them in order before the LORD—a covenant the Israelites keep forever. [9] The bread belongs to Aaron and his sons. They will eat it in a holy place, because it is most holy to him from the LORD's fire-offerings—a portion fixed for all time.

[10] Now a man went out among the Israelites—son of an Israelite woman and an Egyptian father. He got into a fight in the camp with an Israelite. [11] And the son of the Israelite woman cursed the Name. He blasphemed. They hauled him to Moses. (His mother was Shelomith, daughter of Dibri, from the tribe of Dan.) [12] They held him in custody until the LORD's will on the matter was made clear.

[13] The LORD said to Moses: [14] Take the blasphemer outside the camp. Everyone who heard him will press their hands on his head, and the whole assembly will stone him. [15] Then say this to the Israelites: Anyone who curses his God bears his sin. [16] Whoever blasphemes the name of the LORD must die. The entire assembly will stone him—foreigner or native-born, makes no difference. Blaspheme the Name, and you die.

[17] If a man strikes another man down—he dies. [18] If he strikes down an animal, he replaces it: life for life. [19] If a man injures his neighbor—what he did, it will be done to him: [20] fracture for fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth. The wound he inflicted is the wound he wears. [21] Whoever kills an animal pays for it. Whoever kills a man dies. [22] One law for all—foreigner and native alike—because I am the LORD your God.

[23] Moses spoke to the Israelites, and they took the blasphemer outside the camp and stoned him. The Israelites did exactly what the LORD commanded Moses.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses on Mount Sinai:

[2] "Tell the Israelites: When you enter the land I'm giving you, the land itself must keep a Sabbath to the LORD.

[3] Six years you'll sow your fields, six years you'll prune your vineyards, six years you'll gather the harvest.

[4] But the seventh year is a Sabbath of complete rest for the land — a Sabbath to the LORD. Don't sow your field. Don't prune your vineyard.

[5] Don't harvest what grows on its own from the last planting. Don't gather grapes from the unpruned vines. The land takes a year off.

[6] Whatever the land produces during its rest — that's food for everyone: for you, your servants, your hired hands, the foreigner living among you,

[7] even your livestock and the wild animals of your land. Whatever grows is for eating.

[8] Count off seven Sabbath-years — seven times seven — forty-nine years.

[9] Then on the tenth day of the seventh month — the Day of Atonement — sound the ram's horn loud and long across the entire land.

[10] Set apart the fiftieth year. Proclaim freedom throughout the land for everyone who lives there. It's a Jubilee. Every person returns to their ancestral property. Every person returns to their family.

[11] The fiftieth year is your Jubilee. Don't sow. Don't harvest what grows on its own. Don't gather from the unpruned vines.

[12] It's Jubilee — holy ground. You'll eat straight from the field, whatever it yields.

[13] In this year of Jubilee, every person goes home to their inheritance.

[14] When you sell property to your neighbor — or buy it from them — don't cheat each other.

[15] Price it by the number of years since the last Jubilee. The seller charges by the harvests remaining.

[16] More years left, higher price. Fewer years left, lower price. What's actually being sold is a number of harvests.

[17] Don't exploit each other. Fear your God. I am the LORD your God.

[18] Live by my statutes. Keep my judgments. Do them. Then you'll dwell in the land secure.

[19] The land will yield its fruit. You'll eat your fill. You'll live there without fear.

[20] You're going to ask: 'What will we eat in the seventh year if we can't plant or harvest?'

[21] I'll command my blessing on you in the sixth year. The land will produce enough for three years.

[22] When you plant in the eighth year, you'll still be eating from the old harvest. Until the ninth year's crop comes in, you'll be eating stored grain.

[23] The land is never sold permanently. The land is mine. You're foreigners and guests living on my property.

[24] So in every inheritance you hold, you must allow the land to be redeemed.

[25] If your brother falls into poverty and has to sell part of his property, his closest relative comes and buys back what he sold.

[26] If a man has no one to redeem it but recovers enough himself to buy it back,

[27] he calculates the years since the sale, refunds the difference to the buyer, and returns to his property.

[28] But if he can't afford to redeem it, what he sold stays with the buyer until the year of Jubilee. In Jubilee it reverts, and he goes home.

[29] If a man sells a house inside a walled city, he has one full year from the sale to redeem it. After that, the right runs out.

[30] If it isn't redeemed within the year, the house in the walled city belongs permanently to the buyer and his descendants. It does not revert in Jubilee.

[31] But houses in unwalled villages count as open country. They can always be redeemed. They return in Jubilee.

[32] As for the Levites — they always retain the right to redeem their houses in the Levite cities.

[33] If a Levite doesn't redeem his house, it still reverts to him in Jubilee, because the houses in the Levite cities are their inheritance among the Israelites.

[34] The pastureland surrounding their cities can never be sold. It's their permanent possession.

[35] If your brother becomes poor and can't support himself among you, sustain him — even if he's a foreigner or a temporary resident — so he can live with you.

[36] Don't charge him interest. Don't take a profit off him. Fear your God. Let your brother live among you.

[37] Don't lend him money at interest. Don't sell him food for profit.

[38] I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of Egypt to give you the land of Canaan, to be your God.

[39] If your brother becomes poor enough to sell himself to you, don't work him like a slave.

[40] Treat him like a hired worker, like a temporary resident. He works for you until the year of Jubilee.

[41] Then he goes free — he and his children with him — and returns to his family, back to his ancestral property.

[42] Because they're my servants — I brought them out of Egypt. They cannot be sold the way slaves are sold.

[43] Don't rule over him harshly. Fear your God.

[44] Slaves — if you must — come from the nations around you. From them you may buy male and female slaves.

[45] You may also buy from the foreigners living among you, and from the families they have on your land. They become your property.

[46] You can pass them down to your children as an inheritance, hold them as property forever. But over your own people, the Israelites, no one rules another harshly.

[47] If a foreigner living among you grows wealthy and your brother falls into poverty and sells himself to that foreigner — or to a member of the foreigner's clan —

[48] he keeps the right of redemption. One of his brothers can redeem him.

[49] An uncle or cousin or any close relative from his family can redeem him. Or if he prospers, he can redeem himself.

[50] He and his buyer count the years from the sale to the Jubilee. The price gets calculated by the years remaining — figured at the rate of a hired worker's wages.

[51] If many years remain, he refunds a larger portion of the purchase price.

[52] If only a few years remain until Jubilee, he calculates accordingly and refunds for those years.

[53] He's to be treated like a worker hired by the year. Don't let the foreigner rule him harshly while you watch.

[54] If he isn't redeemed any of these ways, he and his children go free in the year of Jubilee.

[55] Because the Israelites belong to me as servants. They're my servants — I brought them out of Egypt. I am the LORD your God."

[1] Make no idols for yourselves. Set up no carved image, no sacred pillar, no engraved stone in your land to bow before. I am the Lord your God.

[2] Keep my Sabbaths. Honor my sanctuary. I am the Lord.

[3] If you walk in my statutes and guard my commands and live them out—

[4] I will send rain in its season. The land will yield its harvest. The trees will hang heavy with fruit.

[5] Your threshing will run into the grape harvest. Your grape harvest will run into the planting. You will eat your bread to the full and sleep secure in your land.

[6] I will give peace to the land—you will lie down and no one will frighten you. I will clear the dangerous beasts from the country. No sword will pass through your land.

[7] You will chase your enemies, and they will fall by the sword before you.

[8] Five of you will chase a hundred. A hundred of you will chase ten thousand. Your enemies will fall by the sword before you.
