[9] I will turn toward you. I will make you fruitful. I will multiply you. I will keep my covenant with you.

[10] You will still be eating last year's harvest when you have to clear it out to make room for the new.

[11] I will pitch my dwelling among you, and I will not turn from you in disgust.

[12] I will walk among you. I will be your God, and you will be my people.

[13] I am the Lord your God who brought you out of Egypt so you would no longer be slaves there. I broke the bars of your yoke and made you walk upright.

[14] But if you will not listen to me—if you will not do all these commands—

[15] if you reject my statutes and your soul recoils from my judgments so that you refuse to obey and break my covenant—

[16] then here is what I will do to you: I will unleash terror against you. Wasting disease and burning fever that will drain the light from your eyes and the life from your body. You will plant your seed for nothing—your enemies will eat it.

[17] I will set my face against you. You will be struck down before your foes. Those who hate you will rule over you. You will run when no one is chasing you.

[18] And if after all this you still will not listen to me, I will discipline you seven times over for your sins.

[19] I will shatter the arrogance of your strength. I will make your sky like iron and your ground like bronze.

[20] Your strength will be spent for nothing. Your land will not yield its harvest. The trees of the land will not give their fruit.

[21] If you keep walking against me and refuse to listen, I will strike you seven times over—a measure for your sins.

[22] I will send wild animals against you. They will tear your children from you, destroy your livestock, and cut your numbers down until your roads stand empty.

[23] And if after all this you are still not corrected by me—if you keep walking against me—

[24] then I will walk against you. I myself will strike you seven times over for your sins.

[25] I will bring a sword against you—vengeance for the broken covenant. When you gather inside your cities, I will send plague among you, and you will be handed over to the enemy.

[26] When I break your staff of bread, ten women will bake your bread in a single oven. They will hand it back to you by weight. You will eat and not be filled.

[27] And if after all this you still will not listen to me—if you keep walking against me—

[28] then I will walk against you in fury. I myself will discipline you seven times over for your sins.

[29] You will eat the flesh of your sons. You will eat the flesh of your daughters.

[30] I will tear down your high places. I will smash your incense altars. I will pile your corpses on the carcasses of your lifeless idols. My soul will recoil from you.

[31] I will turn your cities into rubble and your sanctuaries into desolation. I will not breathe in the pleasing aroma of your offerings.

[32] I myself will lay waste the land—so devastated that your enemies who settle there will be appalled at the sight of it.

[33] I will scatter you among the nations. I will draw the sword and chase you. Your land will lie desolate. Your cities will lie in ruins.

[34] Then the land will finally enjoy its sabbaths—all the days it lies desolate while you are in the land of your enemies. Then the land will rest and enjoy its sabbaths.

[35] All the days it lies desolate it will have the rest it never had in your sabbath years when you lived on it.

[36] As for those of you who survive, I will put a softness in their hearts in the lands of their enemies. The sound of a windblown leaf will send them running. They will run as one runs from the sword. They will fall, though no one is chasing.

[37] They will stumble over each other as if fleeing a sword when no one is pursuing. You will have no strength to stand before your enemies.

[38] You will perish among the nations. The land of your enemies will swallow you.

[39] Those who are left will rot away in their enemies' lands because of their guilt. They will rot away because of the guilt of their fathers, layered on their own.

[40] But if they confess their guilt and the guilt of their fathers—the treachery they committed against me, the way they walked against me—

[41] which is why I walked against them and brought them into the land of their enemies—if then their uncircumcised hearts are humbled and they accept the punishment for their guilt—

[42] then I will remember my covenant with Jacob. I will remember my covenant with Isaac. I will remember my covenant with Abraham. And I will remember the land.

[43] The land will be abandoned by them and will enjoy its sabbaths while it lies desolate without them. They will accept their punishment because they rejected my judgments and their soul recoiled from my statutes.

[44] But even then—even when they are in the land of their enemies—I will not reject them. I will not recoil from them so as to destroy them and break my covenant with them. I am the Lord their God.

[45] For their sake I will remember the covenant with their ancestors—the ones I brought out of Egypt before the eyes of the nations to be their God. I am the Lord.

[46] These are the statutes, the judgments, and the instructions the Lord established between himself and the people of Israel on Mount Sinai, through Moses.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[2] "Tell the Israelites: When someone makes a special vow dedicating a person to the LORD by their valuation,

[3] here are the rates. A man between twenty and sixty: fifty shekels of silver, by sanctuary weight.

[4] A woman: thirty shekels.

[5] A youth between five and twenty: twenty shekels for a male, ten for a female.

[6] A child between one month and five years: five shekels of silver for a male, three for a female.

[7] Sixty and older: fifteen shekels for a male, ten for a female.

[8] If the one making the vow cannot afford the set rate, bring the person before the priest, and the priest will assess what they can pay — the valuation will match what the vower's hand can reach.

[9] If the vow involves an animal that can be offered to the LORD, then whatever is handed over becomes holy.

[10] No swapping. No upgrades, no downgrades — not a good one for a bad one or a bad one for a good one. If anyone tries to substitute, both the original and the replacement become holy.

[11] If the vow involves an unclean animal — one that cannot be offered to the LORD — bring it before the priest.

[12] The priest will set its value, high or low, and his word is final.

[13] If the owner wants to redeem it, add a fifth on top of the valuation.

[14] If someone dedicates their house as holy to the LORD, the priest sets the value, high or low, and his word stands.

[15] If the dedicator wants to redeem the house, add a fifth of the value, and it returns to them.

[16] If someone dedicates part of their ancestral land to the LORD, the value is set by how much seed it takes to plant it — fifty shekels of silver for every homer of barley seed.

[17] If they dedicate it starting from the Jubilee year, the full valuation stands.

[18] If they dedicate it after the Jubilee, the priest prorates the value based on the years remaining until the next Jubilee, and the price drops accordingly.

[19] If the one who dedicated it wants to redeem the field, add a fifth to the valuation, and the field is theirs again.

[20] But if they do not redeem it — or if they sell it to someone else — it can never be redeemed again.

[21] When the field is released at Jubilee, it belongs to the LORD as something permanently devoted, like a field under the ban. It becomes the priest's inheritance.

[22] If someone dedicates a field they purchased — not part of their ancestral land —

[23] the priest calculates the valuation up to the year of Jubilee, and they pay it that same day as something holy to the LORD.

[24] At Jubilee, the field returns to the original owner, the one whose ancestral property it was.

[25] All valuations use sanctuary shekels — twenty gerahs to the shekel.

[26] No one can dedicate the firstborn of livestock — it already belongs to the LORD. Whether ox or sheep, it is his.

[27] If it is an unclean animal, redeem it at the set valuation plus a fifth. If not redeemed, it is sold at the valuation.

[28] But nothing a person devotes to the LORD under the ban — person, animal, or ancestral field — can be sold or redeemed. Every devoted thing is most holy to the LORD.

[29] No human being placed under the ban can be ransomed. They must be put to death.

[30] Every tenth of the land — whether from the crops of the soil or the fruit of the trees — belongs to the LORD. It is holy.

[31] If someone wants to redeem any of their tithe, they add a fifth.

[32] Every tenth animal from the herd or flock — every tenth one that passes under the shepherd's rod — is holy to the LORD.

[33] No inspecting for quality, no swapping. If anyone tries to substitute, both the animal and its replacement become holy. Neither can be redeemed."

[34] These are the commands the LORD gave Moses for the Israelites on Mount Sinai.

[1] Many have set their hands to assembling an account of what has played out among us, [2] drawing from those who were there at the start — eyewitnesses who became servants of the word. [3] So I too, having traced everything carefully from the beginning, decided to write it out for you in order, most excellent Theophilus, [4] so you can know with certainty the truth of what you've been taught.

[5] In the days when Herod ruled Judea, there was a priest named Zechariah, from the division of Abijah. His wife, Elizabeth, came from the line of Aaron. [6] Both of them walked straight before God — blameless in every commandment, every requirement of the Lord. [7] But they had no child. Elizabeth could not conceive. And now they were both old.

[8] One day, Zechariah's division was on duty, and he was serving as priest before God. [9] By the priestly lot, it fell to him to enter the sanctuary of the Lord and burn incense. [10] Outside, the whole gathered crowd was praying at the hour of incense.

[11] Then an angel of the Lord appeared to him, standing at the right side of the altar of incense. [12] Zechariah saw him and froze. Fear crashed over him.

[13] But the angel said: "Do not be afraid, Zechariah. Your prayer has been heard. Your wife Elizabeth will bear you a son, and you will name him John. [14] He will be joy to you, delight — and many will rejoice at his birth, [15] because he will be great in the sight of the Lord. No wine. No strong drink. Filled with the Holy Spirit from his mother's womb. [16] He will turn many of Israel's children back to the Lord their God. [17] He will go ahead of the Lord in the spirit and power of Elijah — turning fathers' hearts toward their children, turning the disobedient toward the wisdom of the righteous, making a people ready for the Lord."

[18] Zechariah said to the angel, "How can I know this? I'm an old man. My wife is past her years."

[19] The angel answered, "I am Gabriel. I stand in the presence of God, and I was sent to speak to you, to bring you this good news. [20] But because you did not believe my words — words that will come true in their time — you will be silent. Unable to speak. Until the day this happens."

[21] Outside, the people were waiting for Zechariah, wondering at his delay in the sanctuary. [22] When he finally came out, he could not speak to them. They realized he had seen a vision in the sanctuary. He kept gesturing to them, but remained mute.

[23] When his days of service ended, he went home. [24] Soon after, Elizabeth conceived. She kept herself hidden for five months, saying, [25] "This is what the Lord has done for me. He looked on me, and took away my disgrace among the people."

[26] In the sixth month, God sent the angel Gabriel to a town in Galilee called Nazareth, [27] to a virgin engaged to a man named Joseph, of the house of David. Her name was Mary.

[28] The angel came to her and said: "Rejoice, favored one. The Lord is with you."

[29] She was shaken at his words, turning over what kind of greeting this could be.

[30] And the angel said, "Don't be afraid, Mary. You have found favor with God. [31] Listen — you will conceive in your womb and bear a son, and you will name him Jesus. [32] He will be great. He will be called Son of the Most High. The Lord God will give him the throne of his father David, [33] and he will reign over the house of Jacob forever. His kingdom will never end."

[34] Mary said to the angel, "How can this be? I haven't been with a man."

[35] The angel answered, "The Holy Spirit will come upon you, and the power of the Most High will overshadow you. So the child to be born will be holy — he will be called Son of God. [36] And look — Elizabeth, your relative, has conceived a son in her old age. The one they called barren is in her sixth month. [37] Nothing is impossible with God."

[38] Mary said, "I am the Lord's servant. Let it be to me as you have spoken." And the angel left her.

[39] In those days Mary got up and hurried into the hill country, to a town in Judah. [40] She entered Zechariah's house and greeted Elizabeth. [41] The moment Elizabeth heard Mary's greeting, the child leapt in her womb. And Elizabeth was filled with the Holy Spirit. [42] She cried out: "Blessed are you among women, and blessed is the fruit of your womb! [43] Who am I, that the mother of my Lord should come to me? [44] The instant your greeting hit my ears, the child in my womb leapt for joy. [45] Blessed is she who believed — what the Lord promised her will be fulfilled."

[46] And Mary said:

"My soul magnifies the Lord,
[47] my spirit leaps in God my Savior,
[48] for he looked on his servant in her lowliness.
From now on every generation will call me blessed,
[49] because the Mighty One has done great things for me —
holy is his name.
[50] His mercy reaches generation to generation,
to those who fear him.
[51] He has flexed his arm in strength;
he has scattered the proud in their own scheming.
[52] He has thrown rulers from their thrones
and lifted up the lowly.
[53] He has filled the hungry with good things
and sent the rich away empty.
[54] He has come to the help of his servant Israel,
remembering mercy —
[55] just as he promised our ancestors,
to Abraham and his offspring forever."

[56] Mary stayed with Elizabeth about three months, then returned home.

[57] The time came for Elizabeth, and she gave birth to a son. [58] Her neighbors and relatives heard how the Lord had magnified his mercy to her, and they celebrated with her.

[59] On the eighth day they came to circumcise the child, and they were going to name him Zechariah, after his father. [60] But his mother spoke up: "No. He is to be called John."

[61] They said, "But none of your relatives has that name." [62] They made signs to his father, asking what he wanted the child called.

[63] He asked for a writing tablet and wrote: His name is John. And they were all astonished.

[64] At that moment his mouth was opened, his tongue freed, and he began to speak, praising God. [65] Awe fell on everyone living around them. Throughout the hill country of Judea, people talked about all this. [66] Everyone who heard kept it in their heart, asking, "What will this child become?" Because the hand of the Lord was on him.

[67] His father Zechariah was filled with the Holy Spirit and prophesied:

[68] "Blessed be the Lord, the God of Israel —
he has come to his people and set them free.
[69] He has raised up a horn of salvation for us
in the house of his servant David,
[70] just as he said through his holy prophets long ago —
[71] salvation from our enemies,
from the hand of all who hate us;
[72] showing mercy to our ancestors,
remembering his holy covenant,
[73] the oath he swore to our father Abraham:
[74] to rescue us from our enemies' grip
so we could serve him without fear,
[75] in holiness and righteousness before him, all our days.

[76] And you, child — you will be called prophet of the Most High.
You will go ahead of the Lord to prepare his way,
[77] to give his people the knowledge of salvation
through the forgiveness of their sins —
[78] because of the tender mercy of our God,
by which the dawn from on high will break upon us,
[79] to shine on those sitting in darkness, in the shadow of death,
to guide our feet into the way of peace."

[80] The child grew. He grew strong in spirit. And he lived in the wilderness until the day he appeared to Israel.

[1] In those days a decree went out from Caesar Augustus that the whole empire should be registered. [2] This was the first census, taken while Quirinius governed Syria. [3] So everyone traveled to be enrolled, each to their own town.

[4] Joseph went too — up from Galilee, from the town of Nazareth, into Judea, to David's town called Bethlehem, because he belonged to David's house and line. [5] He went to register with Mary, who was promised to him and far along in pregnancy.

[6] While they were there, her time came. [7] She gave birth to her firstborn — a son. She wrapped him tight in strips of cloth and laid him in a feeding trough, because there was no space for them in the guest room.

[8] Out in those fields, shepherds were camped under the open sky, keeping watch over their flock through the night. [9] An angel of the Lord blazed into view, and the glory of the Lord poured over them like daylight. They were terrified. [10] The angel said: "Don't be afraid. Listen — I'm bringing you good news, a joy that will reach every people on earth. [11] Today, in David's town, a Savior was born for you. He is the Messiah. He is the Lord. [12] Here's how you'll know him: you'll find a baby wrapped in cloth, lying in a feeding trough."

[13] Suddenly the sky filled with armies of heaven, an enormous choir alongside the angel, praising God:

[14] "Glory to God in the highest heavens,
    and on earth peace
    to those he favors."

[15] When the angels withdrew into heaven, the shepherds said to each other, "Let's go. Let's get to Bethlehem and see this thing that has happened — the thing the Lord told us about." [16] They hurried off and found Mary and Joseph, and the baby lying in the feeding trough. [17] After seeing him, they spread the word about what they'd been told concerning this child, [18] and everyone who heard it was stunned by what the shepherds said. [19] But Mary held all of it close, turning it over and over in her heart. [20] The shepherds went back, glorifying and praising God for everything they had seen and heard — it had all come exactly as they were told.

[21] On the eighth day, when it was time to circumcise him, they named him Jesus — the name the angel had given before he was conceived in the womb.

[22] When the days of their purification under the Law of Moses were complete, they brought him up to Jerusalem to present him to the Lord — [23] as it is written in the Law of the Lord: "Every firstborn male shall be called holy to the Lord" — [24] and to offer the sacrifice the Law required: a pair of turtledoves or two young pigeons.

[25] There was a man in Jerusalem named Simeon — a righteous and devout man, waiting for the consolation of Israel, with the Holy Spirit resting on him. [26] The Spirit had told him he would not see death before he saw the Lord's Messiah. [27] Moved by the Spirit, he came into the temple courts. When the parents brought in the child Jesus to do for him what the Law required, [28] Simeon took the boy into his arms, blessed God, and said:

[29] "Now, Master, you can release your servant in peace,
    just as you promised.
[30] My own eyes have seen your salvation —
[31] the salvation you prepared in full view of every people:
[32] a light to unveil you to the nations,
    and the glory of your people Israel."

[33] His father and mother stood there marveling at what was being said about him. [34] Then Simeon blessed them and said to Mary, his mother: "This child is appointed for the fall and rise of many in Israel — a sign that will be spoken against. [35] And a sword will run through your own soul too. The hidden thoughts of many hearts will be exposed."

[36] There was also a prophet, Anna, daughter of Phanuel, of the tribe of Asher. She was very old. She had been married for seven years after her girlhood, [37] then widowed, and now she was eighty-four. She never left the temple — fasting and praying, worshiping night and day. [38] At that very moment she stepped up, gave thanks to God, and began speaking about the child to everyone waiting for the redemption of Jerusalem.

[39] When they had finished everything required by the Law of the Lord, they returned to Galilee, to their own town of Nazareth. [40] The child grew. He became strong. Wisdom filled him, and the favor of God rested on him.

[41] Every year his parents went to Jerusalem for the Passover festival. [42] When he was twelve, they went up as usual. [43] When the festival ended and they started home, the boy Jesus stayed behind in Jerusalem — and his parents didn't know. [44] Assuming he was somewhere in the caravan, they traveled a full day before they started looking for him among relatives and friends. [45] When they couldn't find him, they turned back to Jerusalem to search.

[46] Three days later they found him — in the temple courts, sitting among the teachers, listening to them and asking questions. [47] Everyone who heard him was astonished at his understanding and his answers. [48] His parents were stunned when they saw him. His mother said, "Son, why have you done this to us? Your father and I have been searching for you in agony."

[49] He said, "Why were you searching? Didn't you know I had to be in my Father's house?" [50] But they didn't understand what he meant.

[51] He went back down with them to Nazareth and lived under their authority. His mother held all these things in her heart.

[52] And Jesus kept growing — in wisdom, in stature, in favor with God and with people.

[1] Year fifteen of Tiberius Caesar's reign. Pontius Pilate governed Judea. Herod ruled Galilee as tetrarch, his brother Philip ruled Iturea and Trachonitis, Lysanias ruled Abilene. [2] Annas and Caiaphas held the high priesthood. And in that lineup of power, the word of God landed — not on any of them, but on John, son of Zechariah, out in the wilderness.

[3] He swept through the whole Jordan region announcing a baptism of repentance — a complete turnaround — for the wiping away of sins. [4] Just as Isaiah had written in the scroll of his prophecies:

A voice. Shouting in the wasteland.
Clear the road for the Lord.
Make his pathways straight.
[5] Every ravine — filled in.
Every mountain and hill — leveled.
The crooked — straightened.
The rough places — smoothed out.
[6] And all humanity will see the rescue God brings.

[7] To the crowds streaming out to be baptized by him, he said: "You brood of vipers — who tipped you off to run from the wrath about to fall? [8] Then produce fruit that proves the repentance is real. And don't start telling yourselves, 'We've got Abraham as our father.' Listen — God can raise up children for Abraham from these stones at your feet. [9] The axe is already laid against the root of the trees. Every tree that doesn't yield good fruit gets cut down and thrown into the fire."

[10] The crowds asked him, "Then what do we do?"

[11] He told them, "If you have two coats, give one to someone who has none. If you have food, do the same."

[12] Tax collectors came to be baptized too. "Teacher," they said, "what about us?"

[13] "Don't collect more than what's owed," he answered.

[14] Soldiers asked him as well, "And us — what should we do?"

He said, "Don't shake anyone down. Don't make false accusations. Be satisfied with your wages."

[15] Expectation was electric. Everyone was wondering in their hearts whether John might actually be the Messiah. [16] So John answered them all: "I baptize you with water. But someone stronger than me is coming — I'm not even worthy to untie his sandals. He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and with fire. [17] His winnowing fork is in his hand, ready to clear the threshing floor. The wheat he gathers into his barn. The chaff he burns — with fire that will not be put out."

[18] With many such words — and many more — John kept preaching the good news to the people.

[19] But when he confronted Herod the tetrarch over Herodias, his brother's wife, and over all the other evil Herod had done, [20] Herod added one more thing to the list: he locked John in prison.

[21] When all the people were being baptized, Jesus was baptized too. And as he was praying, heaven split open, [22] and the Holy Spirit descended on him in bodily form, like a dove. A voice came from heaven: "You are my Son, the one I love. In you I delight."

[23] Jesus was about thirty when he began. He was, as everyone supposed, the son of Joseph — son of Heli, [24] son of Matthat, son of Levi, son of Melchi, son of Jannai, son of Joseph, [25] son of Mattathias, son of Amos, son of Nahum, son of Esli, son of Naggai, [26] son of Maath, son of Mattathias, son of Semein, son of Josech, son of Joda, [27] son of Joanan, son of Rhesa, son of Zerubbabel, son of Shealtiel, son of Neri, [28] son of Melchi, son of Addi, son of Cosam, son of Elmadam, son of Er, [29] son of Joshua, son of Eliezer, son of Jorim, son of Matthat, son of Levi, [30] son of Simeon, son of Judah, son of Joseph, son of Jonam, son of Eliakim, [31] son of Melea, son of Menna, son of Mattatha, son of Nathan, son of David, [32] son of Jesse, son of Obed, son of Boaz, son of Salmon, son of Nahshon, [33] son of Amminadab, son of Admin, son of Arni, son of Hezron, son of Perez, son of Judah, [34] son of Jacob, son of Isaac, son of Abraham, son of Terah, son of Nahor, [35] son of Serug, son of Reu, son of Peleg, son of Eber, son of Shelah, [36] son of Cainan, son of Arphaxad, son of Shem, son of Noah, son of Lamech, [37] son of Methuselah, son of Enoch, son of Jared, son of Mahalalel, son of Cainan, [38] son of Enosh, son of Seth, son of Adam — son of God.

[1] Jesus, full of the Holy Spirit, left the Jordan behind and let the Spirit drive him into the wilderness, [2] where for forty days the devil worked him over. He ate nothing the whole time, and when the days were done, hunger hollowed him out.

[3] The devil said, "If you really are the Son of God, tell this stone to turn into bread."

[4] Jesus answered, "It is written: a human being doesn't live on bread alone."

[5] Then the devil hauled him up high and flashed before him every kingdom of the world in a single instant. [6] "All this authority is yours for the taking," he said, "and all their glory too. It's been handed to me, and I give it to whoever I please. [7] Just bow down and worship me, and the whole thing is yours."

[8] Jesus said, "It is written: worship the Lord your God — him and no one else."

[9] Then the devil took him to Jerusalem, set him on the pinnacle of the temple, and said, "If you really are the Son of God, jump. [10] After all, it is written: he will put his angels on assignment to guard you, [11] and they will catch you in their hands so your foot never even grazes a stone."

[12] Jesus shot back, "It also says: don't put the Lord your God to the test."

[13] The devil, having exhausted every angle of attack, withdrew — biding his time.

[14] Jesus came back to Galilee charged with the power of the Spirit, and word of him spread through the whole region. [15] He taught in their synagogues, and everyone was singing his praises.

[16] He came to Nazareth, where he'd grown up. On the Sabbath, as was his custom, he walked into the synagogue and stood up to read. [17] They handed him the scroll of the prophet Isaiah. He unrolled it and found the place where it was written:

[18] "The Spirit of the Lord is on me, because he has anointed me to bring good news to the poor. He has sent me to announce release for prisoners and sight for the blind, to set the oppressed free, [19] to proclaim the year of the Lord's favor."

[20] He rolled up the scroll, handed it back to the attendant, and sat down. Every eye in the synagogue was locked on him. [21] He began: "Today, in your hearing, this scripture has come true."

[22] At first they all spoke well of him, amazed at the grace pouring from his mouth. "Isn't this Joseph's son?" they said.

[23] He said, "I know what you're about to throw at me — that proverb, 'Doctor, heal yourself.' You'll say: do here in your hometown what we hear you did in Capernaum.

[24] "The truth is," he said, "no prophet gets a welcome in his own hometown. [25] Listen — there were plenty of widows in Israel back in Elijah's day, when the sky locked shut for three and a half years and famine ravaged the whole land. [26] But Elijah wasn't sent to any of them. He was sent to a foreign widow in Zarephath, in Sidon. [27] And there were plenty of lepers in Israel in the time of Elisha the prophet. Not one of them was cleansed — only Naaman the Syrian."

[28] The whole synagogue erupted in fury at his words. [29] They jumped up, drove him out of town, and marched him to the brow of the hill their town was built on, ready to throw him off the cliff. [30] But he walked right through the middle of them and went on his way.

[31] He came down to Capernaum, a town in Galilee, and on the Sabbath he was teaching the people. [32] They were stunned by his teaching — his words carried authority.

[33] In the synagogue there was a man possessed by an unclean spirit, a demon, and he screamed at the top of his lungs: [34] "What do you want with us, Jesus of Nazareth? Have you come to destroy us? I know who you are — the Holy One of God!"

[35] Jesus shut him down: "Silence. Get out of him." The demon threw the man down in front of everyone and came out without doing him any harm.

[36] Shock rolled through the room. They were saying to each other, "What is this? With authority and power he gives orders to unclean spirits — and they come out!" [37] News of him spilled out into every corner of the surrounding country.

[38] He left the synagogue and went to Simon's house. Simon's mother-in-law was burning with a high fever, and they begged him to help. [39] He stood over her, rebuked the fever, and it left. She got up immediately and started waiting on them.

[40] As the sun went down, everyone who had family members sick with anything brought them to him. He laid his hands on each one, and they were healed. [41] Demons came shrieking out of many of them, yelling, "You are the Son of God!" He rebuked them and refused to let them speak, because they knew he was the Messiah.

[42] At daybreak he slipped out to a deserted place. The crowds went looking for him, and when they found him, they tried to keep him from leaving. [43] But he told them, "I have to bring the good news of the kingdom of God to the other towns too. That's what I was sent for."

[44] And he kept on preaching in the synagogues of Judea.

[1] One day Jesus stood by the lake of Gennesaret while the crowd pressed in on him, hungry for God's word. [2] He spotted two boats beached at the water's edge — the fishermen had stepped out and were rinsing their nets. [3] He climbed into one of them, Simon's, and asked him to push off a little from shore. Then he sat down and taught the crowd from the boat.

[4] When he finished speaking, he said to Simon, "Take her out into the deep water and drop your nets for a catch."

[5] Simon answered, "Master, we worked the whole night and pulled in nothing. But if you say so — I'll let down the nets."

[6] They did, and what came up nearly tore the nets apart — a haul so massive [7] they had to wave their partners over from the other boat to help. Both boats filled up until they were sinking under the weight.

[8] Simon Peter saw it and dropped to his knees in front of Jesus. "Get away from me, Lord — I'm a sinful man." [9] Astonishment had hit him and everyone with him, staring at the catch they'd just pulled in — [10] James and John too, Zebedee's sons, Simon's partners.

Jesus said to Simon, "Don't be afraid. From now on you'll be catching people."

[11] They dragged the boats up onto the shore, walked away from everything, and followed him.

[12] In one of the towns, a man covered in leprosy saw Jesus, fell on his face, and begged him: "Lord, if you're willing, you can make me clean."

[13] Jesus reached out his hand and touched him. "I'm willing. Be clean." Instantly the leprosy was gone.

[14] Jesus told him, "Don't say a word to anyone. Go, show yourself to the priest and bring the offering Moses commanded for your cleansing — let it stand as proof to them."

[15] But the news spread anyway, faster than ever, and huge crowds gathered to hear him and to be healed of their illnesses. [16] He kept slipping away to deserted places to pray.

[17] One day he was teaching, and Pharisees and teachers of the law were sitting there — they had come from every village in Galilee and Judea and from Jerusalem itself. The power of the Lord was on him to heal.

[18] Some men showed up carrying a paralyzed man on a mat, trying to get him inside and lay him in front of Jesus. [19] When the crowd made that impossible, they climbed up onto the roof, pulled apart the tiles, and lowered him down — mat and all — right into the middle of the room, right in front of Jesus.

[20] Seeing their faith, he said, "Friend, your sins are forgiven."

[21] The scribes and Pharisees started muttering: "Who is this man, blaspheming like that? Who can forgive sins but God alone?"

[22] Jesus knew exactly what they were thinking. "Why are you reasoning like this in your hearts? [23] Which is easier — to say 'Your sins are forgiven,' or to say 'Get up and walk'? [24] But so you know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins —" he turned to the paralyzed man — "I'm telling you: get up, pick up your mat, and go home."

[25] The man stood up right there in front of them, picked up what he'd been lying on, and walked out praising God. [26] Everyone was floored. They glorified God, awestruck, saying, "We've seen things today we can't even believe."

[27] After this, Jesus went out and saw a tax collector named Levi sitting at his booth. "Follow me," he said. [28] Levi got up, left everything, and followed him.

[29] Then Levi threw a massive banquet for Jesus at his house — a huge crowd of tax collectors and others were at the table with them. [30] The Pharisees and their scribes grumbled to his disciples: "Why do you eat and drink with tax collectors and sinners?"

[31] Jesus answered them, "Healthy people don't need a doctor — sick people do. [32] I didn't come to call the righteous. I came to call sinners to a change of heart."

[33] They pressed him: "John's disciples fast often and pray, and so do the Pharisees'. But yours eat and drink."

[34] Jesus said, "Can you make the wedding guests fast while the bridegroom is right there with them? [35] The days are coming when the bridegroom will be torn away from them — then they'll fast."

[36] He gave them this picture too: "Nobody rips a patch off a new coat to mend an old one. If they do, they ruin the new and the patch won't match the old anyway. [37] And nobody pours new wine into old wineskins. If they do, the new wine bursts the skins, the wine spills out, and the skins are wrecked. [38] New wine goes into new wineskins. [39] And nobody after drinking the old wants the new — they say, 'The old is better.'"

[1] One Sabbath, as Jesus walked through the grainfields, his disciples were stripping heads of grain, rubbing them in their palms, and eating them. [2] Some Pharisees said, "Why are you doing what's forbidden on the Sabbath?"

[3] Jesus answered, "Haven't you read what David did when he and his men were starving? [4] He walked into God's house, took the consecrated bread—the bread only priests are allowed to eat—and ate it. He even gave some to his men." [5] Then he said, "The Son of Man is Lord of the Sabbath."

[6] On another Sabbath, he went into the synagogue and started teaching. A man was there whose right hand was withered. [7] The scribes and Pharisees watched him like hawks—would he heal on the Sabbath?—looking for any excuse to bring charges. [8] But Jesus knew exactly what they were thinking. He said to the man with the withered hand, "Get up. Come stand here in front of everyone." The man rose and stood there. [9] Jesus said to them, "Let me ask you something: on the Sabbath, is it lawful to do good or to do harm? To save a life or to destroy it?" [10] He swept his eyes across all of them, then said to the man, "Stretch out your hand." He did—and it was completely restored. [11] They went berserk. They started plotting together: what could they do to Jesus?

[12] In those days he went up the mountain to pray, and he spent the whole night there, praying to God. [13] When morning came, he called his disciples to him and chose twelve of them, naming them apostles: [14] Simon, whom he named Peter, and his brother Andrew; James and John; Philip and Bartholomew; [15] Matthew and Thomas; James son of Alphaeus, and Simon called the Zealot; [16] Judas son of James, and Judas Iscariot—who became the traitor.

[17] He came down with them and stood on a level place, surrounded by a huge crowd of his disciples and an immense gathering of people from all over Judea, Jerusalem, and the coastal region of Tyre and Sidon. [18] They had come to hear him and to be healed of their diseases. Those tormented by unclean spirits were set free. [19] The whole crowd kept trying to touch him, because power was pouring out of him and healing every last one of them.

[20] He lifted his eyes to his disciples and said:

"Blessed are you who are poor—
the kingdom of God is yours.
[21] Blessed are you who are starving now—
you will be filled.
Blessed are you who are weeping now—
you will laugh.

[22] Blessed are you when people hate you, when they shut you out and slander you and spit your name into the dirt because of the Son of Man. [23] When that day comes, leap for joy—because your reward in heaven is staggering. That's exactly what their fathers did to the prophets.

[24] But trouble is coming for you who are rich—
you've already had your comfort.
[25] Trouble for you who are full now—
hunger is coming.
Trouble for you who laugh now—
you'll grieve and weep.

[26] Trouble for you when everyone speaks well of you—that's exactly what their fathers did for the false prophets.

[27] But to all of you listening, I say: love your enemies. Do good to those who hate you. [28] Bless those who curse you. Pray for those who abuse you. [29] If someone slaps you on the cheek, offer the other one too. If someone takes your coat, give him your shirt as well. [30] Give to everyone who asks. If someone takes what's yours, don't demand it back. [31] Do for others what you want others to do for you.

[32] If you only love people who love you back, what credit is that? Even sinners do that. [33] If you only do good to people who do good to you, what credit is that? Sinners do exactly the same. [34] If you only lend to people you expect to be repaid by, what credit is that? Sinners lend to sinners expecting to get every penny back. [35] No—love your enemies. Do good. Lend without expecting anything in return. Your reward will be enormous, and you will be sons of the Most High, because he is kind to the ungrateful and the wicked. [36] Be merciful, just as your Father is merciful.

[37] Stop judging, and you won't be judged. Stop condemning, and you won't be condemned. Forgive, and you'll be forgiven. [38] Give—and it will be given to you. A full measure, pressed down, shaken together, overflowing, will be poured into your lap. The measure you use is the measure you'll get back."

[39] He told them a parable: "Can a blind man lead another blind man? Won't they both tumble into the same ditch? [40] A student isn't above his teacher—but every student, fully trained, becomes like his teacher.

[41] Why do you stare at the speck in your brother's eye and miss the plank in your own? [42] How can you say to your brother, 'Brother, let me get that speck out of your eye,' when you can't even see the plank sticking out of yours? You hypocrite—first pull the plank out of your own eye. Then you'll see clearly enough to deal with the speck in your brother's.

[43] No good tree produces rotten fruit, and no rotten tree produces good fruit. [44] Each tree is known by what it grows. You don't pick figs from thornbushes or grapes from brambles. [45] The good person brings good out of the good stored in his heart. The evil person brings evil out of the evil stored in his. The mouth speaks what the heart is full of.

[46] Why do you call me 'Lord, Lord,' and not do what I say? [47] Anyone who comes to me, hears my words, and acts on them—let me show you who that person is like. [48] He's like a man building a house who dug down deep and laid the foundation on bedrock. When the flood came and the river hammered that house, it couldn't shake it—because it was well built. [49] But anyone who hears my words and doesn't act on them is like a man who built a house on bare ground with no foundation. The river hit it and immediately it collapsed—and the wreckage was total."

[1] When Jesus finished saying all this to the people listening, he went into Capernaum.

[2] There was a centurion there whose servant — a man he valued deeply — was sick and about to die.

[3] When the centurion heard about Jesus, he sent some Jewish elders to him, asking him to come and save his servant's life.

[4] They came to Jesus and pleaded earnestly: "He deserves this. Do it for him.

[5] He loves our people. He's the one who built our synagogue."

[6] So Jesus went with them. But when he was nearly at the house, the centurion sent friends with this message: "Lord, don't trouble yourself. I'm not worthy to have you under my roof.

[7] That's why I didn't even come to you myself. Just say the word — my servant will be healed.

[8] I understand authority. I'm a man under orders, with soldiers under me. I tell one 'Go' and he goes, another 'Come' and he comes, my servant 'Do this' and he does it."

[9] Jesus heard this and was stunned. He turned to the crowd trailing him and said, "I'm telling you — I haven't found faith like this anywhere in Israel."

[10] The messengers returned to the house and found the servant well.

[11] Soon after, Jesus traveled to a town called Nain. His disciples were with him, along with a large crowd.

[12] As he approached the town gate, a funeral procession was coming out — a dead man being carried, the only son of his mother, and she was a widow. A sizable crowd from the town was with her.

[13] When the Lord saw her, his heart broke for her. "Don't cry," he said.

[14] He stepped forward and touched the bier. The pallbearers stopped. "Young man," he said, "I'm telling you — get up."

[15] The dead man sat up and started speaking. And Jesus gave him back to his mother.

[16] Fear swept through them all, and they began praising God. "A great prophet has appeared among us!" they said. "God has come for his people!"

[17] The news about him spread across all of Judea and the surrounding region.

[18] John's disciples brought him reports of everything happening.

[19] So John called two of them and sent them to the Lord with a question: "Are you the one who's coming, or should we be watching for someone else?"

[20] When they reached Jesus, they said, "John the Baptist sent us to ask: Are you the one who's coming, or should we be watching for someone else?"

[21] Right then Jesus was healing many people of diseases and afflictions and evil spirits, and giving sight to many blind.

[22] He answered them: "Go back and tell John what you've seen and heard. The blind see. The lame walk. Lepers are cleansed. The deaf hear. The dead are raised. The poor are hearing good news.

[23] And blessed is the one who doesn't stumble over me."

[24] When John's messengers left, Jesus started talking to the crowd about John: "What did you go out into the wilderness to look at? A reed swaying in the wind?

[25] No? Then what? A man dressed in soft clothes? Look — people who wear fine clothes and live in luxury are in palaces.

[26] So what did you go to see? A prophet? Yes — and more than a prophet.

[27] This is the one Scripture is talking about: 'Look, I'm sending my messenger ahead of you, the one who will prepare your road.'

[28] I'm telling you — no one born of a woman is greater than John. And yet the least person in God's kingdom is greater than he is."

[29] When the people heard this — even the tax collectors — they acknowledged God was right, because they'd been baptized by John.

[30] But the Pharisees and the legal experts rejected God's purpose for themselves, refusing John's baptism.

[31] "What can I compare this generation to?" Jesus said. "What are they like?

[32] They're like kids sitting in the marketplace shouting at each other: 'We played the flute and you didn't dance! We sang a dirge and you didn't cry!'

[33] John the Baptist came not eating bread, not drinking wine — and you said, 'He's got a demon.'

[34] The Son of Man came eating and drinking — and you said, 'Look at him, a glutton and a drunk, a friend of tax collectors and sinners.'

[35] But wisdom is proved right by all her children."

[36] One of the Pharisees invited Jesus to dinner, so he went to the Pharisee's house and reclined at the table.

[37] A woman in that town — a woman known as a sinner — found out Jesus was eating at the Pharisee's house. She brought an alabaster jar of perfume,

[38] stood behind him at his feet, weeping. Her tears began wetting his feet, and she wiped them with her hair, kissed them, and poured the perfume on them.

[39] The Pharisee who'd invited him watched this and thought: "If this man were really a prophet, he'd know who's touching him — what kind of woman she is. A sinner."

[40] Jesus spoke up: "Simon, I have something to say to you."

"Go ahead, Teacher," he said.

[41] "Two men owed money to a moneylender. One owed five hundred denarii, the other fifty.

[42] Neither could pay, so he canceled both debts. Which one will love him more?"

[43] Simon answered, "The one who had the bigger debt canceled, I'd think."

"Exactly," Jesus said.

[44] Then he turned toward the woman and said to Simon: "Do you see this woman? I came into your house — you didn't give me water for my feet. She drenched my feet with her tears and wiped them with her hair.

[45] You didn't greet me with a kiss. From the moment she came in, she hasn't stopped kissing my feet.

[46] You didn't put oil on my head. She poured perfume on my feet.

[47] So I'm telling you — her many sins have been forgiven. That's why she loves so much. The one forgiven little loves little."

[48] Then he said to her, "Your sins are forgiven."

[49] The others at the table started muttering among themselves: "Who is this who even forgives sins?"

[50] But he told her, "Your faith has saved you. Go in peace."

[1] Soon after this, Jesus moved from town to town, village to village, announcing the news — the kingdom of God is breaking in. The Twelve traveled with him,

[2] and so did some women he had freed from evil spirits and diseases: Mary called Magdalene, out of whom seven demons had been driven;

[3] Joanna, wife of Chuza, Herod's household manager; Susanna; and many others. They bankrolled the mission out of their own resources.

[4] A massive crowd gathered, people streaming in from every town, so Jesus told them a story:

[5] "A farmer went out to sow his seed. As he scattered it, some landed on the path — trampled underfoot, snatched up by birds.

[6] Some hit rocky ground. It sprouted, but with no moisture beneath it, it withered.

[7] Some fell into a tangle of thorns, and the thorns choked the life out of it.

[8] But some fell into good soil, took root, and produced a hundred times what was planted." Then he raised his voice: "If you have ears, listen."

[9] His disciples asked him what the story meant.

[10] He told them: "You've been given the secrets of God's kingdom. To the rest it comes in parables — so that *looking, they don't see; hearing, they don't understand.*

[11] Here's the meaning: the seed is the word of God.

[12] The seed on the path — those are people who hear, then the devil comes and steals the word out of their hearts before it can save them.

[13] The seed on the rocks — those who hear and welcome the word with joy, but it has no roots. They believe for a moment, then collapse the second testing comes.

[14] The seed among thorns — those who hear, but as they go on, the worries, riches, and pleasures of this life strangle them, and they never come to anything.

[15] But the seed in good soil — those who hear the word with an honest, open heart, hold onto it, and bear fruit by sticking with it.

[16] Nobody lights a lamp and then hides it under a jar or shoves it under the bed. You put it on a stand so anyone walking in can see.

[17] Everything hidden will come to light. Every secret will be exposed.

[18] So pay attention to how you listen. Whoever has, will be given more. Whoever has nothing — even what they think they have will be taken from them."

[19] His mother and brothers showed up but couldn't get to him through the crowd.

[20] Someone passed the word: "Your mother and brothers are outside. They want to see you."

[21] He answered, "My mother and my brothers? They're the ones who hear God's word and act on it."

[22] One day he got into a boat with his disciples and said, "Let's cross to the other side of the lake." They pushed off,

[23] and as they sailed, he fell asleep. Then a violent squall swept down on the lake. The boat was taking on water. They were in real danger.

[24] They woke him, shouting, "Master! Master! We're going down!" He stood up, rebuked the wind and the surging water — and everything went still. Dead calm.

[25] "Where is your faith?" he asked them. They were terrified and stunned. "Who *is* this man?" they whispered to each other. "He gives orders to wind and water, and they obey him."

[26] They landed in the region of the Gerasenes, across the lake from Galilee.

[27] As Jesus stepped onto shore, a man from the town met him — a man possessed by demons. He hadn't worn clothes or lived in a house for a long time. He lived in the tombs.

[28] The moment he saw Jesus, he screamed and threw himself down before him, shouting at the top of his lungs: "What do you want with me, Jesus, Son of the Most High God? I'm begging you — don't torture me!"

[29] Because Jesus had already commanded the unclean spirit to come out. It had seized the man again and again. They'd chained his hands and feet, kept him under guard, but he'd snap the chains and the demon would drive him into the wilderness.

[30] Jesus asked him, "What's your name?" "Legion," he said — because many demons had taken him over.

[31] They begged him not to send them into the Abyss.

[32] A large herd of pigs was feeding on the hillside, and the demons pleaded with Jesus to let them go into the pigs. He gave them permission.

[33] The demons came out of the man and rushed into the pigs, and the whole herd stampeded down the cliff into the lake and drowned.

[34] The herdsmen saw it, fled, and spread the news through the town and countryside.

[35] People came out to see what had happened. They came to Jesus and found the man — the one the demons had left — sitting at Jesus' feet, dressed, sane. And they were terrified.

[36] Those who had witnessed it told them how the possessed man had been healed.

[37] The whole crowd from the Gerasene region begged Jesus to leave. The fear was overwhelming. So he got in the boat and started back.

[38] The man who'd been freed begged to go with him, but Jesus sent him away, saying,

[39] "Go home. Tell everyone what God has done for you." So he went, broadcasting through the whole town what Jesus had done for him.

[40] When Jesus returned, the crowd welcomed him back — they'd been waiting.

[41] A man named Jairus came up — a synagogue official — and fell at Jesus' feet, pleading with him to come to his house

[42] because his only daughter, twelve years old, was dying. As Jesus made his way there, the crowd pressed in on him from every side.

[43] In that crowd was a woman who'd been bleeding for twelve years. She'd spent everything on doctors and no one could heal her.

[44] She came up behind him and touched the edge of his cloak — and instantly the bleeding stopped.

[45] "Who touched me?" Jesus said. Everyone denied it. Peter spoke up: "Master, the crowd is crushing in on you."

[46] But Jesus said, "Someone touched me. I felt power go out from me."

[47] The woman, realizing she couldn't hide, came forward trembling. She fell down before him and told him, in front of everyone, why she'd touched him and how she'd been healed in an instant.

[48] He said to her, "Daughter, your faith has healed you. Go in peace."

[49] He was still speaking when someone arrived from the synagogue official's house. "Your daughter is dead," the man said. "Don't trouble the teacher anymore."

[50] Jesus heard it and said to Jairus, "Don't be afraid. Just believe. She will be made well."

[51] When he got to the house, he wouldn't let anyone in with him except Peter, John, James, and the girl's father and mother.

[52] Everyone was weeping and wailing for her. "Stop crying," he said. "She's not dead. She's sleeping."

[53] They laughed at him — they knew she was dead.

[54] He took her hand and said, "Child, get up."

[55] Her spirit returned. She stood up immediately. He told them to give her something to eat.

[56] Her parents were beside themselves. But he told them not to say a word to anyone about what had happened.

[1] He called the Twelve together and handed them power and authority over every demon and the ability to heal diseases. [2] Then he commissioned them to announce God's kingdom and to make the sick well.

[3] "Travel light," he told them. "No staff, no bag, no bread, no money — not even a second tunic. [4] Whatever house takes you in, stay there until you leave that town. [5] If a place refuses you, walk out and shake the dust off your feet — let that be the verdict against them."

[6] So they went. Village to village, carrying good news and leaving healing in their wake.

[7] Word reached Herod the tetrarch, and he was rattled. People were saying all kinds of things — that John had risen from the dead, [8] that Elijah had returned, that some ancient prophet had come back to life. [9] "I beheaded John," Herod said. "So who is this man I keep hearing about?" And he was determined to see him.

[10] The apostles returned and reported everything they had done. Jesus took them aside and slipped away to a town called Bethsaida. [11] But the crowds figured it out and followed. He welcomed them, talked to them about God's kingdom, and healed everyone who needed healing.

[12] As the sun started to drop, the Twelve came to him. "Send the crowd away. Let them find lodging and food in the surrounding villages — we're out here in the middle of nowhere."

[13] "You feed them," Jesus said.

"We have five loaves and two fish," they said. "Unless you want us to go buy food for this entire mob." [14] There were about five thousand men.

He told the disciples, "Sit them down in groups of fifty." [15] They did. Everyone sat. [16] Jesus took the five loaves and the two fish, looked up to heaven, blessed them, broke them, and kept handing pieces to the disciples to distribute. [17] Everyone ate. Everyone was full. And when they gathered the leftovers — twelve baskets.

[18] One day Jesus was praying alone, with only the disciples nearby, and he asked them, "Who do the crowds say I am?"

[19] "Some say John the Baptist. Others say Elijah. Others, one of the old prophets risen again."

[20] "And you?" he said. "Who do you say I am?"

Peter answered: "God's Anointed."

[21] Jesus warned them — sharply — not to breathe a word of this to anyone. [22] "The Son of Man has to suffer," he told them. "He has to be rejected by the elders, the chief priests, the scribes. He has to be killed. And on the third day, he has to be raised."

[23] Then he turned to all of them: "If anyone wants to come after me, they have to say no to themselves, pick up their cross — daily — and walk where I walk. [24] Anyone who clutches their life will lose it. Anyone who lets their life go for my sake will find it. [25] What's the use of gaining the whole world if you forfeit yourself in the process? [26] If you're ashamed of me and my words, the Son of Man will be ashamed of you when he comes in his glory, and the Father's, and the holy angels'. [27] I'm telling you the truth — some standing here will not taste death before they see God's kingdom."

[28] About eight days after this, Jesus took Peter, John, and James and climbed a mountain to pray. [29] As he prayed, his face changed. His clothes turned blinding white. [30] And then — two men, talking with him. Moses and Elijah. [31] They appeared in glory and were speaking of his departure, the one he was about to accomplish in Jerusalem.

[32] Peter and the others were heavy with sleep, but they fought it off and saw his glory and the two men standing with him. [33] As Moses and Elijah were leaving, Peter blurted out, "Master, it's good for us to be here. Let's build three shelters — one for you, one for Moses, one for Elijah." He had no idea what he was saying.

[34] While he was still talking, a cloud rolled in and covered them. They were terrified as the cloud closed around them. [35] And a voice came from the cloud: "This is my Son, my Chosen One. Listen to him."

[36] When the voice stopped, Jesus was alone. They kept it to themselves. In those days, they told no one what they had seen.

[37] The next day, when they came down from the mountain, a huge crowd met him. [38] A man shouted from the crowd: "Teacher — I'm begging you — look at my son. He's my only child. [39] A spirit grabs hold of him and he screams, and it throws him into convulsions until he's foaming at the mouth. It mauls him and barely lets go. [40] I begged your disciples to drive it out. They couldn't."

[41] "You unbelieving, twisted generation," Jesus said. "How long do I have to be with you? How long do I have to put up with you?" Then to the father: "Bring your son here."

[42] The boy was still on his way when the demon hurled him down and threw him into convulsions. Jesus rebuked the unclean spirit, healed the boy, and gave him back to his father. [43] Everyone was stunned at the greatness of God.

While they were still marveling at everything he was doing, Jesus said to his disciples, [44] "Let these words sink deep into your ears: the Son of Man is about to be handed over into human hands." [45] But they didn't understand. The meaning was hidden from them — they couldn't grasp it. And they were afraid to ask.

[46] An argument broke out among them: which of them was greatest. [47] Jesus, knowing what was running through their hearts, took a child and stood the child beside him. [48] "Whoever welcomes this child in my name welcomes me. And whoever welcomes me welcomes the One who sent me. Because the smallest one among you — that's the great one."

[49] John spoke up. "Master, we saw someone driving out demons in your name, and we tried to stop him because he's not one of us."

[50] "Don't stop him," Jesus said. "Anyone who isn't against you is for you."

[51] As the days drew near for him to be taken up, Jesus set his face toward Jerusalem. [52] He sent messengers ahead. They went into a Samaritan village to make arrangements, [53] but the village wouldn't receive him — because he was heading for Jerusalem.

[54] When James and John saw this, they said, "Lord, do you want us to call down fire from heaven to incinerate them?"

[55] He turned and rebuked them. [56] And they went on to another village.

[57] As they walked the road, someone said to him, "I'll follow you wherever you go."

[58] Jesus answered: "Foxes have dens. Birds of the air have nests. The Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head."

[59] To another, he said: "Follow me."

The man said, "Lord, let me first go and bury my father."

[60] Jesus said, "Let the dead bury their dead. You — go and announce God's kingdom."

[61] Another said, "I'll follow you, Lord. But first let me say goodbye to my family."

[62] Jesus said, "No one who puts a hand to the plow and then looks back is fit for God's kingdom."

[1] After this, the Lord appointed seventy-two others and sent them out two by two, ahead of him, into every town and place he was about to visit.

[2] He told them: "The harvest is massive, but the workers are few. So beg the Lord of the harvest to send out workers into his fields.

[3] Go. I'm sending you out as lambs into a wolf pack.

[4] Don't carry a wallet, a bag, or extra sandals. Don't stop to greet anyone on the road.

[5] When you enter a house, the first thing out of your mouth should be: 'Peace to this house.'

[6] If a person of peace lives there, your peace will rest on them. If not, it will return to you.

[7] Stay in that one house. Eat and drink whatever they put in front of you—the worker has earned his wages. Don't shop around from house to house.

[8] When you enter a town that welcomes you, eat what's set before you,

[9] heal the sick there, and tell them: 'The kingdom of God has arrived at your doorstep.'

[10] But when you enter a town that won't welcome you, go out into its streets and say,

[11] 'Even the dust of your town that clings to our feet—we wipe it off as a witness against you. But know this: the kingdom of God has arrived.'

[12] I'm telling you, on that day Sodom will fare better than that town.

[13] Disaster, Chorazin. Disaster, Bethsaida. If the miracles done in you had been done in Tyre and Sidon, they'd have repented long ago—sitting in sackcloth and ashes.

[14] Tyre and Sidon will fare better than you at the judgment.

[15] And you, Capernaum—you think you'll be lifted to the skies? You'll be dropped to the depths.

[16] Whoever listens to you listens to me. Whoever rejects you rejects me. And whoever rejects me rejects the One who sent me."

[17] The seventy-two came back electric with joy. "Lord, even the demons surrender to us when we use your name."

[18] He told them, "I watched Satan fall from heaven like a lightning strike.

[19] Listen—I've given you authority to walk on snakes and scorpions, to crush every power of the enemy. Nothing will harm you.

[20] But don't celebrate that the spirits obey you. Celebrate this: your names are written in heaven."

[21] In that moment, the Holy Spirit set Jesus on fire with joy, and he said: "Father, Lord of heaven and earth, I praise you—because you hid these things from the brilliant and the educated, and revealed them to children. Yes, Father. This is exactly what pleased you.

[22] Everything has been handed over to me by my Father. No one knows who the Son is except the Father, and no one knows who the Father is except the Son—and anyone the Son chooses to reveal him to."

[23] Then he turned to his disciples and said privately, "Blessed are the eyes that see what you see.

[24] I'm telling you—prophets and kings longed to see what you're seeing and never saw it, longed to hear what you're hearing and never heard it."

[25] A legal expert stood up to test him. "Teacher, what do I do to inherit eternal life?"

[26] Jesus replied, "What's written in the Law? How do you read it?"

[27] He answered: "Love the Lord your God with all your heart, all your soul, all your strength, and all your mind—and love your neighbor as yourself."

[28] "Exactly right," Jesus said. "Do that, and you'll live."

[29] But the man, wanting to justify himself, pressed further: "And who is my neighbor?"

[30] Jesus answered: "A man was traveling down from Jerusalem to Jericho when bandits jumped him. They stripped him, beat him, and left him half-dead in the dirt.

[31] A priest happened to come down the same road. He saw the man—and crossed to the other side.

[32] Then a Levite came along, looked at him—and crossed to the other side.

[33] But a Samaritan, traveling the road, came upon him. And when he saw him, his gut wrenched with compassion.

[34] He went over, poured oil and wine on the wounds, bandaged them up, lifted the man onto his own animal, brought him to an inn, and stayed up with him through the night.

[35] The next morning he pulled out two days' wages and gave them to the innkeeper. 'Take care of him,' he said. 'Whatever else it costs, I'll cover when I come back.'

[36] Now—which of these three was a neighbor to the man who fell among bandits?"

[37] The expert said, "The one who showed him mercy." Jesus told him, "Go and do the same."

[38] As they traveled on, Jesus came to a village where a woman named Martha opened her home to him.

[39] She had a sister named Mary, who sat at the Lord's feet, drinking in every word.

[40] Martha, meanwhile, was unraveling under the weight of all the serving. She marched over and said, "Lord, doesn't it bother you that my sister has left me to do everything alone? Tell her to help me."

[41] The Lord answered, "Martha, Martha. You're anxious and pulled apart by so many things,

[42] but only one thing is needed. Mary has chosen what matters—and it won't be taken from her."

[1] One day Jesus was praying somewhere, and when he finished, one of his disciples said, "Lord, teach us to pray. John taught his disciples."

[2] He told them: "When you pray, say this—

Father,
let your name be held sacred.
Let your kingdom come.

[3] Give us, day by day, the bread we need for the day.

[4] Forgive us our sins,
as we forgive everyone who owes us.
And don't lead us into the test."

[5] Then he said to them: "Imagine one of you has a friend, and you go to him at midnight and say, 'Friend, lend me three loaves—

[6] a friend of mine just showed up from a journey, and I have nothing to set before him.'

[7] And from inside he answers, 'Don't bother me. The door's already shut. My kids are in bed with me. I can't get up and give you anything.'

[8] I'm telling you—even if he won't get up out of friendship, he'll get up because of the man's sheer nerve and give him whatever he needs.

[9] So I tell you: ask, and it will be given. Search, and you'll find. Knock, and the door will open.

[10] Because everyone who asks receives. The one who searches finds. To the one who knocks, the door opens.

[11] What father among you, if his son asks for a fish, hands him a snake instead?

[12] Or if he asks for an egg, gives him a scorpion?

[13] If you—flawed as you are—know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will the Father in heaven give the Holy Spirit to those who ask him?"

[14] He was casting out a demon—a mute one. The moment the demon left, the mute man spoke, and the crowd was stunned.

[15] But some of them said, "He drives out demons by Beelzebul, the prince of demons."

[16] Others, testing him, demanded a sign from heaven.

[17] He knew exactly what they were thinking. He said: "Every kingdom split against itself collapses. A house divided crashes down on itself.

[18] If Satan is divided against himself, how does his kingdom stand? You say I cast out demons by Beelzebul.

[19] But if I drive them out by Beelzebul, by whom do your own people drive them out? They'll be your judges.

[20] But if I cast out demons by the finger of God—then the kingdom of God has just landed on you.

[21] When a strong man, fully armed, guards his own courtyard, his possessions are safe.

[22] But when someone stronger comes and overpowers him, he strips off the armor he trusted in and divides the spoils.

[23] Whoever isn't with me is against me. Whoever doesn't gather with me scatters.

[24] When an unclean spirit goes out of a person, it roams arid places looking for rest. Finding none, it says, 'I'll go back to the house I left.'

[25] It arrives and finds the place swept clean, everything in order.

[26] Then it goes and brings along seven other spirits more vicious than itself, and they all move in. The person ends up worse than before."

[27] As he was saying this, a woman in the crowd raised her voice and called out, "Blessed is the womb that bore you and the breasts that nursed you!"

[28] He answered, "Blessed rather are those who hear the word of God and keep it."

[29] As the crowds pressed in, he began to say: "This generation is a wicked generation. It demands a sign, and no sign will be given it—except the sign of Jonah.

[30] Just as Jonah became a sign to the Ninevites, so will the Son of Man be to this generation.

[31] The Queen of the South will rise at the judgment with the people of this generation and condemn them—because she traveled from the ends of the earth to hear Solomon's wisdom, and look: something greater than Solomon is right here.

[32] The men of Nineveh will stand up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it—because they repented at Jonah's preaching, and look: something greater than Jonah is right here.

[33] No one lights a lamp and hides it in a cellar or under a basket. You put it on a stand so people walking in can see the light.

[34] Your eye is the lamp of your body. When your eye is clear, your whole body is flooded with light. When it's diseased, your body is full of darkness.

[35] So watch out—make sure the light in you isn't actually darkness.

[36] If your whole body is light, with no shadow anywhere, it will be radiant through and through, like a lamp shining its beam on you."

[37] When he finished speaking, a Pharisee invited him to dinner. He went in and reclined at the table.

[38] The Pharisee was shocked to see him skip the ritual washing before the meal.

[39] The Lord said to him: "You Pharisees—you scrub the outside of the cup and the dish, but inside you're packed with greed and malice.

[40] Fools. Didn't the one who made the outside make the inside too?

[41] Give what's inside as alms, and watch—everything becomes clean for you.

[42] But woe to you Pharisees. You tithe your mint and rue and every garden herb, but you walk right past justice and the love of God. These are the things you should have done—without neglecting the others.

[43] Woe to you Pharisees. You love the front-row seats in the synagogues and the bows in the marketplaces.

[44] Woe to you. You're like unmarked graves—people walk over you without knowing it."

[45] One of the legal experts spoke up: "Teacher, when you say these things, you're insulting us too."

[46] He said: "Woe to you experts in the law as well. You strap impossible loads on people's backs, and you won't lift a finger to help carry them.

[47] Woe to you. You build tombs for the prophets—and your ancestors are the ones who killed them.

[48] So you're witnesses. You approve of what your ancestors did. They killed; you build.

[49] That's why the Wisdom of God said: 'I'll send them prophets and apostles. Some they'll kill. Others they'll hunt down.'

[50] So the blood of every prophet shed since the foundation of the world will be charged to this generation—

[51] from the blood of Abel to the blood of Zechariah, who died between the altar and the sanctuary. Yes—I'm telling you—it will all be charged to this generation.

[52] Woe to you experts in the law. You took away the key to knowledge. You didn't go in yourselves, and you blocked the ones trying to enter."

[53] When he walked out of there, the scribes and Phariseesseething—started hounding him relentlessly, trying to make him talk about all sorts of things,

[54] lying in wait, hunting for something out of his mouth they could use against him.

[1] Meanwhile a crowd had swelled into the thousands — packed so tight people were trampling each other — and Jesus turned first to his disciples. "Watch out for the yeast of the Pharisees," he said. "I mean their hypocrisy.

[2] Nothing stays covered. Everything hidden gets uncovered. Every secret gets out.

[3] What you whispered in the dark will be shouted in daylight. What you murmured behind closed doors will be broadcast from the rooftops.

[4] Listen to me, friends — don't be afraid of those who can kill the body and after that have nothing left to do.

[5] I'll tell you who to fear. Fear the One who, after killing, has authority to throw you into hell. Yes — that's the One to fear.

[6] Five sparrows go for two cents, right? And not one of them slips God's mind.

[7] He's counted every hair on your head. Don't be afraid. You're worth more than a whole sky full of sparrows.

[8] Here's the truth: anyone who claims me publicly — the Son of Man will claim them before God's angels.

[9] But whoever disowns me in front of others will be disowned in front of the angels of God.

[10] Say something against the Son of Man, you can be forgiven. But slander the Holy Spirit? That door stays shut.

[11] When they drag you in front of synagogues, rulers, authorities — don't panic about what you'll say or how you'll defend yourself.

[12] When the moment comes, the Holy Spirit will give you the words."

[13] Someone in the crowd called out, "Teacher, tell my brother to split the inheritance with me."

[14] Jesus answered, "Friend, who appointed me your judge or referee?"

[15] Then to everyone: "Watch yourselves. Be on guard against every form of greed. A person's life is not measured by how much they own."

[16] He told them a story. "There was a rich man whose fields produced a bumper crop.

[17] He thought to himself, 'What am I going to do? I've got nowhere to store all this.'

[18] Then he said, 'I know. I'll tear down my barns and build bigger ones, and store all my grain and goods there.

[19] And I'll tell myself: You've got it made — enough stockpiled for years. Relax. Eat. Drink. Enjoy life.'

[20] But God said to him, 'You fool. Tonight your life will be demanded back. Then whose will all this be — everything you piled up?'

[21] That's how it goes for anyone who hoards for himself but is bankrupt toward God."

[22] Then to the disciples: "So I'm telling you — stop worrying about your life, what you'll eat. Stop worrying about your body, what you'll wear.

[23] Life is more than food. The body is more than clothes.

[24] Look at the ravens. They don't plant, don't harvest, don't have a pantry or a barn — and God feeds them. You matter so much more than birds.

[25] Can any of you, by stewing over it, add a single hour to your life?

[26] If you can't manage even that, why worry about the rest?

[27] Look at the wildflowers. They don't slave or sew, and I'm telling you — Solomon in all his royal glory was never dressed like one of these.

[28] If that's how God clothes the grass — here today, kindling tomorrow — how much more will he take care of you? You with your thimble-sized faith.

[29] Don't be consumed with what you'll eat and drink. Stop spinning.

[30] The pagan world chases after all of this. Your Father knows you need it.

[31] Chase his kingdom instead, and these things will be handed to you.

[32] Don't be afraid, little flock. Your Father is delighted to give you the kingdom.

[33] Sell what you have. Give to the poor. Get yourselves wallets that don't wear out — a treasure in heaven that won't run dry, where no thief comes near, no moth chews through.

[34] Because wherever your treasure sits, that's where your heart lives.

[35] Stay dressed and ready. Keep the lamps burning.

[36] Be like servants waiting for the master to come home from the wedding — so when he knocks, they can open the door instantly.

[37] Blessed are those servants the master finds awake when he arrives. I'm telling you the truth — he'll tie an apron on himself, sit them down at the table, and serve them.

[38] Whether he comes at midnight or just before dawn — if he finds them ready, they're blessed.

[39] Understand this: if the homeowner knew what hour the thief was coming, he wouldn't let his house be broken into.

[40] So be ready. The Son of Man is coming at an hour you're not expecting."

[41] Peter said, "Lord — is this parable just for us, or for everyone?"

[42] The Lord answered, "Who, then, is the faithful and shrewd manager — the one the master puts in charge of the household staff, to dole out their food at the right time?

[43] Blessed is that servant whose master finds him doing exactly that when he comes home.

[44] I'm telling you — he'll put him in charge of everything he owns.

[45] But suppose that servant says to himself, 'My master's taking his time,' and starts knocking around the other servants, eating, drinking, getting drunk.

[46] The master will show up on a day he doesn't expect, at an hour he doesn't know — and will cut him to pieces and assign him a place with the faithless.

[47] The servant who knew his master's will but didn't get ready or do it will be beaten with many blows.

[48] The one who didn't know but did things deserving punishment will be beaten with few. From everyone given much, much will be required. From the one entrusted with more, more will be asked.

[49] I came to throw fire on the earth — and how I wish it were already blazing.

[50] There's a baptism I have to undergo, and I am in agony until it's finished.

[51] You think I came to hand out peace on earth? No. I'm telling you — I came to bring division.

[52] From now on, five in one house will be split — three against two, two against three.

[53] Father against son, son against father. Mother against daughter, daughter against mother. Mother-in-law against daughter-in-law, daughter-in-law against mother-in-law."

[54] Then to the crowds: "When you see a cloud rising in the west, you say, 'Rain's coming' — and it does.

[55] When the south wind blows, you say, 'It's going to be hot' — and it is.

[56] Frauds. You can read the face of the earth and sky — how is it you can't read this moment?

[57] Why don't you judge for yourselves what's right?

[58] When you're heading to court with your accuser, do everything you can to settle on the way — or he'll drag you before the judge, the judge will hand you to the officer, and the officer will throw you in jail.

[59] I'm telling you — you won't get out until you've paid every last penny."

[1] Right then, some people showed up with news about the Galileans Pilate had butchered — their blood mingled with the blood of the sacrifices they were offering.

[2] Jesus answered them: "You think these Galileans suffered like this because they were worse sinners than every other Galilean?

[3] No. And I'm telling you — unless you turn, you'll all die the same way.

[4] Or those eighteen people the tower at Siloam fell on and crushed — you think they were guiltier than everyone else living in Jerusalem?

[5] No. And I'm telling you — unless you turn, you'll all die just like them."

[6] Then he told them this story: "A man had a fig tree planted in his vineyard. He came looking for fruit on it and found none.

[7] So he said to the gardener, 'Look — three years I've come to this tree hunting for fruit. Nothing. Cut it down. Why should it suck the life out of the soil?'

[8] But the gardener said, 'Sir, give it one more year. Let me dig around it, work in some manure.

[9] Maybe next season it'll produce. If not — then cut it down.'"

[10] He was teaching in one of the synagogues on a Sabbath.

[11] And there was a woman there — bent double for eighteen years by a spirit that had crippled her. She couldn't straighten up at all.

[12] Jesus saw her, called her over, and said, "Woman, you're released from your weakness."
