[13] He laid his hands on her, and instantly she stood up straight and started praising God.

[14] The synagogue ruler was furious that Jesus had healed on the Sabbath. He turned to the crowd and announced, "There are six days for work. Come on those days to be healed — not on the Sabbath."

[15] The Lord answered him: "You hypocrites. Every one of you unties your ox or your donkey from the stall on the Sabbath and leads it out to water, don't you?

[16] And this woman — a daughter of Abraham, bound by Satan for eighteen long years — shouldn't she be untied from her chains on the Sabbath?"

[17] That shut his opponents up. And the whole crowd was thrilled at every glorious thing he was doing.

[18] So he asked, "What is the kingdom of God like? What can I compare it to?

[19] It's like a mustard seed a man took and planted in his garden. It grew into a tree, and the birds of the air nested in its branches."

[20] Again he said, "What can I compare the kingdom of God to?

[21] It's like yeast a woman took and worked into a huge batch of flour — until the whole thing was alive with it."

[22] He kept moving through towns and villages, teaching as he made his way toward Jerusalem.

[23] Someone asked him, "Lord, are only a few people going to be saved?"

He told them,

[24] "Fight your way through the narrow door. I'm telling you — many will try to get in and won't be able to.

[25] Once the master of the house gets up and locks the door, you'll be left outside knocking, pleading, 'Sir, open up for us.' And he'll answer, 'I don't know you. I don't know where you're from.'

[26] Then you'll start protesting, 'But we ate with you. We drank with you. You taught in our streets.'

[27] And he'll say, 'I'm telling you — I don't know you or where you're from. Get away from me, all of you — you workers of evil.'

[28] There'll be weeping out there. Grinding of teeth. When you see Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and all the prophets inside the kingdom of God — and you yourselves thrown out.

[29] People will come streaming in from east and west, from north and south, and take their seats at the feast in the kingdom of God.

[30] And listen — there are last who will be first, and first who will be last."

[31] At that moment some Pharisees came up and warned him, "Get out of here. Move on. Herod wants you dead."

[32] He answered, "Go tell that fox: 'Watch me — I'm driving out demons and healing people today and tomorrow, and on the third day I finish my work.

[33] But I have to keep moving today, tomorrow, the next day — because it's unthinkable for a prophet to die anywhere but Jerusalem.'

[34] Jerusalem, Jerusalem — you who slaughter the prophets and stone the ones sent to you — how many times I've wanted to gather your children the way a hen gathers her chicks under her wings. And you wouldn't have it.

[35] Look at your house now. Abandoned. I'm telling you — you will not see me again until you say, 'Blessed is the one who comes in the name of the Lord.'"

[1] One Sabbath, Jesus went to eat at the home of a leading Pharisee, and every eye in the room was tracking him.

[2] Right there in front of him sat a man whose body was swollen with fluid.

[3] Jesus turned to the legal experts and the Pharisees and asked, "Is it permitted to heal on the Sabbath, or not?"

[4] Silence. So he took hold of the man, healed him, and sent him on his way.

[5] Then he said to them, "If your son — or even your ox — fell into a well on the Sabbath, would any one of you not pull him out the same minute?"

[6] They had no answer.

[7] He had been watching the guests jockey for the best seats, so he told them a story:

[8] "When someone invites you to a wedding feast, do not park yourself in the seat of honor. Someone more distinguished than you may have been invited,

[9] and the host will come over and say, 'Give this person your seat,' and you will have to slink down to the lowest spot, humiliated in front of everyone.

[10] Instead, when you arrive, take the lowest seat. Then when your host comes, he will say, 'Friend, move up higher.' And you will be honored in front of every guest at the table.

[11] Because anyone who exalts himself will be brought down, and anyone who humbles himself will be lifted up."

[12] Then he turned to his host. "When you throw a lunch or a dinner, do not invite your friends, your brothers, your relatives, or your wealthy neighbors. They will just invite you back, and you will have your payment.

[13] When you throw a banquet, invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, the blind —

[14] and you will be blessed, because they cannot repay you. Your repayment comes at the resurrection of the righteous."

[15] One of the guests at the table, hearing this, said, "Blessed is the one who gets to eat at the feast in God's kingdom!"

[16] Jesus answered him: "A man was throwing a great banquet, and he invited many guests.

[17] When the hour arrived, he sent his servant to tell those who had been invited, 'Come — everything is ready.'

[18] But every one of them, as if they had agreed beforehand, started making excuses. The first said, 'I just bought a field. I have to go see it. Please excuse me.'

[19] Another said, 'I just bought five pairs of oxen and I am on my way to try them out. Please excuse me.'

[20] Another said, 'I just got married. I cannot come.'

[21] The servant came back and reported all of this. The master of the house was furious. He said to his servant, 'Go out into the streets and the alleys of the city — fast — and bring back the poor, the crippled, the blind, and the lame.'

[22] The servant came back: 'Master, I have done what you said, and there is still room.'

[23] The master said, 'Then go out to the country roads and the hedgerows and pressure people to come in until my house is full.

[24] Because I am telling you — not one of those original guests will so much as taste my banquet.'"

[25] Massive crowds were traveling with him. He turned and faced them:

[26] "If anyone comes to me and does not hate his father and mother, his wife and children, his brothers and sisters — yes, even his own life — he cannot be my disciple.

[27] Anyone who does not carry his own cross and walk behind me cannot be my disciple.

[28] Think about it: which of you, planning to build a tower, does not first sit down and count the cost to see if he can actually finish it?

[29] Otherwise, he lays the foundation, runs out of money, and everyone watching starts mocking him,

[30] saying, 'This man started building and could not finish.'

[31] Or which king, marching out to fight another king, does not first sit down and figure out whether his ten thousand troops can stand against the twenty thousand coming at him?

[32] If he cannot, he sends a delegation while the other king is still far off and sues for peace.

[33] In the same way, none of you can be my disciple unless you give up everything you have.

[34] Salt is good. But if salt loses its bite, how will you season it again?

[35] It is useless — no good for the soil, no good for the manure pile. People just throw it out. If you have ears, listen."

[1] The tax collectors and the moral wreckage of society kept pressing in to hear him. [2] The Pharisees and scribes muttered to each other: "This man welcomes sinners. He eats with them."

[3] So he told them a story.

[4] "Suppose one of you has a hundred sheep and loses one. Don't you leave the ninety-nine in open country and go after the lost one until you find it? [5] And when you find it, you hoist it onto your shoulders, thrilled. [6] You get home and call your friends and neighbors over: 'Celebrate with me! I found my lost sheep!' [7] I'm telling you, that's exactly what heaven looks like when one sinner turns around — more joy over that one than over ninety-nine upstanding people who figure they don't need to.

[8] "Or imagine a woman with ten silver coins. She loses one. Doesn't she light a lamp, sweep the house, hunt every corner until she finds it? [9] And when she does, she gathers her friends and neighbors: 'Celebrate with me! I found the coin I lost!' [10] I'm telling you, that's exactly the joy among God's angels over one sinner turning around."

[11] Then he said: "A man had two sons. [12] The younger one came to him and said, 'Father, give me my share of the estate now.' So the father divided his property between them.

[13] "Not long after, the younger son cashed everything in, took off for a distant country, and burned through it all on reckless living. [14] When he'd spent every last coin, a brutal famine swept that country, and he started to go hungry. [15] He latched on to one of the local landowners, who sent him out to feed pigs. [16] He was so desperate he would have gladly filled his stomach with the slop the pigs were eating. Nobody gave him a thing.

[17] "Then he came to his senses. 'How many of my father's hired hands have food to spare, and here I am dying of hunger? [18] I'm getting up. I'm going back to my father. I'll tell him: Father, I've sinned against heaven and against you. [19] I don't deserve to be called your son anymore. Just take me on as a hired hand.'

[20] "So he got up and went back to his father.

"But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him. Something broke open inside the old man — he took off running, threw his arms around his son, and kissed him.

[21] "The son started his speech: 'Father, I've sinned against heaven and against you. I don't deserve to be called your son —'

[22] "But the father was already shouting to the servants: 'Quick! Bring the best robe — put it on him. A ring for his finger. Sandals for his feet. [23] Get the fattened calf — slaughter it. We're going to eat. We're going to celebrate. [24] Because this son of mine was dead, and he's alive. He was lost, and he's found.'

"And they started the party.

[25] "Meanwhile the older son was out in the field. As he came in and got near the house, he heard music and dancing. [26] He grabbed one of the servants and asked what was going on. [27] 'Your brother's home,' the servant said. 'Your father killed the fattened calf because he got him back safe and sound.'

[28] "The older brother went hot with rage and refused to go in.

"So his father came out and pleaded with him. [29] But he fired back: 'Look — all these years I've slaved for you. I've never disobeyed a single order. And you never gave me so much as a young goat to celebrate with my friends. [30] But this son of yours shows up — the one who devoured your estate on prostitutes — and for him you kill the fattened calf?'

[31] "'Son,' the father said, 'you're always with me. Everything I have is yours. [32] But we had to celebrate. We had to. Your brother was dead, and he's alive. He was lost, and he's found.'"

[1] Jesus turned to his disciples and said: "A certain rich man had a manager, and word reached him that this manager was burning through his assets.

[2] So he summoned him and said, 'What's this I keep hearing about you? Hand over the books. You're done managing my estate.'

[3] The manager thought to himself, 'What now? My boss is firing me. I can't dig — too weak. I won't beg — too proud.

[4] I know exactly what to do — something that'll get me welcomed into people's homes once I'm out on the street.'

[5] So he called in his master's debtors one by one. To the first he said, 'What do you owe?'

[6] 'A hundred jugs of oil,' the man said. 'Take your bill,' the manager said. 'Sit down. Make it fifty.'

[7] To the next: 'And you — what do you owe?' 'A hundred sacks of wheat.' 'Take your bill. Make it eighty.'

[8] And the master actually commended the dishonest manager for his shrewdness. Because here's the thing — the children of this age handle their own kind with more cunning than the children of light handle theirs.

[9] So I'm telling you: use the tainted money of this world to make friends — so that when it finally fails you, they'll welcome you into homes that last forever.

[10] Whoever can be trusted with little can be trusted with much. Whoever cheats in small things will cheat in large ones.

[11] If you can't be trusted with dirty money, who's going to trust you with what's real?

[12] If you can't be trusted with what belongs to someone else, who's going to give you anything of your own?

[13] No servant can serve two masters. Either he'll hate one and love the other, or he'll cling to one and despise the other. You cannot serve God and money."

[14] The Pharisees — money-lovers, all of them — were listening to this and sneering at him.

[15] So he turned on them: "You're the ones who polish your image in front of people. But God sees straight through to your hearts. What humans exalt, God finds repulsive.

[16] The Law and the Prophets ran until John. Since then, the kingdom of God is being announced, and everyone is forcing their way in.

[17] But it's easier for heaven and earth to vanish than for a single stroke of the Law to fall.

[18] Anyone who divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery. And the man who marries a woman divorced from her husband — adultery too.

[19] There was a rich man. Dressed in purple and fine linen. Feasting in luxury every single day.

[20] And at his gate, a beggar named Lazarus had been dumped — body covered in sores,

[21] desperate for the scraps falling from the rich man's table. Even the dogs came and licked his wounds.

[22] Then the beggar died, and the angels carried him to Abraham's side. The rich man also died, and was buried.

[23] In Hades, tormented, he looked up — and there, far in the distance, was Abraham, with Lazarus beside him.

[24] He cried out, 'Father Abraham! Have mercy on me! Send Lazarus to dip the tip of his finger in water and cool my tongue — I'm in agony in this fire!'

[25] But Abraham said, 'Child, remember — you got your good things during your lifetime. Lazarus got nothing but bad. Now he's comforted here, and you're in torment.

[26] Besides all that, there's a massive chasm fixed between us and you. No one can cross from here to there, and no one from there to here.'

[27] He said, 'Then I'm begging you, Father — send him to my father's house.

[28] I have five brothers. Let him warn them, so they don't end up in this place of torment too.'

[29] Abraham answered, 'They have Moses and the Prophets. Let them listen to them.'

[30] 'No, Father Abraham,' he said, 'but if someone goes to them from the dead, they'll repent.'

[31] Abraham said to him, 'If they won't listen to Moses and the Prophets, they won't be convinced even if someone rises from the dead.'"

[1] Jesus said to his disciples, "Traps are inevitable — they're going to come. But hell waits for the one who sets them. [2] Better to have a millstone strapped around your neck and be dumped in the sea than to trip up one of these little ones. [3] Watch yourselves. If your brother sins, call him out. If he changes his heart, forgive him. [4] If he wrongs you seven times in a day and seven times comes back saying, 'I was wrong' — forgive him. Every time."

[5] The apostles said to the Lord, "Give us more faith." [6] He answered, "If your faith were even the size of a mustard seed, you could tell this mulberry tree, 'Rip yourself up and plant yourself in the sea' — and it would obey you.

[7] "Imagine you have a servant out plowing or tending sheep. When he comes in from the field, do you say, 'Come sit down, dinner's ready'? [8] No — you tell him, 'Get my meal ready. Put on your apron and serve me. After I've eaten and drunk, then you can eat.' [9] Do you thank the servant for doing what he was told to do? [10] Same with you. When you've done everything you were commanded, say this: 'We're unworthy servants. We only did our job.'"

[11] On the road to Jerusalem, Jesus traveled along the border between Samaria and Galilee. [12] As he entered a village, ten men with leprosy met him. They kept their distance [13] and shouted, "Jesus! Master! Have mercy on us!" [14] He looked at them and said, "Go. Show yourselves to the priests." And as they went — they were healed. Clean.

[15] One of them, the moment he saw he was healed, spun around praising God at the top of his lungs. [16] He threw himself face-down at Jesus' feet, thanking him. He was a Samaritan.

[17] Jesus said, "Weren't there ten? Where are the other nine? [18] Not one came back to give glory to God — except this foreigner?" [19] Then he told the man, "Get up. Go. Your faith has made you whole."

[20] The Pharisees asked him when the kingdom of God was coming. He answered, "The kingdom of God doesn't arrive with a parade you can watch. [21] No one's going to point and say, 'Look, here it is!' or 'There!' Because the kingdom of God is already among you."

[22] He turned to the disciples: "Days are coming when you'll ache to see just one of the days of the Son of Man — and you won't. [23] People will tell you, 'There he is!' or 'Here he is!' Don't go. Don't chase it. [24] Because when the Son of Man comes, it'll be like lightning — a flash that splits the sky from one side to the other. [25] But first, he has to suffer terribly. He has to be rejected by this generation.

[26] "It'll be like the days of Noah when the Son of Man comes. [27] People ate, drank, married, gave their kids away in marriage — right up to the day Noah walked into the ark. Then the flood came and swept them all away. [28] Same as the days of Lot. People ate, drank, bought, sold, planted, built. [29] The day Lot walked out of Sodom, fire and sulfur rained from the sky and wiped them all out. [30] That's how it'll be the day the Son of Man is unveiled.

[31] "On that day — if you're up on the roof and your things are in the house, don't climb down to grab them. If you're out in the field, don't turn back. [32] Remember Lot's wife. [33] Whoever clutches at his life will lose it. Whoever lets it go will keep it. [34] I'm telling you, that night two people will be in one bed — one taken, one left. [35] Two women will be grinding grain together — one taken, one left." [37] They asked him, "Where, Lord?" He said, "Where the corpse is, the vultures gather."

[1] Then he told them a story to drive home one point: keep praying and never cave in.

[2] "In a certain town there was a judge who didn't fear God and didn't care what anyone thought of him.

[3] And there was a widow in that town who kept showing up at his door, saying, 'Give me justice against the man who's wronged me.'

[4] For a long stretch he refused. But finally he said to himself, 'I don't fear God and I don't care about people,

[5] but this widow is wearing me out. I'll grant her justice just so she'll stop hammering at me.'"

[6] Then the Lord said, "Listen to what the corrupt judge said.

[7] So will God not bring justice for his chosen ones who cry out to him day and night? Will he keep them waiting?

[8] I tell you, he'll bring them justice — and fast. But when the Son of Man arrives, will he find any faith still burning on the earth?"

[9] He aimed this next one at people who were dead certain of their own righteousness and looked down on everyone else.

[10] "Two men went up to the temple to pray — one a Pharisee, the other a tax collector.

[11] The Pharisee stood front and center and prayed to himself like this: 'God, thank you that I'm not like the rest of them — the crooks, the cheats, the adulterers — or even like this tax collector here.

[12] I fast twice a week. I give a tenth of everything I earn.'

[13] But the tax collector stood at a distance. He wouldn't even lift his eyes. He just beat his chest and said, 'God, have mercy on me. I'm a sinner.'

[14] I'm telling you — that man went home right with God. The other didn't. Because anyone who lifts himself up will be brought low, and anyone who lowers himself will be lifted."

[15] People were even bringing infants for him to touch. When the disciples saw it, they tried to shoo them off.

[16] But Jesus called the children over and said, "Let them come to me. Don't stop them. The kingdom of God belongs to people like these.

[17] I'm telling you the truth — anyone who won't welcome the kingdom of God like a child will never set foot in it."

[18] A certain ruler asked him, "Good teacher, what do I have to do to inherit eternal life?"

[19] Jesus said, "Why do you call me good? No one is good — only God.

[20] You know the commandments: don't commit adultery, don't murder, don't steal, don't lie, honor your father and mother."

[21] "All of these," he said, "I've kept since I was a boy."

[22] When Jesus heard this, he told him, "One thing still missing. Sell everything you have. Give it to the poor. You'll have treasure in heaven. Then come, follow me."

[23] When the man heard this, his face fell. He was extremely wealthy.

[24] Jesus watched him and said, "How hard it is for those with money to enter the kingdom of God.

[25] It's easier to thread a camel through the eye of a needle than for a rich man to enter the kingdom of God."

[26] The ones listening said, "Then who in the world can be saved?"

[27] He said, "What's impossible for humans is possible for God."

[28] Peter said, "Look — we've walked away from everything we had to follow you."

[29] Jesus said, "I'm telling you the truth: nobody who has left house or wife or brothers or parents or children for the sake of the kingdom of God

[30] will fail to receive many times more in this present age — and in the age to come, eternal life."

[31] He pulled the Twelve aside and told them, "Listen — we're heading up to Jerusalem. Everything the prophets wrote about the Son of Man is about to come true.

[32] He'll be handed over to the Gentiles. They'll mock him. They'll insult him. They'll spit on him.

[33] They'll flog him and they'll kill him. And on the third day he'll rise."

[34] They didn't get any of it. The meaning was hidden from them. They had no idea what he was talking about.

[35] As he was approaching Jericho, a blind man was sitting by the road, begging.

[36] When he heard the crowd passing, he asked what was going on.

[37] They told him, "Jesus of Nazareth is going by."

[38] He shouted out, "Jesus, Son of David — have mercy on me!"

[39] The people in front told him to shut up. He only shouted louder: "Son of David — have mercy on me!"

[40] Jesus stopped and ordered the man brought to him. When he got close, Jesus asked,

[41] "What do you want me to do for you?" He said, "Lord — I want to see."

[42] Jesus said, "See, then. Your faith has healed you."

[43] Instantly he could see. He followed Jesus, glorifying God. And everyone who saw it broke into praise to God.

[1] Jesus entered Jericho and was passing through. [2] There was a man there named Zacchaeus — chief tax collector, and rich. [3] He was desperate to see who Jesus was, but he couldn't — the crowd was too thick, and he was too short. [4] So he sprinted ahead and climbed a sycamore-fig tree to catch a glimpse, because Jesus was about to pass that way.

[5] When Jesus reached the spot, he looked up and said, "Zacchaeus, get down here — fast. I'm staying at your house today." [6] Zacchaeus scrambled down and welcomed him, beaming. [7] The crowd saw it and started muttering: "He's gone to be the guest of a sinner."

[8] But Zacchaeus stood his ground and said to the Lord, "Look — half of everything I own, Lord, I'm giving to the poor. And anyone I've cheated, I'm paying back four times over." [9] Jesus said to him, "Salvation walked through this door today. This man too is a son of Abraham. [10] The Son of Man came to find what was lost — and bring it home."

[11] They were still listening when he launched into a parable, because he was close to Jerusalem and people assumed the kingdom of God was about to detonate on the spot. [12] He said: "A nobleman set out for a distant country to receive royal authority and return. [13] He called ten of his servants, gave each one a mina, and said, 'Put this to work until I get back.'

[14] But his countrymen hated him. They sent a delegation after him to say, 'We refuse to have this man rule over us.' [15] He received the kingdom anyway and came home. He summoned the servants he had entrusted with money to see what each had earned. [16] The first stepped up: 'Master, your mina has produced ten more.' [17] 'Excellent work, good servant,' he said. 'Because you were faithful in something small, take charge of ten cities.'

[18] The second came: 'Master, your mina made five more.' [19] He told him, 'You — take charge of five cities.' [20] Then another came forward: 'Master, here's your mina. I kept it tucked away in a cloth. [21] I was afraid of you — you're a hard man. You pick up what you didn't put down. You reap what you didn't sow.'

[22] The master said, 'I'll judge you by your own words, you worthless servant. So you knew I was a hard man, picking up what I didn't put down, reaping what I didn't sow? [23] Then why didn't you at least deposit my money with the bankers? I'd have collected it back with interest.'

[24] He turned to those standing by: 'Take the mina from him and give it to the one with ten.' [25] They said, 'Master — he already has ten.' [26] 'I'm telling you — everyone who has will be given more. From the one who has nothing, even what he has will be stripped away. [27] And those enemies of mine who didn't want me to reign over them — bring them here and execute them in front of me.'"

[28] After this, Jesus pushed on, climbing toward Jerusalem.

[29] As he came near Bethphage and Bethany, at the hill called the Mount of Olives, he sent two disciples ahead [30] with these instructions: "Go to the village over there. The moment you enter, you'll find a colt tied up — one no one has ever ridden. Untie it and bring it. [31] If anyone asks why you're untying it, just say, 'The Lord needs it.'"

[32] The ones he sent went and found everything exactly as he'd said. [33] As they were untying the colt, its owners said, "Why are you untying our colt?" [34] They said, "The Lord needs it." [35] They brought it to Jesus, threw their cloaks over it, and lifted him on.

[36] As he rode, people spread their cloaks across the road. [37] When he reached the descent of the Mount of Olives, the entire crowd of disciples erupted, praising God at full volume for every miracle they'd seen, [38] shouting:

"Blessed is the King
   who comes in the name of the Lord!
Peace in heaven,
   and glory in the highest!"

[39] Some Pharisees in the crowd said, "Teacher, shut your disciples up." [40] He answered, "I'm telling you — if these people went silent, the stones themselves would start screaming."

[41] As he got closer and saw the city, he broke down and wept over it. [42] "If only you had known — even you, even today — what would bring you peace. But now it's hidden from your eyes. [43] The days are coming when your enemies will throw up siege works against you, surround you, hem you in from every side. [44] They will smash you to the ground — you and your children inside you. They won't leave one stone stacked on another. Because you did not recognize the hour when God came to you."

[45] Then he walked into the temple and began driving out the merchants, [46] telling them, "Scripture says, 'My house will be a house of prayer.' You've turned it into a hideout for thieves."

[47] Every day he was teaching in the temple. The chief priests, the scribes, and the leading men of the people were trying to kill him, [48] but they couldn't figure out how — the people were hanging on every word.

[1] One day, as he was teaching the people in the temple courts and announcing the good news, the chief priests and the scribes came at him, along with the elders. [2] "Tell us," they demanded, "by what authority are you doing these things? Who gave you this authority?"

[3] He answered, "Let me ask you one thing. Tell me: [4] John's baptism — was it from heaven, or from human hands?"

[5] They huddled. "If we say 'from heaven,' he'll say, 'Then why didn't you believe him?' [6] But if we say 'from human hands,' the people will stone us — they're convinced John was a prophet."

[7] So they answered that they didn't know where it came from.

[8] "Then I'm not telling you either," Jesus said, "by what authority I do what I do."

[9] He turned to the people and told them this story: "A man planted a vineyard, leased it to tenant farmers, and went away on a long journey. [10] When harvest time came, he sent a servant to collect his share of the fruit. The tenants beat him and sent him back empty-handed. [11] So he sent another. They beat him too, humiliated him, sent him away with nothing. [12] He sent a third. They wounded him and threw him out.

[13] "The owner of the vineyard said, 'What do I do now? I'll send my son — the one I love. Surely they'll respect him.'

[14] "But when the tenants saw him, they conspired. 'This is the heir,' they said. 'Kill him, and the inheritance is ours.' [15] So they dragged him out of the vineyard and killed him.

"Now — what will the owner of the vineyard do to them? [16] He'll come. He'll destroy those tenants. And he'll give the vineyard to others."

When they heard it, they said, "God forbid!"

[17] But he locked eyes with them and said, "Then what does this Scripture mean —

*The stone the builders threw away
became the cornerstone*?

[18] Everyone who falls on that stone will be broken to pieces. And anyone it falls on — it will crush to dust."

[19] The scribes and chief priests wanted to seize him right there — they knew the story was aimed at them — but they were afraid of the crowd.

[20] So they watched him. They sent spies pretending to be sincere, hoping to catch him in something he said — something they could use to hand him over to the governor's authority. [21] They approached him. "Teacher," they said, "we know you speak and teach what's right. You don't play favorites. You teach the way of God honestly. [22] So — is it lawful for us to pay taxes to Caesar, or not?"

[23] He saw straight through them. [24] "Show me a denarius. Whose face is on it? Whose name?"

"Caesar's," they said.

[25] "Then give Caesar what belongs to Caesar," he said, "and give God what belongs to God."

[26] They couldn't trap him in anything he said in front of the people. Stunned by his answer, they fell silent.

[27] Then some Sadducees came up — the ones who say there's no resurrection — and they put a question to him: [28] "Teacher, Moses wrote that if a man's brother dies leaving a widow but no children, the man must marry her and raise up offspring for his brother. [29] Well — there were seven brothers. The first married a woman and died childless. [30] The second [31] and then the third married her, and so on — all seven, and none of them left children. They all died. [32] Finally the woman died too. [33] So at the resurrection — whose wife is she? All seven had her."

[34] Jesus said, "The people of this age marry and are given in marriage. [35] But those counted worthy of the age to come and the resurrection from the dead — they don't marry, and they aren't given in marriage. [36] They can't die anymore. They're like the angels. They're children of God, children of the resurrection.

[37] "And as for the dead being raised — even Moses showed it, at the bush, when he called the Lord *the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, the God of Jacob.* [38] He is not the God of the dead but of the living. To him, they are all alive."

[39] Some of the scribes spoke up: "Teacher, well said." [40] After that, no one dared ask him anything else.

[41] Then he turned the question on them. "How is it that people say the Messiah is David's son? [42] David himself says in the book of Psalms,

*The Lord said to my Lord,
Sit at my right hand
[43] until I make your enemies
a footstool under your feet.*

[44] David calls him *Lord*. So how is he his son?"

[45] With all the people listening, he said to his disciples, [46] "Watch out for the scribes. They love parading around in long robes. They love being greeted in the marketplace, taking the best seats in the synagogues, the places of honor at banquets. [47] They devour widows' houses and dress it up with long prayers. Their judgment will be heavier than anyone's."

[1] He looked up and watched the wealthy dropping their gifts into the temple treasury. [2] Then he saw a destitute widow drop in two small copper coins. [3] "I'm telling you the truth," he said, "this poor widow has out-given them all. [4] They gave from their surplus. She gave from her emptiness — everything she had to live on."

[5] Some were marveling at the temple — the magnificent stonework, the offerings dedicated to God. He cut in: [6] "Take a long look. The day is coming when not one of these stones will be left stacked on another. Every last one will be thrown down."

[7] "Teacher," they pressed him, "when? What's the sign that it's about to happen?"

[8] "Don't let anyone fool you," he said. "Many will come wearing my name, saying 'I'm the one' and 'The time is here.' Don't chase them. [9] When you hear of wars and revolutions, don't panic. These things have to happen first — but the end isn't immediate."

[10] He went on: "Nation will rise against nation, kingdom against kingdom. [11] Massive earthquakes. Famines. Plagues, region after region. Terrors and signs blazing across the sky.

[12] "But before any of that, they'll come for you. They'll seize you, persecute you, drag you into synagogues and prisons, haul you before kings and governors — all because of my name. [13] That's your moment to testify. [14] So settle it in your minds: don't rehearse your defense. [15] I'll give you words and wisdom that not one of your opponents will be able to contradict or resist. [16] Your own parents, brothers, relatives, friends — they'll turn you in. Some of you they'll kill. [17] Everyone will hate you because of me. [18] And yet — not a hair of your head will be lost. [19] By your endurance, you'll gain your souls.

[20] "When you see Jerusalem ringed by armies, know this: her devastation is at the door. [21] Then anyone in Judea — run for the hills. Anyone in the city — get out. Anyone in the countryside — don't go back in. [22] These are the days of reckoning, every word of Scripture coming due. [23] God help the pregnant and the nursing mothers in those days. Anguish will crush this land, wrath will fall on this people. [24] They'll fall to the sword. They'll be marched off as captives to every nation. And Jerusalem will be trampled by the Gentiles until the Gentiles' time runs out.

[25] "There will be signs in the sun, the moon, the stars. On earth, nations will writhe in dread, bewildered by the roaring and surging of the sea. [26] People will faint from terror, bracing for what's coming on the world — because the powers of the heavens will be shaken. [27] And then they will see the Son of Man arriving in a cloud, with power and blinding glory. [28] When this begins to unfold, stand up. Lift your heads. Your liberation is near."

[29] He gave them a picture: "Look at the fig tree, look at any tree. [30] When the leaves push out, you see it for yourselves — summer's almost here. [31] Same with this. When you see these things happening, know that God's kingdom is right at the door.

[32] "I'm telling you the truth — this generation will not pass away until it has all happened. [33] Heaven and earth will pass away. My words will not.

[34] "Stay sharp. Don't let your hearts get sluggish with hangovers, hangups, and the worries of life — or that Day will spring on you [35] like a trap. It's coming for everyone on the face of the earth. [36] Stay awake. Pray constantly for the strength to come through everything that's about to break loose, and to stand before the Son of Man."

[37] By day he taught in the temple. By night he'd go out and stay on the Mount of Olives. [38] And every morning at dawn the people came streaming to him in the temple to hear him.

[1] Passover was closing in — the Feast of Unleavened Bread, as people called it.

[2] The chief priests and the scribes were hunting for a way to kill him, but they were afraid of the crowd.

[3] Then Satan slipped into Judas — the one called Iscariot, one of the Twelve.

[4] He went off and cut a deal with the chief priests and the temple guard captains: how he could hand Jesus over.

[5] They were thrilled. They promised him money.

[6] He agreed. From that moment on, he was watching for the right moment — a moment when the crowd wouldn't be around.

[7] Then came the day of Unleavened Bread, when the Passover lamb had to be slaughtered.

[8] Jesus sent Peter and John ahead: "Go get the Passover ready for us. We're going to eat together."

[9] "Where do you want us to set it up?" they asked.

[10] He said, "Listen — when you walk into the city, a man carrying a water jar will cross your path. Follow him. Whatever house he enters,

[11] tell the owner, 'The Teacher asks: Where's the guest room where I can eat the Passover with my disciples?'

[12] He'll show you a large upstairs room, already furnished. Set it up there."

[13] They went. Everything was exactly as he said. So they got the Passover ready.

[14] When the hour came, he took his place at the table, and the apostles with him.

[15] He said to them, "I have ached to eat this Passover with you before I suffer.

[16] Because I'm telling you — I will not eat it again until it finds its fulfillment in the kingdom of God."

[17] He took a cup, gave thanks, and said, "Take this. Pass it among yourselves.

[18] I tell you, from now on I won't drink the fruit of the vine until the kingdom of God arrives."

[19] Then he took bread, gave thanks, broke it, and handed it to them, saying, "This is my body, given for you. Do this in remembrance of me."

[20] After the meal he did the same with the cup: "This cup is the new covenant in my blood — poured out for you.

[21] But look — the hand of the one betraying me is right here on this table with mine.

[22] The Son of Man goes the way that's been written for him. But God help the man who hands him over."

[23] They started questioning each other — who among them could possibly do such a thing?

[24] Then they slid into an argument about which of them was the greatest.

[25] Jesus said, "The kings of the Gentiles lord it over their people. Their power brokers get themselves called 'Benefactors.'

[26] Not you. With you, the greatest acts like the youngest. The leader acts like the servant.

[27] Who's greater — the one reclining at the table, or the one serving the meal? The one at the table, obviously. But I'm here among you as the one serving.

[28] You've stood by me through everything I've been through.

[29] So I confer on you a kingdom — just as my Father conferred one on me —

[30] that you'll eat and drink at my table in my kingdom, seated on thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel."

[31] "Simon, Simon — Satan has demanded to have you all, to sift you like wheat.

[32] But I have prayed for you, Simon — that your faith won't collapse. And when you turn back, strengthen your brothers."

[33] Peter said, "Lord, I'm ready to go with you — to prison, to death."

[34] Jesus said, "I'm telling you, Peter — before the rooster crows today, you will deny three times that you know me."

[35] Then he said to them, "When I sent you out with no purse, no bag, no sandals — did you lack anything?"

"Nothing," they said.

[36] "But now," he said, "if you have a purse, take it. Same with a bag. And if you don't have a sword, sell your cloak and buy one.

[37] Because I'm telling you — this scripture has to be fulfilled in me: 'He was counted among the lawless.' What's written about me is reaching its end."

[38] They said, "Lord — look, here are two swords."

He said, "Enough."

[39] He went out and headed, as usual, to the Mount of Olives. The disciples followed.

[40] When they reached the place, he said, "Pray. Pray that you don't fall into the test."

[41] He pulled away from them, a stone's throw, dropped to his knees, and prayed —

[42] "Father, if you're willing, take this cup away from me. But not what I want. What you want."

[43] An angel from heaven appeared and strengthened him.

[44] In agony he prayed harder. His sweat became like drops of blood falling to the ground.

[45] He got up from prayer, came back to the disciples, and found them asleep — exhausted from grief.

[46] "Why are you sleeping?" he said. "Get up. Pray, so you don't fall into the test."

[47] He was still speaking when a crowd showed up — and there at the front was Judas, one of the Twelve. He stepped up to Jesus to kiss him.

[48] Jesus said, "Judas — with a kiss? You're handing over the Son of Man with a kiss?"

[49] The men around him saw what was coming and said, "Lord, should we strike with the sword?"

[50] One of them did — slashed the high priest's slave and cut off his right ear.

[51] "Stop. No more of this," Jesus said. He touched the man's ear and healed him.

[52] Then he turned to the chief priests, the temple captains, the elders who had come for him — "You came out with swords and clubs as if I were some bandit?

[53] Day after day I was right there with you in the temple. You never lifted a hand. But this — this is your hour. The hour when darkness gets its way."

[54] They seized him and led him away, into the high priest's house. Peter followed — at a distance.

[55] They built a fire in the middle of the courtyard and sat around it. Peter sat with them.

[56] A servant girl saw him in the firelight, stared, and said, "This man was with him."

[57] He denied it. "Woman, I don't know him."

[58] A little later someone else looked at him and said, "You're one of them too."

"Man, I'm not," Peter said.

[59] About an hour passed. Another man pressed it: "No question — this guy was with him. He's a Galilean."

[60] Peter said, "Man, I don't know what you're talking about." And right then, while the words were still in his mouth, a rooster crowed.

[61] The Lord turned and looked at Peter. And Peter remembered the word the Lord had spoken to him: "Before the rooster crows today, you will deny me three times."

[62] He went outside and broke. He wept bitterly.

[63] The men holding Jesus started mocking him, beating him.

[64] They blindfolded him and kept demanding, "Prophesy! Who hit you?"

[65] And on and on with the insults.

[66] When daylight came, the council of elders — chief priests and scribes — gathered, and they hauled him before their court.

[67] "If you're the Messiah," they said, "tell us."

He said, "If I tell you, you won't believe me.

[68] And if I ask you anything, you won't answer.

[69] But from now on, the Son of Man will be seated at the right hand of the power of God."

[70] They all said, "So you're the Son of God?"

He said, "You're the ones saying it. I am."

[71] They said, "What more testimony do we need? We've heard it ourselves, from his own mouth."

[1] The whole assembly rose as one and marched him to Pilate.

[2] They opened the case against him: "We caught this man corrupting our nation — telling people not to pay taxes to Caesar, claiming to be Messiah, a king."

[3] Pilate asked him, "Are you the king of the Jews?"

"You said it," Jesus answered.

[4] Pilate turned to the chief priests and the crowd: "I find nothing criminal in this man."

[5] But they kept pressing: "He stirs up the people with his teaching everywhere in Judea — started in Galilee, made his way here."

[6] At the word Galilee, Pilate's ears perked up.

[7] When he learned Jesus belonged to Herod's jurisdiction, he sent him over — Herod happened to be in Jerusalem those days.

[8] Herod was delighted. He'd wanted to see Jesus for ages — heard all about him, hoped to catch a miracle.

[9] He fired off question after question. Jesus said nothing.

[10] The chief priests and scribes stood there, hammering accusations.

[11] So Herod and his soldiers turned it into theater — mocking him, dressing him in royal finery, then shipping him back to Pilate.

[12] That day Herod and Pilate became friends. Before, they had been enemies.

[13] Pilate gathered the chief priests, the rulers, the people,

[14] and addressed them: "You brought me this man as a revolutionary. I examined him in front of you and found nothing — none of your charges stick.

[15] Neither did Herod — he sent him back. Look at him. He's done nothing to deserve death.

[16] I'll have him flogged and release him."

[17] —

[18] But the whole crowd erupted: "Get rid of him! Give us Barabbas!"

[19] (Barabbas had been thrown in prison for a riot in the city and for murder.)

[20] Pilate tried again — he wanted to release Jesus —

[21] but they kept shouting him down: "Crucify him! Crucify him!"

[22] A third time he pleaded: "Why? What has he done wrong? I find nothing in him that warrants death. I'll flog him and release him."

[23] But they pressed harder, voices rising, demanding crucifixion — and their shouting won.

[24] Pilate ruled in their favor.

[25] He released the man they wanted — the one jailed for insurrection and murder — and handed Jesus over to their will.

[26] As they led him out, they grabbed a man named Simon, from Cyrene, coming in from the countryside, and loaded the cross on his back to carry behind Jesus.

[27] A huge crowd followed — including women beating their chests and wailing for him.

[28] Jesus turned to them: "Daughters of Jerusalem, don't weep for me. Weep for yourselves and your children.

[29] Days are coming when people will say, 'Blessed are the barren — wombs that never carried, breasts that never nursed.'

[30] They'll beg the mountains, 'Fall on us,' and the hills, 'Bury us.'

[31] If they do this to the green wood, what happens to the dry?"

[32] Two others — criminals — were led out with him for execution.

[33] When they reached the place called The Skull, they crucified him there, and the criminals — one on his right, one on his left.

[34] Jesus said, "Father, forgive them. They don't know what they're doing." And they divided his clothes, casting lots.

[35] The people stood watching. The rulers sneered: "He saved others. Let him save himself, if he really is God's Chosen — the Messiah."

[36] The soldiers joined the mockery, coming up to offer him sour wine,

[37] taunting, "If you're the king of the Jews, save yourself!"

[38] Above him hung the inscription: THIS IS THE KING OF THE JEWS.

[39] One of the crucified criminals spat insults at him: "Aren't you the Messiah? Save yourself — and us!"

[40] But the other rebuked him: "Have you no fear of God? You're under the same sentence.

[41] We deserve this — we're getting what our actions earned. But this man has done nothing wrong."

[42] Then he said, "Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom."

[43] Jesus answered, "I promise you — today you'll be with me in paradise."

[44] By now it was about noon, and darkness swallowed the whole land until three in the afternoon —

[45] the sun extinguished. The curtain in the temple ripped down the middle.

[46] Jesus cried out with a loud voice: "Father, into your hands I commit my spirit." And with that, he was gone.

[47] The centurion, seeing what had happened, gave glory to God: "This man really was innocent."

[48] The crowds who had gathered for the spectacle saw what unfolded and went home pounding their chests.

[49] All who knew him — including the women who had followed him from Galilee — stood at a distance, watching.

[50] There was a man named Joseph — a council member, a good and upright man,

[51] who hadn't agreed with their decision or their actions. He was from Arimathea, a Jewish town, and he was waiting for the kingdom of God.

[52] He went to Pilate and asked for Jesus' body.

[53] He took it down, wrapped it in linen, and laid it in a tomb cut from rock — one no one had ever been laid in.

[54] It was the day of Preparation. Sabbath was breathing down on them.

[55] The women who had come with him from Galilee followed — they saw the tomb, saw how his body was placed.

[56] Then they went home and prepared spices and perfumes. And on the Sabbath they rested, as the commandment said.

[1] At the first hint of dawn on the first day of the week, they came to the tomb carrying the spices they'd prepared. [2] The stone — rolled away from the entrance. [3] They stepped inside. No body. The Lord Jesus was gone.

[4] While they stood there, baffled, two men suddenly appeared beside them in clothing that blazed like lightning. [5] The women dropped their faces to the ground, terrified. The men said: "Why are you searching for the living among the dead? [6] He isn't here. He has risen. Remember what he told you back in Galilee — [7] that the Son of Man had to be handed over to sinners, crucified, and on the third day rise."

[8] Then they remembered his words.

[9] They ran from the tomb and told everything to the Eleven and to everyone else. [10] It was Mary Magdalene, Joanna, Mary the mother of James, and the other women with them who brought word to the apostles. [11] The apostles thought it was nonsense. They didn't believe a syllable.

[12] But Peter jumped up and ran to the tomb. He stooped, looked in, saw only the burial cloths lying there, and walked away amazed, wondering what on earth had happened.

[13] That same day, two of them were traveling to a village called Emmaus, about seven miles from Jerusalem. [14] They were talking it all over — everything that had just happened. [15] As they talked and argued back and forth, Jesus himself drew near and walked with them. [16] But something kept them from recognizing him.

[17] "What are you two discussing as you walk?" he asked.

They stopped, faces heavy. [18] One of them, Cleopas, answered: "Are you the only visitor in Jerusalem who hasn't heard what's happened these last few days?"

[19] "What things?" he said.

"About Jesus of Nazareth," they said. "A prophet, powerful in word and deed before God and the whole nation. [20] Our chief priests and rulers handed him over to be condemned to death and crucified him. [21] We were hoping he was the one who would set Israel free. And now it's the third day since all this. [22] Some women from our group rattled us this morning. They went to the tomb at dawn [23] and didn't find his body. They came back saying they'd seen a vision of angels who said he was alive. [24] Some of our men went to the tomb and found it just as the women said. But him — they didn't see."

[25] Then he said to them: "How thick you are. How slow your hearts are to trust everything the prophets said. [26] Wasn't it necessary for the Messiah to suffer all this and then enter his glory?" [27] And starting with Moses and working through all the Prophets, he opened up for them everything in the Scriptures that pointed to himself.

[28] As they neared the village, he made as if to keep going. [29] But they pressed him: "Stay with us. It's nearly evening. The day is almost gone." So he went in to stay with them.

[30] At the table, he took the bread, blessed it, broke it, and handed it to them — [31] and their eyes flew open. They recognized him. And he vanished from their sight.

[32] They turned to each other: "Weren't our hearts burning inside us while he talked with us on the road, while he opened up the Scriptures?"

[33] They got up that same hour and went back to Jerusalem. They found the Eleven and the others gathered together, [34] all saying: "It's true. The Lord has risen. He appeared to Simon." [35] Then the two told their own story — what happened on the road, how they recognized him when he broke the bread.

[36] While they were still talking, Jesus himself stood among them. "Peace to you," he said.

[37] They were terrified — convinced they were seeing a ghost. [38] He said: "Why are you shaken? Why these doubts crawling through your hearts? [39] Look at my hands. Look at my feet. It's me. Touch me and see. A ghost doesn't have flesh and bones — and as you can see, I do."

[40] He showed them his hands and his feet.

[41] They still couldn't believe it — for sheer joy and amazement. So he said: "Got anything to eat?" [42] They handed him a piece of broiled fish, [43] and he took it and ate it right in front of them.

[44] Then he said: "This is what I told you while I was still with you — that everything written about me in the law of Moses, the Prophets, and the Psalms had to be fulfilled."

[45] Then he opened their minds to understand the Scriptures. [46] "This is what's written," he said. "The Messiah will suffer and rise from the dead on the third day, [47] and in his name a change of heart — and forgiveness of sins — will be announced to every nation, starting from Jerusalem. [48] You are the witnesses of all this. [49] And I am sending what my Father promised down on you. Stay in the city until you're clothed with power from on high."

[50] Then he led them out as far as Bethany, lifted his hands, and blessed them. [51] And as he was blessing them, he was carried away from them, lifted up into heaven. [52] They worshiped him and returned to Jerusalem with explosive joy. [53] And they spent their days in the temple, blessing God.

[1] A burden. The word of the LORD to Israel, delivered through Malachi.

[2] "I have loved you," says the LORD. And you answer: "Loved us? How?"

Wasn't Esau Jacob's brother? — the LORD's declaration. Yet I loved Jacob [3] and rejected Esau. I turned his mountains into wasteland, handed his inheritance over to the jackals of the desert.

[4] Edom may say, "We've been crushed, but we'll rebuild the ruins." The LORD of Armies answers: They can build. I will tear it down. They'll be known as the Wicked Territory, the people the LORD's anger never leaves. [5] You'll see it with your own eyes, and you'll say: "The LORD is great — even beyond the borders of Israel."

[6] A son honors his father. A servant honors his master. So if I am Father — where is my honor? If I am Master — where is my reverence? — the LORD of Armies asks the priests who treat my name like dirt.

And you say: "How have we treated your name like dirt?"

[7] By bringing defiled food to my altar.

And you say: "How have we defiled you?"

By saying the LORD's table is nothing worth respecting. [8] When you bring a blind animal to sacrifice — that's not wrong? When you bring the lame, the sick — that's not wrong? Try offering it to your governor! See if he's pleased. See if he accepts you. — says the LORD of Armies.

[9] So now — go on, beg God to be gracious to us. With offerings like these from your hands, why would he show any of you favor? — says the LORD of Armies.

[10] If only one of you would just shut the temple doors and stop lighting useless fires on my altar! I take no pleasure in you — says the LORD of Armies — and I will not accept an offering from your hands.

[11] From where the sun rises to where it sets, my name will be great among the nations. Everywhere, incense will rise to my name, and pure offerings — because my name will be great among the nations, says the LORD of Armies.

[12] But you — you profane it. You say the Lord's table is polluted, its food contemptible. [13] You say, "What a burden," and sniff at it in disgust — says the LORD of Armies. You drag in the stolen, the lame, the sick, and call it an offering. Should I take that from your hand? — says the LORD.

[14] Cursed is the cheat — who has a healthy male in his flock, vows it, then sacrifices the broken animal to the Lord. I am a great King — says the LORD of Armies — and my name is feared among the nations.

[1] Now hear this, priests — this command is for you.

[2] If you refuse to listen, if you refuse to take it to heart and give my name the weight it deserves, says the LORD of Armies, then I will unleash the curse on you. I will curse your blessings — in fact, I already have, because not one of you is taking this seriously.

[3] Watch what I do to your offspring. I will smear the dung of your festival sacrifices across your faces, and you will be carted off with the garbage.

[4] Then you will know I sent this warning so my covenant with Levi could stand, says the LORD of Armies.

[5] My covenant with him was life and peace, and I gave him both — gave him reverence, and he revered me. He stood in awe of my name.

[6] True instruction came from his mouth. No deceit on his lips. He walked with me in peace and integrity, and he turned many away from sin.

[7] A priest's lips should guard knowledge. People should come to him to learn — because he is the messenger of the LORD of Armies.

[8] But you — you walked off the path. Your teaching has tripped up countless people. You have shattered the covenant with Levi, says the LORD of Armies.

[9] So I have made you contemptible and humiliated before everyone — because you ignored my ways and played favorites in matters of the law.

[10] Don't we all have one Father? Didn't one God create us? Then why do we betray each other and trample the covenant of our ancestors?

[11] Judah has betrayed. An abomination has been committed in Israel and Jerusalem. Judah has defiled the sanctuary the LORD loves — by marrying the daughter of a foreign god.

[12] May the LORD cut off from the tents of Jacob every man who does this — anyone awake or alert — even if he's the one bringing offerings to the LORD of Armies.

[13] And here is the second thing you do: you drown the LORD's altar in tears. You weep and you wail because he no longer regards your offerings or accepts them from your hand.

[14] You ask why. Because the LORD witnessed it — the day you betrayed the wife of your youth. She was your partner. She was the wife of your covenant.

[15] Didn't the One make them one, with a portion of his Spirit in their union? And what does the One seek? Godly offspring. So guard yourselves. Do not betray the wife of your youth.

[16] "I hate divorce," says the LORD, the God of Israel. "I hate the man who cloaks himself in violence like a garment," says the LORD of Armies. So guard yourselves. Do not break faith.

[17] You have worn the LORD out with your words. You ask, "How have we worn him out?" By saying, "Anyone who does evil looks just fine to the LORD — he's delighted with them." Or by asking, "Where is the God of justice?"

[1] Watch — I am sending my messenger, and he will clear the road before me. Then suddenly the Lord you keep asking for will arrive at his temple. The messenger of the covenant, the one you say you want — he is coming, says the LORD of Armies.

[2] But who can endure the day he shows up? Who can stand when he appears? He comes like a refiner's furnace, like the harshest soap in a launderer's hands.

[3] He will take his seat as a smelter, working the silver, scouring the sons of Levi, burning them pure as gold and silver until they bring offerings to the LORD in righteousness again.

[4] Then the offerings of Judah and Jerusalem will please the LORD the way they did in the old days, the way they did in ancient years.

[5] I am coming for the trial myself — and I will be a swift witness against the sorcerers, the adulterers, the perjurers, against those who cheat their workers of wages, who crush the widow and the orphan, who shove the foreigner aside and have no fear of me, says the LORD of Armies.

[6] I am the LORD — I do not change. That is the only reason you children of Jacob are not already finished.

[7] From your fathers' day until now you have turned from my statutes and refused to keep them. Come back to me, and I will come back to you, says the LORD of Armies. But you ask, "Come back from what?"

[8] Will a man rob God? Yet you are robbing me. And you ask, "How are we robbing you?" In the tithes. In the offerings.

[9] You are under a curse — the whole nation of you — because you are robbing me.

[10] Bring the full tithe into the storehouse. Let there be food in my house. Test me on this, says the LORD of Armies — see if I do not throw open the floodgates of heaven and pour out a blessing until there is no room left to hold it.

[11] I will rebuke the devourer for your sake; it will not destroy the fruit of your fields, and your vines will not drop their grapes before harvest, says the LORD of Armies.

[12] Then every nation will call you blessed, because your land will be a delight, says the LORD of Armies.

[13] Your words have been brutal against me, says the LORD. And you ask, "What have we said against you?"

[14] You have said: "Serving God is pointless. What did we ever gain from keeping his commands, from walking around in mourning before the LORD of Armies?

[15] These days we call the arrogant blessed. The wicked thrive. They put God to the test and walk away untouched."

[16] Then those who feared the LORD spoke with one another, and the LORD bent close and listened. A scroll of remembrance was written in his presence — for those who fear the LORD and treasure his name.

[17] They will be mine, says the LORD of Armies, on the day I act — my own treasured possession. I will spare them the way a father spares the son who serves him.

[18] And once again you will see the difference — between the righteous and the wicked, between the one who serves God and the one who never did.

[1] Listen — the day is coming, blazing like a furnace. Every arrogant soul, every evildoer, will be stubble. That day will set them on fire, says the LORD of Armies — leaving them neither root nor branch.

[2] But for you who hold my name in awe, the sun of righteousness will rise with healing in its wings. You'll burst out and leap like calves released from the stall.

[3] You'll trample the wicked — they'll be ash beneath your feet on the day I act, says the LORD of Armies.

[4] Remember the Torah of Moses, my servant — the statutes and judgments I commanded him at Horeb for all Israel.

[5] Watch: I am sending you the prophet Elijah before the great and terrible day of the LORD arrives.

[6] He will turn the hearts of fathers back to their children, and the hearts of children back to their fathers — or I will come and strike the land with utter ruin.

[1] This is where it starts — the good news of Jesus, the Anointed One, the Son of God.

[2] The prophet Isaiah saw it coming: "Watch — I'm sending my messenger ahead of you, the one who will clear your path.

[3] A voice, shouting in the wilderness: Get the road ready for the Lord. Straighten every twist in it."

[4] And there he was — John, out in the wilderness, dunking people in water and calling them to turn their lives around so their sins could be wiped clean.

[5] All of Judea came streaming out to him. Everyone from Jerusalem. They confessed what they'd done, and he plunged them into the Jordan.

[6] John wore camel hair, cinched with a leather belt. He ate locusts and wild honey.

[7] His message: "Someone is coming after me who outranks me by a mile. I'm not even fit to kneel down and untie his sandals.

[8] I baptize you with water. He's going to baptize you with the Holy Spirit."

[9] That's when Jesus showed up — walking down from Nazareth in Galilee — and John baptized him in the Jordan.

[10] The moment Jesus came up out of the water, he saw the sky rip open and the Spirit descending on him like a dove.

[11] And a voice tore through from heaven: "You are my Son. The one I love. My joy is in you."

[12] Right away the Spirit drove him out into the wilderness.

[13] Forty days out there, with Satan testing him, wild animals all around — and angels taking care of him.

[14] After John was thrown in prison, Jesus came back to Galilee, announcing God's good news:

[15] "The time has arrived. God's kingdom is right here. Turn around. Trust this news."

[16] Walking along the Sea of Galilee, he spotted Simon and his brother Andrew throwing their nets into the water — they were fishermen.

[17] "Come with me," Jesus said. "I'll teach you to fish for people."

[18] They dropped their nets on the spot and went with him.

[19] A little further down the shore, he saw James son of Zebedee and his brother John, mending their nets in the boat.

[20] He called them. They left their father Zebedee right there with the hired hands and walked off after Jesus.

[21] They went into Capernaum, and on the Sabbath Jesus walked into the synagogue and started teaching.

[22] People were stunned. He didn't sound like the scribes — he taught like someone with real authority.

[23] Right then, a man with an unclean spirit was in the synagogue, and he started screaming:

[24] "What do you want with us, Jesus of Nazareth? Have you come to destroy us? I know who you are — the Holy One of God."

[25] Jesus shut him down: "Silence. Come out of him."

[26] The spirit convulsed the man, let out a shriek, and was gone.

[27] Everyone froze. They started asking each other, "What is this? A new kind of teaching — backed by authority. He gives orders to unclean spirits and they obey him."

[28] News about him spread like wildfire across the whole region of Galilee.

[29] Right out of the synagogue, they went to Simon and Andrew's house, James and John with them.

[30] Simon's mother-in-law was in bed with a fever, and they told Jesus immediately.

[31] He went to her, took her hand, and lifted her up. The fever broke. She got up and started serving them.

[32] That evening, after sunset, they brought him every sick and demon-possessed person in town.

[33] The whole city was crowded at the door.

[34] He healed crowds of people suffering from every kind of illness and drove out many demons. He wouldn't let the demons speak — they knew exactly who he was.

[35] Long before dawn, while it was still dark, he slipped out of the house, found a deserted place, and prayed there.

[36] Simon and the others tracked him down.

[37] When they found him, they said, "Everyone is looking for you."

[38] "Let's go somewhere else," he said. "The nearby villages. I need to preach there too. That's why I came."

[39] So he went all over Galilee, preaching in their synagogues and driving out demons.

[40] A man with leprosy came to him, dropped to his knees, and begged: "If you're willing, you can make me clean."

[41] Jesus, moved deep in his gut, stretched out his hand and touched him. "I'm willing. Be clean."

[42] The leprosy vanished instantly. He was clean.

[43] Jesus sent him off with a stern warning:

[44] "Don't say a word to anyone. Just go — show yourself to the priest and offer what Moses commanded for your cleansing. Let it stand as testimony to them."

[45] But the man went out and started telling everyone, broadcasting the story everywhere — and Jesus could no longer walk openly into any town. He stayed out in the wild places, and people kept coming to him from every direction.

[1] A few days later, Jesus came back to Capernaum. Word spread — he's home. [2] So many people packed in there was no room left, not even at the door. And he was speaking the word over them. [3] Four men showed up carrying a paralyzed man between them. [4] They couldn't get anywhere near Jesus through the crowd, so they climbed up on the roof, dug straight through it, and lowered the mat — paralyzed man and all — right down in front of him. [5] Jesus saw their faith and said to the man, "Son — your sins are forgiven."

[6] Some of the scribes sitting there started churning it over in their minds: [7] "Who does he think he is? That's blasphemy. Only God can forgive sins."

[8] Jesus knew instantly what they were thinking. "Why are you turning this over in your hearts?" he said. [9] "Which is easier — to tell this paralyzed man his sins are forgiven, or to tell him to get up, grab his mat, and walk? [10] But so you'll know the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins—" he turned to the paralyzed man — [11] "Get up. Pick up your mat. Go home."

[12] And he did. Right in front of everyone, he stood up, took his mat, and walked out. The whole room was undone. They praised God and said, "We've never seen anything like this."

[13] Jesus went back out by the sea. The whole crowd kept coming to him, and he kept teaching them. [14] As he walked along, he saw Levi son of Alphaeus sitting at the tax booth. "Follow me," he said. Levi got up and followed.

[15] Later, Jesus was reclining at dinner in Levi's house, and a whole crowd of tax collectors and sinners had pulled up to the table with him and his disciples — there were a lot of them, and they were following him. [16] When the scribes of the Pharisees saw him eating with that crowd, they cornered his disciples: "Why does he eat with tax collectors and sinners?"

[17] Jesus heard them and said, "Healthy people don't need a doctor — sick people do. I didn't come for the righteous. I came for sinners."

[18] John's disciples and the Pharisees were fasting. Some people came to Jesus and asked, "Why do John's disciples fast, and the Pharisees' disciples fast, but yours don't?"

[19] Jesus said, "Can the wedding guests fast while the groom is right there with them? As long as he's there, fasting's off the table. [20] But the day is coming when the groom will be taken from them — then they'll fast.

[21] "No one sews a patch of unshrunk cloth onto an old garment. The new patch pulls away from the old, and the tear gets worse. [22] And no one pours new wine into old wineskins. The wine will burst the skins — wine lost, skins ruined. New wine goes into new skins."

[23] One Sabbath, Jesus was walking through the grainfields, and his disciples started picking heads of grain as they went. [24] The Pharisees jumped on him: "Look — why are they doing what's forbidden on the Sabbath?"

[25] He said, "Have you never read what David did when he was desperate and hungry — he and his men? [26] He walked into the house of God, back when Abiathar was high priest, and ate the consecrated bread — which only priests are allowed to eat — and he gave some to his men too."

[27] Then he said to them, "The Sabbath was made for humanity, not humanity for the Sabbath. [28] So the Son of Man is Lord even of the Sabbath."

[1] He went back into the synagogue. A man was there with a withered hand. [2] They watched him closely — would he heal on the Sabbath? They wanted ammunition. [3] Jesus said to the man with the shriveled hand, "Stand up. Come here in front of everyone." [4] Then to them: "What does the Sabbath permit — doing good or doing harm? Saving a life or destroying one?" Silence. [5] He swept his eyes across them, furious and grieved at how locked-down their hearts were. He said to the man, "Stretch out your hand." The man stretched it out. It was whole. [6] The Pharisees walked out and immediately started plotting with Herod's people how to kill him.

[7] Jesus pulled back toward the sea with his disciples, and a massive crowd followed — from Galilee, [8] from Judea, from Jerusalem, from Idumea, from across the Jordan, from the regions around Tyre and Sidon. Word of what he was doing had spread, and they came in droves. [9] He told his disciples to keep a small boat ready for him so the crowd wouldn't crush him. [10] He had healed so many that anyone with an affliction was pressing in just to touch him. [11] And whenever the unclean spirits saw him, they collapsed in front of him and screamed, "You are the Son of God!" [12] He kept ordering them — sharply — not to expose who he was.

[13] He climbed the mountain and called the ones he wanted. They came to him. [14] He appointed twelve — calling them apostles — to be with him, and to be sent out to preach, [15] with authority to drive out demons. [16] The twelve he appointed: Simon, whom he renamed Peter; [17] James son of Zebedee and his brother John, whom he nicknamed Boanerges — Sons of Thunder; [18] Andrew, Philip, Bartholomew, Matthew, Thomas, James son of Alphaeus, Thaddaeus, Simon the Zealot, [19] and Judas Iscariot — the one who handed him over.

[20] He went home, and again the crowd swarmed so thick they couldn't even eat. [21] When his family heard, they came to take charge of him. People were saying, "He's out of his mind."

[22] The scribes who'd come down from Jerusalem said, "He's possessed by Beelzebul. He drives out demons by the prince of demons." [23] Jesus called them over and spoke in parables: "How can Satan drive out Satan? [24] A kingdom divided against itself can't stand. [25] A house divided against itself can't stand. [26] If Satan turns on Satan, he's finished. That's the end of him. [27] No one walks into a strong man's house and loots his property without first tying the strong man up. Then you can plunder the house. [28] I'm telling you the truth: every sin will be forgiven the children of humanity, every blasphemy they utter — [29] but whoever blasphemes the Holy Spirit will never be forgiven. That sin is permanent." [30] He said this because they were claiming he had an unclean spirit.

[31] Then his mother and his brothers arrived. They stood outside and sent word for him. [32] A crowd was sitting around him. They told him, "Your mother and your brothers are outside, asking for you." [33] He answered, "Who is my mother? Who are my brothers?" [34] He looked around at the circle of people sitting with him and said, "Here. Here is my mother. Here are my brothers. [35] Whoever does what God wants — that's my brother, my sister, my mother."

[1] He started teaching again by the sea. The crowd that gathered around him was so massive he had to climb into a boat and push off from shore. He sat there on the water while the whole crowd lined the beach. [2] He taught them through stories — many of them — and as he taught he said:

[3] "Listen. A farmer went out to sow. [4] As he scattered seed, some of it fell on the path. Birds came and devoured it. [5] Some landed on rocky ground where the soil was thin. It shot up fast because the earth had no depth — [6] but when the sun rose it scorched the sprouts, and without roots they withered. [7] Some fell into the weeds. The weeds grew up and choked it, and it produced nothing. [8] But some fell on good soil. It came up, it grew, it bore fruit — thirty times what was sown, sixty times, a hundred times."

[9] Then he said, "Anyone with ears — listen."

[10] Later, when he was alone, the Twelve and the others around him asked about the stories. [11] He told them, "You've been given the secret of God's kingdom. To those outside, everything comes wrapped in stories — [12] so that

*looking, they look but don't see;
hearing, they hear but don't grasp;
otherwise they'd turn around
and be forgiven.*"

[13] He said to them, "You don't get this story? Then how will you understand any of them? [14] The farmer sows the word. [15] Some people are the path — the word lands on them, and Satan shows up immediately and snatches away what was planted in them. [16] Others are the rocky ground — they hear the word and grab it with joy, [17] but they have no roots in themselves. They last a moment. When pressure comes, when persecution comes because of the word, they're gone. [18] Others are the weed patch — they hear the word, [19] but the anxieties of life, the seduction of wealth, the cravings for everything else come crowding in, choke the word, and nothing comes of it. [20] But the ones sown on good soil — they hear the word, they take it in, they bear fruit: thirty, sixty, a hundred times what was planted."

[21] He said to them, "Does anyone bring in a lamp to shove it under a basket or under the bed? Don't you put it on a stand? [22] Nothing is hidden that won't be exposed. Nothing is secret that won't come into the open. [23] Anyone with ears — listen.

[24] "Pay attention to what you're hearing," he said. "The measure you use will be measured back to you — and then some. [25] Whoever has, gets more. Whoever has nothing — even what they have will be taken."

[26] He said, "God's kingdom is like this: a man scatters seed on the ground. [27] He sleeps, he wakes, night after night, day after day — and the seed sprouts and grows. How? He has no idea. [28] The soil produces on its own — first the blade, then the head, then the full grain in the head. [29] And when the grain is ready, he swings the sickle, because harvest has come."

[30] He said, "How should we picture God's kingdom? What story can we put it in? [31] It's like a mustard seed. Plant it in the ground — it's the smallest seed there is. [32] But once it's planted, up it comes, larger than any garden plant, sending out branches so big that birds can nest in its shade."

[33] He kept telling them the word through stories like these — as much as they could handle. [34] He didn't speak to them without a story. But when he was alone with his own disciples, he unpacked everything.

[35] That same day, as evening came, he said to them, "Let's cross to the other side." [36] They left the crowd and took him just as he was, in the boat. Other boats went with him. [37] A violent squall hit. Waves crashed over the boat — it was filling up. [38] And he was in the stern, asleep on a cushion. They shook him awake: "Teacher — do you not care that we're drowning?"

[39] He got up. He rebuked the wind. He said to the sea, "Quiet. Be still." The wind dropped. A great calm settled over everything.

[40] He turned to them: "Why are you so afraid? Do you still have no faith?"

[41] They were terrified — a different kind of terror now — and they said to each other, "Who *is* this? Even the wind and the sea do what he says."

[1] They reached the other side of the lake, the region of the Gerasenes.

[2] The moment Jesus stepped out of the boat, a man came at him from the tombs — a man owned by an unclean spirit.

[3] He lived among the graves. No one could restrain him anymore — not even with chains.

[4] They had tried. Over and over they had shackled his hands and bound his feet, and every time he ripped the chains apart and shattered the irons. No one was strong enough to subdue him.

[5] Day and night he prowled the tombs and the hills, howling, cutting himself with stones.

[6] When he saw Jesus from a distance, he sprinted toward him and threw himself down at his feet.

[7] And he screamed at the top of his lungs, "What do you want with me, Jesus, Son of the Most High God? Swear to God you won't torture me!"

[8] Because Jesus had been saying to him, "Come out of this man, you unclean spirit."

[9] Jesus asked him, "What is your name?" He answered, "Legion — because there are so many of us."

[10] And he begged Jesus, over and over, not to send them out of that region.

[11] On the hillside nearby, a large herd of pigs was feeding.

[12] The spirits pleaded, "Send us into the pigs. Let us go into them."

[13] He let them. The unclean spirits came out and rushed into the pigs, and the whole herd — about two thousand of them — stampeded down the cliff into the lake and drowned.

[14] The herdsmen bolted, broadcasting the news through the town and out across the countryside. People came running to see for themselves.

[15] They came to Jesus and saw the demon-possessed man — the one who had been Legion — sitting there, clothed, in his right mind. And they were afraid.

[16] Those who had witnessed it told the rest what had happened to the man — and to the pigs.

[17] And they began to beg Jesus to leave their region.

[18] As Jesus was getting back into the boat, the man who had been delivered begged to go with him.

[19] Jesus wouldn't let him. "Go home," he said. "Go to your own people. Tell them everything the Lord has done for you — how he had mercy on you."

[20] So the man left and began proclaiming throughout the Decapolis what Jesus had done for him. And everyone was astonished.

[21] When Jesus crossed back over by boat to the other side, a huge crowd gathered around him on the shore.

[22] One of the synagogue leaders, a man named Jairus, came up. When he saw Jesus, he collapsed at his feet
