[23] He got into the boat, and his disciples followed. [24] Without warning, a violent storm churned the sea. Waves swamped the boat. Jesus was asleep. [25] They came and shook him awake: "Lord — save us! We're going under!" [26] He said, "Why are you terrified? Where's your faith?" Then he stood, rebuked the wind and the sea — and there was a great stillness. [27] The men were stunned. "Who is this? Even wind and sea obey him."

[28] They reached the other side, the region of the Gadarenes, and two demon-possessed men came charging out of the tombs to meet him — so violent that no one dared travel that road. [29] They screamed: "What do you want with us, Son of God? Have you come to torture us before the time?" [30] Some distance off, a large herd of pigs was feeding. [31] The demons begged him: "If you drive us out, send us into the pigs." [32] "Go," he said. They came out and went into the pigs — and the whole herd stampeded down the cliff into the sea and drowned in the water. [33] The herdsmen bolted, ran into town, and reported everything, including what had happened to the demon-possessed men. [34] The whole town came out to meet Jesus. When they saw him, they begged him to leave their region.

[1] He stepped into a boat, crossed the water, and came back to his own town.

[2] They brought him a paralyzed man stretched out on a mat. Jesus saw their faith and said to the man, "Take heart, son. Your sins are released."

[3] Some of the scribes muttered to themselves, "This is blasphemy."

[4] Jesus saw straight through them. "Why are your hearts running so dark?

[5] Which is easier — to say, 'Your sins are released,' or to say, 'Get up and walk'?

[6] But so you'll know the Son of Man has authority on earth to release sins—" he turned to the paralyzed man, "Get up. Pick up your mat. Go home."

[7] And the man got up and went home.

[8] The crowds saw it and a kind of awe rolled through them, and they praised God for giving such authority to human beings.

[9] Moving on, Jesus saw a man named Matthew sitting at the tax booth. "Follow me," he said. Matthew stood up and followed.

[10] Later, at dinner in the house, a whole crowd of tax collectors and notorious sinners showed up and reclined at the table with Jesus and his disciples.

[11] The Pharisees saw it and went to the disciples: "Why does your teacher eat with tax collectors and sinners?"

[12] Jesus overheard. "Healthy people don't need a doctor. Sick people do.

[13] Go figure out what this means: 'I want mercy, not sacrifice.' I didn't come for the righteous. I came for sinners."

[14] Then John's disciples came to him. "Why is it that we fast, and so do the Pharisees, but your disciples don't?"

[15] Jesus said, "Can the wedding guests grieve while the groom is still with them? But the day is coming when the groom will be torn away from them — then they'll fast.

[16] Nobody patches an old coat with unshrunk cloth. The patch pulls away and the tear gets worse.

[17] And nobody pours new wine into old wineskins. The skins burst, the wine spills, the whole thing's ruined. New wine goes into new skins — and both are kept."

[18] While he was still speaking, a synagogue leader came up, dropped to his knees, and said, "My daughter has just died. But come, lay your hand on her, and she'll live."

[19] Jesus got up and went with him, and the disciples followed.

[20] And then — a woman who'd been bleeding for twelve years came up behind him and touched the edge of his cloak.

[21] She'd been telling herself, "If I can just touch his clothes, I'll be healed."

[22] Jesus turned and saw her. "Take heart, daughter. Your faith has healed you." And from that moment, she was whole.

[23] When Jesus reached the leader's house and saw the flute players and the wailing crowd,

[24] he said, "Clear out. The girl isn't dead. She's sleeping." They laughed in his face.

[25] But once the crowd had been put outside, he went in, took her by the hand, and the girl stood up.

[26] The news of it tore through the whole region.

[27] As Jesus moved on, two blind men trailed after him, shouting, "Have mercy on us, Son of David!"

[28] When he went indoors, they came right in after him. Jesus said, "Do you believe I can do this?" "Yes, Lord," they said.

[29] He touched their eyes. "Let it be done according to your faith."

[30] And their eyes opened. Jesus warned them sharply, "See that no one finds out about this."

[31] They went out and spread the news through the whole region.

[32] As they were leaving, a man was brought to him — mute, possessed by a demon.

[33] The demon was driven out, the mute man spoke, and the crowds were stunned. "Nothing like this has ever been seen in Israel."

[34] But the Pharisees said, "He drives out demons by the prince of demons."

[35] Jesus moved through all the towns and villages, teaching in their synagogues, announcing the good news of the kingdom, healing every disease, every sickness.

[36] When he saw the crowds, something broke in him — they looked harassed and helpless, like sheep with no shepherd.

[37] He said to his disciples, "The harvest is massive. The workers are few.

[38] So beg the Lord of the harvest to send workers out into his field."

[1] He called the Twelve to him and handed them authority — to drive out unclean spirits, to heal every disease, every affliction.

[2] Here are the names of the twelve apostles: first, Simon (the one called Peter) and his brother Andrew; James son of Zebedee and his brother John;

[3] Philip and Bartholomew; Thomas and Matthew the tax collector; James son of Alphaeus and Thaddaeus;

[4] Simon the Zealot and Judas Iscariot — the one who would hand him over.

[5] These twelve Jesus sent out with marching orders: "Don't head down the roads of the Gentiles. Don't step into any Samaritan town.

[6] Go instead to the lost sheep of Israel.

[7] As you go, announce it: the kingdom of heaven is here.

[8] Heal the sick. Raise the dead. Cleanse the lepers. Cast out demons. You received it free — give it free.

[9] Don't pack gold or silver or copper in your belts.

[10] No bag for the road, no extra shirt, no sandals, no walking stick. The worker is worth his keep.

[11] Whatever town or village you enter, find someone worthy there and stay with them until you leave.

[12] When you step into the house, speak peace over it.

[13] If the house is worthy, let your peace settle on it. If not, let your peace come back to you.

[14] And if anyone refuses you — refuses to listen — walk out of that house, that town, and shake the dust off your feet as you go.

[15] I'm telling you straight: Sodom and Gomorrah will get off easier on judgment day than that town.

[16] Listen — I'm sending you out like sheep into a pack of wolves. So be shrewd as snakes and innocent as doves.

[17] Watch your back. They'll drag you into their courts. They'll flog you in their synagogues.

[18] You'll stand before governors and kings because of me — and that's exactly the point: a witness to them, to the nations.

[19] When they haul you in, don't sweat what to say or how to say it. The words will come, right when you need them.

[20] You won't be the one speaking — the Spirit of your Father will be speaking through you.

[21] Brother will betray brother to death. Fathers will turn on their children. Children will rise up against their parents and have them killed.

[22] Everyone will hate you because of my name. But the one who holds on to the end — that one will be saved.

[23] When they come for you in one town, run to the next. I'm telling you the truth: you won't have finished the towns of Israel before the Son of Man arrives.

[24] A student isn't above the teacher. A servant isn't above the master.

[25] It's enough that the student becomes like his teacher, the servant like his master. If they called the head of the house Beelzebul, what do you think they'll call the rest of the household?

[26] So don't be afraid of them. There's nothing hidden that won't be uncovered, nothing buried that won't come to light.

[27] What I tell you in the dark — say it in the daylight. What I whisper in your ear — shout it from the rooftops.

[28] Don't fear the ones who can kill the body but can't touch the soul. Fear the One who can destroy both body and soul in hell.

[29] Two sparrows go for a penny. And not one of them drops to the ground without your Father.

[30] As for you — every hair on your head is counted.

[31] So don't be afraid. You're worth more than a whole sky full of sparrows.

[32] Anyone who claims me in front of others, I'll claim in front of my Father in heaven.

[33] Anyone who disowns me in front of others, I'll disown in front of my Father in heaven.

[34] Don't think I came to drop peace on the earth. I didn't come with peace. I came with a sword.

[35] I came to turn a son against his father, a daughter against her mother, a daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law —

[36] your worst enemies will be the people under your own roof.

[37] Whoever loves father or mother more than me isn't worthy of me. Whoever loves son or daughter more than me isn't worthy of me.

[38] Whoever won't take up their cross and walk behind me isn't worthy of me.

[39] Cling to your life and you'll lose it. Lose your life for my sake and you'll find it.

[40] Whoever welcomes you welcomes me. And whoever welcomes me welcomes the One who sent me.

[41] Welcome a prophet because he's a prophet, and you'll share a prophet's reward. Welcome a righteous person because she's righteous, and you'll share a righteous person's reward.

[42] And if anyone gives even a cup of cold water to one of these little ones — just because they're my disciple — I'm telling you, they will not lose their reward."

[1] When Jesus finished briefing his twelve, he moved on from there to teach and preach in the surrounding towns.

[2] John, locked away in prison, heard the reports about what the Messiah was doing. He sent word through his disciples,

[3] asking him point-blank: "Are you the one we've been waiting for, or do we keep looking?"

[4] Jesus answered, "Go back to John. Tell him what you're hearing and seeing right now:

[5] the blind see, the lame walk, lepers are made clean, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, and the poor are getting the good news.

[6] Blessed is the one who doesn't stumble over me."

[7] As John's disciples were leaving, Jesus turned to the crowds and started talking about John. "What did you go out into the wilderness to see? A reed bending in the wind?

[8] No? Then what — some man dressed in silk? People in silk live in palaces.

[9] So what then — a prophet? Yes, I'm telling you, and more than a prophet.

[10] He's the one Scripture is talking about: 'Look — I'm sending my messenger ahead of you. He'll clear the road before you arrive.'

[11] I'll say it plainly: no one born of a woman has ever been greater than John the Baptist. And yet the smallest person in the kingdom of heaven outranks him.

[12] From the days of John until now, the kingdom of heaven has been under assault, and violent people are trying to seize it by force.

[13] Every prophet and the Law itself — all of them pointed forward to this moment, up until John.

[14] And if you're willing to hear it: John is the Elijah who was promised.

[15] If you have ears, use them.

[16] What can I compare this generation to? They're like children sitting in the marketplace, shouting at their friends:

[17] 'We played you wedding music — you wouldn't dance. We sang you a funeral song — you wouldn't mourn.'

[18] John came neither eating nor drinking, and they said, 'He's possessed.'

[19] The Son of Man came eating and drinking, and they said, 'Look at him — a glutton, a drunk, friend to tax collectors and sinners.' But wisdom is proven right by what it produces."

[20] Then Jesus started laying into the towns where most of his miracles had been performed — because they hadn't turned around.

[21] "Disaster's coming for you, Chorazin. Disaster, Bethsaida. If the miracles done in your streets had been done in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago — sitting in sackcloth and ashes.

[22] Hear me: Tyre and Sidon will have it easier on judgment day than you will.

[23] And you, Capernaum — do you think you'll be lifted up to heaven? You'll be brought down to the grave. If the miracles done in you had happened in Sodom, it would still be standing.

[24] So I'm telling you: the land of Sodom will have it easier on judgment day than you."

[25] At that moment Jesus said, "Father, Lord of heaven and earth, I praise you — because you've hidden these things from the wise and the clever, and revealed them to children.

[26] Yes, Father. This is what pleased you.

[27] Everything has been handed over to me by my Father. No one knows the Son except the Father, and no one knows the Father except the Son — and anyone the Son chooses to show him to.

[28] Come to me, all of you who are exhausted and crushed under the weight you're carrying. I'll give you rest.

[29] Take my yoke on you and learn from me. I'm gentle. I'm humble at heart. You'll find rest for your souls.

[30] Because my yoke fits easy, and the load I give you is light."

[1] Around that time, Jesus was walking through grainfields on the Sabbath. His disciples were hungry, so they started plucking heads of grain and eating them.

[2] The Pharisees saw it and pounced: "Look — your disciples are doing what's forbidden on the Sabbath."

[3] He shot back: "Have you not read what David did when he and his men were starving?

[4] He walked into the house of God and ate the consecrated bread — bread he had no right to touch, bread reserved for the priests alone. And he gave it to his men.

[5] Or here's another one: have you not read in the Law that the priests in the temple desecrate the Sabbath every week and stand guiltless?

[6] I'll tell you something — something greater than the temple is standing right here.

[7] If you'd grasped what this means — 'I want mercy, not sacrifice' — you wouldn't be condemning the innocent.

[8] The Son of Man owns the Sabbath."

[9] He moved on from there and entered their synagogue.

[10] A man was there with a withered hand. They were watching — looking for ammunition — so they asked, "Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath?"

[11] He answered, "Which of you, if you owned one sheep and it fell into a pit on the Sabbath, wouldn't grab it and haul it out?

[12] And a person is worth infinitely more than a sheep. So yes — doing good on the Sabbath is lawful."

[13] Then to the man: "Stretch out your hand." He stretched it out, and it was whole — restored, as good as the other.

[14] The Pharisees walked out and began plotting how to kill him.

[15] Jesus knew. He withdrew. Crowds followed him, and he healed every last one of them,

[16] warning them not to broadcast who he was.

[17] All of this fulfilled what the prophet Isaiah had spoken:

[18] "Look at my servant, the one I chose, my beloved, the joy of my soul. I'll pour my Spirit on him, and he'll announce justice to the nations.

[19] He won't quarrel or shout. No one will hear his voice rising in the streets.

[20] A bruised reed he will not break. A flickering wick he will not snuff out — not until he drives justice to victory.

[21] And in his name the nations will stake their hope."

[22] Then they brought him a man — blind, mute, demon-possessed. Jesus healed him. The man spoke. The man saw.

[23] The crowd went wild: "Could this actually be the Son of David?"

[24] The Pharisees heard it and sneered: "He only casts out demons by Beelzebul, prince of demons."

[25] Jesus read their thoughts and said: "Every kingdom torn against itself collapses. Every city, every household split against itself can't stand.

[26] If Satan throws out Satan, he's at war with himself. How does his kingdom survive?

[27] And if I cast out demons by Beelzebul, who do your own sons cast them out by? They'll be your judges.

[28] But if it's by the Spirit of God that I drive demons out — then the kingdom of God has just crashed into your world.

[29] How do you walk into a strong man's house and rob him? You don't — unless you tie him up first. Then you plunder at will.

[30] Whoever isn't with me is against me. Whoever doesn't gather with me scatters.

[31] So hear me: every sin, every blasphemy can be forgiven — but blasphemy against the Spirit will not be forgiven.

[32] Speak against the Son of Man and you can be forgiven. Speak against the Holy Spirit and you cannot — not in this age, not in the one to come.

[33] Either call the tree good and its fruit good, or call the tree rotten and its fruit rotten. A tree is known by what it produces.

[34] You brood of snakes — how can you say anything good when you're evil? The mouth pours out what floods the heart.

[35] A good person pulls good from the treasury of the heart. An evil person pulls evil from a stockpile of evil.

[36] I'm telling you — on judgment day, people will answer for every careless word they ever spoke.

[37] Your words will vindicate you. Your words will condemn you."

[38] Then some of the scribes and Pharisees said: "Teacher, we want to see a sign from you."

[39] He answered: "An evil and adulterous generation hunts for a sign. None will be given — except the sign of the prophet Jonah.

[40] Just as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the sea monster, so the Son of Man will be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth.

[41] The men of Nineveh will rise at the judgment with this generation and condemn it — because they repented at Jonah's preaching, and look: something greater than Jonah is here.

[42] The Queen of the South will rise at the judgment with this generation and condemn it — because she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon, and look: something greater than Solomon is here.

[43] When an unclean spirit exits a person, it wanders through arid wastelands looking for rest and finds none.

[44] Then it says, 'I'll return to the house I left.' It arrives and finds the place empty, swept clean, everything in order.

[45] So it goes and brings back seven other spirits more vicious than itself, and they all move in. The final state of that person is worse than the first. That's exactly how it will be with this evil generation."

[46] He was still speaking to the crowd when his mother and brothers showed up outside, wanting a word with him.

[47] Someone told him, "Your mother and your brothers are standing outside, asking for you."

[48] He answered the messenger: "Who is my mother? Who are my brothers?"

[49] Then, stretching his hand toward his disciples, he said: "Here — here is my mother. Here are my brothers.

[50] Anyone who does the will of my Father in heaven — that one is my brother, my sister, my mother."

[1] That same day Jesus walked out of the house and sat down by the lake. [2] The crowds packed in around him so tight he climbed into a boat and sat there while everyone stood on the shore. [3] He told them story after story.

"A farmer went out to sow. [4] As he scattered seed, some landed on the path — and the birds swooped in and ate it. [5] Some hit rocky ground where the soil ran thin. It shot up fast — [6] but when the sun came, it burned. With no roots, it withered. [7] Some fell among thornbushes, which grew up and choked it out. [8] But some landed in good soil and produced a harvest — a hundred times what was sown, sixty times, thirty times. [9] If you have ears, listen."

[10] The disciples came over. "Why do you talk to them in stories?"

[11] He answered, "You've been let in on the secrets of the kingdom of heaven. They haven't. [12] Those who have, get more — overflowing. Those who don't have, lose even what they think they have. [13] That's why I speak to them in stories: they look but don't see, they listen but don't hear, and nothing registers. [14] Isaiah called it:

*You'll hear and hear and never understand,
you'll look and look and never see.
[15] This people's heart has gone fat and dull,
their ears half-deaf, their eyes squeezed shut —
otherwise they might actually see,
actually hear,
actually understand,
turn around,
and I would heal them.*

[16] But your eyes — blessed. They see. Your ears — blessed. They hear. [17] I'm telling you, prophets and righteous men hungered to see what you're seeing and never saw it, to hear what you're hearing and never heard it.

[18] So here's the farmer story decoded. [19] When someone hears the message of the kingdom and doesn't grasp it, the evil one swoops in and snatches what was planted in their heart. That's the seed on the path. [20] The rocky ground? That's the person who hears it and grabs it with joy — [21] but it never roots down. They last only as long as it's easy. The moment pressure or persecution shows up because of the word, they're gone. [22] The thorn-choked seed? That's the one who hears, but the worries of this life and the seduction of money strangle the word, and nothing comes of it. [23] The good soil? That's the one who hears, understands, and yields — a hundredfold, sixty, thirty."

[24] Another story:

"The kingdom of heaven is like a man who planted good seed in his field. [25] But while everyone slept, his enemy crept in, sowed weeds through the wheat, and slipped away. [26] When the wheat sprouted and headed out, the weeds showed up too.

[27] The workers came to the owner. 'Sir — didn't you sow good seed? Where did the weeds come from?'

[28] 'An enemy did this,' he said.

'Want us to pull them?'

[29] 'No. You'll uproot the wheat with them. [30] Let them grow together until harvest. Then I'll tell the harvesters: gather the weeds first, bundle them, burn them — but bring the wheat into my barn.'"

[31] Another:

"The kingdom of heaven is like a mustard seed someone planted in his field. [32] Smallest of all the seeds — but when it grows, it's the biggest thing in the garden, a tree where birds nest in its branches."

[33] Another:

"The kingdom of heaven is like yeast a woman folded into a massive batch of flour until the whole thing was alive with it."

[34] Jesus told the crowds everything in stories. He didn't say a thing to them without a story. [35] This fulfilled what the prophet said:

*I'll open my mouth in parables,
I'll spill out what's been hidden since the world began.*

[36] Then he left the crowds and went into the house. The disciples came to him. "Explain the weeds in the field."

[37] He said: "The one sowing good seed is the Son of Man. [38] The field is the world. The good seed is the children of the kingdom. The weeds are the children of the evil one. [39] The enemy who planted them is the devil. The harvest is the end of the age. The harvesters are angels. [40] Just as the weeds get pulled and burned, that's what'll happen at the end of the age. [41] The Son of Man will send his angels, and they'll comb his kingdom of everything that causes ruin and everyone who works evil. [42] Into the furnace. There'll be weeping there, and grinding teeth. [43] Then the righteous will blaze like the sun in their Father's kingdom. If you have ears, listen.

[44] The kingdom of heaven is like treasure buried in a field. A man stumbles on it, covers it back up, and — pulse pounding — sells everything he owns and buys that field.

[45] The kingdom of heaven is like a merchant on the hunt for fine pearls. [46] He finds one — the one — and he sells everything to get it.

[47] The kingdom of heaven is like a net thrown into the sea that catches every kind of fish. [48] When it's full, they drag it ashore, sit down, and sort: good fish in baskets, bad fish thrown out. [49] That's how it'll be at the end of the age. The angels will come, separate the wicked from the righteous, [50] and throw them into the blazing furnace. Weeping there. Teeth grinding.

[51] You understand all this?"

"Yes," they said.

[52] He said, "Then every scholar of the law trained for the kingdom of heaven is like the master of a house who pulls treasure out of his storeroom — new things and old."

[53] When Jesus finished these stories, he moved on. [54] He came to his hometown and started teaching in the synagogue, and they couldn't get over it. "Where did this guy get this wisdom? These miracles? [55] Isn't this the carpenter's son? Isn't his mother Mary? His brothers James, Joseph, Simon, Judas? [56] His sisters — they all live here. Where did he get any of this?" [57] And they couldn't accept him.

Jesus said, "A prophet is honored everywhere except his hometown and his own house." [58] And because they wouldn't believe, he didn't do many miracles there.

[1] About that time, Herod the tetrarch caught wind of what Jesus was doing,

[2] and he said to his servants, "This has to be John the Baptist — risen from the dead. That's why these powers surge through him."

[3] Because Herod had arrested John, chained him, and locked him away — all because of Herodias, his brother Philip's wife.

[4] John had been telling him to his face: "You have no right to her."

[5] Herod wanted him dead, but he feared the crowds — they considered John a prophet.

[6] Then came Herod's birthday. Herodias's daughter danced before the guests and stole the show.

[7] Herod was so dazzled he swore an oath — whatever she asked, it was hers.

[8] Prompted by her mother, she said: "Give me John the Baptist's head. On a platter. Right here."

[9] The king's stomach turned — but the oath had been spoken, and his guests had heard it. He gave the order.

[10] They beheaded John in the prison.

[11] His head was carried in on a platter and handed to the girl, who brought it to her mother.

[12] John's disciples came, took the body, buried him, and went to tell Jesus.

[13] When Jesus heard, he withdrew by boat to a deserted place, alone. But the crowds caught wind and followed on foot from the towns.

[14] When he stepped ashore and saw the massive crowd, compassion moved him, and he healed their sick.

[15] As evening came on, the disciples approached him: "This place is empty and it's getting late. Send the crowds off to the villages to buy themselves food."

[16] Jesus answered, "They don't need to go anywhere. You feed them."

[17] "We've got five loaves and two fish — that's it," they said.

[18] "Bring them to me," he said.

[19] He told the crowds to sit on the grass. He took the five loaves and the two fish, looked up to heaven, blessed them, broke the loaves, and handed them to the disciples — who handed them to the crowd.

[20] Everyone ate. Everyone was full. And they gathered up twelve baskets of leftovers.

[21] Five thousand men ate that day — and that's not counting the women and children.

[22] Immediately he made the disciples get in the boat and cross ahead of him while he dismissed the crowds.

[23] Once the crowds were gone, he climbed the mountain alone to pray. Evening fell, and he was still up there, alone.

[24] The boat was already far from shore, getting hammered by waves — the wind was dead against them.

[25] In the last watch of the night, he came to them — walking on the sea.

[26] When the disciples saw him gliding across the water, they panicked. "A ghost!" they screamed in terror.

[27] But immediately Jesus spoke: "Take heart. It's me. Don't be afraid."

[28] Peter answered, "Lord — if it's really you, tell me to come to you on the water."

[29] "Come," he said. Peter climbed out of the boat, walked on the water, and headed for Jesus.

[30] But when he saw the wind, fear took him, and he started to sink. "Lord — save me!" he cried.

[31] Instantly Jesus reached out and grabbed him. "You of little faith. Why did you doubt?"

[32] They climbed into the boat, and the wind died.

[33] Those in the boat fell at his feet: "You really are the Son of God."

[34] They crossed over and landed at Gennesaret.

[35] The men of that place recognized him and sent word through the whole region. They brought him everyone who was sick,

[36] begging him to let them just touch the hem of his cloak. And everyone who touched it was made whole.

[1] Then Pharisees and scribes came down from Jerusalem and confronted Jesus: [2] "Why do your disciples trample the tradition of the elders? They don't wash their hands before they eat."

[3] He fired back: "And why do you trample the command of God for the sake of your tradition? [4] God said, 'Honor your father and mother,' and 'Anyone who curses father or mother dies for it.' [5] But you teach: if a man tells his father or mother, 'Whatever I owed you is Corban — dedicated to God' — [6] then he's off the hook, free to ignore them. You've gutted the word of God to protect your tradition. [7] Hypocrites. Isaiah saw you coming:

[8] *This people honors me with their lips,*
*but their hearts are a thousand miles away.*
[9] *Their worship is theater —*
*they teach human rules as if they were mine.*"

[10] He called the crowd close. "Listen. Understand this. [11] What goes into your mouth doesn't defile you. What comes out of your mouth — that defiles you."

[12] The disciples sidled up: "You know the Pharisees were scandalized by what you just said?"

[13] He answered, "Every plant my Father didn't plant will be uprooted. [14] Leave them be. They're blind men leading blind men. Both of them are heading for the ditch."

[15] Peter said, "Explain the saying to us."

[16] "You're still not getting it?" Jesus said. [17] "Don't you see? Whatever enters the mouth goes to the stomach and out into the latrine. [18] But what comes out of the mouth comes from the heart — and *that* is what defiles a person. [19] From the heart come murderous thoughts, adultery, sexual corruption, theft, lies, slander. [20] These are what defile you. Eating with unwashed hands? That defiles nothing."

[21] Jesus left and withdrew into the regions of Tyre and Sidon. [22] A Canaanite woman from that territory came out, crying after him: "Have mercy on me, Lord, Son of David! My daughter is tormented — a demon has her."

[23] He didn't answer a word. The disciples pressed him: "Send her away — she keeps shouting after us."

[24] He said, "I was sent only to the lost sheep of the house of Israel."

[25] She came and fell at his feet. "Lord — help me."

[26] "It isn't right," he said, "to take the children's bread and throw it to the dogs."

[27] "Yes, Lord," she said. "Even the dogs eat the crumbs that fall from their master's table."

[28] Then Jesus said to her, "Woman — your faith is enormous. Let it be done for you exactly as you want." And her daughter was healed in that very hour.

[29] Jesus moved on from there, traveled along the Sea of Galilee, climbed the mountain, and sat down. [30] Huge crowds streamed to him, dragging the lame, the blind, the crippled, the mute, and many others. They laid them at his feet. He healed them. [31] The crowd was stunned — watching the mute speak, the crippled made whole, the lame walking, the blind seeing. And they glorified the God of Israel.

[32] Jesus called his disciples together: "My heart breaks for this crowd. They've been with me three days now and have nothing to eat. I won't send them off hungry — they'll collapse on the road."

[33] The disciples said, "Out here in this wilderness — where would we get enough bread to feed a crowd this size?"

[34] "How many loaves do you have?" Jesus asked.

"Seven," they said. "And a few small fish."

[35] He told the crowd to sit down on the ground. [36] He took the seven loaves and the fish, gave thanks, broke them, handed them to the disciples, and the disciples to the crowd. [37] Every person ate and was full. They picked up the leftover pieces — seven baskets full. [38] Four thousand men ate, not counting women and children.

[39] Then he dismissed the crowds, got into the boat, and crossed over to the region of Magadan.

[1] The Pharisees and Sadducees came at him together, testing him, demanding he produce a sign from heaven. [2] He answered: "When evening comes you say, 'Fair weather—the sky is red.' [3] And at dawn, 'Storm today—red sky, dark clouds.' You read the face of the sky but you cannot read the signs of this moment. [4] A wicked, faithless generation hunts for a sign, but none will be given except the sign of Jonah." And he turned and walked away from them.

[5] Crossing to the other side, the disciples realized they had forgotten to bring bread. [6] Jesus said, "Watch yourselves—stay clear of the yeast of the Pharisees and Sadducees." [7] They started muttering to each other: "He's saying this because we didn't bring bread."

[8] Jesus knew. "You people of paper-thin faith—why are you talking about bread? [9] Still not getting it? Don't you remember the five loaves and the five thousand—how many baskets you hauled away? [10] Or the seven loaves and the four thousand—how many baskets then? [11] How do you not understand I wasn't talking about bread? Stay clear of the yeast of the Pharisees and Sadducees." [12] Then it clicked—he wasn't warning them about bread-yeast. He was warning them about the teaching of the Pharisees and Sadducees.

[13] When Jesus reached the region of Caesarea Philippi, he asked his disciples, "Who do people say the Son of Man is?" [14] They said, "Some say John the Baptist. Others, Elijah. Still others, Jeremiah or one of the prophets." [15] "And you," he said. "Who do you say I am?" [16] Simon Peter answered: "You are the Messiah. The Son of the living God."

[17] Jesus said, "Blessed are you, Simon son of Jonah. No human being told you this—my Father in heaven did. [18] And I tell you: you are Peter—Rock—and on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of hell will not hold against it. [19] I'll hand you the keys of the kingdom of heaven. Whatever you bind on earth will stand bound in heaven. Whatever you loose on earth will stand loosed in heaven." [20] Then he ordered the disciples to tell no one he was the Messiah.

[21] From that moment Jesus began laying it out plainly: he had to go to Jerusalem, suffer terribly at the hands of the elders, chief priests, and scribes, be killed, and on the third day rise. [22] Peter pulled him aside and started in on him: "God forbid it, Lord! This will never happen to you." [23] Jesus wheeled on him: "Get behind me, Satan. You're a stumbling block in my path. Your mind is on human things, not God's."

[24] Then Jesus said to his disciples, "If anyone wants to come after me, let him deny himself, pick up his cross, and follow. [25] Whoever clings to his life will lose it. Whoever loses his life for my sake will find it. [26] What good is it to gain the whole world if you forfeit your soul? What would anyone trade for their soul? [27] Because the Son of Man is coming in the glory of his Father with his angels, and then he will give back to each according to what they have done. [28] I tell you the truth—some standing here will not taste death before they see the Son of Man coming in his kingdom."

[1] Six days later Jesus took Peter, James, and John — James's brother — and led them up a high mountain, just the four of them.

[2] And there, right in front of them, he transformed. His face blazed like the sun. His clothes turned white as light itself.

[3] Then suddenly Moses and Elijah were there — actually there — deep in conversation with him.

[4] Peter broke in: "Lord, this is incredible. If you want, I'll build three shelters here — one for you, one for Moses, one for Elijah."

[5] He was still talking when a luminous cloud swept over them, and a voice spoke from inside the cloud: "This is my Son. The one I love. The one who delights me. Listen to him."

[6] The disciples hit the ground, terrified.

[7] Jesus came over, touched them, and said, "Get up. Don't be afraid."

[8] When they looked up, no one was there. Just Jesus.

[9] On the way down the mountain, Jesus gave them an order: "Tell no one what you saw — not until the Son of Man has been raised from the dead."

[10] His disciples asked him, "Then why do the scribes insist Elijah has to come first?"

[11] He answered, "Elijah does come — and he sets everything right.

[12] But here's the truth: Elijah already came, and they didn't recognize him. They did whatever they wanted to him. The Son of Man is about to suffer the same way at their hands."

[13] Then the disciples understood — he was talking about John the Baptist.

[14] When they reached the crowd, a man came up, dropped to his knees in front of Jesus,

[15] and said, "Lord, have mercy on my son. He's losing his mind — it's torture. He keeps throwing himself into fire, into water.

[16] I brought him to your disciples, and they couldn't heal him."

[17] Jesus answered, "You faithless, twisted generation — how long do I have to be with you? How long do I have to put up with this? Bring him here to me."

[18] Jesus rebuked the demon, it came out of the boy, and from that moment he was well.

[19] Later, privately, the disciples came to Jesus and asked, "Why couldn't we drive it out?"

[20] He told them, "Because your faith is too small. I'm telling you the truth — if you had faith the size of a single mustard seed, you could say to this mountain, 'Move from here to there,' and it would move. Nothing would be impossible for you."

[21]

[22] When they regrouped in Galilee, Jesus told them, "The Son of Man is about to be handed over into human hands.

[23] They will kill him. And on the third day he will be raised." They were devastated.

[24] When they arrived in Capernaum, the temple-tax collectors came to Peter and said, "Doesn't your teacher pay the two-drachma tax?"

[25] "He does," Peter said. When Peter went into the house, Jesus spoke first: "Simon, what do you think? When earthly kings collect tolls and taxes — who pays? Their own children, or everyone else?"

[26] "Everyone else," Peter answered. Jesus said, "Then the children are exempt.

[27] But we don't want to give them offense. Go to the lake. Throw in a hook. Take the first fish you catch, open its mouth — you'll find a coin. Take it and pay the tax for both of us."

[1] Right then the disciples came to Jesus with a question: "So who actually ranks highest in the kingdom of heaven?"

[2] Jesus called a child over, set the child in the middle of them,

[3] and said: "I'm telling you straight—unless you turn around and become like children, you won't even get in the kingdom of heaven, let alone rank in it.

[4] Whoever makes themselves low—small as this child—that's who ranks highest in the kingdom of heaven.

[5] And whoever welcomes one child like this in my name welcomes me.

[6] But anyone who trips up one of these little ones who trusts me? Better for them if a millstone were hung around their neck and they were dropped to the bottom of the sea.

[7] The world is in trouble because of the traps it sets. Traps are inevitable—but God help the one who sets them.

[8] If your hand or foot trips you up, cut it off and throw it away. Better to enter life maimed than to be tossed whole into the fire that never goes out.

[9] If your eye trips you up, gouge it out and throw it away. Better to enter life one-eyed than to be thrown two-eyed into the fire of hell.

[10] Watch yourselves—don't look down on a single one of these little ones. I'm telling you, their angels in heaven are always looking straight at the face of my Father in heaven.

[12] What do you think? A man has a hundred sheep, and one wanders off. Doesn't he leave the ninety-nine on the hillside and go hunting for the one that strayed?

[13] And if he finds it—I'm telling you—he celebrates over that one more than over the ninety-nine that never strayed.

[14] That's exactly how it is with your Father in heaven: he refuses to lose a single one of these little ones.

[15] If a brother or sister wrongs you, go to them. Just the two of you. Lay it out. If they hear you, you've got them back.

[16] If they won't listen, take one or two others with you, so every word is backed by two or three witnesses.

[17] If they still won't listen, take it to the church. And if they won't even listen to the church, treat them like an outsider.

[18] I'm telling you: whatever you bind on earth will already be bound in heaven, and whatever you loose on earth will already be loosed in heaven.

[19] And another thing: if two of you on earth come into agreement about anything you ask for, my Father in heaven will do it.

[20] Because wherever two or three gather in my name—I'm right there in the middle of them."

[21] Then Peter stepped up: "Lord, how many times does my brother get to wrong me and I keep forgiving him? Seven times?"

[22] Jesus said: "Not seven. Try seventy times seven.

[23] Here's what the kingdom of heaven is like. A king decided to settle accounts with his servants.

[24] First one in owed him ten thousand talents—an unpayable fortune.

[25] He couldn't pay, so the king ordered him sold—him, his wife, his kids, everything he owned—to cover the debt.

[26] The servant hit the ground at the king's feet: 'Please. Give me time. I'll pay you back—every coin.'

[27] The king's gut twisted with pity. He let him go. He wiped the debt clean.

[28] That servant walked out and ran into a fellow servant who owed him a hundred denarii—pocket change. He grabbed him by the throat: 'Pay up. Now.'

[29] The man fell at his feet: 'Please. Give me time. I'll pay you back.'

[30] He refused. He had the man thrown in prison until the debt was paid.

[31] The other servants saw it and were sick over it. They went and told the king everything.

[32] The king called him back in: 'You wicked man. I wiped out your entire debt because you begged me.

[33] Shouldn't you have shown your fellow servant the same mercy I showed you?'

[34] And in fury the king handed him over to the torturers until he paid back every last cent.

[35] That's what my Father in heaven will do to you—every one of you—if you don't forgive your brother from the heart."

[1] When Jesus finished saying these things, he left Galilee and crossed into the region of Judea, east of the Jordan. [2] Massive crowds followed him there, and he healed them.

[3] Some Pharisees came at him with a trap: "Is it legal for a man to divorce his wife for any reason at all?"

[4] He answered, "Have you not read it? The One who made them at the start made them male and female. [5] And he said: 'For this reason a man will leave his father and mother and bond with his wife, and the two will become one flesh.' [6] So they are no longer two. They are one. What God has fused together, no human being gets to tear apart."

[7] They shot back: "Then why did Moses command a man to hand his wife a certificate and send her away?"

[8] He said, "Moses permitted you to divorce your wives because your hearts were stone. But it was not like that from the beginning. [9] I am telling you: anyone who divorces his wife — except for sexual betrayal — and marries another commits adultery."

[10] His disciples said, "If that is how it is between a man and his wife, better not to marry at all."

[11] He told them, "Not everyone can absorb this teaching. Only those to whom it has been given. [12] Some are eunuchs from birth. Some were made eunuchs by other men. And some make themselves eunuchs for the sake of the kingdom of heaven. Whoever can take this in — take it in."

[13] Then they brought little children to him so he could lay his hands on them and pray. The disciples scolded them for it. [14] But Jesus said, "Let the children come to me. Do not block them. The kingdom of heaven belongs to ones like these." [15] He laid his hands on them and moved on.

[16] A man approached him and asked, "Teacher, what good thing must I do to have eternal life?"

[17] Jesus answered, "Why ask me about what is good? There is only One who is good. But if you want to enter life — keep the commandments."

[18] "Which ones?" the man asked.

Jesus said, "Do not murder. Do not commit adultery. Do not steal. Do not bear false witness. [19] Honor your father and mother. And love your neighbor as yourself."

[20] The young man said, "I have kept all of these. What am I still missing?"

[21] Jesus said, "If you want to be complete — go. Sell what you own. Give it to the poor. You will have treasure in heaven. Then come, follow me."

[22] The young man heard this and walked away grieving, because he owned a great deal.

[23] Jesus turned to his disciples: "I am telling you the truth — it is brutally hard for a rich person to enter the kingdom of heaven. [24] I will say it again: easier for a camel to thread through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God."

[25] The disciples were stunned. "Then who can possibly be saved?"

[26] Jesus looked straight at them and said, "With humans — impossible. With God — everything is possible."

[27] Peter spoke up: "Look — we have left everything behind to follow you. What do we get?"

[28] Jesus said, "I am telling you the truth — in the renewal of all things, when the Son of Man takes his throne in glory, you who have followed me will also sit on twelve thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel. [29] And anyone who has left houses or brothers or sisters or father or mother or children or fields for my name — will get back a hundred times more, and inherit eternal life. [30] But many who are first will end up last, and the last will end up first."

[1] The kingdom of heaven works like this: A landowner went out at dawn to hire workers for his vineyard.

[2] He settled on a denarius for the day and sent them into the field.

[3] Around nine in the morning he went out again and saw others standing in the marketplace doing nothing.

[4] "Go work in my vineyard," he told them. "I'll pay you what's fair."

[5] So they went. He did the same thing at noon and again at three.

[6] At five in the afternoon he went out and found still more standing around. "Why have you been here all day with nothing to do?" he asked.

[7] "Because no one hired us," they said. "Then go," he told them. "Work my vineyard."

[8] When evening came, the owner said to his foreman, "Call the workers in and pay them their wages — start with the ones hired last and work back to the first."

[9] The five o'clock workers came up and each received a denarius.

[10] When the dawn crew came forward, they assumed they'd get more. But they each received a denarius too.

[11] They took the money and started grumbling against the landowner.

[12] "These last guys put in one hour," they said, "and you've made them equal to us — we who carried the load and burned through the heat of the day."

[13] He answered one of them: "Friend, I haven't cheated you. Didn't we agree on a denarius?

[14] Take what's yours and go. I want to give this last man the same as you.

[15] Is it not my right to do what I want with my own money? Or is your eye dark because I am generous?"

[16] So the last will be first, and the first last.

[17] As Jesus was heading up to Jerusalem, he pulled the Twelve aside on the road and said,

[18] "Look — we're going up to Jerusalem, and the Son of Man is going to be handed over to the chief priests and the scribes. They will condemn him to death

[19] and turn him over to the Gentiles to be mocked, flogged, and crucified. And on the third day he will be raised."

[20] Then the mother of Zebedee's sons came to him with her two boys, knelt down, and asked a favor.

[21] "What do you want?" he asked. She said, "Promise me that in your kingdom these two sons of mine will sit — one at your right, one at your left."

[22] Jesus replied, "You have no idea what you're asking. Can you drink the cup I'm about to drink?" "We can," they said.

[23] "You will drink my cup," he told them, "but the seats at my right and left aren't mine to assign. Those belong to whoever my Father has prepared them for."

[24] When the other ten heard about it, they were furious with the two brothers.

[25] Jesus called them together and said, "You know how it works out there — the rulers of the nations lord it over people, and the powerful throw their weight around.

[26] That's not how it goes among you. Whoever wants to be great among you must serve.

[27] Whoever wants to be first must be your slave —

[28] just as the Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many."

[29] As they were leaving Jericho, a huge crowd followed him.

[30] Two blind men were sitting by the road. When they heard Jesus was passing by, they shouted, "Lord, Son of David — have mercy on us!"

[31] The crowd told them to shut up. They shouted louder: "Lord, Son of David — have mercy on us!"

[32] Jesus stopped. He called them over. "What do you want me to do for you?"

[33] "Lord," they said, "we want to see."

[34] Moved with compassion, Jesus touched their eyes. Instantly they could see — and they followed him.

[1] As they neared Jerusalem and reached Bethphage on the Mount of Olives, Jesus sent two disciples ahead [2] with instructions: "Go into the village in front of you. The moment you walk in, you'll find a donkey tied up, a colt beside her. Untie them. Bring them to me. [3] If anyone challenges you, just say, 'The Lord needs them.' They'll release them on the spot."

[4] This happened to fulfill what the prophet had spoken: [5] "Tell the daughter of Zion — look, your King is coming to you, humble, riding on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a beast of burden."

[6] The disciples went and did exactly as Jesus instructed. [7] They brought the donkey and the colt, threw their cloaks across them, and Jesus mounted. [8] A massive crowd spread their cloaks on the road. Others cut branches from the trees and laid them down like a carpet. [9] The crowds in front of him and behind him erupted: "Hosanna to the Son of David! Blessed is the one who comes in the name of the Lord! Hosanna in the highest!"

[10] When he entered Jerusalem, the whole city shook. "Who is this?" they kept asking. [11] And the crowds answered, "This is the prophet — Jesus, from Nazareth in Galilee."

[12] Jesus walked into the temple and drove out everyone buying and selling there. He flipped the tables of the money changers and kicked over the benches of those selling doves. [13] "It is written," he told them, "'My house will be called a house of prayer' — but you've turned it into a hideout for thieves."

[14] The blind and the lame came to him in the temple, and he healed them. [15] But when the chief priests and scribes saw the wonders he was doing and heard the children in the temple shouting, "Hosanna to the Son of David!" — they were furious. [16] "Do you hear what these kids are saying?" they demanded. "Yes," Jesus replied. "Have you never read: 'From the mouths of children and nursing infants you have ordained praise'?"

[17] Then he walked out, left the city, and spent the night in Bethany.

[18] Early the next morning, returning to the city, he was hungry. [19] He spotted a fig tree by the road and went over to it — but found nothing on it except leaves. "May no fruit ever come from you again," he said. And the tree withered on the spot.

[20] The disciples were stunned. "How did the fig tree wither so fast?" they asked. [21] Jesus answered, "I'm telling you the truth — if you have faith and don't doubt, you'll do more than what happened to this fig tree. You could say to this mountain, 'Get up and throw yourself into the sea,' and it would happen. [22] Whatever you ask for in prayer, believing — you'll receive it."

[23] He entered the temple, and as he was teaching, the chief priests and the elders of the people confronted him. "By what authority are you doing these things?" they demanded. "Who gave you this authority?"

[24] Jesus answered, "I'll ask you one question. Answer me, and I'll tell you by what authority I do these things. [25] John's baptism — where did it come from? Heaven, or human invention?"

They huddled together: "If we say 'from heaven,' he'll ask why we didn't believe him. [26] But if we say 'human' — the crowd will turn on us. They all hold John as a prophet."

[27] So they answered, "We don't know." And Jesus said, "Then I won't tell you by what authority I do these things either.

[28] What do you think? A man had two sons. He went to the first and said, 'Son, go work in the vineyard today.' [29] 'I won't,' the son said. But later he changed his mind and went. [30] The father went to the second and said the same thing. 'Sure, sir,' he said — and never went. [31] Which of the two did what the father wanted?"

"The first," they answered.

Jesus said to them, "I'm telling you the truth — the tax collectors and the prostitutes are entering the kingdom of God ahead of you. [32] John came to you walking the path of righteousness, and you didn't believe him. But the tax collectors and prostitutes believed him. And even after seeing that, you didn't change your minds and believe him.

[33] Listen to another parable. There was a landowner who planted a vineyard. He built a wall around it, dug a winepress in it, put up a watchtower, leased it to tenant farmers, and went away on a journey. [34] When harvest time came, he sent his servants to the tenants to collect his share of the fruit. [35] The tenants seized his servants — beat one, killed another, stoned a third. [36] He sent more servants, a bigger group this time. They did the same to them.

[37] Finally, he sent his son. 'They'll respect my son,' he thought.

[38] But when the tenants saw the son, they said to each other, 'This is the heir. Come on — let's kill him and take the inheritance for ourselves.' [39] So they grabbed him, threw him out of the vineyard, and killed him.

[40] When the owner of the vineyard comes — what will he do to those tenants?"

[41] "He'll bring those wretches to a wretched end," they said, "and lease the vineyard to other tenants who'll give him his fruit when it's due."

[42] Jesus said to them, "Have you never read in the Scriptures: 'The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone. This is the Lord's doing, and it's wonderful in our eyes'?

[43] So I'm telling you — the kingdom of God will be taken from you and given to a people who will produce its fruit. [44] Whoever falls on this stone will be broken to pieces. Whoever it falls on will be crushed."

[45] When the chief priests and Pharisees heard his parables, they knew he was talking about them. [46] They wanted to arrest him, but they were afraid of the crowds — because the people considered him a prophet.

[1] Jesus came at them again with another story.

[2] "The kingdom of heaven works like this: a king threw a wedding feast for his son.

[3] He sent his servants to call in those he'd already invited. They refused to come.

[4] So he sent more servants with a fresh message: 'Tell them everything is ready. The oxen are slaughtered, the prime cuts are roasting, the tables are set. Come to the wedding.'

[5] They couldn't be bothered. One headed off to his farm, another to his business.

[6] The rest grabbed his servants, beat them, killed them.

[7] The king's fury was volcanic. He sent his army, wiped out those murderers, and burned their city to the ground.

[8] Then he turned to his servants: 'The wedding is ready. The ones I invited weren't worthy.

[9] Go out to the street corners, the main roads, and invite anyone you find.'

[10] So the servants flooded the streets and brought in everyone they could find — good and bad alike. The wedding hall filled with guests.

[11] The king walked in to see who'd come, and he spotted a man with no wedding clothes on.

[12] 'Friend,' he said, 'how did you get in here without wedding clothes?' The man had nothing to say.

[13] The king told his attendants, 'Tie him up, hands and feet, and throw him out into the dark — where there's weeping and grinding of teeth.'

[14] Many are called. Few are chosen."

[15] The Pharisees huddled up, plotting how to trap him with his own words.

[16] They sent their disciples to him, along with some of Herod's people. "Teacher," they said, "we know you're honest. You teach God's way truthfully. You don't bend for anyone. You don't care about reputations.

[17] So tell us what you think: is it lawful to pay taxes to Caesar or not?"

[18] Jesus saw straight through them. "You frauds — why are you testing me?

[19] Show me the coin used for the tax." They handed him a denarius.

[20] "Whose face is this?" he asked. "Whose name?"

[21] "Caesar's," they said. "Then give Caesar what belongs to Caesar," he said, "and give God what belongs to God."

[22] They were stunned. They left him there and walked away.

[23] That same day the Sadducees came at him — the ones who say there's no resurrection. They had a question.

[24] "Teacher, Moses said: if a man dies without children, his brother must marry the widow and raise up offspring for him.

[25] Well, there were seven brothers in our scenario. The first married, died childless, and left his wife to his brother.

[26] Same with the second. Same with the third. All the way down to the seventh.

[27] Finally the woman died too.

[28] So in the resurrection — whose wife is she? All seven had her."

[29] Jesus answered, "You're lost — because you don't know the Scriptures and you don't know God's power.

[30] In the resurrection, people don't marry. They don't get married off. They're like the angels in heaven.

[31] And about the resurrection of the dead — haven't you read what God himself said to you?

[32] 'I am the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, the God of Jacob.' He is not the God of the dead. He is the God of the living."

[33] The crowds heard this and were floored by his teaching.

[34] When the Pharisees heard he'd silenced the Sadducees, they regrouped.

[35] One of them, an expert in the law, tested him with a question:

[36] "Teacher, which command in the law is the greatest?"

[37] Jesus said, "'Love the Lord your God with everything you are — all your heart, all your soul, all your mind.'

[38] That's the great one. That's the first.

[39] The second is like it: 'Love your neighbor as yourself.'

[40] Every line of the Law, every word of the Prophets — it all hangs on these two."

[41] While the Pharisees were still gathered, Jesus turned the tables and asked them:

[42] "What's your take on the Messiah? Whose son is he?" "David's," they said.

[43] "Then how is it," he asked, "that David, speaking by the Spirit, calls him Lord? He says:

[44] 'The Lord said to my Lord: Sit at my right hand until I put your enemies under your feet.'

[45] If David calls him Lord — how is he David's son?"

[46] Nobody could come up with an answer. And from that day on, no one dared ask him another question.

[1] Then Jesus turned to the crowds and to his disciples and said:

[2] "The scribes and Pharisees have planted themselves in Moses' seat. [3] So do what they tell you — but don't live the way they live. They preach one thing and practice another. [4] They strap crushing loads onto people's backs but won't lift a finger to help carry them. [5] Everything they do is performance. They widen their prayer boxes and lengthen their tassels so everyone can see. [6] They love the head table at banquets and the front row in the synagogue, [7] the bows in the marketplace, the title 'Rabbi' rolling off everyone's tongue.

[8] But you — don't let anyone call you Rabbi. You have one Teacher, and you are all brothers. [9] Don't call anyone on earth your Father. You have one Father, and he is in heaven. [10] Don't let yourselves be called Instructors. You have one Instructor — the Messiah. [11] The greatest among you will be your servant. [12] Lift yourself up and you'll be brought down. Bring yourself low and you'll be lifted up.

[13] Disaster on you, scribes and Pharisees, frauds — you slam the door of the kingdom in people's faces. You won't go in yourselves, and you block everyone trying to enter.

[15] Disaster on you, scribes and Pharisees, frauds — you cross sea and continent to make a single convert, and once you've got him, you make him twice the son of hell you are.

[16] Disaster on you, blind guides. You say, 'Swear by the temple — meaningless. Swear by the gold of the temple — binding.' [17] You blind fools — which is greater, the gold or the temple that makes the gold sacred? [18] You say, 'Swear by the altar — meaningless. Swear by the gift on the altar — binding.' [19] Blind men. Which is greater, the gift or the altar that makes the gift sacred? [20] Swear by the altar and you swear by everything on it. [21] Swear by the temple and you swear by the One who lives there. [22] Swear by heaven and you swear by God's throne and the One seated on it.

[23] Disaster on you, scribes and Pharisees, frauds. You meticulously tithe your mint, your dill, your cumin — and you've abandoned the weight of the Law: justice, mercy, faith. These you should have done without leaving the others undone. [24] Blind guides — you strain a gnat out of your drink and swallow a camel whole.

[25] Disaster on you, scribes and Pharisees, frauds. You polish the outside of the cup till it shines, but inside it's full of greed and excess. [26] Blind Pharisee — clean the inside of the cup first, and the outside will be clean too.

[27] Disaster on you, scribes and Pharisees, frauds. You're whitewashed tombs — gleaming on the outside, but inside, nothing but bones and rot. [28] That's you. Polished righteousness on the surface, lawlessness and pretense beneath.

[29] Disaster on you, scribes and Pharisees, frauds. You build monuments for the prophets and decorate the graves of the righteous, [30] and you say, 'If we'd lived back then, we'd never have spilled the prophets' blood with our ancestors.' [31] In saying it, you confess your own bloodline — you are the sons of the murderers. [32] So go ahead. Finish what they started.

[33] Snakes. Viper's brood. How do you expect to escape the sentence of hell? [34] Watch — I'm sending you prophets and wise men and scribes. Some you'll kill and crucify. Some you'll flog in your synagogues and chase from town to town. [35] And all the righteous blood ever spilled on this earth will land on you — from the blood of righteous Abel to the blood of Zechariah son of Berekiah, whom you murdered between the sanctuary and the altar. [36] I'm telling you straight: all of it falls on this generation.

[37] Jerusalem, Jerusalem — killer of prophets, stoner of those sent to you. How many times I wanted to gather your children the way a hen gathers her chicks under her wings — and you refused. [38] Look at your house now. Empty. Abandoned. [39] I tell you, you won't see me again until you say, 'Blessed is the one who comes in the name of the Lord.'"

[1] Jesus walked out of the temple and was leaving when his disciples came over to point out the temple buildings to him. [2] "You see all this?" he said. "I'm telling you the truth — not one stone will be left standing on another. Every last one will be torn down."

[3] Later, while he was sitting on the Mount of Olives, the disciples came to him privately. "Tell us," they said, "when will this happen? And what will be the sign of your coming and the end of the age?"

[4] Jesus answered: "Watch yourselves. Don't let anyone deceive you. [5] Plenty will come using my name, claiming, 'I am the Messiah,' and they'll pull crowds in. [6] You'll hear about wars and you'll hear the drumbeat of wars — don't lose your nerve. This has to happen, but it isn't the end yet. [7] Nation will rise against nation, kingdom against kingdom. There will be famines and earthquakes in place after place. [8] All of this is just the first contractions of labor.

[9] "Then they'll hand you over to suffer, and they'll kill you. Every nation will hate you because of my name. [10] Many will fall away. They'll betray each other. They'll hate each other. [11] False prophets will swarm out and pull masses of people into deception. [12] Lawlessness will multiply until the love of most people goes cold. [13] But the one who endures to the end — that one will be saved. [14] And this good news of the kingdom will be proclaimed across the entire inhabited world as a witness to every nation. Then the end will come.

[15] "So when you see the abomination that brings desolation — the one the prophet Daniel spoke about — standing in the holy place (reader, understand what this means), [16] then those in Judea need to run for the mountains. [17] If you're on the roof, don't go back down for anything in the house. [18] If you're out in the field, don't turn back for your coat. [19] God help the pregnant women and the nursing mothers in those days. [20] Pray that your flight won't happen in winter or on the Sabbath. [21] Because what's coming is anguish unlike anything from the beginning of the world until now — and nothing like it will ever come again. [22] If those days weren't cut short, no flesh would survive. But for the sake of the chosen, those days will be cut short.

[23] "If anyone tells you then, 'Look, here's the Messiah!' or 'There he is!' — don't believe it. [24] Because false messiahs and false prophets will rise up and pull off signs and wonders so convincing they'd deceive even the chosen, if that were possible. [25] I'm telling you this in advance.

[26] "So if they say, 'He's out in the wilderness,' don't go out there. If they say, 'He's hidden in the inner rooms,' don't believe it. [27] Because the coming of the Son of Man will be like lightning that splits the sky from east to west — visible everywhere at once. [28] Wherever the carcass is, the vultures will gather.

[29] "Immediately after the anguish of those days,

the sun will go black,
the moon will withhold her light,
the stars will fall from the sky,
and the powers of the heavens will be shaken.

[30] "Then the sign of the Son of Man will appear in the sky, and every tribe on earth will beat their chests in grief, and they'll see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of heaven with power and blazing glory. [31] He'll send his angels with a thunderous trumpet blast, and they'll gather his chosen ones from the four winds, from one end of the heavens to the other.

[32] "Learn this lesson from the fig tree: when its branch goes tender and the leaves push out, you know summer is close. [33] In the same way, when you see all these things, you know he's close — right at the door. [34] I'm telling you the truth: this generation will not pass away until every one of these things has happened. [35] Heaven and earth will pass away — my words will not.

[36] "But that day and hour — no one knows. Not the angels in heaven, not even the Son. Only the Father. [37] When the Son of Man comes, it will be just like the days of Noah. [38] Before the flood, people were eating, drinking, marrying, getting married, right up to the day Noah boarded the ark. [39] They had no idea what was coming until the flood swept them all away. That's how it will be when the Son of Man comes. [40] Two men will be working in a field — one is taken, one is left. [41] Two women will be grinding at the mill — one is taken, one is left.

[42] "So stay alert. You don't know what day your Lord is coming. [43] Understand this: if the master of the house had known what hour the thief was coming, he would have stayed awake and not let his house be broken into. [44] So you have to be ready, because the Son of Man is coming at an hour you don't expect.

[45] "Who then is the faithful and wise servant — the one the master puts in charge of his household to give them their food at the right time? [46] Blessed is that servant whose master finds him doing exactly that when he comes home. [47] I'm telling you the truth: he'll put that servant in charge of everything he owns. [48] But if that servant is wicked and says to himself, 'My master is taking his time,' [49] and starts beating the other servants and eating and drinking with drunks — [50] the master of that servant will come on a day he doesn't expect, at an hour he doesn't know. [51] He'll cut him to pieces and throw him in with the hypocrites, where there will be weeping and the grinding of teeth.

[1] Then the kingdom of heaven will look like this: ten bridesmaids grabbed their lamps and went out to meet the groom.

[2] Five were fools. Five had sense.

[3] The fools snatched their lamps but brought no oil.

[4] The wise ones brought flasks of oil along with their lamps.

[5] The groom took his time. Eyelids grew heavy. They all fell asleep.

[6] At midnight — a shout: "The groom is here! Go out to meet him!"

[7] The bridesmaids jolted awake and started trimming their wicks.

[8] The fools begged the wise ones: "Give us some of your oil — our lamps are dying."

[9] But the wise ones answered, "No — there won't be enough for us and you. Go to the dealers and buy your own."

[10] While they were off shopping, the groom arrived. The ready ones walked into the wedding feast with him. The door shut.

[11] Later, the others showed up. "Sir! Sir! Open the door for us!"

[12] His answer cut through: "I'm telling you the truth — I don't know you."

[13] So stay awake. You do not know the day. You do not know the hour.

[14] It's like a man heading out of the country. He called in his servants and put his fortune in their hands.

[15] To one he gave five talents, to another two, to another one — measuring out by what each could handle. Then he left.

[16] The man with five went to work immediately, traded hard, and doubled it.

[17] The man with two did the same — doubled it.

[18] But the man with one dug a hole in the ground and buried his master's money.

[19] A long time passed. Then the master came back to settle accounts.

[20] The first stepped forward with ten talents in hand: "Master, you gave me five. Look — I've made five more."

[21] His master said, "Well done. You're a good servant, a faithful one. You were trustworthy with a little — I'll put you in charge of much. Step into your master's joy."

[22] Next came the one with two. "Master, you gave me two. Look — I've made two more."

[23] His master said, "Well done. You're a good servant, a faithful one. You were trustworthy with a little — I'll put you in charge of much. Step into your master's joy."

[24] Then the one with the single talent came forward. "Master, I knew you — a hard man, harvesting where you never planted, gathering where you never scattered seed.

[25] I was afraid. So I went and hid your money in the dirt. Here — take what belongs to you."

[26] His master fired back: "You wicked, lazy servant. So you knew I harvest where I didn't plant and gather where I didn't scatter?

[27] Then you should have at least put my money with the bankers — I'd have come home and collected it with interest.

[28] Take the talent from him. Give it to the one with ten.

[29] Because everyone who has — more will be given, and they'll overflow. But the one who has nothing — even what they have will be ripped away.

[30] As for this useless servant — throw him into the dark outside. There will be weeping out there. Teeth grinding."

[31] When the Son of Man arrives in his glory, with every angel beside him, he will take his seat on a throne ablaze with majesty.

[32] Every nation will be assembled in front of him, and he will sort them out the way a shepherd separates sheep from goats —

[33] the sheep on his right, the goats on his left.

[34] Then the King will say to those on his right: "Come, you who carry my Father's blessing. Take possession of the kingdom prepared for you since the foundation of the world.

[35] Because I was hungry and you fed me. I was thirsty and you gave me a drink. I was a stranger and you took me in.

[36] I was naked and you clothed me. I was sick and you cared for me. I was in prison and you came to me."

[37] Then the righteous will answer, bewildered: "Lord — when did we see you hungry and feed you, or thirsty and give you a drink?

[38] When did we see you a stranger and take you in, or naked and clothe you?

[39] When did we see you sick or in prison and come to you?"

[40] And the King will answer: "I'm telling you the truth — whatever you did for one of the least of these brothers and sisters of mine, you did for me."

[41] Then he will turn to those on his left: "Get away from me — you're cursed. Into the eternal fire prepared for the devil and his angels.

[42] Because I was hungry and you gave me nothing. Thirsty and you gave me nothing.

[43] A stranger and you turned me away. Naked and you left me exposed. Sick and in prison and you couldn't be bothered."

[44] Then they too will answer: "Lord — when did we see you hungry or thirsty or a stranger or naked or sick or in prison and not help you?"

[45] He will answer: "I'm telling you the truth — whatever you failed to do for one of the least of these, you failed to do for me."

[46] And they will walk away into endless punishment, but the righteous into endless life.

[1] When Jesus finished saying all this, he turned to his disciples.

[2] "Two days from now—Passover. And the Son of Man will be handed over to be crucified."

[3] At that very hour, the chief priests and the elders of the people were gathering in the courtyard of Caiaphas, the high priest,

[4] plotting how to seize Jesus by stealth and kill him.

[5] "Not during the festival," they said. "The crowd will riot."

[6] Meanwhile, in Bethany, Jesus was at the home of Simon the leper,

[7] when a woman approached him with an alabaster jar of perfume—extravagant, priceless—and poured it over his head as he reclined at the table.

[8] The disciples saw it and were furious. "What a waste.

[9] This could have been sold for a fortune and given to the poor."

[10] Jesus knew exactly what they were thinking. "Why are you giving this woman a hard time? What she's done for me is beautiful.

[11] The poor will always be with you. I won't.

[12] When she poured this perfume on my body, she was preparing me for burial.

[13] I'm telling you—wherever this gospel is proclaimed, in all the world, what she just did will be told. Her memorial."

[14] Then one of the Twelve—Judas Iscariot—went to the chief priests.

[15] "What will you give me if I hand him over to you?" They counted out thirty pieces of silver.

[16] From that moment on, he was watching for his chance.

[17] On the first day of Unleavened Bread, the disciples came to Jesus. "Where do you want us to prepare the Passover for you?"

[18] "Go into the city, to a certain man," he said. "Tell him: 'The Teacher says, My time is close. I'm keeping the Passover at your house, with my disciples.'"

[19] They did exactly what Jesus told them. They got the Passover ready.

[20] Evening came. He took his place at the table with the Twelve.

[21] As they were eating, he said it: "One of you is going to betray me."

[22] They were devastated. One after another: "Surely not me, Lord?"

[23] He answered, "The one who dipped his hand into the bowl with me—he's the one who will betray me.

[24] The Son of Man goes the way it is written about him. But the man who hands him over—better for that man if he had never been born."

[25] Judas, the betrayer, said, "Surely not me, Rabbi?" Jesus answered, "You said it."

[26] While they were eating, Jesus took bread, blessed it, broke it, gave it to the disciples. "Take. Eat. This is my body."

[27] Then he took the cup, gave thanks, handed it to them. "All of you—drink.

[28] This is my blood of the covenant, poured out for many, for the forgiveness of sins.

[29] I'm telling you—I won't drink from the fruit of the vine again until that day when I drink it new with you in my Father's kingdom."

[30] They sang a hymn and went out to the Mount of Olives.

[31] Then Jesus told them: "Tonight—every one of you will fall away because of me. It's written: I will strike the shepherd, and the sheep of the flock will be scattered.

[32] But after I am raised, I'll go ahead of you into Galilee."

[33] Peter answered, "Everyone else may fall away because of you—I never will."

[34] Jesus said, "I'm telling you the truth. Tonight, before the rooster crows, you'll deny me three times."

[35] Peter insisted, "Even if I have to die with you, I will never deny you." All the disciples said the same.

[36] Then Jesus went with them to a place called Gethsemane. "Sit here while I go over there and pray."

[37] He took Peter and the two sons of Zebedee with him. Grief crashed into him. Anguish.

[38] "My soul is drowning in sorrow—I'm dying with it. Stay here. Keep watch with me."

[39] He went a little farther, fell on his face, and prayed: "My Father, if it's possible, take this cup away from me. Yet not what I want—what you want."

[40] He came back to the disciples and found them sleeping. He said to Peter, "So—you couldn't keep watch with me for one hour?

[41] Stay awake. Pray, so you don't fall into the test. The spirit is willing, but the flesh is weak."

[42] He went away a second time and prayed: "My Father, if this cup cannot pass unless I drink it—your will be done."

[43] He came back. Again, sleeping. Their eyes were too heavy.

[44] He left them, went off again, prayed a third time, the same words.

[45] Then he came to the disciples and said, "Still sleeping? Still resting? Look—the hour has arrived. The Son of Man is being handed over into the hands of sinners.

[46] Get up. Let's go. Here comes the one who's betraying me."

[47] He was still speaking when Judas arrived—one of the Twelve—and with him a mob, swords and clubs, sent by the chief priests and elders of the people.

[48] The betrayer had given them a sign: "The one I kiss—that's him. Arrest him."

[49] He walked straight up to Jesus. "Hello, Rabbi." And he kissed him.

[50] Jesus said, "Friend—do what you came to do." Then they closed in on Jesus, seized him, took him.

[51] One of those with Jesus reached for his sword, drew it, struck the high priest's servant—cut off his ear.

[52] Jesus said to him, "Put your sword back where it belongs. Everyone who picks up the sword dies by the sword.

[53] Do you think I can't call on my Father, and he'd send me—right now—more than twelve legions of angels?

[54] But then how would the Scriptures be fulfilled, saying it must happen this way?"

[55] Then Jesus said to the crowd, "You've come out with swords and clubs to capture me, like I'm some kind of bandit? Day after day I sat in the temple teaching, and you didn't lay a hand on me.

[56] All of this is happening so that the writings of the prophets will be fulfilled." Then every disciple abandoned him and ran.

[57] Those who had arrested Jesus took him to Caiaphas the high priest, where the scribes and elders had gathered.

[58] Peter followed at a distance, all the way to the high priest's courtyard. He went inside and sat with the guards, waiting to see how it would end.

[59] The chief priests and the whole council were trying to find false testimony against Jesus so they could put him to death.

[60] They couldn't find any, though many false witnesses came forward. Finally two stepped up

[61] and said, "This man said, 'I can tear down the temple of God and build it back in three days.'"

[62] The high priest stood. "You have no answer? What about this testimony against you?"

[63] Jesus said nothing. The high priest said, "I charge you under oath by the living God—tell us. Are you the Messiah, the Son of God?"

[64] Jesus said, "You said it. But I tell you all—from now on you will see the Son of Man seated at the right hand of Power, coming on the clouds of heaven."

[65] The high priest tore his robes. "Blasphemy! Why do we need any more witnesses? You heard the blasphemy yourselves.

[66] What's your verdict?" They answered, "He deserves death."

[67] Then they spat in his face. They punched him. Others slapped him
