[27] Don't withhold good from those it belongs to, when it's in your hand to give.

[28] Don't tell your neighbor, "Come back tomorrow, I'll help you then" — when you have it right now.

[29] Don't scheme against your neighbor — the one who lives next door, trusting you.

[30] Don't pick a fight with someone for no reason, when they've done you no harm.

[31] Don't envy the violent. Don't choose any of their roads.

[32] The crooked are disgusting to the Lord, but the upright — he lets them in on his secrets.

[33] The Lord's curse sits on the house of the wicked, but he blesses the home of the righteous.

[34] He mocks the mockers, but to the humble he gives grace.

[35] The wise inherit honor. Fools inherit shame.

[1] Listen, sons—a father is teaching. Pay attention, and you'll gain understanding.

[2] What I'm giving you is solid. Don't walk away from it.

[3] I was a son once too—my father's boy, my mother's tender favorite.

[4] And he taught me. He said: "Lock my words inside your heart. Guard my commands and live.

[5] Get wisdom. Get understanding. Don't forget. Don't drift from what I'm telling you.

[6] Don't abandon her, and she'll keep you safe. Love her, and she'll stand guard over your life.

[7] Here it is, the first thing: get wisdom. Whatever it costs you, get understanding.

[8] Treasure her, and she lifts you. Embrace her, and she crowns you with honor.

[9] She sets a wreath of grace on your head, hands you a crown of beauty."

[10] Listen, my son. Take in what I'm saying. The years of your life will multiply.

[11] I'm showing you the road of wisdom. I'm walking you down straight paths.

[12] When you move, nothing will trip you. When you run, you won't fall.

[13] Grip this instruction. Don't let it slip. Keep it close—it's your life.

[14] Don't set foot on the road of the wicked. Don't take a single step where evil walks.

[15] Avoid it. Don't even cross through. Turn away. Keep going.

[16] Those people can't sleep until they've done damage. Rest evades them until they've tripped someone up.

[17] They eat wickedness like bread. They drink violence like wine.

[18] But the path of the righteous is dawn light—getting brighter, brighter, until the day stands at full noon.

[19] The way of the wicked is pitch dark. They stumble and never know what hit them.

[20] My son—listen. Lean in to what I'm saying.

[21] Don't let these words slip from view. Keep them buried deep in your heart.

[22] They are life to whoever finds them, healing to the whole body.

[23] Above everything else you guard—guard your heart. Every stream of your life flows from that spring.

[24] Cut the crooked talk out of your mouth. Push twisted speech far from your lips.

[25] Fix your eyes forward. Keep your gaze locked on what's ahead.

[26] Measure the ground beneath your feet. Make all your paths solid.

[27] Don't drift right. Don't drift left. Pull your foot back from anything evil.

[1] My son, lock in on what wisdom is telling you. Lean toward understanding. [2] Hold onto discernment. Let your lips guard what you know.

[3] Because the lips of the seductress drip honey, and her mouth slides smoother than oil — [4] but the aftertaste is wormwood, and what felt like silk becomes a blade with two edges. [5] Her feet are walking straight down to death. Every step she takes is sinking toward the grave. [6] She refuses to consider the road of life. Her paths wander and she has no idea — and that is exactly how she wants it.

[7] So listen to me, sons. Do not turn from what I am saying. [8] Keep your distance from her. Do not even come near the door of her house. [9] Or you will hand your dignity to strangers, your years to someone merciless. [10] Outsiders will feast on what you built. Everything you earned will end up in another man's house. [11] And at the end — when your flesh and your body are spent — you will groan. [12] You will say: How did I despise correction? How did my heart sneer at every warning? [13] I would not listen to my teachers. I refused to lean in when they spoke. [14] I was a breath away from total ruin, right there in front of the whole assembly.

[15] Drink from your own cistern. Drink the running water of your own well. [16] Should your springs spill into the street, your channels of water into the public square? [17] They belong to you. To you alone. Not to strangers sharing in what is yours. [18] Let your fountain be blessed. Take joy in the wife of your youth — [19] a graceful doe, a beautiful gazelle. Let her body satisfy you always. Let her love intoxicate you, every time. [20] Why, my son, would you be drunk on a stranger? Why pull a foreign woman to your chest?

[21] Because every road a man walks is laid bare before the LORD. He examines every path. [22] The wicked man's own crimes will hunt him down. The ropes of his sin will hold him fast. [23] He dies for lack of discipline. He staggers, lost, drowning in his own folly.

[1] My son, if you've cosigned for your neighbor, shaken hands on a stranger's debt — [2] you're snared. Your own words have trapped you. Your own mouth has tied the knot. [3] So do this, son, and get yourself out — because you've fallen into your neighbor's hands. Go. Humble yourself. Hound him until he releases you. [4] Don't sleep. Don't even let your eyes close. [5] Tear free like a gazelle from the hunter's grip, like a bird breaking the trapper's net.

[6] Go to the ant, you sluggard. Watch how she works. Get wise. [7] No chief over her. No officer. No king. [8] Yet she stockpiles her food in summer, hoards her harvest before the cold. [9] How long will you lie there, lazy one? When will you finally get up? [10] "A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest" — [11] and poverty stalks in like a thief, want like an armed man.

[12] A worthless man, a wicked man — he walks around with a crooked mouth, [13] winking, signaling with his feet, gesturing with his fingers, [14] twisted inside, plotting evil nonstop, sowing discord wherever he goes. [15] So disaster will jump him without warning. He'll be broken in an instant — beyond repair.

[16] Six things the LORD hates. Seven that disgust him: [17] arrogant eyes, a lying tongue, hands that spill innocent blood, [18] a heart that drafts evil schemes, feet that race to do harm, [19] a false witness breathing out lies, and anyone who sets brothers against each other.

[20] My son, hold tight to your father's command. Don't throw away your mother's teaching. [21] Tie them over your heart. Wear them around your neck. [22] When you walk, they'll guide you. When you sleep, they'll guard you. When you wake, they'll speak to you. [23] Because the command is a lamp, the teaching a light, and the corrections of discipline are the road to life — [24] keeping you from the dangerous woman, from the smooth tongue of the stranger's wife.

[25] Don't crave her beauty in your heart. Don't let her eyelids hunt you down. [26] A prostitute costs you a loaf of bread — but another man's wife will hunt your very life. [27] Can a man scoop fire into his lap and his clothes not burn? [28] Can he walk on hot coals and his feet not be scorched? [29] So with the man who sleeps with his neighbor's wife. No one who touches her walks away clean.

[30] People don't despise a thief who steals to fill his empty stomach. [31] Still — if caught, he pays sevenfold. He hands over everything he owns. [32] But the man who commits adultery has no sense. He's destroying himself, and he knows it. [33] Wounds and disgrace are what he gets. His shame will never wash off. [34] Because jealousy enrages a husband — and on the day of revenge, he will not hold back. [35] No ransom will satisfy him. No bribe, no matter how large, will buy him off.

[1] Son, hold tight to what I say. Store my commands deep inside you. [2] Guard them and live. Treat my teaching like the pupil of your eye. [3] Tie them around your fingers. Carve them onto the walls of your heart.

[4] Tell wisdom, "You're my sister." Call understanding your closest friend. [5] They'll keep you clear of the seductress — the smooth-talking stranger who knows exactly what she's doing.

[6] One day I stood at my window, peering through the lattice, [7] and I watched the street. Among the clueless, the easily-led, I spotted a young man with no sense at all. [8] He took the corner near her house — strolling, casual — and headed straight down her street. [9] Twilight was falling. Evening dimmed into night, and the dark closed in.

[10] And there she came to meet him — dressed like a prostitute, calculating behind her eyes. [11] Loud. Restless. The kind of woman whose feet refuse to stay home — [12] always out in the street, in the squares, lurking on every corner. [13] She grabbed him. Kissed him. Looked him dead in the face and said, without a flicker of shame:

[14] "I owed a peace offering today, and I paid my vows. [15] So I came looking for you — wanted you specifically — and here you are. [16] I've made the bed up with colored linens, fine Egyptian fabric. [17] I've drenched the sheets in myrrh, aloes, cinnamon. [18] Come on. Let's drink love till morning. Let's lose ourselves in each other. [19] My husband isn't home. He's gone — far away. [20] Took the money bag with him. Won't be back till the full moon."

[21] She wore him down with her words. Her smooth lips reeled him in. [22] He followed her like an ox walking to slaughter — like a deer stepping into the snare, [23] until an arrow goes through its liver. Like a bird flying straight into the net, never seeing it was about to die.

[24] So listen to me, sons. Pay attention. Hear what I'm telling you. [25] Don't let your heart wander her direction. Don't drift onto her streets. [26] She has dropped countless men. Her body count is staggering. [27] Her house is a highway to the grave, descending straight to the chambers of death.

[1] Listen — Wisdom is calling. Insight is raising her voice.

[2] She takes her stand on the high ground, at the crossroads, where every path meets.

[3] At the city gates, at the entrance to town, at the open doors she cries out:

[4] "I'm calling to you, all of you. My voice is for every human alive.

[5] You who are naive — get smart. You who are foolish — wake up and learn.

[6] Listen — what I have to say matters. My lips open to speak what is right.

[7] My mouth tells the truth. Wickedness disgusts me.

[8] Every word I speak is straight. Nothing twisted, nothing crooked in any of it.

[9] To anyone with sense, my words are clear. To anyone seeking knowledge, they ring true.

[10] Take my correction over silver. Take knowledge over the finest gold.

[11] Wisdom outvalues rubies. Nothing you could ever want compares.

[12] I am Wisdom — I live with shrewdness, and I know how to find knowledge and discretion.

[13] To revere the Lord is to hate evil. I detest arrogance, pride, the corrupt road, the lying tongue.

[14] Good counsel and clear-eyed judgment belong to me. I have understanding. I have power.

[15] Kings reign because of me. Rulers decree what is just because of me.

[16] By me, princes govern. By me, nobles — every righteous judge on earth.

[17] I love those who love me. Those who search for me find me.

[18] Wealth and honor walk with me — lasting riches, true success.

[19] What I produce beats gold — even the purest gold. What I yield beats top-grade silver.

[20] I walk the road of righteousness, the well-worn path of justice,

[21] handing out inheritance to those who love me, filling their storehouses to the brim.

[22] The Lord formed me at the very beginning of his way, before any of his ancient works.

[23] I was set in place from eternity — from the start, before the earth existed.

[24] When there were no oceans, I was born — when no springs gushed with water.

[25] Before the mountains were settled, before the hills — I was born.

[26] Before he made the earth, the open fields, the first specks of the world's dust.

[27] When he stretched the heavens into place — I was there. When he drew the horizon across the face of the deep,

[28] when he fastened the clouds above, when he locked the springs of the deep,

[29] when he set the sea its boundary so the waters would not breach his command, when he laid the foundations of the earth —

[30] I was right there beside him, a master craftsman, his daily delight, dancing in his presence always,

[31] dancing across the wide world he had made, delighting in the human family.

[32] So now, my children, listen to me. The ones who keep my ways are blessed.

[33] Take the instruction. Get wise. Don't throw it away.

[34] Blessed is the one who listens to me — who shows up at my door day after day, waiting at the threshold,

[35] because whoever finds me finds life and walks away with the Lord's favor.

[36] But the one who misses me wounds himself. Everyone who hates me has chosen death."

[1] Wisdom built her house. She carved its seven pillars.

[2] She slaughtered the meat, mixed the wine, set the table.

[3] She sent out her servant girls. From the city's highest point she calls,

[4] "Anyone naive? Come in here. Anyone empty-headed?" — she says to him —

[5] "Come. Eat my bread. Drink the wine I mixed.

[6] Walk away from your simplicity and live. Step onto the road of understanding."

[7] Correct a mocker and you collect insults. Confront the wicked and you walk away bruised.

[8] Do not bother correcting a mocker — he will only hate you. Correct a wise person and he will love you for it.

[9] Teach the wise and they grow wiser. Instruct the righteous and they deepen their grasp.

[10] Reverence for the LORD — that is where wisdom begins. Knowing the Holy One — that is understanding.

[11] Through me your days multiply. Through me years are added to your life.

[12] If you are wise, the wisdom is yours to keep. If you mock, you alone will pay.

[13] Folly is a loud woman — reckless, knowing nothing.

[14] She sits at the door of her house, perched on a chair at the city's heights,

[15] calling out to everyone passing by, everyone minding their own business on the road,

[16] "Anyone naive? Come in here." And to the empty-headed she says,

[17] "Stolen water is sweet. Bread eaten in secret tastes best."

[18] But he has no idea — the dead are in there. Her guests are already in the grave's basement.

[1] The proverbs of Solomon.

A wise son brings his father joy; a foolish son breaks his mother's heart.

[2] Treasure built on corruption pays nothing in the end. Integrity, though, pulls you back from the grave.

[3] The Lord will not let the righteous starve, but he slams the door on the greed of the wicked.

[4] Lazy hands invite poverty. Diligent hands build wealth.

[5] Gather in summer and you're a son worth having. Sleep through harvest and you've shamed your family.

[6] Blessings crown the righteous; violence muzzles the mouth of the wicked.

[7] The memory of the righteous becomes a benediction. The name of the wicked rots.

[8] The wise heart absorbs instruction. The babbling fool collapses.

[9] Walk straight, walk safe. Walk crooked, get found out.

[10] A sly wink wounds; a babbling fool collapses.

[11] The mouth of the righteous is a fountain of life. The mouth of the wicked hides violence behind its teeth.

[12] Hatred picks fights. Love throws a blanket over every offense.

[13] Wisdom rests on the lips of the discerning. A rod waits on the back of the senseless.

[14] The wise stockpile knowledge. The fool's mouth is a disaster waiting to break loose.

[15] The rich man's wealth is his fortress; the poor man's poverty is his ruin.

[16] What the righteous earns becomes life. What the wicked gains becomes sin.

[17] Take correction and you blaze a trail toward life. Refuse it and you drag others into the wilderness with you.

[18] Whoever buries hatred behind a smile is a liar, and whoever spreads slander is a fool.

[19] Words multiply, sin multiplies. The wise know when to shut their mouth.

[20] The tongue of the righteous is refined silver. The heart of the wicked is worthless scrap.

[21] The righteous feed multitudes with their words. Fools die because they have no sense.

[22] It is the Lord's blessing that makes a person rich, and he adds no heartache to it.

[23] The fool finds entertainment in evil. The wise find it in understanding.

[24] What the wicked dreads will hunt him down. What the righteous longs for will be handed to him.

[25] When the storm rolls through, the wicked are gone. The righteous stand — foundations that nothing can move.

[26] Like vinegar to teeth and smoke to eyes — that's a lazy worker to the one who sent him.

[27] Fear of the Lord stretches your life out. The years of the wicked get cut short.

[28] The hope of the righteous opens into joy. The hope of the wicked dies stillborn.

[29] The way of the Lord is a stronghold for the upright and a wrecking ball to those who do evil.

[30] The righteous will never be uprooted. The wicked will not get a home in the land.

[31] The mouth of the righteous blossoms with wisdom. The deceitful tongue gets torn out by the roots.

[32] The lips of the righteous know what fits the moment. The mouth of the wicked spits out only what twists.

[1] A rigged scale disgusts the LORD; an honest weight is what He delights in.

[2] Arrogance shows up, and shame walks in right behind it — but wisdom keeps company with the humble.

[3] Integrity steers the upright; duplicity destroys the treacherous.

[4] Wealth means nothing on the day wrath falls. Righteousness is what pulls you out of death.

[5] The clean life of the blameless smooths their road; the wicked collapse under the weight of their own wickedness.

[6] Righteousness rescues the upright; the treacherous get trapped by their own appetites.

[7] When the wicked die, hope dies with them — every expectation built on power evaporates.

[8] The righteous get pulled out of trouble; the wicked walk right into it.

[9] The godless gut their neighbor with their mouth — but knowledge sets the righteous free.

[10] When the righteous flourish, the city throws a party. When the wicked perish, the streets erupt in cheers.

[11] A city rises on the blessing of the upright and is torn down by the mouth of the wicked.

[12] Anyone who belittles a neighbor lacks sense; a person of understanding holds their tongue.

[13] A gossip leaks secrets everywhere they go; a trustworthy spirit keeps a confidence sealed.

[14] Without guidance, a nation falls. Salvation lives in a roomful of wise counselors.

[15] Co-sign for a stranger and you'll suffer for it. Refuse to shake on it and you're safe.

[16] A gracious woman holds onto honor; ruthless men hold onto nothing but cash.

[17] Kindness pays you back in your own soul; cruelty wrecks the one who wields it.

[18] The wicked earn wages made of smoke; the one who sows righteousness collects a real reward.

[19] Lock onto righteousness and you lock onto life. Chase evil and you chase your own grave.

[20] Twisted hearts disgust the LORD; lives lived straight delight Him.

[21] You can count on this: the wicked will not escape judgment, but the children of the righteous will walk free.

[22] A beautiful woman without discretion is a gold ring in a pig's snout.

[23] What the righteous long for ends in good. What the wicked hope for ends in fury.

[24] One person scatters and grows richer; another hoards and slides into poverty.

[25] The generous soul gets fat. Pour yourself out, and you'll be poured into.

[26] The one who hoards grain — the people will curse his name. Blessing crowns the head of the one who sells.

[27] Hunt for good and you find favor. Hunt for evil and evil hunts you down.

[28] Trust your money and watch yourself wither. The righteous spring up green like new leaves.

[29] Wreck your own household and you'll inherit the wind — and the fool ends up enslaved to the wise.

[30] The fruit of a righteous life is a tree of life, and the wise one wins souls.

[31] If even the righteous get what's coming to them on earth — how much more the wicked and the sinner?

[1] Love correction, love knowledge. Hate being called out? That's not principle — that's stupidity.

[2] The good earn favor from the LORD. The schemer earns His verdict.

[3] No one builds a life on wickedness and keeps it standing. But the roots of the righteous? Unshakable.

[4] A wife of substance is her husband's crown. One who shames him is rot in his bones.

[5] The righteous think straight. The wicked scheme crooked.

[6] The words of the wicked are an ambush. The words of the upright pull people out of the trap.

[7] Topple the wicked and they vanish. But the house of the righteous stands.

[8] You'll be praised for the sharpness of your mind — and despised for the warp in your heart.

[9] Better to be a nobody with bread on the table than a somebody starving for show.

[10] The righteous care even how their animals feel. The "kindness" of the wicked is cruelty dressed up.

[11] Work your field and you'll eat your fill. Chase fantasies and you're a fool.

[12] The wicked crave the spoils of evil men. The righteous have roots that bear their own fruit.

[13] A liar gets tangled in his own words. The righteous walk free.

[14] What comes out of your mouth feeds you. What your hands do comes home to you.

[15] The fool is certain he's right. The wise listen.

[16] The fool's anger erupts on contact. The shrewd swallow the insult and move on.

[17] Truthful witness, honest verdict. False witness, fraud all the way down.

[18] Some words stab like swords. The tongue of the wise brings healing.

[19] Truth lasts forever. A lie has the lifespan of a blink.

[20] Plotting evil fills the heart with deceit. Counseling peace fills it with joy.

[21] No real harm overtakes the righteous. The wicked are buried in trouble.

[22] The LORD detests lying lips. He delights in those who tell the truth.

[23] The shrewd hold their knowledge close. Fools broadcast their foolishness.

[24] Diligent hands take charge. Lazy ones end up enslaved.

[25] Anxiety crushes the heart. A kind word lifts it.

[26] The righteous guide their neighbors well. The wicked lead them straight off the cliff.

[27] The lazy man won't even cook what he caught. But the diligent — wealth is his treasure.

[28] On the path of righteousness — life. On that road, death has no address.

[1] A wise child takes a father's discipline to heart; a mocker won't even hear a rebuke.

[2] What you say feeds you well—if your words are good. But the treacherous crave only violence.

[3] Guard your mouth, guard your life. Open it carelessly, and you're finished.

[4] The lazy man wants and wants and gets nothing. The diligent? Everything he touches turns rich.

[5] The righteous can't stand a lie. The wicked stink up the room and shame everyone in it.

[6] Integrity stands watch over the honest. Wickedness drags sinners down to ruin.

[7] One man parades his wealth and owns nothing. Another hides his fortune behind rags.

[8] A rich man's ransom is his wealth. A poor man? No one even threatens him.

[9] The light of the righteous burns bright. The lamp of the wicked sputters out.

[10] Arrogance breeds nothing but fighting. Wisdom belongs to those willing to be advised.

[11] Money made fast bleeds away fast. Money built up handful by handful grows.

[12] Hope deferred makes the heart sick. A longing fulfilled is a tree of life.

[13] Despise the word, and the word will collect what you owe. Respect the command, and you'll be repaid in full.

[14] The teaching of the wise is a fountain of life, pulling you back from the snares of death.

[15] Real insight wins favor. The road of the treacherous is a hard road that never ends.

[16] Every shrewd person acts with understanding. The fool spreads his stupidity wide for all to see.

[17] A bad messenger lands in trouble. A faithful envoy brings healing.

[18] Refuse correction and you'll end up broke and despised. Welcome it and you'll be honored.

[19] A craving satisfied is sweet to the soul. That's why fools won't turn from their evil—they love the taste of it.

[20] Walk with the wise and become wise. Run with fools and you'll be wrecked.

[21] Trouble chases sinners down. Prosperity tracks the righteous home.

[22] A good man leaves an inheritance to his grandchildren. The sinner's stockpile? It ends up in the hands of the righteous.

[23] The poor man's field could yield a feast—but injustice sweeps it all away.

[24] Spare the rod and you hate your child. Love them enough to discipline them.

[25] The righteous eat until they're full. The belly of the wicked is always empty.

[1] A wise woman builds her house; a foolish one tears it down with her own hands.

[2] Walk straight and you honor the LORD; walk crooked and you despise him.

[3] A fool's mouth grows a rod for his own back; the wise speak words that guard them.

[4] Empty stalls mean a clean barn — but it takes an ox to bring in the harvest.

[5] An honest witness will not lie; a false witness breathes deceit like air.

[6] The mocker hunts for wisdom and comes up empty; the discerning find it lying everywhere.

[7] Walk away from a fool — you will not find a single word worth keeping on his lips.

[8] Wisdom for the prudent is knowing where they are going; folly for fools is the lie they tell themselves.

[9] Fools laugh off their guilt; among the upright there is grace.

[10] The heart knows its own bitterness, and no stranger shares its joy.

[11] The house of the wicked will fall; the tent of the upright will stand and flourish.

[12] There is a road that looks right — and it ends at the grave.

[13] Even in laughter the heart can ache, and joy can collapse into grief.

[14] The backslider gets a bellyful of his own ways; the good person, a bellyful of his own.

[15] The naive swallow every word; the prudent watch where they step.

[16] The wise fear and turn from evil; the fool charges in, reckless and sure.

[17] A short fuse acts the fool; a schemer earns nothing but hate.

[18] The naive inherit folly; the prudent are crowned with knowledge.

[19] Sooner or later the wicked bow before the good, and the guilty at the gates of the righteous.

[20] The poor are abandoned even by their neighbors; the rich have friends to spare.

[21] Despise your neighbor and you sin; show kindness to the poor and you are blessed.

[22] Those who plot evil — do they not lose their way? Those who plan good find loyalty and truth.

[23] Hard work always pays; talk alone leaves you poor.

[24] Wealth is a crown to the wise; folly produces nothing but more folly.

[25] A truthful witness saves lives; a liar betrays them.

[26] The fear of the LORD is a fortress, and a refuge for one's children.

[27] The fear of the LORD is a wellspring of life, pulling you back from the snares of death.

[28] A king's glory is a vast people; a thin population is a ruler's ruin.

[29] Slow to anger, deep in understanding; quick-tempered, deep in folly.

[30] A peaceful heart gives life to the body; envy rots the bones.

[31] Crush the poor and you insult their Maker; show kindness to the needy and you honor him.

[32] The wicked are flattened by their own evil; the righteous have refuge even in death.

[33] Wisdom rests quietly in the discerning heart; among fools, it makes itself known by its absence.

[34] Righteousness lifts a nation; sin is the disgrace of any people.

[35] A wise servant wins the king's favor; a shameful one feels his fury.

[1] A soft answer defuses rage; a sharp word sets it ablaze.

[2] The wise tongue makes knowledge attractive; the fool's mouth gushes nonsense.

[3] The LORD's eyes are everywhere, tracking the wicked and the good alike.

[4] A healing tongue is a tree of life; a twisted one shatters the spirit.

[5] A fool sneers at his father's correction; the one who takes the rebuke grows shrewd.

[6] The house of the righteous holds real wealth; the harvest of the wicked brings only trouble.

[7] The lips of the wise scatter knowledge like seed; the heart of the fool has nothing to give.

[8] The sacrifice of the wicked disgusts the LORD; the prayer of the upright delights him.

[9] The LORD detests the road the wicked walk; he loves the one chasing righteousness.

[10] Harsh discipline waits for the one who abandons the path; whoever hates correction will die.

[11] Death and destruction lie open before the LORD — how much more the human heart.

[12] The mocker refuses correction; he will not go near the wise.

[13] A happy heart lights up the face; a broken heart crushes the spirit.

[14] The discerning mind hunts for knowledge; the mouth of fools feasts on stupidity.

[15] Every day of the afflicted feels grim; the cheerful heart throws a feast that never ends.

[16] Better a little with the fear of the LORD than a fortune wrapped in chaos.

[17] Better a bowl of vegetables where love is than steak served with hatred.

[18] A hot-tempered man kindles fights; the patient one quiets them.

[19] The lazy man's road is choked with thorns; the upright walk a highway.

[20] A wise son makes his father glad; a foolish one despises his mother.

[21] Folly delights the empty-headed; the discerning man walks straight ahead.

[22] Plans collapse without counsel; with many advisers, they stand.

[23] There's joy in giving the right answer — and a word at the right moment, how good it is.

[22] Listen to the father who gave you life. Don't despise your mother when she grows old. [23] Buy truth and never sell it — buy wisdom, instruction, understanding.

[26] My son — give me your heart. Let your eyes hold steady on my ways. [27] A prostitute is a deep pit. A loose woman is a narrow well. [28] She lies in wait like a bandit and multiplies the unfaithful among men.

[29] Who has misery? Who has sorrow? Who has fights, complaints, bruises out of nowhere, bloodshot eyes? [30] Those who linger over wine. Those who go searching for the next mixed drink.

[31] Don't even look at wine when it glows red, when it sparkles in the cup, when it goes down smooth. [32] In the end it bites like a serpent. It strikes like a viper. [33] Your eyes will see strange things. Your mind will spit out twisted words. [34] You'll feel like a man asleep on the open sea, sprawled across the top of a swaying mast. [35] "They hit me," you'll say, "but I felt nothing. They beat me, but I don't remember. When will I wake up? I need another drink."

[1] Don't envy the wicked. Don't crave their company. [2] Their hearts are factories of violence; their lips manufacture trouble.

[10] If you collapse on the day of pressure, your strength was never much to begin with.

[13] Eat honey, my son — it's good. The comb is sweet on your tongue. [14] Know that wisdom is the same to your soul. Find it, and there's a future. Your hope will not be cut off.

[15] Don't lie in wait like a criminal at the home of the righteous. Don't ransack his resting place. [16] The righteous falls seven times and gets back up. The wicked are leveled by a single disaster.

[23] More from the wise:

Playing favorites in court is wrong. [24] Tell the guilty, "You're innocent," and nations will curse you, peoples will damn your name. [25] But those who convict the guilty — they'll be blessed. A rich blessing will fall on them.

[26] An honest answer is like a kiss on the lips.

[30] I walked past the field of a sluggard, past the vineyard of a man without sense. [31] Thorns everywhere. Weeds had swallowed it whole. The stone wall lay in ruins. [32] I looked. I took it in. I saw it and learned: [33] A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest — [34] and poverty arrives like a prowler, want like a man with a weapon.

[10] Don't abandon your friend — or your father's friend. And when disaster hits, don't run to a brother across town. A neighbor close by beats a brother far away.

[11] Be wise, my son. Make my heart glad. Then I'll have an answer for anyone who mocks me.

[12] The clear-eyed see trouble coming and take cover. The naive walk straight into it and pay the price.

[13] If a man pledges himself for a stranger, take his coat. If he backs a foreigner, hold it as collateral.

[14] Bless your neighbor too loudly, too early in the morning, and he'll hear it as a curse.

[15] A nagging wife is the steady drip of rain on a leaky roof — endless, maddening.

[16] Try to restrain her and you might as well bottle the wind or grab oil with your bare hand.

[17] Iron sharpens iron. One man sharpens another.

[18] Tend the fig tree and you'll eat its fruit. Guard your master and you'll be honored.

[19] Water mirrors the face. The heart mirrors the man.

[20] The grave is never full. Neither is human desire.

[21] Silver is tested in the crucible, gold in the furnace, and a man by the praise he receives.

[22] Grind a fool in a mortar with a pestle, crush him with the grain itself — his foolishness will not come out of him.

[23] Know your flocks. Pay attention to your herds.

[24] Wealth doesn't last forever. Neither does a crown, generation to generation.

[25] When the hay is cut and the new growth appears and the mountain grasses are gathered —

[26] your lambs will clothe you, your goats will buy your field,

[27] and there will be enough goat's milk to feed you, feed your household, and keep your servant girls alive.

[1] The wicked run when no one's chasing them. The righteous stand their ground like lions.

[2] When a country rebels, rulers multiply like rabbits. But under one wise leader who knows what he's doing, order holds.

[3] A poor man who crushes the poor is a downpour that wipes out the crop instead of feeding it.

[4] Abandon the law and you'll cheer for criminals. Keep it and you'll fight them.

[5] Evil men understand nothing about justice. Those who seek God understand it all.

[6] Better to be poor and walk straight than rich and walk crooked.

[7] A son with sense keeps the law. A son who runs with gluttons humiliates his father.

[8] Pile up wealth by gouging interest and you're just collecting it for someone who'll spend it on the poor.

[9] Plug your ears to the law, and even your prayers stink to heaven.

[10] Lead good people down a bad road and you'll fall into your own pit. The innocent inherit good things.

[11] The rich man thinks he's wise. A poor man with insight sees right through him.

[12] When the righteous win, everyone celebrates. When the wicked rise, people go into hiding.

[13] Cover your sins and you'll never get free. Confess and walk away from them — that's where mercy lives.

[14] Blessed is the one who never stops fearing God. Harden your heart and you're heading for disaster.

[15] A wicked ruler over helpless people is a roaring lion, a charging bear.

[16] A tyrant without understanding becomes a brutal oppressor. The ruler who hates dirty money will live long.

[17] A man crushed by the blood he's shed will run all the way to the grave. Don't anyone stop him.

[18] Walk with integrity and you'll be rescued. Walk twisted paths and you'll fall — suddenly, completely.

[19] Work your land and you'll eat your fill. Chase fantasies and you'll eat poverty.

[20] The faithful man is drenched in blessings. The man scrambling to get rich won't escape punishment.

[21] Playing favorites is wrong — and a man will sell out his soul for a piece of bread.

[22] The greedy man sprints toward wealth, never noticing the poverty closing in behind him.

[23] Confront someone honestly and in the end they'll thank you more than the flatterer ever could.

[24] Rob your own parents and tell yourself it's nothing — you're brother to a destroyer.

[25] The greedy stir up fights. Those who trust the LORD live full lives.

[26] Trust your own gut and you're a fool. Walk in wisdom and you'll come out alive.

[27] Give to the poor and you'll never lack. Look the other way and curses pile up against you.

[28] When the wicked rise, people disappear. When they fall, the righteous flourish.

[1] The man who keeps stiffening his neck under correction will shatter one day — suddenly, beyond repair.

[2] When the righteous rise, the people celebrate. When the wicked seize power, the people groan.

[3] A son who loves wisdom makes his father glad. A son who runs with prostitutes burns through everything.

[4] A king brings stability to the land through justice. A king who takes bribes tears it apart.

[5] Flattery is a trap — and the one laying it for his neighbor is walking right into it himself.

[6] An evil man is ensnared by his own sin. The righteous run free, singing.

[7] The righteous understand the case of the poor. The wicked cannot even pretend to care.

[8] Cynics set cities on fire. The wise turn the rage aside.

[9] If a wise man tangles with a fool in court, expect noise — raging, mocking, and nothing resolved.

[10] Bloodthirsty men despise the man of integrity, but the upright seek out his life.

[11] A fool unloads everything inside him. A wise man holds it back, waiting.

[12] Let a ruler give ear to lies, and every official under him turns wicked.

[13] The poor man and the oppressor share one thing — the LORD gives light to both their eyes.

[14] A king who judges the poor with honesty — his throne stands secure for generations.

[15] The rod and rebuke grow wisdom. A child left to himself shames his mother.

[16] When the wicked multiply, sin multiplies — but the righteous will watch them fall.

[17] Discipline your son, and he will give you rest. He will become a delight to your soul.

[18] Without vision from God, the people run wild. The one who guards the teaching — that one is blessed.

[19] You cannot correct a servant with words alone. He hears you and does nothing.

[20] You have seen a man quick with his mouth? There is more hope for a fool than for him.

[21] Indulge a servant from boyhood, and in the end he will think the house is his.

[22] An angry man stirs up strife. A hot-tempered man piles up sin.

[23] Pride brings a man low. Humility hands him honor.

[24] Partner with a thief, and you become your own enemy. You hear the curse and stay silent — and the silence damns you.

[25] Fear of man lays a snare. Trust in the LORD lifts you above it.

[26] Many crowd the ruler's door looking for favor. But justice for a man comes from the LORD.

[27] The righteous despise injustice. The wicked despise the straight path. Two worlds. They cannot share a room.

[1] The words of Agur, son of Jakeh — the oracle. This man's confession to Ithiel, to Ithiel and Ucal:

[2] I am more brute than human. I do not have the understanding of a man.

[3] I never learned wisdom. I have no knowledge of the Holy One.

[4] Who has climbed to heaven and come back down? Who has gathered the wind in the cup of his hands? Who has wrapped the seas in his cloak? Who set the boundaries of the earth? What is his name — and his son's name? Surely you know.

[5] Every word God speaks is forged in fire. He is a shield to everyone who runs to him.

[6] Do not add to his words, or he will rebuke you and expose you as a liar.

[7] Two things I ask of you — do not refuse me before I die:

[8] Keep deception and lies far from me. Give me neither poverty nor wealth — feed me only the bread I need.

[9] Or I might have too much and disown you, asking, "Who is the LORD?" Or I might have nothing, steal, and drag your name through the mud.

[10] Do not slander a servant to his master. He will curse you, and you will pay for it.

[11] There is a breed that curses its father and refuses to bless its mother.

[12] A breed pure in its own eyes — and never washed clean of its filth.

[13] A breed with eyes lifted high, eyelids arched in contempt.

[14] A breed whose teeth are swords, whose jaws are knives — devouring the poor off the earth, the needy out from among humanity.

[15] The leech has two daughters: "Give!" "Give!" Three things never have enough. Four never say, "Enough":

[16] the grave, the barren womb, land that drinks and drinks but stays parched, and fire that never says, "Stop."

[17] The eye that mocks a father and despises a mother's old age — the ravens of the valley will peck it out, the young vultures will devour it.

[18] Three things astonish me. Four I cannot fathom:

[19] the way of an eagle through the sky, the way of a snake on a rock, the way of a ship through open sea, and the way of a man with a woman.

[20] This is the way of an adulteress: she eats, wipes her mouth, and says, "I have done nothing wrong."

[21] Under three things the earth trembles. Under four it cannot bear up:

[22] a slave who becomes king, a fool stuffed full of food,

[23] a hated woman who finally gets married, and a servant girl who supplants her mistress.

[24] Four things on earth are small — and wiser than the wise:

[25] ants, a people without strength, yet they store up their food in summer;

[26] rock badgers, a people without power, yet they make their homes in the cliffs;

[27] locusts, who have no king, yet they advance in perfect ranks;

[28] the lizard, you can catch it with your hand, yet it lives in kings' palaces.

[29] Three things stride with majesty. Four move with regal grace:

[30] the lion, mightiest of beasts, who retreats from nothing;

[31] the strutting rooster, the he-goat, and a king at the head of his army.

[32] If you have played the fool by exalting yourself — or if you have schemed — put your hand over your mouth.

[33] Churn milk, you get butter. Twist a nose, you get blood. Stir up anger, you get a fight.

[1] The words of King Lemuel — the oracle his mother drilled into him:

[2] "What, my son? What, son of my womb? What, son of my vows?

[3] Don't pour your strength into women. Don't waste your ways on those who bring kings to ruin.

[4] Kings have no business with wine, Lemuel — kings have no business with wine, rulers no business craving strong drink.

[5] One drink and they forget the law. One drink and they twist justice for the crushed.

[6] Give strong drink to the dying. Give wine to the bitter of soul.

[7] Let them drink and forget their poverty. Let them remember their misery no more.

[8] Open your mouth for the voiceless, for the cause of all who are slipping through the cracks.

[9] Open your mouth. Judge with justice. Defend the poor and the needy."

[10] A woman of valor — who can find her? Her worth runs deeper than rubies.

[11] Her husband's heart is anchored in her. He will not lack a thing.

[12] She is good to him, never harm, every day of her life.

[13] She hunts down wool and flax and works it with eager hands.

[14] Like a merchant fleet, she hauls in provisions from distant shores.

[15] She rises while the night is still dark — sets food on the table for her household, marching orders for her servant girls.

[16] She eyes a field and acquires it. From the fruit of her own hands she plants a vineyard.

[17] She straps strength around her waist. She flexes her arms for the work.

[18] She knows her trade is thriving. Her lamp does not go out at night.

[19] Her hands seize the distaff. Her fingers grip the spindle.

[20] She opens her palm to the poor. She stretches out her hands to the needy.

[21] Snow falls and she does not flinch — her whole household is wrapped in scarlet.

[22] She weaves her own coverings. Her clothing is fine linen and royal purple.

[23] Her husband is known at the city gates, seated among the elders of the land.

[24] She tailors linen garments and sells them. She supplies the merchants with sashes.

[25] Strength and dignity are her clothing. She laughs at the days to come.

[26] She opens her mouth with wisdom. The law of kindness rides on her tongue.

[27] She watches the ways of her household. The bread of idleness — she will not touch it.

[28] Her children rise up and bless her. Her husband too — he praises her:

[29] "Many women have done powerfully — but you, you have surpassed them all."

[30] Charm lies. Beauty fades. But a woman who fears the LORD — she is the one to praise.

[31] Give her the fruit of her hands. Let her own works praise her at the gates.

[1] Blessed is the one who refuses the counsel of the corrupt, who will not loiter on the road the wicked walk, who will not pull up a chair where cynics hold court.

[2] His delight is the instruction of the LORD, and he turns it over in his mind day and night.

[3] He is a tree planted at the water's edge — yielding its fruit in season, its leaves never withering. Everything he sets his hand to thrives.

[4] Not so the wicked. Not so at all. They are chaff — whatever the wind decides.

[5] So when judgment falls, the wicked will not stand their ground; sinners will find no place among the gathered righteous.

[6] The LORD knows the road the righteous travel — but the road of the wicked runs into the dark and ends.

[1] Why do the nations seethe? Why do the peoples mutter their useless plots? [2] Earth's kings rise up, rulers huddle together — scheming against the LORD and against his Anointed. [3] "Let's snap their chains," they say. "Let's throw off their ropes."

[4] The One enthroned in heaven laughs. The Lord mocks them. [5] Then he speaks — and his fury detonates, his rage leaves them shaking: [6] "I have installed my King on Zion, my holy mountain."

[7] Let me announce the decree of the LORD. He said to me: "You are my Son. Today I have fathered you. [8] Ask me — I'll hand you the nations as your inheritance, the ends of the earth as your possession. [9] You'll break them with an iron scepter, shatter them like a clay pot."

[10] So wise up, you kings. Take warning, you rulers of the earth. [11] Serve the LORD with reverence — rejoice, but tremble while you do. [12] Kiss the Son, or his anger will flare and you'll be destroyed on the road, for his wrath ignites in an instant. Blessed is everyone who takes refuge in him.

[1] God — look how they've multiplied against me. So many rising up.

[2] So many of them saying about my soul: "There's no rescue for him in God." *Selah.*

[3] But you, God — you're a shield wrapped around me, my glory, the one who lifts my head.

[4] I cry out to the Lord with my voice, and from his holy mountain he answers. *Selah.*

[5] I lay down. I slept. I woke up — because the Lord holds me together.

[6] I will not fear ten thousand of them, drawn up against me on every side.

[7] Rise up, Lord. Save me, my God. You've struck every enemy across the jaw. You've shattered the teeth of the wicked.

[8] Rescue belongs to the Lord. Your blessing falls on your people. *Selah.*

[1] Answer me when I call, God of my vindication. You opened space for me when I was crushed in on every side. Show me that grace again — hear my prayer.

[2] How long, you sons of status, will you drag my honor through the mud? How long will you chase empty things and hunt after lies?

[3] Know this: the LORD has set apart the one devoted to him. The LORD hears when I call to him.

[4] Tremble, and stop sinning. On your bed, in the dark, search your heart — and be still.

[5] Bring the offerings that count, the ones that cost you something. And trust the LORD.

[6] Everyone's asking, "Who's going to show us something good?" Lift the light of your face on us, LORD —

[7] You've put more joy in my heart than they get when the grain and the wine are pouring in.

[8] I lie down and I sleep — in peace. Because you alone, LORD, make me dwell secure.

[1] Listen to my words, LORD. Hear the groaning I can't put into language.
[2] Pay attention to the sound of my cry, my King, my God — because it's you I'm praying to.
[3] At sunrise, LORD, you hear my voice. At sunrise I lay it all out before you and wait, watching.
[4] You are not a God who takes pleasure in evil. Wickedness can't share space with you.
[5] The arrogant don't get to stand in your presence. You hate everyone who traffics in destruction.
[6] You destroy those who deal in lies. The LORD recoils from the violent and the deceitful.
[7] But me — through the flood of your faithful love — I get to enter your house. I bow toward your holy temple in awe of you.
[8] Lead me, LORD, in your righteousness — my enemies are circling. Make your path straight in front of me.
[9] Nothing they say can be trusted. Their insides are ruin. Their throat is an open grave. Their tongue is a smooth knife.
[10] Hold them accountable, God. Let their own schemes collapse on top of them. Throw them out for all their rebellion — they've defied you.
[11] But everyone who takes shelter in you — let them sing. Let them shout in joy forever. Spread your protection over them, and let those who love your name celebrate in you.
[12] Because you, LORD, bless the righteous. You wrap them in favor like a shield.

[1] LORD, don't rip into me in your anger. Don't discipline me while your fury still burns.

[2] Have mercy on me, LORD — I'm running on empty. Heal me, LORD — my bones are shaking.

[3] My soul is shattered. And you, LORD — how long? How long?

[4] Turn back to me, LORD. Pull my life out of this. Save me — for the sake of your own faithful love.

[5] Because in death, no one remembers you. From the grave, who sings your praise?

[6] I'm worn out from groaning. Every night I drown my bed in tears, soak my pillow through.

[7] My eyes have gone dim from grief, blurred from all the enemies pressing in.

[8] Get away from me, all of you who deal in evil — the LORD has heard me weeping.

[9] The LORD has heard my plea. The LORD takes my prayer.

[10] Every one of my enemies will know shame, gripped by terror. They'll turn and run — and in an instant, disgrace will catch them.

[1] LORD my God, I run to you for shelter. Save me from the pack at my heels — rescue me, [2] or they'll tear me apart like a lion, shredding me with no one to pull them off.

[3] LORD my God, if I've done this thing — if there's blood on my hands, [4] if I've betrayed a friend at peace with me or robbed an enemy without cause — [5] then let the enemy chase me down and catch me. Let him grind my life into the dirt and leave my honor in the dust.

[6] Rise up, LORD, in your fury. Stand tall against the rage of my enemies. Wake up — you ordered justice into being.

[7] Let the nations gather around you. Take your seat above them, high up. [8] The LORD judges the peoples. Judge me, LORD — by my integrity, by the clean record in my hands.

[9] End the violence of the wicked. Hold the righteous steady. You are the God who reads hearts and weighs minds — the God who sees through everyone.

[10] My shield is God himself, who saves the honest in heart. [11] God is a righteous judge — a God whose anger burns every single day.

[12] If a man won't turn back, God sharpens his sword. He strings his bow and takes aim. [13] His weapons are loaded. His arrows are tipped with fire.

[14] Watch the wicked man: he conceives malice, swells up pregnant with trouble, and gives birth to lies. [15] He digs a pit, hollows it out — and falls headlong into the hole he made. [16] His violence loops back on his own skull. His brutality crashes down on his own head.

[17] I'll thank the LORD for his justice. I'll sing the name of the LORD Most High.

[1] LORD, our Lord — your name thunders across the whole earth. You've stretched your glory above the heavens themselves.

[2] From the mouths of infants and nursing babies you've built a fortress against your enemies — silencing every voice that rises against you.

[3] When I look up at your sky — the work of your fingers — the moon and stars you set in place,

[4] what is a human being that you'd even think of us? What are we that you'd come looking for us?

[5] Yet you made us just a little lower than the divine — crowned us with glory, crowned us with honor.

[6] You put us in charge of everything your hands have made. You laid it all at our feet —

[7] flocks and herds, every wild thing roaming the open country,

[8] birds in the sky, fish in the sea, every creature that travels the ocean paths.

[9] LORD, our Lord — your name thunders across the whole earth.

[1] I will thank You, LORD, with everything I am. I will tell every wonder You have done.
[2] I will celebrate. I will sing Your name, Most High.
[3] My enemies turn and run — they stumble, they vanish from Your face.
[4] You upheld my case. You took the throne and judged with a clean verdict.
[5] You roared at the nations. You buried the wicked. You wiped their name out — forever, gone.
[6] The enemy is finished — ruins without end. You uprooted their cities. Even the memory of them is dust.
[7] But the LORD sits enthroned forever. He has set His seat for justice,
[8] and He will judge the world in righteousness, deliver verdicts on the peoples with perfect equity.
[9] The LORD is a high fortress for the crushed, a stronghold when the times go dark.
[10] Those who know Your name will trust You — because You, LORD, have never abandoned anyone who searched for You.
[11] Sing to the LORD, enthroned in Zion. Tell the nations what He has done.
[12] He remembers. He hunts down the blood of the murdered. He does not forget the cry of the afflicted.
[13] Show me mercy, LORD. Look at what my haters are doing to me. Lift me from the gates of death,
[14] so I can stand at the gates of Daughter Zion and shout Your praise — celebrating Your rescue.
[15] The nations dropped into the pit they were digging. Their own hidden net snared their feet.
[16] The LORD has revealed Himself — justice executed. The wicked are trapped in the work of their own hands. *Higgaion. Selah.*
[17] The wicked turn back to the grave — every nation that forgets God.
[18] But the needy will not be forgotten forever. The hope of the afflicted will not perish in the end.
[19] Rise up, LORD. Do not let mortals win. Drag the nations before You for judgment.
[20] Strike them with terror, LORD. Let the nations know they are only flesh. *Selah.*

[1] Why do you stand so far off, LORD? Why hide yourself when everything's falling apart?

[2] The wicked, swollen with pride, hunt the poor like prey— let them be caught in the very traps they set.

[3] The wicked man brags about what he craves. He blesses the greedy and spits on the LORD.

[4] Nose in the air, he never seeks God. Every thought ends the same way: *There is no God.*

[5] His schemes succeed. Always. Your judgments are too high up for him to notice, and he sneers at anyone who stands in his way.

[6] He tells himself, "Nothing can shake me. Trouble? Never. Not in a thousand years."

[7] His mouth is full of curses, lies, and threats. Mischief and malice sit on his tongue.

[8] He crouches in alleys, ambushes the innocent in shadowed corners, eyes fixed on the helpless.

[9] He waits like a lion in its thicket— waits to drag off the poor, snares them, hauls them away in his net.

[10] His victims crumple, broken, crushed beneath his strength.

[11] He tells himself, "God has forgotten. He's looking the other way. He'll never see."

[12] Rise up, LORD. Lift your hand, O God. Do not forget the afflicted.

[13] Why does the wicked man get to spit on God and tell himself, "You'll never call me to account"?

[14] But you do see. You take in every cruelty and every grief, weighing it in your hand. The helpless commit themselves to you— you have always been the orphan's defender.

[15] Break the arm of the wicked. Hunt down his evil until there is nothing left to find.

[16] The LORD is King— forever, beyond forever. The nations will vanish from his land.

[17] You hear the longing of the humble, LORD. You steady their hearts. You bend down to listen,

[18] to vindicate the orphan and the crushed, so that mere mortals of dust will terrorize them no more.

[1] I've taken shelter in the LORD. So how can you say to me, "Fly to the mountains like a bird"? [2] Look — the wicked are stringing their bows, fitting arrows to the string, waiting in the shadows to shoot down anyone whose heart runs straight. [3] When the foundations crumble, what's a righteous person to do?

[4] The LORD is in his holy temple. The LORD's throne stands in the heavens. His eyes are watching. His gaze tests every human alive. [5] He weighs the righteous and the wicked both — and the one who loves violence, his soul despises. [6] On the wicked he'll rain down fire and sulfur and a scorching wind — that's the cup they drink. [7] Because the LORD is righteous. He loves what is right. And the upright will see his face.

[1] Help, Lord — the faithful are vanishing. The loyal have disappeared from the human race.

[2] Everyone lies to their neighbor. Smooth lips, double hearts, words that mean nothing.

[3] Lord, silence every slick tongue, every mouth that brags itself big —

[4] the ones who say, "Our words are our weapons. Our lips belong to us. Who's our master?"

[5] "Because the poor are crushed, because the needy groan — now I rise," says the Lord. "I will set them in the safety they were denied."

[6] The Lord's words are pure words, silver refined in a furnace of earth, purified seven times over.

[7] You, Lord, will guard them. You will shield us from this generation, always.

[8] The wicked strut on every side when garbage is what the world holds up as worthy.

[1] How long, LORD? Will you forget me forever? How long will you hide your face from me?

[2] How long must I wrestle with my own thoughts, grief settled in my chest day after day? How long will my enemy stand over me?

[3] Look at me. Answer me, LORD my God. Light up my eyes again, or I'll close them in death—

[4] and my enemy will say, "I broke him," and everyone who wants me down will celebrate when I fall.

[5] But I—I have staked everything on your faithful love. My heart will dance because you rescue.

[6] I will sing to the LORD. He has been good to me.

[1] The fool whispers in his heart: *There is no God.*
And from that single lie, rot spreads —
their deeds turn vile, every last one.
Not one of them does good.

[2] The LORD leans down from heaven,
scanning the human race,
hunting for someone — anyone —
who actually gets it,
anyone still searching for him.

[3] What he finds is wreckage.
Every one of them has bolted.
Together they've gone bad.
No one does good.
Not a single soul.

[4] Do these evildoers learn nothing?
They devour my people like bread at the table
and never once call on the LORD.

[5] But terror is coming for them —
sheer dread —
because God stands with those who are his.

[6] You mock the dreams of the broken,
but the LORD is their shelter.

[7] Let rescue for Israel break out of Zion!
When the LORD turns his people's fortunes around —
Jacob will erupt in joy,
Israel will sing.

[1] Lord, who gets to stay in your tent? Who gets to live on your holy mountain?

[2] The one who walks with integrity, who does what is right, who speaks truth from the core of who they are.

[3] The one whose tongue carries no poison, who does no harm to a friend, who refuses to traffic in slander against a neighbor.

[4] The one who looks at the corrupt with clear-eyed contempt but honors those who fear the Lord. The one who makes a promise that costs them — and keeps it anyway.

[5] The one who lends without gouging, who won't be bought to ruin the innocent.

Whoever lives like this will never be shaken.

[1] Keep me safe, God — I've taken shelter in you.

[2] I told the LORD, "You are my Master. Every good thing I have starts with you."

[3] As for the holy ones in the land — the magnificent ones — they hold all my delight.

[4] But those who chase other gods multiply their sorrows. I refuse to pour out their blood offerings, refuse to let their names cross my lips.

[5] The LORD himself is my portion, my cup. You hold my future in your hands.

[6] The boundary lines have fallen for me in beautiful places. What an inheritance — stunning.

[7] I bless the LORD who counsels me; even in the night my heart instructs me.

[8] I keep the LORD in front of me always. With him at my right hand, nothing can shake me.

[9] So my heart celebrates, my whole being sings out, and even my body rests in safety.

[10] Because you will not abandon me to the grave or let your faithful one see decay.

[11] You show me the path of life. In your presence — joy overflowing. At your right hand — pleasures that never end.

[1] Hear me out, LORD — this is a just cause. Listen to my cry. Pay attention to my prayer. It comes from lips that aren't lying.

[2] Let the verdict come from your presence. Let your eyes see what's straight.

[3] You've X-rayed my heart, examined me at night, put me through the fire — and found nothing. I made up my mind: my mouth wouldn't betray me.

[4] As for what people do — by the word from your lips I've stayed off the violent man's road.

[5] My steps have held to your paths. My feet have not slipped.

[6] I'm calling on you, God, because you answer. Bend your ear to me. Hear what I'm saying.

[7] Show me the wonder of your unfailing love — you, the rescuer of all who run to your right hand for shelter from those who rise up against them.

[8] Guard me like the pupil of your eye. Hide me in the shadow of your wings

[9] from the wicked who tear at me, from deadly enemies closing in on every side.

[10] They've sealed off their hearts in fat. Their mouths speak nothing but arrogance.

[11] They've tracked me down — now they surround me, eyes fixed, ready to throw me to the ground.

[12] He's a lion hungry to rip me apart, a young lion crouched in the shadows.

[13] Rise up, LORD. Get in his face. Bring him down. With your sword, deliver my life from the wicked.

[14] With your hand, LORD — from these people, from people of this world whose portion is only this life. You fill their bellies with what you've stored up. Their children get their fill. They leave the leftovers to their kids.

[15] But me — I'll see your face in righteousness. When I wake, I'll be satisfied just to see your likeness.
